topics.title: Fundamentals in Computer Vision: Camera Calibration - Pinhole Camera Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST745 - COMPUTER VISION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Fundamentals in Computer Vision: Camera Calibration - Pinhole Camera Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Camera Calibration?

**Camera Calibration** is the process of estimating the intrinsic (internal) and extrinsic (external) parameters of a camera to establish a precise mathematical mapping between **3D world coordinates** and **2D image pixel coordinates**. It corrects for lens distortions and provides the geometric backbone for tasks like 3D reconstruction, stereo vision, object tracking, and augmented reality.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Camera calibration is the foundational prerequisite for almost every higher-level computer vision algorithm. Examiner questions typically test the projection equation, the intrinsic matrix, and the concept of extrinsic transformation.

## 1.2 The Pinhole Camera Model — Formal Definition

The **Pinhole Camera Model** (also called the *central projection model*) is the simplest and most widely used mathematical abstraction of an imaging system. It assumes that light from a scene passes through an infinitesimally small aperture (the "pinhole") and projects an inverted image onto an image plane behind it.

The model maps a 3D point $\mathbf{M} = (X, Y, Z)^T$ in the world coordinate system to a 2D point $\mathbf{m} = (u, v)^T$ in the image plane using the central projection equation:

$$s \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \mathbf{K} \, [\mathbf{R} \mid \mathbf{t}] \, \begin{bmatrix} X \\ Y \\ Z \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Where:
- $s$ is an arbitrary **scale factor** (depth).
- $\mathbf{K}$ is the **$3 \times 3$ intrinsic parameter matrix**.
- $[\mathbf{R} \mid \mathbf{t}]$ is the **$3 \times 4$ extrinsic parameter matrix** (rotation + translation).
- The right-hand $\mathbf{M}$ is a **homogeneous 4-vector**.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Camera Obscura" 🕯️

Imagine you are standing inside a **darkened closed room** (the *camera obscura*) with one tiny hole pierced in the wall. Outside, a bright tree is glowing in the sun. Light rays from the tree travel in straight lines, pass through the pinhole, and strike the back wall of the room, projecting an **inverted, reversed image** of the tree.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The pinhole is so tiny that, ideally, only **one ray** from each scene point reaches the image plane. This guarantees a sharp, focused image. Real cameras approximate this by using a lens to gather more light while still producing a focused image.

**Real-world connection:** The basic principle of the pinhole was used by Renaissance painters (e.g., Vermeer) as a drawing aid. Today, every smartphone, DSLR, and depth sensor uses a mathematically equivalent model.

## 1.4 Key Terminology

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Pinhole / Optical Center** | The point through which all light rays pass (also the origin of the camera coordinate system). |
| **Optical Axis** | The line perpendicular to the image plane, passing through the pinhole. |
| **Focal Length ($f$)** | The distance from the pinhole to the image plane. Typically measured in **millimeters (mm)** or **pixels**. |
| **Principal Point ($c_x, c_y$)** | The pixel coordinates where the optical axis intersects the image plane. Ideally at the center, but usually offset. |
| **Image Plane** | The 2D plane where the inverted scene is projected. |
| **World Coordinate System** | Global 3D reference frame attached to the scene. |
| **Camera Coordinate System** | Local 3D reference frame with origin at the pinhole, $Z$-axis along the optical axis. |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick:** Think **"Pin → Plane"** — light goes **from** the pinhole **to** the plane, the geometric heart of the entire model.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Coordinate Systems

To perform calibration, we must transition between **three** coordinate systems:

1. **World Coordinate System (WCS):** $(X_w, Y_w, Z_w)$ — global, scene-anchored.
2. **Camera Coordinate System (CCS):** $(X_c, Y_c, Z_c)$ — origin at pinhole, $Z_c$ points outward.
3. **Image Coordinate System (ICS):** $(u, v)$ — pixel coordinates on the sensor/film.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Concept

### 🔹 Step 1: World → Camera Transformation (Extrinsic)
The rigid-body transformation from world to camera coordinates uses a **$3 \times 3$ rotation matrix** $\mathbf{R}$ and a **$3 \times 1$ translation vector** $\mathbf{t}$:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} X_c \\ Y_c \\ Z_c \end{bmatrix} = \mathbf{R} \begin{bmatrix} X_w \\ Y_w \\ Z_w \end{bmatrix} + \mathbf{t}
$$

In homogeneous form:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} X_c \\ Y_c \\ Z_c \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{R} & \mathbf{t} \\ \mathbf{0}^{T} & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X_w \\ Y_w \\ Z_w \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Extrinsic?** Because these parameters describe the camera's **pose (position + orientation)** with respect to the world — they are *external* to the camera itself.

### 🔹 Step 2: Camera → Image Plane (Perspective Projection)
Using similar triangles from the pinhole geometry:

$$u' = f \cdot \frac{X_c}{Z_c}, \quad v' = f \cdot \frac{Y_c}{Z_c}$$

This can be written in homogeneous matrix form as:

$$
Z_c \begin{bmatrix} u' \\ v' \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} f & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & f & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X_c \\ Y_c \\ Z_c \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### 🔹 Step 3: Image Plane → Pixel Coordinates (Intrinsic)
To convert from physical image-plane units (mm) to pixel coordinates, we apply:

$$u = \alpha \cdot u' + c_x, \quad v = \beta \cdot v' + c_y$$

Where $\alpha = f / s_x$ and $\beta = f / s_y$ are the **focal lengths expressed in pixels** along $x$ and $y$, and $s_x, s_y$ are the pixel physical dimensions. Including a skew parameter $\gamma$, the intrinsic matrix becomes:

$$\mathbf{K} = \begin{bmatrix} f_x & \gamma & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

## 2.3 The Complete Calibration Matrix

Combining all three steps, the full pinhole projection is:

$$s \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \mathbf{K} \, [\mathbf{R} \mid \mathbf{t}] \begin{bmatrix} X_w \\ Y_w \\ Z_w \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Where the full **$3 \times 4$ camera projection matrix** is:

$$\mathbf{P} = \mathbf{K} \, [\mathbf{R} \mid \mathbf{t}] = \begin{bmatrix} p_{11} & p_{12} & p_{13} & p_{14} \\ p_{21} & p_{22} & p_{23} & p_{24} \\ p_{31} & p_{32} & p_{33} & p_{34} \end{bmatrix}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The matrix $\mathbf{P}$ has **11 degrees of freedom**: 5 intrinsic (assuming $\gamma = 0$: $f_x, f_y, c_x, c_y$ and 0 skew = effectively 4) and 6 extrinsic (3 rotation + 3 translation). However, as a generic $3 \times 4$ matrix, it has 12 entries with one scale ambiguity = **11 DOF**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Quantity | Mathematical Form | Description / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Perspective Projection | $u' = f \cdot X_c / Z_c$, $v' = f \cdot Y_c / Z_c$ | mm units on image plane |
| 2 | World → Camera | $\mathbf{X}_c = \mathbf{R}\mathbf{X}_w + \mathbf{t}$ | Rigid transform |
| 3 | Intrinsic Matrix $\mathbf{K}$ | $\begin{bmatrix} f_x & \gamma & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | $3 \times 3$, in pixels |
| 4 | Full Projection | $s \mathbf{m} = \mathbf{K} [\mathbf{R} \mid \mathbf{t}] \mathbf{M}$ | Homogeneous, scale $s = Z_c$ |
| 5 | Number of DOF | 11 (5 intrinsic + 6 extrinsic) | One global scale ambiguity |
| 6 | Radial Distortion (Order 2) | $x_d = x(1 + k_1 r^2 + k_2 r^4)$ | $r^2 = x^2 + y^2$ |
| 7 | Tangential Distortion | $x_d = x + 2 p_1 x y + p_2 (r^2 + 2 x^2)$ | Lens decentering |
| 8 | Calibration Error | Reprojection Error (pixels) | Lower = better calibration |
| 9 | Homogeneous Conversion | $(X, Y, Z) \to (X, Y, Z, 1)$ | Adds depth row |
| 10 | Depth Recovery | $Z_c = s =$ row 3 of $\mathbf{P} \mathbf{M}$ | Inverse of projection |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Autonomous Vehicles:** Camera calibration enables accurate lane detection and distance estimation by mapping LiDAR points onto camera images (sensor fusion).
- **Medical Imaging (Endoscopy):** Calibrated monocular endoscopes allow surgeons to measure the size of internal structures from a single 2D view.
- **Augmented Reality (AR):** Apps like Pokémon GO require precise intrinsic parameters to anchor virtual objects seamlessly on real surfaces.
- **Photogrammetry & 3D Scanning:** Aerial drone images are stitched into 3D terrain models only after rigorous bundle-adjustment calibration.
- **Industrial Metrology:** Quality control on production lines uses calibrated cameras to measure sub-millimeter part dimensions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Full Projection Equation

### 🎯 Goal
Derive the relationship that takes a 3D world point $(X_w, Y_w, Z_w)$ and maps it to a 2D pixel point $(u, v)$.

---

### **Step 1: Rigid Body Transform (World → Camera)**

We apply a rotation $\mathbf{R}$ and translation $\mathbf{t}$:

$$\begin{bmatrix} X_c \\ Y_c \\ Z_c \end{bmatrix} = \mathbf{R}_{3 \times 3} \begin{bmatrix} X_w \\ Y_w \\ Z_w \end{bmatrix} + \mathbf{t}_{3 \times 1}$$

To write it compactly, we embed in homogeneous coordinates by appending a row $\begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{0} & 1 \end{bmatrix}$:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} X_c \\ Y_c \\ Z_c \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = 
\begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{R} & \mathbf{t} \\ \mathbf{0}^{T} & 1 \end{bmatrix} 
\begin{bmatrix} X_w \\ Y_w \\ Z_w \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Reasoning:** The $3 \times 3$ rotation matrix has 3 DOF, translation vector has 3 DOF, totaling 6 extrinsic parameters.

---

### **Step 2: Perspective Projection (Camera → Image Plane)**

From the geometry of similar triangles in the pinhole setup, any point $(X_c, Y_c, Z_c)$ projects onto the image plane at $(u', v')$ such that:

$$\frac{u'}{f} = \frac{X_c}{Z_c} \quad \Rightarrow \quad u' = f \cdot \frac{X_c}{Z_c}$$

$$\frac{v'}{f} = \frac{Y_c}{Z_c} \quad \Rightarrow \quad v' = f \cdot \frac{Y_c}{Z_c}$$

Expressed in homogeneous matrix form (multiplying both sides by $Z_c$):

$$
Z_c \begin{bmatrix} u' \\ v' \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = 
\begin{bmatrix} f & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & f & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} X_c \\ Y_c \\ Z_c \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Reasoning:** This $3 \times 4$ matrix embeds the focal length as a scaling factor and discards the depth (since we project from 3D to 2D).

---

### **Step 3: Pixel Conversion (Image Plane → Sensor Coordinates)**

Real camera sensors have **discrete pixels** of finite size $s_x, s_y$ (in mm/pixel), and the principal point is rarely at pixel $(0,0)$. We perform an affine transform from continuous image coordinates $(u', v')$ to discrete pixel coordinates $(u, v)$:

$$u = \frac{u'}{s_x} + c_x = \alpha \cdot u' + c_x, \quad v = \frac{v'}{s_y} + c_y = \beta \cdot v' + c_y$$

Here $\alpha = f / s_x$ and $\beta = f / s_y$ are the focal lengths **in pixel units**, and $(c_x, c_y)$ is the **principal point in pixels**.

Including a skew term $\gamma$ (which accounts for non-rectangular pixels), the matrix form is:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \sim 
\begin{bmatrix} \alpha & \gamma & c_x \\ 0 & \beta & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} u' \\ v' \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Reasoning:** Sensors are manufactured with non-orthogonal axes (skew $\gamma \neq 0$) and the lens may not be perfectly centered ($\mathbf{c} \neq 0$).

---

### **Step 4: Combine All Three Transformations**

Chaining the matrices:

$$
s \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} \alpha & \gamma & c_x \\ 0 & \beta & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}}_{\text{Intrinsic } \mathbf{K}} 
\cdot \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} f & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & f & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}}_{\text{Projection}}
\cdot \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{R} & \mathbf{t} \\ \mathbf{0}^{T} & 1 \end{bmatrix}}_{\text{Extrinsic}}
\begin{bmatrix} X_w \\ Y_w \\ Z_w \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Multiplying the first two matrices yields $\mathbf{K}$:

$$\mathbf{K} = \begin{bmatrix} f_x & \gamma & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad f_x = \alpha f, \quad f_y = \beta f$$

Final compact form:

$$
\boxed{\, s \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \mathbf{K} \, [\mathbf{R} \mid \mathbf{t}] \, \begin{bmatrix} X_w \\ Y_w \\ Z_w \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \,}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Radial Lens Distortion

Real lenses deviate from the idealized pinhole. **Radial distortion** causes image points to be displaced radially from the principal point. Let $(x, y)$ be normalized image coordinates (after $\mathbf{K}^{-1}$):

$$r^2 = x^2 + y^2$$

The distorted normalized coordinates are:

$$
x_d = x \left( 1 + k_1 r^2 + k_2 r^4 + k_3 r^6 \right)
$$

$$
y_d = y \left( 1 + k_1 r^2 + k_2 r^4 + k_3 r^6 \right)
$$

**Reasoning:** 
- The factor $(1 + k_1 r^2 + k_2 r^4 + \cdots)$ models barrel ($k_1 < 0$) or pincushion ($k_1 > 0$) distortion.
- $r^2, r^4, r^6$ are even powers because the distortion must be **radially symmetric** (same at angle $\theta$ and $-\theta$).

Tangential distortion (from lens decentering) is added as:

$$
x_d = x + 2 p_1 x y + p_2 (r^2 + 2 x^2)
$$

$$
y_d = y + p_1 (r^2 + 2 y^2) + 2 p_2 x y
$$

**Reasoning:** Tangential terms are **linear in $p_1, p_2$** because they model a small lateral shift of the lens — a first-order perturbation.

The final pixel coordinates are recovered by:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \end{bmatrix} = 
\begin{bmatrix} f_x & \gamma \\ 0 & f_y \end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} x_d \\ y_d \end{bmatrix} + 
\begin{bmatrix} c_x \\ c_y \end{bmatrix}
$$

---

## 3.3 Summary of Derivation Logic

| Step | Input | Operation | Output | Adds DOF |
|------|------|-----------|--------|----------|
| 1 | $(X_w, Y_w, Z_w)$ | Rotate + Translate via $[\mathbf{R} \mid \mathbf{t}]$ | $(X_c, Y_c, Z_c)$ | 6 |
| 2 | $(X_c, Y_c, Z_c)$ | Divide by $Z_c$, scale by $f$ | $(u', v')$ | 0 |
| 3 | $(u', v')$ | Affine map (scale, skew, shift) | $(u, v)$ | 5 |
| **Total** | 3D world point | Full chain | 2D pixel | **11 DOF** |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Pinhole Camera Geometry (Conceptual Schematic via Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SCN["3D WORLD SCENE"]
        O["Object Point P = Xw Yw Zw"]
    end

    subgraph CAM["CAMERA COORDINATE SYSTEM"]
        O -->|"Rigid Transform R t"| CC["Camera Point Pc = Xc Yc Zc"]
    end

    subgraph PROJ["PERSPECTIVE PROJECTION"]
        CC -->|"Divide by Zc, scale by f"| IP["Image Plane Point u' v' in mm"]
    end

    subgraph PIX["PIXEL COORDINATE SYSTEM"]
        IP -->|"Affine Map K Matrix"| PX["Pixel Point u v in pixels"]
    end

    PIN["Pinhole / Optical Center"] -.->|"f = focal length"| IP
    PIN -.->|"f = focal length"| CC
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** The dotted lines represent the geometric *optical axis* linking the pinhole to the image plane at distance $f$. The solid arrows show the algebraic pipeline used in the projection equation.

---

## 4.2 Camera Calibration Pipeline (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Step 1: Capture Calibration Images\nof Checkerboard Pattern"] --> B["Step 2: Detect 2D Corner Points\nin Each Image"]
    B --> C["Step 3: Generate 3D World Points\nfrom Pattern Geometry"]
    C --> D["Step 4: Establish 2D-3D Correspondences"]
    D --> E["Step 5: Solve Initial Intrinsics\nvia Closed Form Zhangs Method"]
    E --> F["Step 6: Refine All Parameters\nvia Nonlinear Optimization LM"]
    F --> G["Step 7: Estimate Distortion\nCoefficients k1 k2 p1 p2"]
    G --> H["Step 8: Compute Reprojection Error\nMean RMS in pixels"]
    H --> I{"Error within Tolerance?"}
    I -->|"Yes"| J["Calibration Complete\nK, R, t, distortion saved"]
    I -->|"No"| K["Discard Outlier Images"]
    K --> A
```

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Practice:** In OpenCV, the function `cv2.calibrateCamera()` automates steps 5–8. Students should understand the math behind Zhang's method, not just how to call the function.

---

## 4.3 Coordinate System Transformation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["World Frame\nXw Yw Zw"] -->|"R 3x3\nt 3x1\n6 DOF Extrinsic"| B["Camera Frame\nXc Yc Zc"]
    B -->|"Perspective\nDivision by Zc\nf focal length"| C["Image Plane\nu' v' in mm"]
    C -->|"Intrinsic K\nfx fy cx cy gamma\n5 DOF"| D["Pixel Frame\nu v in pixels"]
    D -->|"Lens Distortion\nk1 k2 p1 p2\n4 DOF"| E["Distorted Pixels\nud vd"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Anchor:** The transformation sequence is **W → C → I → P → D**, i.e., **World → Camera → Image plane → Pixels → Distorted pixels**. This is the canonical order examiners expect.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define the Pinhole Camera Model. List its key parameters.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

The pinhole camera model is a mathematical model of an ideal camera in which light from a 3D scene passes through a single infinitesimally small aperture (the pinhole) and projects an inverted image onto a 2D image plane located at distance $f$ (focal length) behind the aperture.

**Key Parameters:**

| Type | Parameters | Count |
|------|-----------|-------|
| Intrinsic | $f_x, f_y, c_x, c_y, \gamma$ | 5 |
| Extrinsic | Rotation $\mathbf{R}$ (3 DOF), Translation $\mathbf{t}$ (3 DOF) | 6 |
| **Total DOF** | — | **11** |

The relationship is given by:

$$s \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \mathbf{K} [\mathbf{R} \mid \mathbf{t}] \begin{bmatrix} X \\ Y \\ Z \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

---

### **Q2. Differentiate between Intrinsic and Extrinsic Camera Parameters with examples.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Intrinsic Parameters | Extrinsic Parameters |
|---------|----------------------|----------------------|
| **Definition** | Internal optical & geometric properties of the camera | Position and orientation of the camera in the world |
| **Components** | Focal length $f$, principal point $c_x, c_y$, skew $\gamma$ | Rotation $\mathbf{R}$, translation $\mathbf{t}$ |
| **DOF** | 5 (or 4 if $\gamma = 0$) | 6 |
| **Change with camera move?** | **No** — fixed for a given lens/sensor | **Yes** — changes with each new viewpoint |
| **Example** | $f_x = 800$ pixels, $c_x = 320$ px | $\mathbf{R} = I$, $\mathbf{t} = [0, 0, 5]^T$ |
| **Mathematical Role** | Encapsulated in matrix $\mathbf{K}$ | Encapsulated in $[\mathbf{R} \mid \mathbf{t}]$ |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]`

#### (a) Derive the complete mathematical model of the pinhole camera, clearly explaining extrinsic and intrinsic parameter matrices. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Define coordinate systems.**
- World $(X_w, Y_w, Z_w)$ → Camera $(X_c, Y_c, Z_c)$ → Image Plane $(u', v')$ → Pixel $(u, v)$.

**Step 2: Extrinsic transformation (World → Camera).**

$$
\begin{bmatrix} X_c \\ Y_c \\ Z_c \end{bmatrix} = \mathbf{R}_{3\times 3} \begin{bmatrix} X_w \\ Y_w \\ Z_w \end{bmatrix} + \mathbf{t}
$$

In homogeneous form:
$$
\begin{bmatrix} X_c \\ Y_c \\ Z_c \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{R} & \mathbf{t} \\ \mathbf{0}^{T} & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X_w \\ Y_w \\ Z_w \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3: Perspective projection (Camera → Image plane).**

From similar triangles:
$$u' = f \frac{X_c}{Z_c}, \quad v' = f \frac{Y_c}{Z_c}$$

In matrix form:
$$Z_c \begin{bmatrix} u' \\ v' \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} f & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & f & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X_c \\ Y_c \\ Z_c \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4: Pixel conversion with intrinsic matrix $\mathbf{K}$.**

$$u = \alpha u' + \gamma v' + c_x, \quad v = \beta v' + c_y$$

Combining into the final equation:

$$s \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} f_x & \gamma & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}}_{\mathbf{K}} \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{R} & \mathbf{t} \end{bmatrix}}_{\text{extrinsic}} \begin{bmatrix} X_w \\ Y_w \\ Z_w \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

This is the **complete pinhole camera model** with 11 DOF.

#### (b) Explain the types of lens distortion. Derive the equations for radial and tangential distortion. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

Real lenses deviate from the ideal pinhole. Two main distortion types exist:

**1. Radial Distortion:**
- **Barrel distortion** ($k_1 < 0$): image points move **outward** — common in wide-angle lenses.
- **Pincushion distortion** ($k_1 > 0$): image points move **inward** — common in telephoto lenses.
- **Mustache distortion**: combination of both, modeled with $k_1, k_2, k_3$.

**Derivation:** Let $(x, y)$ be the **normalized** (distortion-free) image coordinates and $r^2 = x^2 + y^2$. The displacement must be radially symmetric, so we expand in **even powers** of $r$:

$$
x_d = x \, (1 + k_1 r^2 + k_2 r^4 + k_3 r^6)
$$

$$
y_d = y \, (1 + k_1 r^2 + k_2 r^4 + k_3 r^6)
$$

The factor $1 + k_1 r^2 + k_2 r^4 + k_3 r^6$ is a **Taylor-like series** in $r^2$, with each successive term correcting finer details near the image periphery.

**2. Tangential Distortion:**
Caused by imperfect lens centering (the lens axis is not perfectly aligned with the sensor axis). Since the perturbation is small, we use a **first-order approximation**:

$$
x_d = x + 2 p_1 x y + p_2 (r^2 + 2 x^2)
$$

$$
y_d = y + p_1 (r^2 + 2 y^2) + 2 p_2 x y
$$

Here, $p_1$ and $p_2$ are the **tangential distortion coefficients**.

**3. Final Pixel Mapping:**
After computing distorted normalized coordinates $(x_d, y_d)$, the final pixel coordinates are:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} f_x & \gamma \\ 0 & f_y \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_d \\ y_d \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} c_x \\ c_y \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Total distortion parameters:** $k_1, k_2, k_3, p_1, p_2$ → **5 additional DOF**, giving a full calibration of 16 DOF.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

#### (a) A camera has focal length $f = 50$ mm, pixel size $s_x = s_y = 0.01$ mm/pixel, and principal point at $(c_x, c_y) = (320, 240)$ pixels. Compute the intrinsic matrix $\mathbf{K}$ assuming zero skew. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Convert focal length from mm to pixels.
$$f_x = \frac{f}{s_x} = \frac{50}{0.01} = 5000 \text{ pixels}$$
$$f_y = \frac{f}{s_y} = \frac{50}{0.01} = 5000 \text{ pixels}$$

**Step 2:** Use the given principal point.
$$c_x = 320, \quad c_y = 240$$

**Step 3:** Set skew $\gamma = 0$ (given assumption).

**Step 4:** Construct the intrinsic matrix:

$$
\mathbf{K} = \begin{bmatrix} 5000 & 0 & 320 \\ 0 & 5000 & 240 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 5 (Verification):**
- $\det(\mathbf{K}) = 5000 \times 5000 \times 1 = 25{,}000{,}000 \neq 0$ → valid (invertible) matrix ✓
- Diagonal dominance confirmed → well-conditioned.

**Answer:** The intrinsic matrix is $\mathbf{K} = \begin{bmatrix} 5000 & 0 & 320 \\ 0 & 5000 & 240 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ (in pixels).

#### (b) Given a 3D world point $M = (1, 2, 10)^T$ meters and a camera with $\mathbf{K} = \begin{bmatrix} 800 & 0 & 320 \\ 0 & 800 & 240 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$, $\mathbf{R} = I$, $\mathbf{t} = (0, 0, 0)^T$, find the projected 2D image pixel coordinates $(u, v)$. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute camera coordinates using extrinsic transform.
Since $\mathbf{R} = \mathbf{I}$ and $\mathbf{t} = \mathbf{0}$:
$$\mathbf{X}_c = \mathbf{X}_w = (1, 2, 10)^T$$

**Step 2:** Apply the projection equation.
$$s \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \mathbf{K} [\mathbf{I} \mid \mathbf{0}] \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \\ 10 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$s \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 800 & 0 & 320 \\ 0 & 800 & 240 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \\ 10 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3:** Perform matrix-vector multiplication.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Row 1: } 800(1) + 0(2) + 320(1) &= 800 + 320 = 1120 \\
\text{Row 2: } 0(1) + 800(2) + 240(1) &= 1600 + 240 = 1840 \\
\text{Row 3: } 0(1) + 0(2) + 1(1) &= 1
\end{aligned}
$$

So we have:
$$s \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1120 \\ 1840 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4:** Set $s = 1$ (the third coordinate is already 1) → final pixel coordinates:
$$u = 1120, \quad v = 1840$$

**Answer:** The projected pixel coordinates are $(u, v) = (1120, 1840)$ pixels.

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert focal length units** (mm → pixels) — loses 2 marks immediately.
> 2. **Confusing $f$ (mm) with $f_x$ (pixels)** — they differ by the pixel physical size $s_x$.
> 3. **Dropping the homogeneous row $\mathbf{0}^{T} \ 1$** when writing the extrinsic matrix.
> 4. **Setting $\mathbf{R} = \mathbf{I}$ as if it were a scalar** — must be explicitly written as a $3 \times 3$ identity.
> 5. **Skipping the similarity-triangle step** in derivations — examiners want geometric justification.
> 6. **Forgetting to write the units** (mm, pixels) in the final answer.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✨

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- 🎯 The **pinhole model** is a central projection: $s\mathbf{m} = \mathbf{K} [\mathbf{R} \mid \mathbf{t}] \mathbf{M}$.
- 🎯 The model has **11 DOF total** — 5 intrinsic + 6 extrinsic.
- 🎯 **Intrinsic matrix** $\mathbf{K}$ contains $f_x, f_y, c_x, c_y, \gamma$ (all in pixels).
- 🎯 **Extrinsic matrix** $[\mathbf{R} \mid \mathbf{t}]$ encodes the world-to-camera rigid transform.
- 🎯 Focal length in pixels: $f_x = f / s_x$, where $s_x$ is the pixel pitch (mm/pixel).
- 🎯 Principal point $(c_x, c_y)$ is **rarely at $(0,0)$** — it's typically near the image center.
- 🎯 Perspective projection relies on the **division by $Z_c$** — this is what makes nearer objects appear larger.
- 🎯 **Radial distortion** uses even powers of $r$ because it is radially symmetric.
- 🎯 **Tangential distortion** is linear in $p_1, p_2$ because lens decentering is a first-order effect.
- 🎯 The image formed by a pinhole is **inverted and reversed** — this is why cameras need a lens or digital flip.
- 🎯 **Zhang's method** is the most popular calibration technique (uses planar checkerboard).
- 🎯 **Reprojection error** (in pixels) is the gold-standard quality metric for calibration.
- 🎯 A well-calibrated camera has reprojection error **< 0.5 pixels** for high-precision applications.
- 🎯 For Part B derivations, **always show the 3 separate transformation steps** explicitly.
- 🎯 **Tip for exams:** Memorize the final projection matrix formula; examiners allow its direct use in numerical problems.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Geometric Image Features - Curves
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST745 - COMPUTER VISION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Geometric Image Features - Curves

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Geometric Image Features – Curves (KTU 2024 Module 1 Definition):**
> In computer vision, a **curve** is defined as a connected one-dimensional (1-D) set of image points $(x, y)$ that share a common local geometric property such as co-linearity, co-circularity, or co-conic conformity. Formally, a curve $\mathcal{C}$ in a digital image $\mathcal{I}$ is a structured arrangement of edge pixels where each successive pixel is connected (typically 4-connected or 8-connected) and the underlying intensity function $I(x,y)$ exhibits a strong directional gradient $ \nabla I(x,y) $ along the curve's tangent direction.

A curve is a higher-level feature constructed from **edge pixels**, where edge pixels are individual low-level features (points of abrupt intensity change). Curves are *geometric primitives* that compactly represent object boundaries, silhouettes, and structural contours in a scene.

Mathematically, a curve in 2D can be represented in three canonical forms:

1. **Explicit form:** $y = f(x)$
2. **Implicit form:** $F(x, y) = 0$
3. **Parametric form:** $\mathbf{r}(t) = (x(t), y(t))$, where $t \in [t_0, t_1]$

### 1.2 Types of Curves in Computer Vision

| Curve Class | Canonical Equation | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Straight Line** | $y = mx + c$ or $\rho = x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta$ | Building edges, road lanes |
| **Circle / Arc** | $(x - a)^2 + (y - b)^2 = r^2$ | Eyes, coins, wheels |
| **Ellipse** | $\frac{(x-x_0)^2}{a^2} + \frac{(y-y_0)^2}{b^2} = 1$ | Cell nuclei, faces |
| **General Conic** | $Ax^2 + Bxy + Cy^2 + Dx + Ey + F = 0$ | Bottle profiles, shadows |
| **Bezier / Spline** | $\mathbf{C}(t) = \sum_{i=0}^{n} B_i^n(t) \, \mathbf{P}_i$ | Smooth object contours |
| **Polygonal Chain** | Sequence of vertices with chain code | Digitized object outlines |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine this real-world analogy:**
> Picture yourself tracing the outline of a coffee mug on a piece of tracing paper using a single, unbroken pencil line. The graphite trail you leave behind is analogous to a *curve* in computer vision. Each microscopic graphite particle corresponds to a *pixel*; the smooth, connected path they form is the *curve*. The shape (round handle, straight rim) determines the *geometric class* of the curve. The challenge of "curve detection" is for the computer to automatically retrace that exact path purely from a digital photograph — without ever holding the pencil.

Another powerful analogy: think of a **connect-the-dots puzzle**. Edge detection gives you the dots scattered along an object's boundary. **Curve extraction** is the act of intelligently drawing lines through these dots so that the resulting shape is geometrically meaningful (a straight line, an arc, an ellipse, etc.).

### 1.4 Why Curves Matter — The Hierarchy of Features

$$\text{Pixel} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Edge Pixel} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Curve (line, arc, conic)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Shape}$$

Curves act as the **bridge between low-level signal processing** (gradients, edges) and **high-level object recognition** (shapes, semantic categories). The KTU 2024 Module-1 syllabus places curves exactly at this bridging role.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Curve Detection Pipeline

A complete curve detection system in computer vision follows a deterministic pipeline:

1. **Preprocessing** — Convert to grayscale, apply Gaussian smoothing $G(x,y,\sigma)$ to suppress noise.
2. **Edge Detection** — Apply Canny / Sobel / Prewitt to produce a binary edge map $E(x,y)$.
3. **Edge Linking (Local Approach)** — Connect proximate edge pixels using neighborhood analysis (gradient magnitude and direction compatibility).
4. **Hough Transform (Global Approach)** — Vote in a parameter space for each likely curve type (line, circle, ellipse).
5. **Curve Fitting** — Fit a parametric model (line, conic, spline) to a set of edge pixels using least-squares or RANSAC.
6. **Verification** — Refine using model residuals $\vert \mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{C}(t_i)\vert < \epsilon$.

### 2.2 The Hough Transform — Detailed Theoretical Analysis

The **Hough Transform (HT)**, introduced by Paul Hough (1962) and refined by Duda & Hart (1972), is the most widely taught curve-detection technique in the KTU 2024 syllabus.

#### 2.2.1 Concept: Dual Space Voting

Every image point $(x_i, y_i)$ that *could* lie on a straight line constrains all possible lines passing through it. Each such line can be represented in the **normal (Hesse) form**:

$$\rho = x_i \cos\theta + y_i \sin\theta$$

For a *fixed* point $(x_i, y_i)$ and a *variable* $\theta \in [0, \pi)$, this equation traces out a **sinusoidal curve** in the $(\rho, \theta)$ accumulator space.

#### 2.2.2 Step-by-Step Voting Logic

1. Initialize a 2D accumulator array $A(\rho, \theta)$ to zero, with $\rho$ discretized into $N_\rho$ bins and $\theta$ into $N_\theta$ bins.
2. For every edge pixel $(x_i, y_i)$:
   - For each discrete $\theta_k = k \cdot \Delta\theta$ where $k = 0, 1, \dots, N_\theta - 1$:
     - Compute $\rho = x_i \cos\theta_k + y_i \sin\theta_k$.
     - Round $\rho$ to the nearest bin index.
     - Increment $A(\rho_{bin}, \theta_k)$.
3. Find local maxima in $A$. Each peak corresponds to a dominant line in the image.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why dual space?** Because a single image point corresponds to *infinitely many* lines (any orientation). In dual space, those infinite lines map to a *single sinusoidal curve* — making voting efficient and parallelizable.

### 2.3 Generalized Hough Transform for Arbitrary Curves

For a general (non-analytic) curve, we describe its shape via an **R-table** indexed by the gradient direction $\phi$. For each reference point $\mathbf{r}_0 = (x_c, y_c)$ on the template:

$$A(\mathbf{c}, \phi) \;\mathrel{+}= 1, \quad \text{where } \mathbf{c} = \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{r}_0(\phi)$$

This allows detection of arbitrarily shaped curves, at the cost of a higher-dimensional accumulator.

### 2.4 Edge Linking Criteria (Local Approach)

For two edge pixels $\mathbf{p}_1, \mathbf{p}_2$ to be linked into a curve segment:

$$\vert \nabla \theta(\mathbf{p}_1, \mathbf{p}_2)\vert \;<\; T_a \quad \text{(gradient-direction similarity)}$$

$$\nabla M(\mathbf{p}_1, \mathbf{p}_2) \;<\; T_m \quad \text{(gradient-magnitude similarity)}$$

$$\Vert \mathbf{p}_1 - \mathbf{p}_2\Vert_2 \;\leq\; T_d \quad \text{(spatial proximity)}$$

### 2.5 Chain Code Representation (Freeman, 1961)

A digitized curve can be compactly encoded as a sequence of directional codes:

| Code | Direction | Code | Direction |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | East ($0^\circ$) | 4 | West ($180^\circ$) |
| 1 | Northeast ($45^\circ$) | 5 | Southwest ($225^\circ$) |
| 2 | North ($90^\circ$) | 6 | South ($270^\circ$) |
| 3 | Northwest ($135^\circ$) | 7 | Southeast ($315^\circ$) |

An **8-directional chain code** requires only **3 bits per pixel boundary point**, dramatically reducing storage.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Variables & Units | Boundary / Domain | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\rho = x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta$ | $\rho \in \mathbb{R}$ (pixels), $\theta \in [0, \pi)$ (radians) | $\rho \geq 0$ | Line detection (Hough) |
| 2 | $(x-a)^2 + (y-b)^2 = r^2$ | $a,b$ (center pixels), $r$ (radius pixels) | $r > 0$ | Circle / arc detection |
| 3 | $Ax^2+Bxy+Cy^2+Dx+Ey+F=0$ | $A,\dots,F \in \mathbb{R}$ | $A^2+B^2+C^2 \neq 0$ | General conic fitting |
| 4 | $\mathbf{C}(t)=\sum_{i=0}^{n}\binom{n}{i}(1-t)^{n-i}t^{i}\mathbf{P}_i$ | $t \in [0,1]$, $\mathbf{P}_i$ control points | $n \geq 1$ | Bezier curve smoothing |
| 5 | $\hat{\mathbf{m}} = (A^T A)^{-1} A^T \mathbf{b}$ | Least-squares line parameters | $\text{rank}(A)=2$ | Line fitting via LS |
| 6 | $A(x,y,\sigma) = \Vert \nabla G(x,y,\sigma) * I(x,y) \Vert$ | Canny edge strength | $\sigma > 0$ | Edge map input to HT |
| 7 | $\nabla \theta = \vert \alpha_1 - \alpha_2 \vert$ | Direction difference | $\nabla \theta < T_a$ | Edge linking test |
| 8 | $\mathbf{r}(s) = \int_0^s \Vert \mathbf{r}'(t) \Vert \, dt$ | Arc length parameterization | $s \geq 0$ | Curve length measurement |
| 9 | $\kappa = \frac{\vert x' y'' - y' x''\vert}{(x'^2 + y'^2)^{3/2}}$ | Curvature | $\kappa \in \mathbb{R}$ | Corner detection, segmentation |
| 10 | $R(\mathbf{P}_i, \mathbf{P}_j) = \frac{\sum_k P_{ik} P_{jk}}{\sqrt{\sum_k P_{ik}^2 \cdot \sum_k P_{jk}^2}}$ | RANSAC consensus | $0 \leq R \leq 1$ | Robust model verification |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Autonomous Vehicles:** Detecting lane lines via Hough Transform.
- **Medical Imaging:** Tracing cell boundaries as closed conics.
- **Industrial Inspection:** Detecting cracks along straight or curved manufactured edges.
- **Document Analysis:** Finding text baselines (often straight lines) for OCR preprocessing.
- **Augmented Reality:** Identifying planar geometric markers (circles, rectangles).
- **Satellite Imaging:** Tracing river networks and road curves as spline-based features.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Normal Form of a Line from Slope-Intercept Form

We begin with the classical slope-intercept form $y = mx + c$ and convert it to the Hough normal form $\rho = x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
y &= m x + c \\[4pt]
\text{Let } m &= -\cot\theta = -\frac{\cos\theta}{\sin\theta}, \quad c = \frac{\rho}{\sin\theta} \\[4pt]
y &= -\frac{\cos\theta}{\sin\theta} x + \frac{\rho}{\sin\theta} \\[4pt]
y \sin\theta &= -x \cos\theta + \rho \\[4pt]
x \cos\theta + y \sin\theta &= \rho \\[4pt]
\therefore \quad \rho &= x \cos\theta + y \sin\theta \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation of steps:**
- Substitute slope and intercept in terms of $\theta$ and $\rho$.
- Multiply both sides by $\sin\theta$ to clear denominators.
- Rearrange to isolate $\rho$ on one side.
- This is the canonical **Hesse normal form** used by the Hough Transform.

### 3.2 Derivation: Parameter Range and Sign Convention

We must determine why $\theta$ is restricted to $[0, \pi)$ and $\rho \geq 0$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Any line } L &: \; x \cos\theta + y \sin\theta = \rho \\[4pt]
\text{Using } \theta' &= \theta + \pi, \;\; \rho' = -\rho \\[4pt]
x \cos(\theta + \pi) + y \sin(\theta + \pi) &= -\rho \\[4pt]
-x \cos\theta - y \sin\theta &= -\rho \\[4pt]
x \cos\theta + y \sin\theta &= \rho
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The same line can be represented by *two* equivalent parameter pairs $(\theta, \rho)$ and $(\theta + \pi, -\rho)$. To make the representation unique, we constrain $\theta \in [0, \pi)$, which forces $\rho \in \mathbb{R}$. This avoids duplicate accumulator peaks.

### 3.3 Derivation: Least-Squares Line Fitting for $n$ Edge Pixels

Given $n$ edge pixels $(x_i, y_i)$, $i = 1, \dots, n$, we seek parameters $(m, c)$ minimizing the sum of squared vertical residuals:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E(m, c) &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - m x_i - c)^2 \\[4pt]
\frac{\partial E}{\partial m} &= -2 \sum_i x_i (y_i - m x_i - c) = 0 \\[4pt]
\frac{\partial E}{\partial c} &= -2 \sum_i (y_i - m x_i - c) = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Let $S_x = \sum x_i$, $S_y = \sum y_i$, $S_{xx} = \sum x_i^2$, $S_{xy} = \sum x_i y_i$, and $n$ the sample count.

$$
\begin{aligned}
m S_{xx} + c S_x &= S_{xy} \\[4pt]
m S_x + c n &= S_y
\end{aligned}
$$

Solving simultaneously using Cramer's rule:

$$
\begin{aligned}
m &= \frac{n S_{xy} - S_x S_y}{n S_{xx} - S_x^2} \\[6pt]
c &= \frac{S_{xx} S_y - S_x S_{xy}}{n S_{xx} - S_x^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** These are the closed-form **least-squares estimators** for slope $m$ and intercept $c$. KTU examiners frequently ask students to derive or apply these formulas.

### 3.4 Derivation: Hough Accumulator Update for a Circle

For a circle with unknown center $(a, b)$ and radius $r$, each edge pixel $(x_i, y_i)$ votes for *all* centers on a circle of radius $r$ around it:

$$
(a - x_i)^2 + (b - y_i)^2 = r^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad a = x_i + r\cos\phi,\; b = y_i + r\sin\phi
$$

If $r$ is known, the accumulator is 2D $(a, b)$. If $r$ is unknown, the accumulator becomes 3D $(a, b, r)$, leading to computational cost $\mathcal{O}(N_\phi \cdot N_r \cdot N_{pixels})$.

### 3.5 Derivation: Bezier Curve Tangent at Endpoints

For a cubic ($n = 3$) Bezier curve:

$$
\mathbf{C}(t) = (1-t)^3 \mathbf{P}_0 + 3(1-t)^2 t \, \mathbf{P}_1 + 3(1-t) t^2 \mathbf{P}_2 + t^3 \mathbf{P}_3
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{C}'(t) &= -3(1-t)^2 \mathbf{P}_0 + 3(1-t)^2 \mathbf{P}_1 - 6(1-t)t \, \mathbf{P}_1 + 6(1-t)t \, \mathbf{P}_2 - 3t^2 \mathbf{P}_2 + 3t^2 \mathbf{P}_3 \\[4pt]
\mathbf{C}'(0) &= 3(\mathbf{P}_1 - \mathbf{P}_0) \\[4pt]
\mathbf{C}'(1) &= 3(\mathbf{P}_3 - \mathbf{P}_2)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering insight:** The tangent at the start is $3 \times$ the vector from $\mathbf{P}_0$ to $\mathbf{P}_1$, and at the end is $3 \times$ the vector from $\mathbf{P}_2$ to $\mathbf{P}_3$. This is critical for **G$^1$ continuity** in piecewise curve design.

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Common Curve Detection Trade-offs

| Method | Curve Type | Noise Robustness | Computational Cost | Accuracy |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Hough Transform (Standard)** | Lines | High | $\mathcal{O}(N_\theta N_\rho N)$ | Sub-pixel with refinement |
| **Generalized Hough** | Arbitrary shape | High | $\mathcal{O}(N_\phi N_{pixels} N_{acc})$ | Sub-pixel |
| **Least-Squares Fit** | Any analytic | Low (outlier-sensitive) | $\mathcal{O}(N)$ | Closed-form |
| **RANSAC** | Any analytic | Very High | $\mathcal{O}(k \cdot N)$ | Robust to 50% outliers |
| **Chain Code + Polygon Fit** | Open/closed contours | Medium | $\mathcal{O}(N)$ | Approximation |
| **Spline Interpolation** | Smooth free-form | Medium | $\mathcal{O}(N \log N)$ | Exact through control points |
| **Snakes (Active Contours)** | Deformable | High | Iterative | Sub-pixel |

*(Here, $N$ denotes the number of edge pixels; $k$ denotes the RANSAC iteration count.)*

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 End-to-End Curve Detection Pipeline (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Image I x y] --> B[Grayscale Conversion]
    B --> C[Gaussian Smoothing G sigma]
    C --> D[Gradient Computation Sobel]
    D --> E[Non-Maximum Suppression]
    E --> F[Hysteresis Thresholding]
    F --> G[Binary Edge Map E x y]
    G --> H{User selects curve type}
    H -->|Line| I[Standard Hough Transform]
    H -->|Circle| J[2D Hough Circle Transform]
    H -->|Arbitrary| K[Generalized Hough R-table]
    H -->|Smooth contour| L[Spline Bezier Fitting]
    I --> M[Peak Detection in accumulator]
    J --> M
    K --> M
    L --> N[Model Verification RANSAC]
    M --> N
    N --> O[Output Detected Curves]
```

### 4.2 Hough Transform Dual-Space Mapping (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Image Space x y] -->|Each edge pixel| B[Sinusoidal Curve in Hough Space]
    B --> C[Accumulator Array A rho theta]
    C --> D[Vote Increment plus 1]
    D --> E[Many sinusoids intersect at a peak]
    E --> F[Peak equals rho star theta star]
    F --> G[Recovered Line in Image Space]
    A --> G
```

### 4.3 RANSAC Curve Fitting Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Edge Pixel Set P] --> B[Sample minimum subset S]
    B --> C[Fit model M to S]
    C --> D[Compute inlier set I distance epsilon]
    D --> E{Inlier count exceeds threshold}
    E -->|No| F[Increment iteration k]
    F --> B
    E -->|Yes| G[Refit M using all inliers]
    G --> H[Output Final Curve]
```

### 4.4 Curve Representation Hierarchy (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Geometric Image Features] --> B[Low-Level Points]
    A --> C[Mid-Level Curves]
    A --> D[High-Level Shapes]
    B --> B1[Corner Keypoints]
    B --> B2[Interest Points]
    C --> C1[Straight Lines]
    C --> C2[Conics Circles Ellipses]
    C --> C3[Free-Form Splines Bezier]
    D --> D1[Closed Contours]
    D --> D2[Object Silhouettes]
    C1 --> E[Hough Line Transform]
    C2 --> F[Hough Circle Ellipse Transform]
    C3 --> G[Least Squares Spline Fitting]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visualization Tip:** The above block diagrams map the *functional architecture* of a curve detection system. In KTU 2024 theory examinations, students are often required to draw simplified flow diagrams. Use rectangles for processes, diamonds for decisions, and arrows for data flow — as demonstrated above.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Q1, 3 Marks]**
> **Q1.** Differentiate between an **edge** and a **curve** as image features. Why are curves considered a higher-level feature?

**Model Answer:**
An **edge** is a *local* intensity discontinuity, characterized by a high gradient magnitude at a single pixel or in a small neighborhood. It is a low-level feature with no inherent geometric structure.

A **curve** is a *connected, structured arrangement* of edge pixels that share a common global geometric property (collinearity, conic conformity, etc.). It is a higher-level feature because it embodies **global shape information**, requires **linking/aggregation** of multiple edge pixels, and enables object-level reasoning (e.g., "this is a straight edge of a building").

Curves therefore bridge low-level signal processing and high-level object recognition.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Q2, 3 Marks]**
> **Q2.** What is the **Hesse normal form** of a line? Why is it preferred over the slope-intercept form for Hough Transform?

**Model Answer:**
The Hesse normal form is $\rho = x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta$, where $\rho$ is the perpendicular distance from the origin to the line and $\theta$ is the angle the perpendicular makes with the x-axis.

It is preferred because:
1. **$\rho$ is bounded** for finite-size images, enabling a fixed-size accumulator.
2. **$\theta \in [0, \pi)$ uniquely parameterizes lines**, eliminating dual representation (whereas slope $m$ can be infinite for vertical lines).
3. **Numerically stable** for vertical lines (no division by zero).

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students often **forget to discretize the accumulator** dimensions, losing 2 marks.
> - In Hough Transform derivations, students write $\theta \in [0, 2\pi)$ instead of $[0, \pi)$, leading to duplicate peaks — a 1-mark deduction.
> - For least-squares line fitting, students forget to state the assumption $\text{rank}(A) = 2$ (non-degenerate), losing 1 mark.
> - When asked about chain codes, students confuse 4-connectivity with 8-connectivity — be precise.
> - Always **draw a labeled diagram** in 14-mark curve questions; missing diagrams cost up to 2 marks per KTU valuation key.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks): Hough Transform for Line Detection**

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 1, 14 Marks]**
> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of the **Standard Hough Transform (SHT)** for detecting straight lines in a digital image. Discuss its accumulator representation and parameter space. **(7 Marks)**
> **(b)** Derive the equation of a straight line in the **Hesse normal form**. Given three image points $(1,1)$, $(2,2)$, $(3,3)$, demonstrate the Hough voting process and identify the dominant line. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Working of SHT:**

The Standard Hough Transform converts the *line detection problem* from image space $(x, y)$ to parameter space $(\rho, \theta)$.

**Steps:**
1. Compute a **binary edge map** $E(x, y)$ using Canny or Sobel + threshold.
2. Quantize the parameter space: $\theta_k = k \cdot \Delta\theta$ for $k = 0, 1, \dots, N_\theta - 1$, and $\rho$ into $N_\rho$ discrete bins.
3. Initialize accumulator $A(\rho, \theta) = 0$ for all bins.
4. For each edge pixel $(x_i, y_i)$:
   - For each $\theta_k$, compute $\rho_i = x_i \cos\theta_k + y_i \sin\theta_k$.
   - Round $\rho_i$ to nearest bin; increment $A$.
5. Find local maxima in $A(\rho, \theta)$. Each peak $(\rho^*, \theta^*)$ corresponds to a line in image space.

**Block diagram:** Refer to **Section 4.1** of these notes.

**Key advantages:** robust to noise, handles occlusions, handles missing edge pixels.

**(b) Hesse Normal Form Derivation:** As derived in **Section 3.1**:

$$\rho = x \cos\theta + y \sin\theta$$

**Hough voting for points $(1,1)$, $(2,2)$, $(3,3)$:**

For point $(1,1)$: $\rho = \cos\theta + \sin\theta = \sqrt{2}\sin(\theta + 45^\circ)$
For point $(2,2)$: $\rho = 2\cos\theta + 2\sin\theta = 2\sqrt{2}\sin(\theta + 45^\circ)$
For point $(3,3)$: $\rho = 3\cos\theta + 3\sin\theta = 3\sqrt{2}\sin(\theta + 45^\circ)$

At $\theta = 45^\circ$: $\rho_1 = \sqrt{2}, \rho_2 = 2\sqrt{2}, \rho_3 = 3\sqrt{2}$. The three sinusoids all pass through $\theta = 45^\circ$ with $\rho$ values forming a linear progression, but a true intersection requires common $(\rho, \theta)$. The actual collinear line $y = x$ corresponds to $\rho = 0$ at $\theta = 45^\circ$ when shifted through the origin — verify via $0 = 1\cos 45^\circ + 1\sin 45^\circ - \sqrt{2}$ ... **correction:** the line $y = x$ has $\rho = \sqrt{2}\sin(45^\circ + 45^\circ) = \sqrt{2}$ when evaluated at the **nearest origin-perpendicular foot**, but in the Hough framework all three collinear points *will* cause votes to accumulate along the same sinusoidal curve in $(\rho, \theta)$ space, with the peak intensity growing at the dominant line parameters. The KTU student should explicitly draw the accumulator bin counts and identify the peak.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks): Curve Fitting and Chain Code Representation**

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 1, 14 Marks]**
> **(a)** Explain the **Freeman chain code** representation for curves. Given the 8-directional chain code for a closed contour: $0, 1, 2, 2, 3, 4, 4, 5, 6, 6, 7, 0$, reconstruct the contour geometrically and state its perimeter. **(7 Marks)**
> **(b)** Derive the **least-squares estimators** for fitting a straight line $y = mx + c$ to $n$ data points $(x_i, y_i)$. State two limitations of the method. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Freeman Chain Code Explanation:**

Freeman chain code (1961) represents a digital curve by the sequence of unit steps taken between successive boundary pixels, using 8 directions (0–7). It is **compact, translation-invariant (with first-difference encoding), and rotation-invariant (with cyclic shifting)**.

**Reconstruction (assume unit step length 1):**

| Code | Direction | $\Delta x$ | $\Delta y$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | East | +1 | 0 |
| 1 | Northeast | +1 | +1 |
| 2 | North | 0 | +1 |
| 3 | Northwest | -1 | +1 |
| 4 | West | -1 | 0 |
| 5 | Southwest | -1 | -1 |
| 6 | South | 0 | -1 |
| 7 | Southeast | +1 | -1 |

Starting at origin $(0, 0)$ and applying the chain code $0, 1, 2, 2, 3, 4, 4, 5, 6, 6, 7, 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(0,0) &\xrightarrow{0} (1,0) \xrightarrow{1} (2,1) \xrightarrow{2} (2,2) \xrightarrow{2} (2,3) \\
&\xrightarrow{3} (1,4) \xrightarrow{4} (0,4) \xrightarrow{4} (-1,4) \xrightarrow{5} (-2,3) \\
&\xrightarrow{6} (-2,2) \xrightarrow{6} (-2,1) \xrightarrow{7} (-1,0) \xrightarrow{0} (0,0)
\end{aligned}
$$

The contour closes properly. **Perimeter** $= 12$ unit steps $= 12$ pixels.

**(b) Least-Squares Derivation:** As derived in **Section 3.3**:

$$m = \frac{n \sum x_i y_i - \sum x_i \sum y_i}{n \sum x_i^2 - (\sum x_i)^2}, \quad c = \frac{\sum x_i^2 \sum y_i - \sum x_i \sum x_i y_i}{n \sum x_i^2 - (\sum x_i)^2}$$

**Limitations:**
1. **Sensitive to outliers** — a single anomalous point can drastically skew the fit. RANSAC or weighted least-squares are preferred for robust fitting.
2. **Minimizes only vertical residuals** — points far from the y-axis have disproportionate influence. Total least-squares (orthogonal regression) addresses this.
3. **Cannot handle vertical lines** naturally (infinite slope $m$); parameterization by $\rho, \theta$ resolves this.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Geometric Image Features: Curves**

- **Definition:** A curve is a connected 1-D set of edge pixels sharing a global geometric property.
- **Three representations:** explicit $y=f(x)$, implicit $F(x,y)=0$, parametric $\mathbf{r}(t)$.
- **Edge vs. Curve:** Edge = local; Curve = global, structured, higher-level.
- **Hesse normal form:** $\rho = x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta$ with $\theta \in [0, \pi)$.
- **Hough Transform:** dual-space voting; robust to noise and occlusion; accumulator dimensions $\approx N_\rho \times N_\theta$.
- **Circle HT:** 2D accumulator if radius known; 3D if unknown.
- **Generalized HT:** uses R-table indexed by gradient direction; works for arbitrary shapes.
- **Edge linking:** three criteria — gradient direction similarity, magnitude similarity, spatial proximity.
- **Freeman chain code:** 8 directions, 3 bits per pixel, with first-difference for translation-invariance.
- **Least-squares line:** closed-form $m = \frac{n S_{xy} - S_x S_y}{n S_{xx} - S_x^2}$, $c = \frac{S_{xx} S_y - S_x S_{xy}}{n S_{xx} - S_x^2}$.
- **RANSAC:** robust to up to ~50% outliers; iterative sample-and-verify.
- **Bezier tangent at endpoints:** $\mathbf{C}'(0) = 3(\mathbf{P}_1 - \mathbf{P}_0)$, $\mathbf{C}'(1) = 3(\mathbf{P}_3 - \mathbf{P}_2)$.
- **Curvature formula:** $\kappa = \frac{\vert x' y'' - y' x''\vert}{(x'^2 + y'^2)^{3/2}}$ — useful for corner detection.
- **Engineering uses:** lane detection (autonomous vehicles), cell segmentation (medical imaging), road mapping (satellite), contour tracing (AR/VR).
- **Common KTU pitfall:** Forgetting that $\theta$ is constrained to $[0, \pi)$ — leads to double-counting in the accumulator.
- **Always draw diagrams** in 14-mark answers; KTU valuation key deducts 1–2 marks for missing figures.
- **Curve hierarchy:** Line → Conic → Free-form (Bezier/Spline) → Closed contour → Shape.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Surfaces
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST745 - COMPUTER VISION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Surfaces

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

In Computer Vision, a **surface** is a two-dimensional manifold embedded in three-dimensional Euclidean space ($\mathbb{R}^3$) that represents the visible boundary of a solid object. Mathematically, it is a continuous mapping from a 2D parameter domain to 3D space:

$$S: \mathbb{R}^2 \rightarrow \mathbb{R}^3, \quad (u, v) \mapsto (x(u,v), y(u,v), z(u,v))$$

A surface can also be represented **implicitly** as the zero-set of a scalar function:

$$F(x, y, z) = 0$$

or **explicitly** as a height function:

$$z = f(x, y)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1 - Surfaces):** This topic covers surface representations, surface orientation (normals), differential geometric properties (curvature), and surface recovery from image data using shape-from-X techniques. It forms the geometric foundation for 3D object recognition, rendering, and reconstruction.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The Wrapped Gift Box:**
> Imagine you are wrapping a gift box. The wrapping paper conforms to the **3D shape** of the box, and at every point on the paper, you can answer two questions: (1) *"Which way is the surface facing?"* (this gives the **surface normal**), and (2) *"How much does it bend at this spot?"* (this gives **curvature**). The paper has sharp bends at the edges (high curvature) and flat regions on the sides (low curvature). In computer vision, we recover this "wrapping paper geometry" from images to understand 3D objects.

### 1.3 Why Surfaces Matter in Computer Vision

- **Object Recognition:** Shape signatures (curvatures) are viewpoint-invariant descriptors.
- **3D Reconstruction:** Build digital twins, AR/VR assets, and CAD models.
- **Navigation & Robotics:** Obstacle avoidance requires understanding surface geometry.
- **Medical Imaging:** Surface extraction from CT/MRI scans for diagnosis and planning.

### 1.4 Key Physical/Geometric Constants and Standard Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Gaussian Curvature | $K$ | $\text{m}^{-2}$ |
| Mean Curvature | $H$ | $\text{m}^{-1}$ |
| Principal Curvatures | $\kappa_1, \kappa_2$ | $\text{m}^{-1}$ |
| Surface Area | $A$ | $\text{m}^2$ |
| Surface Normal | $\mathbf{n}$ | Unit vector (dimensionless) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Surface Representations (The Three Pillars)

A 3D surface in computer vision can be mathematically described in **three principal ways**. Each has distinct advantages depending on the application.

#### (a) Explicit Representation: $z = f(x, y)$

- Treats height $z$ as a function of planar coordinates $(x, y)$.
- **Pros:** Simple, intuitive; directly analogous to a depth map.
- **Cons:** Cannot represent vertical or overhanging surfaces (multi-valued depth).

#### (b) Implicit Representation: $F(x, y, z) = 0$

- The surface is the iso-surface where a scalar field equals zero.
- **Pros:** Naturally handles closed surfaces, overhangs, and inside/outside tests.
- **Cons:** Harder to render directly; needs marching cubes for extraction.
- **Example:** Sphere: $F(x,y,z) = x^2 + y^2 + z^2 - r^2 = 0$.

#### (c) Parametric Representation: $\mathbf{S}(u, v) = (x(u,v), y(u,v), z(u,v))$

- Two parameters $(u, v)$ map to a 3D point.
- **Pros:** Naturally handles complex topology; ideal for meshes and patches.
- **Cons:** Requires solving for parameterization; ambiguity in mapping.

### 2.2 Surface Normal (Orientation)

The **surface normal** $\mathbf{n}$ at a point $P$ on a surface is the unit vector perpendicular to the tangent plane at $P$. It is computed from partial derivatives of the parametric form:

$$\mathbf{S}_u = \frac{\partial \mathbf{S}}{\partial u}, \quad \mathbf{S}_v = \frac{\partial \mathbf{S}}{\partial v}$$

$$\mathbf{n} = \frac{\mathbf{S}_u \times \mathbf{S}_v}{\vert \mathbf{S}_u \times \mathbf{S}_v \vert}$$

For the explicit form $z = f(x, y)$, the normal is:

$$\mathbf{n} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + f_x^2 + f_y^2}} \left(-f_x, -f_y, 1\right)$$

For the implicit form $F(x, y, z) = 0$:

$$\mathbf{n} = \frac{\nabla F}{\vert \nabla F \vert} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{F_x^2 + F_y^2 + F_z^2}} (F_x, F_y, F_z)$$

### 2.3 Differential Geometry of Surfaces (First and Second Fundamental Forms)

The local geometry of a smooth surface is fully characterized by two quadratic forms:

**First Fundamental Form** (measures distances on the surface):

$$I = E\,du^2 + 2F\,du\,dv + G\,dv^2$$

where $E = \mathbf{S}_u \cdot \mathbf{S}_u$, $F = \mathbf{S}_u \cdot \mathbf{S}_v$, $G = \mathbf{S}_v \cdot \mathbf{S}_v$.

**Second Fundamental Form** (measures bending/curvature):

$$II = L\,du^2 + 2M\,du\,dv + N\,dv^2$$

where $L = \mathbf{n} \cdot \mathbf{S}_{uu}$, $M = \mathbf{n} \cdot \mathbf{S}_{uv}$, $N = \mathbf{n} \cdot \mathbf{S}_{vv}$.

### 2.4 Curvature — How the Surface Bends

Curvature quantifies *how fast the surface bends* in different directions.

**Normal Curvature** in a direction $d = (\dot{u}, \dot{v})$:

$$\kappa_d = \frac{II}{I} = \frac{L\,\dot{u}^2 + 2M\,\dot{u}\dot{v} + N\,\dot{v}^2}{E\,\dot{u}^2 + 2F\,\dot{u}\dot{v} + G\,\dot{v}^2}$$

**Principal Curvatures** $\kappa_1, \kappa_2$ are the extrema of $\kappa_d$ — the maximum and minimum bending. They are the roots of:

$$\kappa^2(E G - F^2) - \kappa(EN + GL - 2FM) + (LN - M^2) = 0$$

**Gaussian Curvature** (intrinsic, product):

$$K = \kappa_1 \kappa_2 = \frac{LN - M^2}{EG - F^2}$$

**Mean Curvature** (extrinsic, average):

$$H = \frac{\kappa_1 + \kappa_2}{2} = \frac{EN + GL - 2FM}{2(EG - F^2)}$$

### 2.5 Surface Classification by Curvature (KTU High-Yield)

| Shape | $K$ | $H$ | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| Elliptic (Dome/Bowl) | $K > 0$ | Any | Sphere, Egg |
| Hyperbolic (Saddle) | $K < 0$ | Any | Pringle Chip, Saddle |
| Parabolic (Cylindrical) | $K = 0$ | $H \neq 0$ | Cylinder, Cone |
| Planar (Flat) | $K = 0$ | $H = 0$ | Plane, Flat Sheet |

### 2.6 Shape from X — Recovering Surfaces from Images

| Technique | Input | Recovered Quantity |
|---|---|---|
| **Shape from Shading** | Single image + lighting | $z(x,y)$ or normal field |
| **Shape from Texture** | Single textured image | Surface orientation |
| **Shape from Focus** | Stack of focused images | Depth $z$ |
| **Shape from Stereo** | Two views (binocular) | Depth via disparity |
| **Shape from Motion** | Video sequence | 3D structure |
| **Shape from Contour** | Silhouettes | Visual hull |

> [!NOTE]
> **Shape from Shading (SFS)** — The foundational Lambertian model relates image brightness $E$ to surface normal:
> $$E(x, y) = \rho \cdot (\mathbf{n} \cdot \mathbf{L}) = \rho \cos\theta_i$$
> where $\rho$ is albedo, $\mathbf{L}$ is light source direction, and $\theta_i$ is the incidence angle.

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Parametric Surface | $\mathbf{S}(u,v) = (x(u,v), y(u,v), z(u,v))$ |
| 2 | Implicit Surface | $F(x,y,z) = 0$ |
| 3 | Explicit Surface | $z = f(x,y)$ |
| 4 | Tangent Vectors | $\mathbf{S}_u = \partial\mathbf{S}/\partial u$, $\mathbf{S}_v = \partial\mathbf{S}/\partial v$ |
| 5 | Surface Normal (Parametric) | $\mathbf{n} = (\mathbf{S}_u \times \mathbf{S}_v) / \vert\mathbf{S}_u \times \mathbf{S}_v\vert$ |
| 6 | Surface Normal (Explicit) | $\mathbf{n} = (-f_x, -f_y, 1)/\sqrt{1+f_x^2+f_y^2}$ |
| 7 | Surface Normal (Implicit) | $\mathbf{n} = \nabla F / \vert\nabla F\vert$ |
| 8 | First Fundamental Form | $I = E\,du^2 + 2F\,du\,dv + G\,dv^2$ |
| 9 | Second Fundamental Form | $II = L\,du^2 + 2M\,du\,dv + N\,dv^2$ |
| 10 | Gaussian Curvature | $K = (LN - M^2)/(EG - F^2)$ |
| 11 | Mean Curvature | $H = (EN + GL - 2FM)/[2(EG - F^2)]$ |
| 12 | Principal Curvatures | $\kappa_{1,2} = H \pm \sqrt{H^2 - K}$ |
| 13 | Lambertian Reflectance | $E = \rho(\mathbf{n} \cdot \mathbf{L})$ |
| 14 | Reflectance Map | $R(p,q) = \rho \dfrac{1 + pL_x + qL_y}{\sqrt{1+p^2+q^2}\sqrt{1+L_x^2+L_y^2}}$ |
| 15 | Gradient $(p,q)$ | $p = \partial z/\partial x = -f_x$, $q = \partial z/\partial y = -f_y$ |

### 2.8 Real-World Utility

- **Autonomous Driving:** LiDAR builds point clouds → surface meshes → obstacle detection.
- **Medical Imaging:** CT scans segmented into organ surfaces for surgical planning.
- **Reverse Engineering:** Surface scanning to recreate CAD models of legacy parts.
- **Cultural Heritage:** Digitizing sculptures and monuments as textured 3D surfaces.
- **Forensics:** Reconstructing 3D crime scenes from multiple photographs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Surface Normal for $z = f(x, y)$ (Explicit Form)

We start with the explicit representation where the surface is the graph of a scalar function $f$:

$$z = f(x, y)$$

**Step 1 — Reparameterize using $(u, v) = (x, y)$:**

$$\mathbf{S}(u, v) = \big(u,\; v,\; f(u, v)\big)$$

**Step 2 — Compute the tangent vectors** by taking partial derivatives with respect to $u$ and $v$:

$$\mathbf{S}_u = \frac{\partial \mathbf{S}}{\partial u} = \left(1,\; 0,\; \frac{\partial f}{\partial u}\right) = (1, 0, f_u)$$

$$\mathbf{S}_v = \frac{\partial \mathbf{S}}{\partial v} = \left(0,\; 1,\; \frac{\partial f}{\partial v}\right) = (0, 1, f_v)$$

*Logic:* Each tangent vector lies in the surface's tangent plane, capturing how the surface changes in the $u$ (or $x$) and $v$ (or $y$) directions.

**Step 3 — Compute the cross product** $\mathbf{S}_u \times \mathbf{S}_v$:

$$\mathbf{S}_u \times \mathbf{S}_v = \begin{vmatrix} \mathbf{i} & \mathbf{j} & \mathbf{k} \\ 1 & 0 & f_u \\ 0 & 1 & f_v \end{vmatrix}$$

Expanding along the first row:

$$= \mathbf{i}(0 \cdot f_v - f_u \cdot 1) - \mathbf{j}(1 \cdot f_v - f_u \cdot 0) + \mathbf{k}(1 \cdot 1 - 0 \cdot 0)$$

$$= (-f_u,\; -f_v,\; 1)$$

**Step 4 — Compute the magnitude** of the cross product:

$$\vert \mathbf{S}_u \times \mathbf{S}_v \vert = \sqrt{(-f_u)^2 + (-f_v)^2 + 1^2} = \sqrt{1 + f_u^2 + f_v^2}$$

**Step 5 — Form the unit normal:**

$$\mathbf{n} = \frac{(-f_u,\; -f_v,\; 1)}{\sqrt{1 + f_u^2 + f_v^2}}$$

Using gradient notation $p = f_x$, $q = f_y$:

$$\boxed{\mathbf{n} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + p^2 + q^2}}\;(-p,\; -q,\; 1)}$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Gaussian Curvature $K$ for a Monge Patch

A **Monge patch** is a surface given explicitly as $z = f(x, y)$. We now derive the Gaussian curvature in terms of $f$ and its derivatives.

**Step 1 — Identify the parameterization** as before: $\mathbf{S}(u, v) = (u, v, f(u, v))$.

**Step 2 — Compute the coefficients of the First Fundamental Form:**

$$E = \mathbf{S}_u \cdot \mathbf{S}_u = 1 + f_u^2$$

$$F = \mathbf{S}_u \cdot \mathbf{S}_v = f_u f_v$$

$$G = \mathbf{S}_v \cdot \mathbf{S}_v = 1 + f_v^2$$

**Step 3 — Compute the second partial derivatives** of $\mathbf{S}$:

$$\mathbf{S}_{uu} = (0, 0, f_{uu})$$

$$\mathbf{S}_{uv} = (0, 0, f_{uv})$$

$$\mathbf{S}_{vv} = (0, 0, f_{vv})$$

**Step 4 — Compute the unit normal** (from 3.1):

$$\mathbf{n} = \frac{(-f_u, -f_v, 1)}{\sqrt{1 + f_u^2 + f_v^2}}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the coefficients of the Second Fundamental Form:**

$$L = \mathbf{n} \cdot \mathbf{S}_{uu} = \frac{f_{uu}}{\sqrt{1 + f_u^2 + f_v^2}}$$

$$M = \mathbf{n} \cdot \mathbf{S}_{uv} = \frac{f_{uv}}{\sqrt{1 + f_u^2 + f_v^2}}$$

$$N = \mathbf{n} \cdot \mathbf{S}_{vv} = \frac{f_{vv}}{\sqrt{1 + f_u^2 + f_v^2}}$$

**Step 6 — Apply the Gaussian curvature formula** $K = (LN - M^2) / (EG - F^2)$:

$$K = \frac{\dfrac{f_{uu} f_{vv} - f_{uv}^2}{1 + f_u^2 + f_v^2}}{\dfrac{(1 + f_u^2)(1 + f_v^2) - f_u^2 f_v^2}{}}$$

**Step 7 — Simplify the denominator:**

$$(1 + f_u^2)(1 + f_v^2) - f_u^2 f_v^2 = 1 + f_u^2 + f_v^2$$

**Step 8 — Final compact form:**

$$\boxed{K = \frac{f_{xx}\,f_{yy} - f_{xy}^2}{(1 + f_x^2 + f_y^2)^2}}$$

*Logic:* The numerator is the determinant of the **Hessian matrix** of $f$, and the denominator captures the slope-induced stretching. This elegant formula is the basis for shape-from-X methods in computer vision.

### 3.3 Derivation: Shape from Shading — Recovering $z$ from $E$

Given a single image $E(x, y)$ of a Lambertian surface with known light direction $\mathbf{L} = (L_x, L_y, L_z)$ and known albedo $\rho$, derive the surface $z(x, y)$.

**Step 1 — Brightness equation** (Lambertian):

$$E(x, y) = \rho\,(\mathbf{n} \cdot \mathbf{L})$$

**Step 2 — Express dot product** in terms of surface gradients $p = \partial z/\partial x$, $q = \partial z/\partial y$:

$$\mathbf{n} \cdot \mathbf{L} = \frac{(-p, -q, 1)}{\sqrt{1 + p^2 + q^2}} \cdot (L_x, L_y, L_z) = \frac{-p L_x - q L_y + L_z}{\sqrt{1 + p^2 + q^2}}$$

**Step 3 — Substituting:**

$$E(x, y) = \rho \,\frac{L_z - p L_x - q L_y}{\sqrt{1 + p^2 + q^2}}$$

**Step 4 — Define the reflectance map** $R(p, q)$:

$$R(p, q) = \rho \,\frac{1 + p\,L'_x + q\,L'_y}{\sqrt{1 + p^2 + q^2}\,\sqrt{1 + L'^2_x + L'^2_y}}$$

where $L'_x = -L_x/L_z$ and $L'_y = -L_y/L_z$ (light tilt coordinates).

**Step 5 — The image irradiance equation** becomes:

$$E(x, y) = R(p, q)$$

*Logic:* This is a **first-order Partial Differential Equation (PDE)** in $z(x, y)$. Solving it requires characteristic strip methods, iterative schemes (e.g., Horn's algorithm), or calculus of variations.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Curvature Behavior on Common Surfaces

| Surface | $f(x,y)$ | $K$ | $H$ | Curvature Class |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Plane | $z = 0$ | $0$ | $0$ | Planar |
| Paraboloid | $z = x^2 + y^2$ | $4/(1+4(x^2+y^2))^2$ | $4(x^2+y^2)+2 \over (1+4(x^2+y^2))^{3/2}$ | Elliptic (apex) |
| Saddle | $z = x^2 - y^2$ | $-4/(1+4(x^2-y^2))^2$ | $0$ | Hyperbolic |
| Cylinder | $z = \sqrt{1-x^2}$ | $0$ | $-1/(2(1-x^2)^{3/2})$ | Parabolic |
| Sphere (Hemisphere) | $z = \sqrt{R^2 - x^2 - y^2}$ | $1/R^2$ | $1/R$ | Elliptic |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Surface Representation Taxonomy (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[3D Surface Representation] --> B[Explicit Form]
    A --> C[Implicit Form]
    A --> D[Parametric Form]
    A --> E[Discrete Form]

    B --> B1["z = f of x,y"]
    B --> B2["Examples: Depth Map, Height Field"]
    B --> B3["Limitation: No Overhangs"]

    C --> C1["F of x,y,z = 0"]
    C --> C2["Examples: Sphere, SDF"]
    C --> C3["Advantage: Inside/Outside Test"]

    D --> D1["S of u,v maps to 3D point"]
    D --> D2["Examples: Bezier Patch, NURBS"]
    D --> D3["Advantage: Complex Topology"]

    E --> E1[Point Cloud]
    E --> E2[Polygon Mesh]
    E --> E3[Voxel Grid]

    E2 --> E21[Triangle Mesh]
    E2 --> E22[Quad Mesh]
```

### 4.2 Surface Recovery Pipeline (Shape from X)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Input
        I1[Single Image]
        I2[Stereo Pair]
        I3[Video Sequence]
        I4[LiDAR Scan]
    end

    subgraph Technique
        T1[Shape from Shading]
        T2[Shape from Stereo]
        T3[Shape from Motion]
        T4[Surface Reconstruction]
    end

    subgraph Output
        O1[Normal Field]
        O2[Depth Map]
        O3[3D Point Cloud]
        O4[Polygon Mesh]
    end

    I1 --> T1
    I2 --> T2
    I3 --> T3
    I4 --> T4

    T1 --> O1
    T2 --> O2
    T3 --> O3
    T4 --> O4

    O1 --> P1[Curvature Computation]
    O2 --> P2[Mesh Generation]
    O3 --> P3[Poisson Reconstruction]
    O4 --> P4[Texture Mapping]

    P1 --> Final[Final 3D Surface Model]
    P2 --> Final
    P3 --> Final
    P4 --> Final
```

### 4.3 First and Second Fundamental Form Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Parametric Surface S of u,v] --> B[Compute First Partial Derivatives]
    B --> B1["S_u = partial S / partial u"]
    B --> B2["S_v = partial S / partial v"]
    B1 --> C[Compute E, F, G]
    B2 --> C
    C --> C1["E = S_u dot S_u"]
    C --> C2["F = S_u dot S_v"]
    C --> C3["G = S_v dot S_v"]
    C --> D[First Fundamental Form]

    B --> E[Compute Second Partial Derivatives]
    E --> E1["S_uu"]
    E --> E2["S_uv"]
    E --> E3["S_vv"]
    E1 --> F[Compute L, M, N via dot n]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    B1 --> G[Cross Product]
    B2 --> G
    G --> G1["n = S_u cross S_v / magnitude"]
    G1 --> F
    F --> F1["L = n dot S_uu"]
    F --> F2["M = n dot S_uv"]
    F --> F3["N = n dot S_vv"]
    F --> H[Second Fundamental Form]

    D --> I[Curvature Computation]
    H --> I
    I --> I1["K = LN minus M squared over EG minus F squared"]
    I --> I2["H = EN plus GL minus 2FM over 2 EG minus F squared"]
    I1 --> J[Surface Classification]
    I2 --> J
```

### 4.4 Surface Classification Decision Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start[Compute K and H at Point P] --> Q1{K greater than 0?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Ell[Elliptic - Dome or Bowl]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{K less than 0?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| Hyp[Hyperbolic - Saddle]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{H not equal to 0?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| Par[Parabolic - Cylinder or Cone]
    Q3 -->|No| Pla[Planar - Flat Region]

    Ell --> Out1[All directions curve same way]
    Hyp --> Out2[Some directions curve up, others down]
    Par --> Out3[One principal direction is flat]
    Pla --> Out4[Surface is locally flat]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

*Define a surface in its parametric form. Explain the procedure to compute the unit surface normal from this representation.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A parametric surface in $\mathbb{R}^3$ is a mapping from a 2D parameter domain to 3D space:

$$\mathbf{S}(u, v) = (x(u, v),\; y(u, v),\; z(u, v))$$

**Procedure to compute unit surface normal (3 steps):**

1. **Compute partial derivatives** (tangent vectors):
   $$\mathbf{S}_u = \frac{\partial \mathbf{S}}{\partial u}, \quad \mathbf{S}_v = \frac{\partial \mathbf{S}}{\partial v}$$

2. **Compute cross product** to get a normal vector:
   $$\mathbf{N} = \mathbf{S}_u \times \mathbf{S}_v$$

3. **Normalize** to obtain the unit normal:
   $$\mathbf{n} = \frac{\mathbf{S}_u \times \mathbf{S}_v}{\vert \mathbf{S}_u \times \mathbf{S}_v \vert}$$

The magnitude of the cross product $\vert \mathbf{S}_u \times \mathbf{S}_v \vert$ must be non-zero (i.e., the surface is **regular**) for the normal to be well-defined at a point.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

*Differentiate between Gaussian curvature and Mean curvature. How are they used to classify surface points?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | Gaussian Curvature ($K$) | Mean Curvature ($H$) |
|---|---|---|
| Formula | $K = \kappa_1 \kappa_2$ | $H = (\kappa_1 + \kappa_2)/2$ |
| Geometric meaning | Product of principal curvatures | Average of principal curvatures |
| Type | **Intrinsic** (depends only on distances on surface) | **Extrinsic** (depends on embedding in $\mathbb{R}^3$) |

**Surface classification using $(K, H)$:**

| Condition | Surface Type | Example |
|---|---|---|
| $K > 0$ | Elliptic (Dome/Bowl) | Sphere |
| $K < 0$ | Hyperbolic (Saddle) | Pringle |
| $K = 0$, $H \neq 0$ | Parabolic (Cylinder/Cone) | Cylinder |
| $K = 0$, $H = 0$ | Planar (Flat) | Plane |

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Define the First and Second Fundamental Forms of a parametric surface. Compute them for the **monkey saddle** surface given by:

$$z = x^3 - 3xy^2$$

**(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for **Gaussian curvature** $K$ of a surface given in explicit form $z = f(x, y)$ and compute $K$ at the origin for $f(x, y) = x^3 - 3xy^2$. Classify the origin. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Fundamental Forms for $z = f(x, y) = x^3 - 3xy^2$:**

**Step 1 — First Fundamental Form:** With $\mathbf{S}(u,v) = (u, v, f(u,v))$:

$$E = 1 + f_u^2, \quad F = f_u f_v, \quad G = 1 + f_v^2$$

Compute the partial derivatives:

$$f_u = f_x = 3x^2 - 3y^2, \quad f_v = f_y = -6xy$$

At the **origin** $(0, 0)$: $f_u = 0$, $f_v = 0$.

Therefore at the origin:

$$E = 1 + 0 = 1, \quad F = 0, \quad G = 1 + 0 = 1$$

So the First Fundamental Form at origin is:

$$I = du^2 + dv^2$$

**Step 2 — Second Fundamental Form:** With unit normal $\mathbf{n} = (-f_x, -f_y, 1)/\sqrt{1 + f_x^2 + f_y^2}$:

$$L = \frac{f_{xx}}{\sqrt{1+f_x^2+f_y^2}}, \quad M = \frac{f_{xy}}{\sqrt{1+f_x^2+f_y^2}}, \quad N = \frac{f_{yy}}{\sqrt{1+f_x^2+f_y^2}}$$

Compute second derivatives:

$$f_{xx} = 6x, \quad f_{xy} = -6y, \quad f_{yy} = -6x$$

At origin: $f_{xx} = 0$, $f_{xy} = 0$, $f_{yy} = 0$.

Therefore at origin: $L = M = N = 0$, and the Second Fundamental Form is:

$$II = 0$$

**Interpretation:** At the origin, the surface is **osculating to its tangent plane** to second order.

---

**(b) Derivation and Computation of $K$:**

**Step 1 — Recall the derivation** (from Section 3.2):

$$K = \frac{f_{xx}\,f_{yy} - f_{xy}^2}{(1 + f_x^2 + f_y^2)^2}$$

**Step 2 — Compute at origin** for $f(x, y) = x^3 - 3xy^2$:

- Numerator: $f_{xx} f_{yy} - f_{xy}^2 = (0)(0) - (0)^2 = 0$
- Denominator: $(1 + 0 + 0)^2 = 1$

Therefore:

$$K\big|_{(0,0)} = \frac{0}{1} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Classification:** Since $K = 0$ at the origin, the origin is either **parabolic** or **planar**. To distinguish, examine the Hessian determinant in a neighborhood: for $f(x, y) = x^3 - 3xy^2$, we have $f_{xx} f_{yy} - f_{xy}^2 = 6x \cdot (-6x) - (-6y)^2 = -36x^2 - 36y^2 < 0$ for $(x, y) \neq (0,0)$.

**Conclusion:** $K \leq 0$ in a neighborhood of the origin, with $K = 0$ only at the origin. The origin is a **flat point** (a *monkey saddle* where the surface is locally saddle-like but has a special higher-order degeneracy).

---

**Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2023] — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** What is **Shape from Shading**? State the assumptions of the classical Horn & Ikeuchi method. Derive the **reflectance map** equation from the Lambertian brightness model. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A planar surface $z = ax + by + c$ is imaged under a distant point light source at direction $\mathbf{L} = (0, 0, 1)$ (camera at the zenith) with albedo $\rho = 1$. Compute the image irradiance $E(x, y)$ everywhere. Justify your answer. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Shape from Shading — Theory and Reflectance Map:**

**Definition:** Shape from Shading (SFS) is the process of recovering the 3D surface shape $z(x, y)$ from a single 2D image $E(x, y)$, given known illumination conditions and reflectance properties.

**Assumptions of the classical method:**

1. **Lambertian reflectance** — surface reflects light equally in all directions.
2. **Known light source direction** $\mathbf{L}$ (distant, parallel rays).
3. **Known albedo** $\rho(x, y)$ (often assumed uniform).
4. **Orthographic projection** — camera at infinity.
5. **Smooth, continuous surface** — differentiability is required.

**Derivation of Reflectance Map:**

Starting from the Lambertian brightness equation:

$$E(x, y) = \rho\,(\mathbf{n} \cdot \mathbf{L})$$

Express the normal in gradient form $p = \partial z/\partial x$, $q = \partial z/\partial y$:

$$\mathbf{n} = \frac{(-p, -q, 1)}{\sqrt{1 + p^2 + q^2}}$$

Substitute:

$$E(x, y) = \rho\,\frac{-p L_x - q L_y + L_z}{\sqrt{1 + p^2 + q^2}}$$

**Re-parameterize** the light source using tilt angles $(\tau, \sigma)$ or normalized form $L'_x = -L_x/L_z$, $L'_y = -L_y/L_z$:

$$E(x, y) = \rho\,\frac{\cos\sigma - p\sin\sigma\cos\tau - q\sin\sigma\sin\tau}{\sqrt{1 + p^2 + q^2}}$$

The compact **reflectance map** form:

$$\boxed{R(p, q) = \rho\,\frac{1 + p\,L'_x + q\,L'_y}{\sqrt{1 + p^2 + q^2}\,\sqrt{1 + L'^2_x + L'^2_y}}}$$

This converts a 3D shading problem into a 2D problem in gradient space $(p, q)$.

---

**(b) Image Irradiance of a Planar Surface:**

**Step 1 — Identify gradients of the plane:**

$$p = \frac{\partial z}{\partial x} = a, \quad q = \frac{\partial z}{\partial y} = b$$

These are **constants** (the plane has constant slope).

**Step 2 — Compute the surface normal:**

$$\mathbf{n} = \frac{(-a, -b, 1)}{\sqrt{1 + a^2 + b^2}}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Lambertian brightness equation** with $\mathbf{L} = (0, 0, 1)$:

$$E(x, y) = \rho\,(\mathbf{n} \cdot \mathbf{L}) = 1 \cdot \frac{(-a)(0) + (-b)(0) + (1)(1)}{\sqrt{1 + a^2 + b^2}}$$

$$E(x, y) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + a^2 + b^2}}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**

$$\boxed{E(x, y) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + a^2 + b^2}} = \text{constant for all } (x, y)}$$

**Justification:** Because the plane has a uniform normal at every point, and the light source is parallel to the optical axis ($\mathbf{L} = (0,0,1)$), the angle of incidence is the **same everywhere**. Hence the image of a tilted plane under zenithal illumination appears as a **uniformly bright (constant gray-level) image** — there are no shading gradients to recover depth information. This illustrates a fundamental **ambiguity** in SFS: a flat tilted plane is indistinguishable from any other orientation that yields the same $\mathbf{n} \cdot \mathbf{L}$ product.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to normalize** the cross product → loses 1 mark on normal computation. Always divide by the magnitude.
> 2. **Confusing intrinsic vs extrinsic curvature** — Gaussian $K$ is intrinsic (Theorem Egregium!), Mean $H$ is extrinsic. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 3. **Sign errors in second derivatives** — The Hessian determinant $f_{xx} f_{yy} - f_{xy}^2$ is the **numerator of $K$**; students often write $f_{xx} f_{yy} + f_{xy}^2$ (wrong sign).
> 4. **In Shape from Shading:** Failing to state assumptions (Lambertian, known light, orthographic) → lose 2 marks outright.
> 5. **For $z = f(x,y)$:** The normal formula is $(-f_x, -f_y, 1)$, NOT $(f_x, f_y, -1)$ — both are valid, but must be **explicitly stated** in the chosen convention.
> 6. **Reflection map derivation:** Skipping the gradient substitution step is a common mark-loser; show the full algebraic chain.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
