> **High-Yield Quick-Fire Revision Points:**

- **Three representations:** Explicit $z = f(x,y)$, Implicit $F(x,y,z) = 0$, Parametric $\mathbf{S}(u,v)$.
- **Normal (parametric):** $\mathbf{n} = (\mathbf{S}_u \times \mathbf{S}_v)/\vert \mathbf{S}_u \times \mathbf{S}_v \vert$.
- **Normal (explicit):** $\mathbf{n} = (-f_x, -f_y, 1)/\sqrt{1 + f_x^2 + f_y^2}$.
- **Normal (implicit):** $\mathbf{n} = \nabla F / \vert \nabla F \vert$.
- **Gradient shorthand:** $p = \partial z/\partial x$, $q = \partial z/\partial y$.
- **First Fundamental Form coefficients:** $E = 1 + p^2$, $F = pq$, $G = 1 + q^2$ (for Monge patch).
- **Second Fundamental Form coefficients:** $L = f_{xx}/W$, $M = f_{xy}/W$, $N = f_{yy}/W$ where $W = \sqrt{1 + p^2 + q^2}$.
- **Gaussian curvature (Monge patch):** $K = (f_{xx} f_{yy} - f_{xy}^2)/(1 + p^2 + q^2)^2$.
- **Mean curvature (Monge patch):** $H = [(1+q^2)f_{xx} - 2pq f_{xy} + (1+p^2)f_{yy}]/[2(1+p^2+q^2)^{3/2}]$.
- **Theorem Egregium (Gauss):** $K$ is **intrinsic** — bending a paper does not change $K$ (which is why you cannot flatten a sphere without tearing).
- **Curvature classification:** $K > 0$ → Elliptic, $K < 0$ → Hyperbolic, $K = 0$ & $H \neq 0$ → Parabolic, $K = H = 0$ → Planar.
- **Lambertian brightness:** $E = \rho(\mathbf{n} \cdot \mathbf{L})$.
- **Reflectance Map:** $R(p,q) = \rho\,\dfrac{1 + p L'_x + q L'_y}{\sqrt{1+p^2+q^2}\sqrt{1+L'^2_x+L'^2_y}}$.
- **Shape from X:** SFS, SFT, SFF, stereo, motion, contour — each recovers surfaces from different cues.
- **Range data → Mesh:** Use Delaunay triangulation, Poisson surface reconstruction, or marching cubes.
- **Point cloud → Surface:** Pipeline = (Point Cloud) → (Normal Estimation) → (Poisson/Marching Cubes) → (Mesh).
- **Most-tested KTU formula:** $K = (f_{xx} f_{yy} - f_{xy}^2)/(1 + f_x^2 + f_y^2)^2$ — **memorize this verbatim**.
- **Curse of SFS:** Concave/convex ambiguity (Bas-relief ambiguity) — cannot distinguish a sculpture from its mold.
- **Boundary conditions for SFS:** Need at least one known depth or known normal to break the integration ambiguity.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analytical Image Features - Elements of Analytical Euclidean Geometry
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST745 - COMPUTER VISION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Analytical Image Features - Elements of Analytical Euclidean Geometry

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Analytical Euclidean Geometry** is the branch of geometry that uses the **Cartesian coordinate system** and **algebraic methods** to represent, manipulate, and reason about geometric entities such as **points, lines, planes, vectors, and curves**. In the context of Computer Vision, it provides the foundational mathematical language to describe image features, pixel locations, transformations, and the spatial relationships between objects in a scene.

In Computer Vision, every image feature (corner, edge, blob) is reduced to a set of **geometric primitives**—most commonly **points** and **vectors**—which are then analyzed, transformed, and matched using Euclidean geometry.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight:**
> *Analytical Euclidean geometry forms the bedrock of feature extraction pipelines. Every keypoint detector (Harris, SIFT, ORB) ultimately outputs points whose spatial relationships are governed by Euclidean laws.*

### 1.2 Core Geometric Entities

| Entity | Notation | Purpose in Computer Vision |
|---|---|---|
| Point | $P = (x, y)$ in 2D, $P = (x, y, z)$ in 3D | Represents pixel location, keypoint, or 3D landmark |
| Vector | $\vec{v} = (v_x, v_y, v_z)$ | Represents direction, motion, gradient, or displacement |
| Line | $\mathbf{r} = \mathbf{p_0} + t\mathbf{d}$ | Represents edges, optical flow direction, or epipolar lines |
| Plane | $\mathbf{n} \cdot (\mathbf{r} - \mathbf{p_0}) = 0$ | Represents image plane, ground plane, or any planar surface |
| Matrix | $\mathbf{M} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ | Encodes transformations (rotation, scaling, projection) |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "GPS for Pixels"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition: The City Grid Analogy**
> Imagine an image as a **perfectly planned city** laid out on an infinite grid. Every building (pixel) has a unique street address $(x, y)$. **Euclidean geometry is the city's rulebook**—it tells you:
> - **How far** is Building A from Building B? (Distance formula)
> - **In what direction** is the road heading? (Vector representation)
> - **How do you rotate the city map** by 90°? (Rotation matrix)
> - **Where do two roads cross?** (Line intersection)
>
> Without this rulebook, a computer vision algorithm would be like a tourist with no map—unable to navigate the image to find features, track objects, or reconstruct 3D scenes.

### 1.4 Why Euclidean Geometry Matters in Computer Vision

The entire pipeline of a vision system—from feature detection to 3D reconstruction—relies on Euclidean principles:

1. **Feature Localization:** Keypoints like SIFT or ORB are stored as $(x, y, \sigma, \theta)$ tuples, where positions are Euclidean coordinates.
2. **Image Transformations:** Rotating, scaling, and translating images are expressed as matrix multiplications in Euclidean space.
3. **Camera Geometry:** The pinhole camera model uses Euclidean projections (focal length, principal point).
4. **Stereo & Depth:** Triangulation between two camera views uses Euclidean distance laws.
5. **Pose Estimation:** Estimating object orientation requires rotation matrices built from Euclidean geometry.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Condition:** Euclidean geometry assumes a **flat (non-curved)** space. For very wide-field or fisheye cameras, **projective or non-Euclidean geometry** must be used. KTU examiners frequently test this distinction.
>
> **Fundamental Constant in 2D/3D:** The **Euclidean norm** $\| \cdot \|_2$ is the standard distance metric, with **dimension** in pixels (2D) or millimeters/meters (3D).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Pillars of Analytical Euclidean Geometry

#### Pillar 1: Points and Coordinate Systems
A **point** $P$ in $n$-dimensional Euclidean space $\mathbb{R}^n$ is an ordered $n$-tuple:
$$P = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)$$

- **2D Image Plane:** $P = (u, v)$ — used for pixel coordinates.
- **3D World Space:** $P = (X, Y, Z)$ — used for object/scene coordinates.
- **Homogeneous Coordinates:** $\tilde{P} = (x, y, w)$ where the actual point is $(x/w, y/w)$ — enables projective transformations using a single matrix multiplication.

#### Pillar 2: Vectors and Vector Operations
A **vector** $\vec{v}$ encodes both **magnitude** (length) and **direction**. The key operations are:

- **Addition:** $\vec{a} + \vec{b} = (a_1+b_1, a_2+b_2, a_3+b_3)$
- **Scalar Multiplication:** $\lambda \vec{v} = (\lambda v_1, \lambda v_2, \lambda v_3)$
- **Dot Product:** $\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b} = a_1 b_1 + a_2 b_2 + a_3 b_3 = \vert \vec{a} \vert \vert \vec{b} \vert \cos\theta$
- **Cross Product (3D only):** $\vec{a} \times \vec{b} = (a_2 b_3 - a_3 b_2, a_3 b_1 - a_1 b_3, a_1 b_2 - a_2 b_1)$

#### Pillar 3: Lines in 2D and 3D

- **2D Line (Implicit Form):** $ax + by + c = 0$, where $\mathbf{n} = (a, b)$ is the normal vector.
- **2D Line (Parametric Form):** $\mathbf{r}(t) = \mathbf{p_0} + t \mathbf{d}$, where $\mathbf{d}$ is the direction vector.
- **3D Line (Symmetric Form):** $\frac{x - x_0}{a} = \frac{y - y_0}{b} = \frac{z - z_0}{c}$

#### Pillar 4: Distance Metrics
The **Euclidean distance** between two points $P_1 = (x_1, y_1)$ and $P_2 = (x_2, y_2)$ in 2D is:
$$d(P_1, P_2) = \sqrt{(x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2}$$

The **point-to-line distance** from point $(x_0, y_0)$ to line $ax + by + c = 0$ is:
$$d = \frac{\vert a x_0 + b y_0 + c \vert}{\sqrt{a^2 + b^2}}$$

#### Pillar 5: Transformations (Rigid and Affine)

- **Translation:** $P' = P + \mathbf{t}$
- **Rotation (2D):** $P' = \mathbf{R} P$, where $\mathbf{R} = \begin{pmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{pmatrix}$
- **Scaling:** $P' = \mathbf{S} P$, where $\mathbf{S} = \begin{pmatrix} s_x & 0 \\ 0 & s_y \end{pmatrix}$

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these formulas — they appear in 80%+ of KTU Computer Vision exam questions.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2D Euclidean Distance | $d = \sqrt{(x_2-x_1)^2 + (y_2-y_1)^2}$ | Pixels |
| 2 | 3D Euclidean Distance | $d = \sqrt{(x_2-x_1)^2 + (y_2-y_1)^2 + (z_2-z_1)^2}$ | Meters/mm |
| 3 | Vector Magnitude | $\vert \vec{v} \vert = \sqrt{v_x^2 + v_y^2 + v_z^2}$ | Same as $P$ |
| 4 | Dot Product | $\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b} = \sum a_i b_i = \vert \vec{a} \vert \vert \vec{b} \vert \cos\theta$ | Scalar |
| 5 | Cross Product Magnitude | $\vert \vec{a} \times \vec{b} \vert = \vert \vec{a} \vert \vert \vec{b} \vert \sin\theta$ | Scalar |
| 6 | Cosine Similarity | $\cos\theta = \frac{\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}}{\vert \vec{a} \vert \vert \vec{b} \vert}$ | Range: $[-1, 1]$ |
| 7 | Point-to-Line Distance | $d = \frac{\vert a x_0 + b y_0 + c \vert}{\sqrt{a^2 + b^2}}$ | Pixels |
| 8 | 2D Rotation Matrix | $\mathbf{R}(\theta) = \begin{pmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{pmatrix}$ | $\mathbf{R}^T = \mathbf{R}^{-1}$ |
| 9 | 3D Rotation about Z-axis | $\mathbf{R}_z(\theta) = \begin{pmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$ | Right-hand rule |
| 10 | Rigid Transform | $P' = \mathbf{R} P + \mathbf{t}$ | 6 DoF (3 rot + 3 trans) |
| 11 | Homogeneous Transform | $\begin{pmatrix} P' \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} \mathbf{R} & \mathbf{t} \\ \mathbf{0}^T & 1 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} P \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}$ | $4 \times 4$ matrix |
| 12 | Parametric Line | $\mathbf{r}(t) = \mathbf{p_0} + t \mathbf{d}$ | $t \in \mathbb{R}$ |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Applications of Euclidean Geometry in Vision:**
> - **Autonomous Vehicles (Tesla, Waymo):** LiDAR points are in 3D Euclidean space; distance between consecutive points is computed via the 3D distance formula.
> - **AR/VR (Apple Vision Pro, Meta Quest):** Head pose tracking uses rotation matrices and translation vectors to render 3D objects aligned with the real world.
> - **Medical Imaging (CT/MRI):** Tumor volume estimation integrates voxel distances in 3D Euclidean space.
> - **Robotics (Boston Dynamics):** Inverse kinematics requires computing distances and angles between robot joints.
> - **Structure from Motion (SfM):** 3D reconstruction from 2D images fundamentally depends on Euclidean triangulation.

### 2.4 The "Why" Behind the Formulas

- **Why the dot product gives cosine similarity:** Because the dot product's algebraic definition $a_1b_1 + a_2b_2$ equals $\vert \vec{a} \vert \vert \vec{b} \vert \cos\theta$ by the law of cosines. This is the cornerstone of feature matching in SIFT/ORB.
- **Why rotation matrices are orthogonal:** $\mathbf{R}^T \mathbf{R} = \mathbf{I}$ because rotations preserve lengths. This is why $\mathbf{R}^{-1} = \mathbf{R}^T$ — a key property tested in KTU exams.
- **Why homogeneous coordinates are used:** They allow translation (an *affine* operation) to be expressed as a *linear* matrix multiplication, unifying all transformations into a single $4 \times 4$ matrix.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: 2D Euclidean Distance Formula

**Starting Premise:** The distance between two points in a plane is the length of the straight line connecting them.

**Step 1: Set up two points in 2D.**
Let $P_1 = (x_1, y_1)$ and $P_2 = (x_2, y_2)$ be two points in $\mathbb{R}^2$.

**Step 2: Form the displacement vector.**
The vector from $P_1$ to $P_2$ is:
$$\vec{d} = P_2 - P_1 = (x_2 - x_1, \ y_2 - y_1)$$

**Step 3: Apply the Pythagorean Theorem.**
The displacement vector forms a right triangle with horizontal leg $\Delta x = x_2 - x_1$ and vertical leg $\Delta y = y_2 - y_1$. By the Pythagorean theorem:
$$\vert \vec{d} \vert^2 = (\Delta x)^2 + (\Delta y)^2$$

**Step 4: Take the square root.**
$$d(P_1, P_2) = \sqrt{(x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic Check:** This satisfies the three axioms of a metric: (1) non-negativity, (2) identity of indiscernibles, (3) triangle inequality.

### 3.2 Derivation: 2D Rotation Matrix

**Starting Premise:** A rotation by angle $\theta$ about the origin transforms every point $(x, y)$ to a new point $(x', y')$.

**Step 1: Represent a point in polar form.**
Any point $(x, y)$ can be written as:
$$x = r \cos\phi, \quad y = r \sin\phi$$
where $r$ is the distance from origin and $\phi$ is the angle with the x-axis.

**Step 2: Apply the rotation.**
After rotating by $\theta$, the new angle is $\phi + \theta$, so:
$$x' = r \cos(\phi + \theta), \quad y' = r \sin(\phi + \theta)$$

**Step 3: Expand using angle-addition identities.**
Using $\cos(A+B) = \cos A \cos B - \sin A \sin B$ and $\sin(A+B) = \sin A \cos B + \cos A \sin B$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
x' &= r(\cos\phi \cos\theta - \sin\phi \sin\theta) \\
y' &= r(\sin\phi \cos\theta + \cos\phi \sin\theta)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Substitute $x = r\cos\phi$ and $y = r\sin\phi$.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
x' &= x \cos\theta - y \sin\theta \\
y' &= x \sin\theta + y \cos\theta
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Write in matrix form.**
$$
\begin{pmatrix} x' \\ y' \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} x \\ y \end{pmatrix}
$$

This is the **2D rotation matrix** $\mathbf{R}(\theta)$.

### 3.3 Derivation: Point-to-Line Distance Formula

**Starting Premise:** Given line $L: ax + by + c = 0$ and point $P_0 = (x_0, y_0)$, find the perpendicular distance from $P_0$ to $L$.

**Step 1: Find the foot of the perpendicular.**
The foot $P_f$ lies on $L$ and the vector $P_0 P_f$ is parallel to the line's normal $\mathbf{n} = (a, b)$.

**Step 2: Parametrize the foot.**
Let $P_f = (x_0 - at, \ y_0 - bt)$ for some scalar $t$.

**Step 3: Substitute into the line equation.**
Since $P_f$ lies on $L$:
$$a(x_0 - at) + b(y_0 - bt) + c = 0$$

**Step 4: Solve for $t$.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
a x_0 - a^2 t + b y_0 - b^2 t + c &= 0 \\
t(a^2 + b^2) &= a x_0 + b y_0 + c \\
t &= \frac{a x_0 + b y_0 + c}{a^2 + b^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Compute the distance.**
The distance is the length of the perpendicular vector:
$$d = \vert t \vert \sqrt{a^2 + b^2} = \frac{\vert a x_0 + b y_0 + c \vert}{\sqrt{a^2 + b^2}}$$

### 3.4 Derivation: Cross Product Magnitude

**Starting Premise:** Prove that $\vert \vec{a} \times \vec{b} \vert = \vert \vec{a} \vert \vert \vec{b} \vert \sin\theta$.

**Step 1: Compute the cross product algebraically.**
For $\vec{a} = (a_1, a_2, a_3)$ and $\vec{b} = (b_1, b_2, b_3)$:
$$\vec{a} \times \vec{b} = (a_2 b_3 - a_3 b_2, \ a_3 b_1 - a_1 b_3, \ a_1 b_2 - a_2 b_1)$$

**Step 2: Square the magnitude.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert \vec{a} \times \vec{b} \vert^2 &= (a_2 b_3 - a_3 b_2)^2 + (a_3 b_1 - a_1 b_3)^2 + (a_1 b_2 - a_2 b_1)^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Expand and simplify (Lagrange's identity).**
After algebraic expansion (shown in detail in standard references), this simplifies to:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert \vec{a} \times \vec{b} \vert^2 &= (a_1^2 + a_2^2 + a_3^2)(b_1^2 + b_2^2 + b_3^2) - (a_1 b_1 + a_2 b_2 + a_3 b_3)^2 \\
&= \vert \vec{a} \vert^2 \vert \vec{b} \vert^2 - (\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b})^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Apply the trigonometric identity.**
Using $\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta = 1$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert \vec{a} \times \vec{b} \vert^2 &= \vert \vec{a} \vert^2 \vert \vec{b} \vert^2 (1 - \cos^2\theta) \\
&= \vert \vec{a} \vert^2 \vert \vec{b} \vert^2 \sin^2\theta
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Take the square root.**
$$\vert \vec{a} \times \vec{b} \vert = \vert \vec{a} \vert \vert \vec{b} \vert \sin\theta \quad \text{(since } \sin\theta \geq 0 \text{ for } \theta \in [0, \pi]\text{)}$$

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Each Formula

| Scenario | Recommended Tool | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Distance between two keypoints | 2D/3D Distance formula | Direct pixel/world measurement |
| Angle between two image gradients | Dot product | Yields $\cos\theta$ directly |
| Area of a triangular image region | Half of cross product magnitude | $\text{Area} = \frac{1}{2}\vert \vec{AB} \times \vec{AC} \vert$ |
| Surface normal of a planar patch | Cross product of two edge vectors | $\mathbf{n} = \vec{e_1} \times \vec{e_2}$ |
| Rotating an image by 30° | 2D Rotation matrix | Preserves pixel distances |
| Projecting a 3D point to 2D | Homogeneous transform | Combines rotation + translation in one matrix |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define Euclidean distance in 2D. Compute the distance between the keypoints $A = (10, 20)$ and $B = (13, 24)$ in an image.*

**Model Answer:**

Euclidean distance in 2D is the straight-line distance between two points $(x_1, y_1)$ and $(x_2, y_2)$ in the Cartesian plane, given by:
$$d = \sqrt{(x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2}$$

**Substitution:**
$$d = \sqrt{(13 - 10)^2 + (24 - 20)^2} = \sqrt{3^2 + 4^2} = \sqrt{9 + 16} = \sqrt{25} = 5 \text{ pixels}$$

**Final Answer:** $d = 5$ pixels. **(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for computation)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is the dot product? Explain its role in computing the angle between two feature descriptor vectors in SIFT matching.*

**Model Answer:**

The dot product of two vectors $\vec{a} = (a_1, a_2, a_3)$ and $\vec{b} = (b_1, b_2, b_3)$ is defined as:
$$\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b} = a_1 b_1 + a_2 b_2 + a_3 b_3 = \vert \vec{a} \vert \vert \vec{b} \vert \cos\theta$$

**Role in SIFT matching:** SIFT generates 128-dimensional descriptor vectors for each keypoint. To find the best match between two keypoints, the **cosine of the angle** between their descriptor vectors is computed using the dot product:
$$\cos\theta = \frac{\vec{d_1} \cdot \vec{d_2}}{\vert \vec{d_1} \vert \vert \vec{d_2} \vert}$$

A higher $\cos\theta$ (closer to 1) indicates greater similarity, so the keypoint with the maximum cosine similarity is selected as the match. **(3 marks: 1 for formula + 2 for application)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> Common mistakes students make in this topic:
> 1. **Confusing degrees and radians** in rotation matrices — KTU strictly uses radians in formula derivation. **(−1 mark)**
> 2. **Forgetting to normalize** vectors when computing cosine similarity. **(−1 mark)**
> 3. **Sign errors** in 2D rotation matrix (the $(2,1)$ entry is $+\sin\theta$, not $-\sin\theta$). **(−2 marks)**
> 4. **Not specifying the coordinate system** (image vs. world) when applying transformations. **(−1 mark)**
> 5. **Skipping intermediate steps** in distance formula derivations. **(−1 mark)**
> 6. **Using the wrong cross product formula** — remember it is 3D-only in standard form. **(−2 marks)**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]

**(a)** Derive the 2D rotation matrix $\mathbf{R}(\theta)$ from first principles. Show that it is orthogonal, i.e., $\mathbf{R}^T \mathbf{R} = \mathbf{I}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Given the 3D point $P = (1, 0, 0)$ and the rotation matrix about the Z-axis by $\theta = 90°$, compute the rotated point $P'$. Also find the magnitude of the original and rotated vectors to show length preservation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of 2D Rotation Matrix:**

**Step 1:** Consider a point $P = (x, y)$ at distance $r$ from the origin making angle $\phi$ with the x-axis:
$$x = r\cos\phi, \quad y = r\sin\phi$$

**Step 2:** After rotation by $\theta$, the new angle is $\phi + \theta$:
$$x' = r\cos(\phi + \theta), \quad y' = r\sin(\phi + \theta)$$

**Step 3:** Expand using angle-sum identities:
$$
\begin{aligned}
x' &= r(\cos\phi\cos\theta - \sin\phi\sin\theta) \\
y' &= r(\sin\phi\cos\theta + \cos\phi\sin\theta)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Substitute $x = r\cos\phi$ and $y = r\sin\phi$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
x' &= x\cos\theta - y\sin\theta \\
y' &= x\sin\theta + y\cos\theta
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** In matrix form:
$$\mathbf{R}(\theta) = \begin{pmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 6:** Prove orthogonality:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{R}^T \mathbf{R} &= \begin{pmatrix} \cos\theta & \sin\theta \\ -\sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{pmatrix} \\
&= \begin{pmatrix} \cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta & -\cos\theta\sin\theta + \sin\theta\cos\theta \\ -\sin\theta\cos\theta + \cos\theta\sin\theta & \sin^2\theta + \cos^2\theta \end{pmatrix} \\
&= \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix} = \mathbf{I}
\end{aligned}
$$

This confirms $\mathbf{R}$ is orthogonal. **(7 marks: 5 for derivation + 2 for orthogonality proof)**

---

**(b) Numerical Application:**

**Step 1:** Convert angle to radians: $\theta = 90° = \pi/2 \Rightarrow \cos\theta = 0, \ \sin\theta = 1$.

**Step 2:** Construct the rotation matrix:
$$\mathbf{R}(90°) = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & -1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 3:** Apply to point $P = (1, 0)$ (using 2D since Z is fixed):
$$P' = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & -1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 4:** Compute magnitudes:
- $\vert P \vert = \sqrt{1^2 + 0^2} = 1$
- $\vert P' \vert = \sqrt{0^2 + 1^2} = 1$

**Conclusion:** $\vert P \vert = \vert P' \vert = 1$, demonstrating that rotation preserves vector length. **(7 marks: 3 for matrix + 2 for result + 2 for length verification)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Define the cross product of two 3D vectors. Derive the formula for the magnitude of the cross product and explain its geometric interpretation as the area of a parallelogram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Two edges of a triangular 3D surface patch are $\vec{e_1} = (1, 2, 0)$ and $\vec{e_2} = (0, 1, 3)$. Compute: (i) the cross product $\vec{n} = \vec{e_1} \times \vec{e_2}$, (ii) the unit normal vector, and (iii) the area of the triangle. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Cross Product Definition and Magnitude Derivation:**

**Definition:** The cross product $\vec{a} \times \vec{b}$ of two 3D vectors is a vector perpendicular to both $\vec{a}$ and $\vec{b}$, with magnitude equal to the area of the parallelogram they span.

**Derivation of Magnitude (Sketch):**
Starting from the algebraic definition:
$$\vec{a} \times \vec{b} = (a_2 b_3 - a_3 b_2, \ a_3 b_1 - a_1 b_3, \ a_1 b_2 - a_2 b_1)$$

Computing $\vert \vec{a} \times \vec{b} \vert^2$ and applying Lagrange's identity:
$$\vert \vec{a} \times \vec{b} \vert^2 = \vert \vec{a} \vert^2 \vert \vec{b} \vert^2 - (\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b})^2 = \vert \vec{a} \vert^2 \vert \vec{b} \vert^2(1 - \cos^2\theta) = \vert \vec{a} \vert^2 \vert \vec{b} \vert^2 \sin^2\theta$$

Therefore:
$$\vert \vec{a} \times \vec{b} \vert = \vert \vec{a} \vert \vert \vec{b} \vert \sin\theta$$

**Geometric Interpretation:** This is the **area of the parallelogram** formed by $\vec{a}$ and $\vec{b}$, since $\text{Area} = \text{base} \times \text{height} = \vert \vec{a} \vert \cdot (\vert \vec{b} \vert \sin\theta)$. **(7 marks: 2 for definition + 3 for derivation + 2 for interpretation)**

---

**(b) Numerical Computation:**

**(i) Cross Product:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\vec{n} = \vec{e_1} \times \vec{e_2} &= \begin{vmatrix} \mathbf{i} & \mathbf{j} & \mathbf{k} \\ 1 & 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 3 \end{vmatrix} \\
&= \mathbf{i}(2 \cdot 3 - 0 \cdot 1) - \mathbf{j}(1 \cdot 3 - 0 \cdot 0) + \mathbf{k}(1 \cdot 1 - 2 \cdot 0) \\
&= \mathbf{i}(6) - \mathbf{j}(3) + \mathbf{k}(1) \\
&= (6, -3, 1)
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Unit Normal Vector:**
$$\vert \vec{n} \vert = \sqrt{6^2 + (-3)^2 + 1^2} = \sqrt{36 + 9 + 1} = \sqrt{46}$$
$$\hat{n} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{46}}(6, -3, 1)$$

**(iii) Area of Triangle:**
$$\text{Area}_{\triangle} = \frac{1}{2} \vert \vec{n} \vert = \frac{\sqrt{46}}{2} \approx 3.39 \text{ square units}$$

**(7 marks: 3 for (i) + 2 for (ii) + 2 for (iii))**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- **Points** are locations, **vectors** are displacements; both are represented as tuples in $\mathbb{R}^n$.
- The **Euclidean distance** formula $d = \sqrt{\sum (x_i - y_i)^2}$ is the **only** metric satisfying all four metric axioms that KTU tests.
- The **dot product** returns a **scalar** and gives $\cos\theta$ between vectors — used in **SIFT/ORB descriptor matching**.
- The **cross product** is **3D-only**, returns a **vector perpendicular to both inputs**, and its magnitude equals the **parallelogram area** — used in **surface normal estimation**.
- The **2D rotation matrix** is $\begin{pmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{pmatrix}$; remember the **$(2,1)$ entry is positive** (most common KTU error).
- Rotation matrices are **orthogonal**: $\mathbf{R}^T = \mathbf{R}^{-1}$ and $\det(\mathbf{R}) = +1$.
- The **point-to-line distance** in 2D is $\frac{\vert a x_0 + b y_0 + c \vert}{\sqrt{a^2 + b^2}}$ — frequently tested in 3-mark and 7-mark questions.
- **Homogeneous coordinates** $(x, y, w)$ allow translation to be combined with rotation in a single $4 \times 4$ matrix — central to camera calibration.
- The **parametric line** form $\mathbf{r}(t) = \mathbf{p_0} + t\mathbf{d}$ is the foundation of **ray casting** and **epipolar geometry**.
- **Cosine similarity** ranges in $[-1, 1]$; **dot product** is unbounded; **always normalize** when using for similarity.
- In **3D rotation about Z-axis**, the Z-coordinate is unchanged; only $x$ and $y$ are transformed.
- A **rigid (Euclidean) transformation** has 6 degrees of freedom: 3 for rotation + 3 for translation.
- **Euclidean geometry is flat-space geometry**; for curved/fisheye images, switch to **projective or Riemannian** geometry.
- **KTU's most-tested identity:** $\mathbf{R}(\theta) \cdot \mathbf{R}(-\theta) = \mathbf{I}$ — proving the inverse of a rotation by $\theta$ is a rotation by $-\theta$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Geometric Camera Parameters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST745 - COMPUTER VISION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Geometric Camera Parameters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Geometric Camera Parameters** are the mathematical quantities that completely describe the imaging geometry of a pinhole (perspective) camera — i.e., how a point in the **3-D world coordinate system** is mapped to a point on the **2-D image (pixel) plane**. They are partitioned into two disjoint sets: **Intrinsic Parameters** (internal to the camera) and **Extrinsic Parameters** (external, relating the camera to the world).

### 1.1.1 Intrinsic Parameters (Internal Geometry)
These parameters describe the camera's internal construction — focal length, sensor format, and lens center. They map a point expressed in the **camera's own 3-D coordinate frame** to a 2-D pixel coordinate. The five classical intrinsic parameters are:

1. **Focal length $f$** — perpendicular distance from the pinhole (optical center) to the image plane, measured in **millimetres (mm)** or **pixels**.
2. **Principal point $(c_x, c_y)$** — pixel coordinates of the point where the optical axis pierces the image plane.
3. **Skew coefficient $s$** — non-orthogonality between the $u$ and $v$ axes of the sensor (often **0** for modern CMOS/CCD sensors).
4. **Pixel aspect ratio $\alpha$** — ratio of pixel width to pixel height.

These five scalars are assembled into the **Camera Calibration Matrix $K \in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times 3}$**.

### 1.1.2 Extrinsic Parameters (External Geometry)
These parameters describe the camera's **pose** (position + orientation) with respect to a fixed world reference frame. They are:
1. **Rotation matrix $R \in SO(3)$** — a $3 \times 3$ orthonormal matrix that re-orients the world axes into camera axes. It has **3 degrees of freedom**.
2. **Translation vector $\mathbf{t} \in \mathbb{R}^{3}$** — the 3-D coordinate of the camera's optical center expressed in the world frame. It has **3 degrees of freedom**.

Together, the extrinsic block has **6 degrees of freedom**, and the intrinsic block has **5**, giving **11 independent parameters** in total for the standard pinhole model.

---

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy — The Tourist Photographer

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Imagine a tourist, "Anu", holding a smartphone to capture the Gateway of India.**

- **Where Anu stands** (say, 5 metres north of the monument) and **the direction she points her phone** (tilted 10° upward, panned 30° to the right) define her **extrinsic parameters**. They tell us *where the camera is in the world* and *how it is rotated*.
- **The phone's internal optics** — focal length of its lens, the position of the principal point on the sensor, the pixel size — define its **intrinsic parameters**. They tell us *how the camera turns light into an image regardless of where it is*.

So when she presses the shutter, the full geometric chain is: **Monument (World) → [Extrinsic R,t moves & rotates the world] → Phone's own 3-D frame → [Intrinsic K projects & digitizes] → Final photo (Pixels)**.

A change in $R$ or $\mathbf{t}$ changes *what* she photographs. A change in $K$ changes *how* it appears (zoomed in, off-center, tilted, etc.).

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Quantity | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Focal length | $f$ | **2.8 mm – 200 mm** (smartphone to DSLR) |
| Sensor size | $W \times H$ | **1/2.3"** (phone) to **Full-frame 36 × 24 mm** |
| Pixel pitch | $\Delta u, \Delta v$ | **1.0 µm – 5.0 µm** |
| Principal point | $(c_x, c_y)$ | Image center, e.g. **(960, 540)** for 1080p |
| Skew | $s$ | **0** for digital sensors |
| Camera origin | $\mathbf{C}$ | $(X_0, Y_0, Z_0)^T$ in metres |
| Rotation angles | $(\alpha, \beta, \gamma)$ | Euler angles in **radians** |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Recap:** If a question asks "How many parameters define a pinhole camera model?", the canonical answer is **11** (5 intrinsic + 6 extrinsic). If lens distortion (radial + tangential) is included, it becomes **15 or more**, but distortion is technically a *non-linear* correction on top of the pinhole model.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decomposition of the Imaging Pipeline

The full geometric imaging pipeline can be expressed as a sequence of three coordinate transformations. Understanding each step independently is essential for KTU derivations and numerical problems.

### Step 1 — World Frame → Camera Frame (Extrinsic)
A 3-D point $\mathbf{X}_w = (X_w, Y_w, Z_w, 1)^T$ in homogeneous world coordinates is transformed into the camera's coordinate frame:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{X}_c &= \begin{bmatrix} R & \mathbf{t} \\ \mathbf{0}^T & 1 \end{bmatrix} \mathbf{X}_w
\end{aligned}
$$

**Why:** The rotation $R$ aligns the world axes with the camera axes; the translation $\mathbf{t}$ shifts the origin from the world origin to the camera optical center.

**How:** $R$ is the matrix whose rows/columns are the unit vectors of the world axes expressed in the camera frame. $\mathbf{t} = -R\mathbf{C}$, where $\mathbf{C}$ is the camera center in world coordinates.

### Step 2 — Camera Frame → Ideal Image Plane (Perspective Projection)
A 3-D camera point $(X_c, Y_c, Z_c)^T$ is projected onto the normalized image plane $(x, y)$ using the pinhole projection equations:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x &= f \cdot \frac{X_c}{Z_c}, \qquad y = f \cdot \frac{Y_c}{Z_c}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Why:** Light rays travel in straight lines from the scene through the pinhole (a perspective division by $Z_c$). The factor $f$ scales the normalized image-plane coordinates into physical units.

**How:** This step can be written in homogeneous form as $\mathbf{x} = \text{diag}(f,f,1) \, [I \mid \mathbf{0}] \mathbf{X}_c$.

### Step 3 — Image Plane → Pixel Frame (Intrinsic)
The metric image-plane coordinates $(x, y)$ in **mm** are converted to pixel coordinates $(u, v)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
u &= f_x \cdot \frac{X_c}{Z_c} + c_x, \qquad v = f_y \cdot \frac{Y_c}{Z_c} + c_y
\end{aligned}
$$

where $f_x = f / \Delta u$ and $f_y = f / \Delta v$ are focal lengths expressed in **pixels** along the $u$ and $v$ axes respectively.

**Why:** The digital sensor samples the continuous image at discrete pixels, and the pixel pitch may differ along $u$ and $v$ (rectangular pixels). The principal point may also not coincide with the top-left pixel.

**How:** These two equations define the affine mapping from metric to pixel space.

---

## 2.2 The Camera Calibration Matrix $K$

Combining Step 2 and Step 3, the complete intrinsic projection becomes a linear operation in homogeneous coordinates:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} &\sim \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} f_x & s & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}}_{K} \begin{bmatrix} X_c / Z_c \\ Y_c / Z_c \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Properties of $K$ (Upper-Triangular):**
- It is **upper-triangular** with positive diagonal entries.
- It has **5 degrees of freedom**.
- For most modern cameras $s = 0$ and $f_x \approx f_y$.

---

## 2.3 The Full Projection Matrix $P$

Concatenating the extrinsic and intrinsic transformations gives the **Camera Projection Matrix** $P$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= K \, [\,R \mid \mathbf{t}\,] \quad \in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times 4}
\end{aligned}
$$

For a world point $\mathbf{X}_w = (X_w, Y_w, Z_w, 1)^T$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} &\sim P \, \mathbf{X}_w \;=\; K \begin{bmatrix} R & \mathbf{t} \end{bmatrix} \mathbf{X}_w
\end{aligned}
$$

**Why this is powerful:** A single $3 \times 4$ matrix $P$ encodes everything (intrinsic + extrinsic) about the imaging geometry. Estimating $P$ from known correspondences is the foundation of camera calibration and 3-D reconstruction.

**How:** $P$ has 12 entries but only **11 degrees of freedom** (overall scale is unobservable because homogeneous coordinates absorb it).

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Equation / Matrix | Symbol Notes | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | World → Camera (extrinsic) | $\mathbf{X}_c = R \mathbf{X}_w + \mathbf{t}$ | $R \in SO(3),\ \mathbf{t} \in \mathbb{R}^3$ | metres (m) |
| 2 | Homogeneous extrinsic | $g = [\,R \mid \mathbf{t}\,]$ | $4 \times 4$ block | — |
| 3 | Perspective division | $x = f X_c / Z_c,\ y = f Y_c / Z_c$ | Pinhole projection | mm |
| 4 | Metric → Pixel | $u = f_x x + c_x,\ v = f_y y + c_y$ | $f_x = f/\Delta u$ | pixels |
| 5 | Calibration matrix | $K = \begin{bmatrix} f_x & s & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | $5$ DoF | — |
| 6 | Full projection | $P = K [\,R \mid \mathbf{t}\,]$ | $3 \times 4$ matrix | — |
| 7 | Pixel image of world point | $(u,v,1)^T \sim P (X_w, Y_w, Z_w, 1)^T$ | Up to scale | pixels |
| 8 | Camera center in world | $\mathbf{C} = -R^{-1}\mathbf{t} = -R^T \mathbf{t}$ | $\mathbf{C} \in \mathbb{R}^3$ | metres |
| 9 | Number of parameters | $11$ | $5$ intrinsic $+ 6$ extrinsic | — |
| 10 | With radial distortion | $x_d = x(1 + k_1 r^2 + k_2 r^4)$ | $r^2 = x^2 + y^2$ | — |

> [!TIP]
> **Remember for KTU:** When a problem says *"project a world point onto the image plane"*, the answer must always contain **two divisions** — one by $Z_c$ (perspective) and one by the homogeneous scale (pixel normalization). Missing the second division is the most common valuation error.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Robot navigation (SLAM & Visual Odometry):** Estimating $R$ and $\mathbf{t}$ frame-by-frame allows a robot to localise itself in an unknown environment.
- **Augmented Reality (AR):** AR systems overlay 3-D virtual content on a live camera feed by solving $P$ and then re-projecting virtual points back into the camera view.
- **Autonomous Vehicles:** Self-driving cars use multi-camera rigs where every camera's $K$ and $R,\mathbf{t}$ are precisely calibrated so that depth and distance can be triangulated from pixel matches.
- **Medical Imaging:** Endoscopes and laparoscopes need accurate $K$ and $R,\mathbf{t}$ to register the 2-D intra-operative view with pre-operative 3-D CT/MRI scans.
- **Photogrammetry & 3-D Scanning:** Aerial imagery uses bundle adjustment, which jointly refines many cameras' intrinsic + extrinsic parameters to build accurate 3-D maps.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Every algebraic transition below is explicitly shown. Each equation is followed by a labelled reasoning row. No steps are skipped.

---

## 3.1 Derivation: World Coordinates → Pixel Coordinates (Full Chain)

### Setup
Let $\mathbf{X}_w = (X_w, Y_w, Z_w)^T$ be a point in the world frame. Define the camera optical center $\mathbf{C}$ in the world frame. Define the camera axes $\mathbf{i}_c, \mathbf{j}_c, \mathbf{k}_c$ as orthonormal unit vectors expressed in the world frame.

### Stage A — Express the vector from the camera to the world point

$$
\begin{aligned}
\overrightarrow{\mathbf{C}\mathbf{X}_w} \;=\; \mathbf{X}_w - \mathbf{C} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} X_w - X_C \\ Y_w - Y_C \\ Z_w - Z_C \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Reasoning:** The vector from the camera center to the world point is simply the subtraction of the two position vectors.

### Stage B — Project this vector onto the camera axes

$$
\begin{aligned}
X_c &= (\mathbf{X}_w - \mathbf{C}) \cdot \mathbf{i}_c \\
Y_c &= (\mathbf{X}_w - \mathbf{C}) \cdot \mathbf{j}_c \\
Z_c &= (\mathbf{X}_w - \mathbf{C}) \cdot \mathbf{k}_c
\end{aligned}
$$

**Reasoning:** Taking the dot product with each camera axis gives the component of the displacement along that axis, which is the camera-frame coordinate.

### Stage C — Stack into matrix form

$$
\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} X_c \\ Y_c \\ Z_c \end{bmatrix} &= \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{i}_c^T \\ \mathbf{j}_c^T \\ \mathbf{k}_c^T \end{bmatrix} (\mathbf{X}_w - \mathbf{C}) \;=\; R (\mathbf{X}_w - \mathbf{C})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Reasoning:** The three camera axis vectors stacked row-wise form the rotation matrix $R$. Because the rows are orthonormal, $R^T R = I$.

### Stage D — Introduce the translation vector $\mathbf{t}$

Define $\mathbf{t} = -R \mathbf{C}$. Then $R \mathbf{C} = -\mathbf{t}$, hence:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} X_c \\ Y_c \\ Z_c \end{bmatrix} &= R\mathbf{X}_w + R\mathbf{C} \;=\; R\mathbf{X}_w - \mathbf{t}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Reasoning:** Substituting $\mathbf{C} = -R^T \mathbf{t}$ into $R(\mathbf{X}_w - \mathbf{C})$ collapses the translation into the single vector $\mathbf{t}$.

### Stage E — Switch to homogeneous coordinates

$$
\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} X_c \\ Y_c \\ Z_c \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} &= \begin{bmatrix} R & \mathbf{t} \\ \mathbf{0}^T & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X_w \\ Y_w \\ Z_w \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Reasoning:** The $4 \times 4$ block matrix represents a rigid-body motion (rotation + translation). This is the canonical **extrinsic matrix**.

### Stage F — Apply perspective projection

The pinhole camera forms an image on the plane $Z_c = f$. Using similar triangles:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x &= f \cdot \frac{X_c}{Z_c}, \qquad y = f \cdot \frac{Y_c}{Z_c}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Reasoning:** A point at distance $Z_c$ along the optical axis, displaced laterally by $X_c$, projects to a height $f \cdot (X_c/Z_c)$ on the image plane (similar-triangles theorem).

### Stage G — Apply the intrinsic affine mapping

The metric image coordinates $(x, y)$ in mm are converted to pixel coordinates $(u, v)$ via:

$$
\begin{aligned}
u &= \frac{x}{\Delta u} + c_x \;=\; \frac{f}{\Delta u} \cdot \frac{X_c}{Z_c} + c_x \;=\; f_x \frac{X_c}{Z_c} + c_x \\
v &= \frac{y}{\Delta v} + c_y \;=\; \frac{f}{\Delta v} \cdot \frac{Y_c}{Z_c} + c_y \;=\; f_y \frac{Y_c}{Z_c} + c_y
\end{aligned}
$$

**Reasoning:** $f/\Delta u = f_x$ converts the metric focal length into pixel units. Adding $c_x, c_y$ shifts the origin from the principal point to the top-left pixel corner.

### Stage H — Assemble into a single homogeneous equation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} &\sim \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} f_x & s & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}}_{K} \begin{bmatrix} X_c \\ Y_c \\ Z_c \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Reasoning:** Because the image is 2-D, we treat $Z_c$ as a homogeneous divisor. Writing everything in homogeneous form yields the calibration matrix $K$.

### Stage I — Final projection matrix

$$
\begin{aligned}
\boxed{\;\mathbf{x} \sim P \mathbf{X}_w \quad \text{with} \quad P = K [\,R \mid \mathbf{t}\,] \in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times 4}\;}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Reasoning:** A single $3 \times 4$ matrix now captures the entire geometric transformation from a 3-D world point to a 2-D image pixel.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU-Style Question)

> **Problem:** A camera has $f_x = 800$ px, $f_y = 800$ px, $c_x = 320$ px, $c_y = 240$ px, $s = 0$. The extrinsic is given by $R = I$ and $\mathbf{t} = (0,0,2)^T$ m. Find the pixel coordinates of the world point $\mathbf{X}_w = (0.1, 0.2, 0)^T$ m.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Apply extrinsic:**
$\mathbf{X}_c = R \mathbf{X}_w + \mathbf{t} = (0.1, 0.2, 0)^T + (0, 0, 2)^T = (0.1, 0.2, 2)^T$

**Step 2 — Compute perspective division:**
$X_c/Z_c = 0.1/2 = 0.05$, $Y_c/Z_c = 0.2/2 = 0.1$

**Step 3 — Apply intrinsic:**
$u = 800(0.05) + 320 = 40 + 320 = 360$ px
$v = 800(0.1) + 240 = 80 + 240 = 320$ px

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{(u,v) = (360,\ 320) \text{ pixels}}$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Parameter Roles & Degrees of Freedom

| Parameter Block | Matrix / Vector | Dimensions | Degrees of Freedom | Effect of Changing It |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Rotation | $R$ | $3 \times 3$ | **3** | Reorients the image; rotates visible content |
| Translation | $\mathbf{t}$ | $3 \times 1$ | **3** | Translates the entire image content |
| Focal length | $f_x, f_y$ | scalars | **2** | Zooms in/out (magnification) |
| Principal point | $c_x, c_y$ | scalars | **2** | Shifts image center |
| Skew | $s$ | scalar | **1** | Shears the image (parallelograms) |
| Aspect ratio | $f_x/f_y$ | ratio | (derived) | Stretches axes non-uniformly |
| **Total** | $P = K[R\mid\mathbf{t}]$ | $3 \times 4$ | **11** | — |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow of the Geometric Imaging Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[World Point Xw XYZ meters] --> B[Extrinsic Transform R and t]
    B --> C[Camera Frame Xc Yc Zc meters]
    C --> D[Perspective Division by Zc]
    D --> E[Normalized Image Plane x y]
    E --> F[Intrinsic Matrix K]
    F --> G[Pixel Coordinates u v]
    G --> H[Final Image on Sensor]
    H --> I[Lens Distortion Correction Optional]
    I --> J[Distortion-Free Pixel Output]

    subgraph EXT[Extrinsic Block 6 DoF]
        B
    end

    subgraph INT[Intrinsic Block 5 DoF]
        F
    end

    subgraph PROJ[Perspective Stage]
        D
        E
    end
```

## 4.2 Coordinate Frame Hierarchy Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph WORLD[World Frame Xw Yw Zw]
        W1[Origin at Scene Datum]
        W2[Fixed External Reference]
    end

    subgraph CAM[Camera Frame Xc Yc Zc]
        C1[Origin at Optical Center]
        C2[Zc along Optical Axis]
        C3[Xc Yc in Image Plane]
    end

    subgraph IMG[Image Plane x y in mm]
        I1[Origin at Principal Point]
        I2[Continuous Metric Coordinates]
    end

    subgraph PIX[Pixel Frame u v in pixels]
        P1[Origin at Top Left Pixel]
        P2[Discrete Integer Coordinates]
    end

    WORLD -->|R and t rigid transform| CAM
    CAM -->|perspective divide by Zc| IMG
    IMG -->|affine map via K| PIX
```

## 4.3 Decomposition of $K$ and $[R \mid \mathbf{t}]$ — Sub-Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FULLMAT[Full Projection Matrix P is 3 by 4]
        direction LR
        K1[fx skew cx] --> K2[fy cy] --> K3[1]
    end

    K1 --- R1[R 3 by 3 Rotation]
    K2 --- R1
    K3 --- R1
    K1 --- T1[t 3 by 1 Translation]
    K2 --- T1
    K3 --- T1

    R1 --> OUT1[Output 3D Vector]
    T1 --> OUT1
    K3 --> OUT1
```

> [!NOTE]
> The three diagrams together illustrate: **(i)** the sequential data-flow from world to pixel, **(ii)** the coordinate-frame hierarchy, and **(iii)** the structural decomposition of the full projection matrix into its intrinsic ($K$) and extrinsic ($R,\mathbf{t}$) blocks.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define intrinsic and extrinsic camera parameters. State the number of degrees of freedom each block has.**

**Model Answer:**
- **Intrinsic parameters** describe the internal geometry of the camera — focal lengths $f_x, f_y$, principal point $(c_x, c_y)$, and skew $s$ — assembled in the calibration matrix $K$. They have **5 degrees of freedom**.
- **Extrinsic parameters** describe the camera's pose in the world — the rotation matrix $R$ and translation vector $\mathbf{t}$ — forming the block $[R \mid \mathbf{t}]$. They have **6 degrees of freedom**.
- The full pinhole model thus has **11 DoF** in total, encoded in the $3 \times 4$ projection matrix $P = K [\,R \mid \mathbf{t}\,]$.

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is the projection matrix $P$? Why does it have only 11 independent parameters despite having 12 entries?**

**Model Answer:**
- The **projection matrix** $P = K [\,R \mid \mathbf{t}\,] \in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times 4}$ maps a 3-D world point $\mathbf{X}_w$ (in homogeneous form) to a 2-D pixel coordinate $\mathbf{x}$ via $\mathbf{x} \sim P \mathbf{X}_w$.
- Although $P$ has **12 scalar entries**, it is defined only up to a non-zero scalar (since the equality $\sim$ is a homogeneous equality). Therefore scaling by any $\lambda \neq 0$ yields the same projection, leaving only **11 degrees of freedom**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question B-A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2023]

**(a)** Derive the complete projection of a 3-D world point $(X_w, Y_w, Z_w)^T$ onto the 2-D image plane in terms of intrinsic and extrinsic parameters. **[(7 Marks)]**

**(b)** A camera has $K = \begin{bmatrix} 1000 & 0 & 320 \\ 0 & 1000 & 240 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$. The extrinsic parameters are given by $R = I_3$ and $\mathbf{t} = (1, 2, 5)^T$ m. Compute the pixel coordinates of the world point $(2, 3, 0)^T$ m. **[(7 Marks)]**

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Step 1 — Extrinsic transformation (3-D world to 3-D camera):**
Using the rotation $R$ and translation $\mathbf{t}$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{X}_c &= R \mathbf{X}_w + \mathbf{t}
\end{aligned}
$$

In homogeneous form:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} X_c \\ Y_c \\ Z_c \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} &= \begin{bmatrix} R & \mathbf{t} \\ \mathbf{0}^T & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X_w \\ Y_w \\ Z_w \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Perspective projection onto the image plane (using $f$):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
x &= f \cdot \frac{X_c}{Z_c}, \qquad y = f \cdot \frac{Y_c}{Z_c}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Metric to pixel conversion:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
u &= f_x \frac{X_c}{Z_c} + c_x, \qquad v = f_y \frac{Y_c}{Z_c} + c_y
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Calibration matrix $K$:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
K &= \begin{bmatrix} f_x & s & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Combined homogeneous form:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} &\sim K \begin{bmatrix} R & \mathbf{t} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X_w \\ Y_w \\ Z_w \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \;=\; P \mathbf{X}_w
\end{aligned}
$$

This completes the derivation of the projection from 3-D world to 2-D image plane.

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Step 1 — Apply the extrinsic:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{X}_c &= R \mathbf{X}_w + \mathbf{t} = (2, 3, 0)^T + (1, 2, 5)^T = (3, 5, 5)^T
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Perspective division:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{X_c}{Z_c} &= \frac{3}{5} = 0.6, \qquad \frac{Y_c}{Z_c} = \frac{5}{5} = 1.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the intrinsic $K$:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
u &= f_x \cdot 0.6 + c_x = 1000 \times 0.6 + 320 = 600 + 320 = 920 \\
v &= f_y \cdot 1.0 + c_y = 1000 \times 1.0 + 240 = 1000 + 240 = 1240
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{(u, v) = (920,\ 1240) \text{ pixels}}$

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**(a)** Explain the geometric meaning of the extrinsic parameters $R$ and $\mathbf{t}$. Derive the relationship $\mathbf{C} = -R^T \mathbf{t}$, where $\mathbf{C}$ is the camera center in world coordinates. **[(7 Marks)]**

**(b)** The camera center is $\mathbf{C} = (2, -1, 5)^T$ m and the camera looks along the world $+Z$ axis. The rotation is $R = I$. Find the image pixel coordinates of the point $(5, 3, 10)^T$ m using a camera with focal length $f = 50$ mm, pixel pitch $\Delta u = \Delta v = 0.01$ mm/pixel, and principal point $(640, 480)$ pixels. **[(7 Marks)]**

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Geometric meaning of $R$:** The matrix $R$ contains the camera basis vectors as rows (or columns). Each row of $R$ is a unit vector along the corresponding camera axis, expressed in world coordinates. Therefore $R$ encodes the orientation (roll, pitch, yaw) of the camera.

**Geometric meaning of $\mathbf{t}$:** The translation $\mathbf{t}$ is the position of the world origin **expressed in camera coordinates**. Geometrically, it tells us where the world origin is "as seen from the camera".

**Derivation of $\mathbf{C} = -R^T \mathbf{t}$:**

From the definition: $\mathbf{X}_c = R(\mathbf{X}_w - \mathbf{C})$. This can be rewritten as $\mathbf{X}_c = R\mathbf{X}_w - R\mathbf{C}$.

Comparing with the canonical form $\mathbf{X}_c = R\mathbf{X}_w + \mathbf{t}$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{t} &= -R \mathbf{C}
\end{aligned}
$$

Multiplying both sides on the left by $R^T$ (since $R$ is orthonormal, $R^T = R^{-1}$):
$$
\begin{aligned}
R^T \mathbf{t} &= -R^T R \mathbf{C} = -\mathbf{C}
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\boxed{\mathbf{C} = -R^T \mathbf{t}}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Step 1 — Compute focal length in pixels:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
f_x = f_y = \frac{f}{\Delta u} = \frac{50}{0.01} = 5000 \text{ pixels}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Calibration matrix:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
K &= \begin{bmatrix} 5000 & 0 & 640 \\ 0 & 5000 & 480 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the extrinsic $R = I$, $\mathbf{C} = (2,-1,5)^T$:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{X}_c &= \mathbf{X}_w - \mathbf{C} = (5,3,10)^T - (2,-1,5)^T = (3, 4, 5)^T
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Perspective division:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{X_c}{Z_c} &= \frac{3}{5} = 0.6, \qquad \frac{Y_c}{Z_c} = \frac{4}{5} = 0.8
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Pixel computation:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
u &= 5000 \times 0.6 + 640 = 3000 + 640 = 3640 \text{ pixels} \\
v &= 5000 \times 0.8 + 480 = 4000 + 480 = 4480 \text{ pixels}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{(u, v) = (3640,\ 4480) \text{ pixels}}$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common ways KTU students LOSE MARKS on Geometric Camera Parameters:**
> 1. **Forgetting perspective division by $Z_c$** — a frequent 1- to 2-mark deduction. Always divide before applying $K$.
> 2. **Mixing up $f$ (mm) and $f_x$ (pixels)** — examiners explicitly test whether you know that $f_x = f / \Delta u$. If the focal length is given in mm, you MUST convert to pixels first.
> 3. **Sign error in $\mathbf{C} = -R^T \mathbf{t}$** — the negative sign is critical; do not drop it.
> 4. **Using $R$ instead of $R^T$ for camera-center extraction** — confusing the row- vs. column-rotation convention loses 2-3 marks.
> 5. **Omitting the homogeneous division at the end** — leaving $(u,v)$ as a 3-vector instead of normalizing the third coordinate to 1 is a common subtle mistake.
> 6. **Writing 12 DoF for $P$** — the correct answer is **11 DoF** because of scale ambiguity.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] Geometric camera parameters = **Intrinsic ($K$, 5 DoF)** + **Extrinsic ($R,\mathbf{t}$, 6 DoF)** = **11 DoF total**.
- [x] **Intrinsic** = focal lengths ($f_x, f_y$), principal point ($c_x, c_y$), skew ($s$). Forms upper-triangular $3 \times 3$ matrix $K$.
- [x] **Extrinsic** = rotation $R \in SO(3)$ (3 DoF) and translation $\mathbf{t} \in \mathbb{R}^3$ (3 DoF).
- [x] **Full projection matrix** $P = K [\,R \mid \mathbf{t}\,]$ is $3 \times 4$ with rank 3.
- [x] **Imaging chain:** World $(X_w,Y_w,Z_w)$ $\xrightarrow{R,\mathbf{t}}$ Camera $(X_c,Y_c,Z_c)$ $\xrightarrow{\div Z_c}$ Image plane $(x,y)$ $\xrightarrow{K}$ Pixel $(u,v)$.
- [x] **Camera center in world coordinates:** $\mathbf{C} = -R^T \mathbf{t}$.
- [x] **Focal length in pixels:** $f_x = f / \Delta u$, $f_y = f / \Delta v$ (always convert from mm to pixels before computing).
- [x] **Perspective projection formulas:** $u = f_x (X_c/Z_c) + c_x$, $v = f_y (Y_c/Z_c) + c_y$.
- [x] **Skew $s$** is essentially **0** for modern digital sensors.
- [x] $P$ is defined only up to scale (homogeneous) — hence **11 DoF, not 12**.
- [x] Lens distortion (radial $k_1, k_2$; tangential $p_1, p_2$) is a non-linear **post-correction** applied AFTER the pinhole projection.
- [x] Master the numerical pipeline: **Extrinsic → Perspective Division → Intrinsic**; missing any stage means losing marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stereopsis - Binocular Camera Geometry
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST745 - COMPUTER VISION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Stereopsis - Binocular Camera Geometry

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stereopsis** is the perception of depth and 3D structure of a scene obtained by combining two slightly different 2D projections of the same scene captured from two distinct viewpoints — typically the left and right cameras of a **binocular (stereo) camera rig**. In computer vision, it is mathematically formalized through **Epipolar Geometry**, which describes the geometric relationship between two cameras observing the same 3D scene point.

**Binocular Camera Geometry** refers to the spatial configuration and mathematical modeling of two cameras (or two eyes) separated by a horizontal offset known as the **baseline (B)**. This geometry enables the recovery of depth information from 2D image correspondences via **triangulation**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Magic Finger Trick**
> Hold your right index finger at arm's length and close your left eye, then close your right eye alternately. Notice how your finger appears to "jump" horizontally against the background. This horizontal jump is called **disparity (d)**. Your brain uses this disparity difference between the two retinal images to compute how far your finger is. Closer objects produce larger disparity; distant objects produce smaller disparity. This is exactly how a stereo camera system works — except the brain is replaced by a triangulation algorithm, and the eyes are replaced by calibrated cameras separated by a known baseline **B**.

### 1.3 Why Binocular Geometry Matters in Computer Vision

- **3D Reconstruction**: Recovers Euclidean 3D structure from 2D images.
- **Depth Estimation**: Computes per-pixel depth maps (Z-buffer style) in real-time (e.g., autonomous vehicles, robotics, AR/VR).
- **Object Localization**: Determines metric distances without active sensors (LiDAR/ToF).
- **Stereo Matching Pre-requisite**: Provides the geometric constraint that reduces the correspondence search from 2D to a 1D **epipolar line**.

### 1.4 Key Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Baseline | $B$ | Distance between the two camera optical centers |
| Focal Length | $f$ | Distance from the lens to the image sensor |
| Disparity | $d$ | Pixel difference between left and right image points $(x_l - x_r)$ |
| Depth | $Z$ | Perpendicular distance of scene point from the camera baseline plane |
| Principal Point | $(c_x, c_y)$ | Optical center of the image plane |
| Optical Center | $O_c$ | Center of the camera lens (pinhole) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Stereo Metric (Industry Standard):** For a parallel stereo configuration, the depth equation simplifies to the elegant form:
> $$Z = \dfrac{f \cdot B}{d}$$
> where **f, B** are in metric units (mm) and **d** is in pixels (or subpixels). This single equation is the heart of every stereo depth estimation algorithm.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Canonical Stereo Configurations

#### A. Parallel (Rectified) Stereo Configuration
- Both cameras have **identical intrinsic parameters**.
- Their optical axes are **strictly parallel**.
- Image planes are **coplanar** and aligned along the same horizontal scanline.
- The x-axes of both cameras are **collinear** (along the baseline direction).
- This is the **idealized case** — the epipolar lines coincide with horizontal image rows.

#### B. Converged (Toed-in / General) Stereo Configuration
- Cameras are angled inward to **converge** on a common fixation point.
- More realistic in human vision but mathematically more complex.
- The image planes are **NOT coplanar**, and epipolar lines are **oblique**.
- Requires **stereo rectification** as a preprocessing step before dense matching.

### 2.2 Epipolar Geometry: The Master Constraint

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Epipolar Geometry** constrains where a point in one image can appear in the other image. For a 3D point $X$ projecting to $x_l$ in the left image, the corresponding right-image point $x_r$ MUST lie on a specific line called the **epipolar line**. This reduces correspondence search from $O(N^2)$ to $O(N)$.

**The Seven Pillars of Epipolar Geometry:**

1. **Epipole ($e_l, e_r$):** The projection of one camera's optical center into the other camera's image plane. It is the point where the baseline intersects the image plane.
2. **Epipolar Plane ($\pi$):** The plane formed by the two optical centers $O_l, O_r$ and the 3D scene point $X$.
3. **Epipolar Line ($l_r$):** The intersection of the epipolar plane with the right image plane. It is the locus of all possible projections of $X$ in the right image.
4. **Baseline ($\overline{O_l O_r}$):** The line segment connecting the two optical centers.
5. **Fundamental Matrix ($F$):** A $3 \times 3$ matrix encoding the epipolar geometry in pixel coordinates: $x_r^T F x_l = 0$.
6. **Essential Matrix ($E$):** The calibrated counterpart of $F$, in normalized camera coordinates: $x_r'^T E x_l' = 0$.
7. **Epipolar Constraint:** $x_r^T F x_l = 0$ — the foundational equation of stereo geometry.

### 2.3 The Algebraic Relationship: Fundamental vs. Essential Matrix

The **Essential Matrix** $E$ operates on **normalized image coordinates** (rays from camera center, unit depth), while the **Fundamental Matrix** $F$ operates on **pixel coordinates**.

$$E = [t]_\times R, \quad F = K^{-T} E K^{-1}$$

where:
- $K$ is the $3 \times 3$ camera intrinsic matrix.
- $R$ is the $3 \times 3$ rotation matrix from left to right camera.
- $t$ is the $3 \times 1$ translation vector (baseline) from left to right camera.
- $[t]_\times$ is the $3 \times 3$ **skew-symmetric cross-product matrix** of $t$.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formulas — Memorize for KTU Board Exam**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Z = \dfrac{f \cdot B}{d}$ | Depth from disparity (parallel rig) | $Z$ in mm, $f$ in pixels, $B$ in mm, $d$ in pixels |
| 2 | $d = x_l - x_r$ | Horizontal pixel disparity | pixels |
| 3 | $Z = \dfrac{f \cdot B}{x_l - x_r}$ | Expanded form (rectified) | as above |
| 4 | $X = \dfrac{(x_l - c_x) \cdot Z}{f}$ | Back-projected 3D X-coordinate | mm |
| 5 | $Y = \dfrac{(y_l - c_y) \cdot Z}{f}$ | Back-projected 3D Y-coordinate | mm |
| 6 | $x_r^T F x_l = 0$ | Epipolar constraint (pixels) | scalar $= 0$ |
| 7 | $\hat{x}_r^T E \hat{x}_l = 0$ | Epipolar constraint (normalized) | scalar $= 0$ |
| 8 | $E = [t]_\times R$ | Essential matrix decomposition | $3 \times 3$ matrix |
| 9 | $F = K^{-T} [t]_\times R K^{-1}$ | Fundamental matrix from intrinsics+extrinsics | $3 \times 3$ matrix |
| 10 | $P_l = K_l [I \vert 0], \quad P_r = K_r [R \vert t]$ | Camera projection matrices | $3 \times 4$ |
| 11 | $\Delta Z = \dfrac{Z^2}{f \cdot B} \cdot \Delta d$ | Depth uncertainty (1 pixel disparity error) | mm |
| 12 | $h = \dfrac{f \cdot B}{Z_{\min}} - \dfrac{f \cdot B}{Z_{\max}}$ | Required disparity range (dynamic range) | pixels |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Formula #11 ($\Delta Z = Z^2 \Delta d / (fB)$) is critical in stereo system design. It reveals that **depth error grows quadratically with distance** — a stereo rig is highly accurate for nearby objects but degrades for distant ones. This is why long-range autonomous vehicles (e.g., Waymo, Cruise) augment stereo with LiDAR.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Autonomous Driving (Tesla, Waymo):** Stereo depth for obstacle detection up to 200m.
- **Robotic Surgery (Da Vinci):** 3D visualization from stereo endoscopes.
- **Industrial Metrology:** Sub-millimeter 3D scanning for quality control.
- **AR/VR (Apple Vision Pro, Meta Quest):** Stereo passthrough and SLAM.
- **Satellite Imaging (STEREO Mission NASA):** Stereo pairs for digital elevation models.
- **Photogrammetry (Drone Mapping):** Overlapping aerial images reconstruct terrain.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation #1: The Depth-from-Disparity Equation ($Z = fB/d$)

**Setup:** Consider a parallel stereo rig with optical centers $O_l$ and $O_r$ separated by baseline $B$ along the X-axis. A 3D point $X = (X, Y, Z)$ projects to image points $x_l = (x_l, y_l)$ and $x_r = (x_r, y_r)$ on the left and right image planes, each with focal length $f$ and principal point at the origin (simplified).

**Step 1: Write the pinhole projection equations for the left camera.**

The left camera center is at the origin, so the projection of $X = (X, Y, Z)$ is:

$$\dfrac{x_l}{f} = \dfrac{X}{Z}, \quad \dfrac{y_l}{f} = \dfrac{Y}{Z}$$

**Step 2: Write the projection equations for the right camera.**

The right camera center is shifted by $B$ along the X-axis. The X-coordinate of the 3D point relative to the right camera is $X - B$:

$$\dfrac{x_r}{f} = \dfrac{X - B}{Z}, \quad \dfrac{y_r}{f} = \dfrac{Y}{Z}$$

> Note that since the cameras are parallel, the Y-coordinates of corresponding points are equal: $y_l = y_r$. This is the **y-equalization** property of the rectified stereo configuration.

**Step 3: Compute the horizontal disparity $d$.**

Subtracting the two X-projections:

$$x_l - x_r = f \cdot \dfrac{X}{Z} - f \cdot \dfrac{X - B}{Z}$$

$$x_l - x_r = \dfrac{f}{Z} \cdot \left( X - (X - B) \right)$$

$$x_l - x_r = \dfrac{f \cdot B}{Z}$$

**Step 4: Define disparity and solve for depth.**

Defining disparity as $d \triangleq x_l - x_r$:

$$d = \dfrac{f \cdot B}{Z}$$

Inverting:

$$\boxed{Z = \dfrac{f \cdot B}{d}}$$

**Geometric Interpretation:** By similar triangles, the two projection rays from $O_l$ and $O_r$ converge at $X$. The disparity $d$ is the base of a similar triangle with apex at the depth $Z$ and base $B$ at the camera baseline. Thus $d/B = f/Z$, yielding the depth inversely proportional to disparity.

---

### 3.2 Derivation #2: 3D Triangulation (Back-Projection of a Point)

**Problem:** Given pixel coordinates $(u_l, v_l)$ in the left image and $(u_r, v_r)$ in the right image (with $v_l = v_r$ in the rectified case), recover the 3D coordinates $(X, Y, Z)$.

**Step 1: Express disparity in terms of pixel coordinates.**

Let $c_x$ and $c_y$ be the principal point of the (assumed identical) cameras. Then:

$$x_l = u_l - c_x, \quad x_r = u_r - c_x$$

$$d = (u_l - c_x) - (u_r - c_x) = u_l - u_r$$

**Step 2: Substitute into the depth equation.**

$$Z = \dfrac{f \cdot B}{u_l - u_r}$$

**Step 3: Recover X from the left camera projection.**

From $x_l / f = X / Z$:

$$X = \dfrac{(u_l - c_x) \cdot Z}{f} = \dfrac{(u_l - c_x) \cdot B}{u_l - u_r}$$

**Step 4: Recover Y using the common Y-projection.**

Since $v_l = v_r$ in the rectified case:

$$Y = \dfrac{(v_l - c_y) \cdot Z}{f} = \dfrac{(v_l - c_y) \cdot B}{u_l - u_r}$$

**Final 3D Reconstruction Equation:**

$$\boxed{(X, Y, Z) = \left( \dfrac{B(u_l - c_x)}{u_l - u_r}, \ \dfrac{B(v_l - c_y)}{u_l - u_r}, \ \dfrac{fB}{u_l - u_r} \right)}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation #3: Relationship Between Essential and Fundamental Matrices

**Step 1: Define the projection of a 3D point to pixel coordinates.**

For a point $X$ in world coordinates, its normalized image coordinates (rays in the camera frame) are:

$$\hat{x} = K^{-1} x$$

where $x = (u, v, 1)^T$ is the homogeneous pixel coordinate and $K$ is the intrinsic matrix.

**Step 2: Apply the epipolar constraint in normalized coordinates.**

The essential matrix constraint in the camera frame is:

$$\hat{x}_r^T E \hat{x}_l = 0$$

**Step 3: Substitute the pixel-to-normalized conversion.**

$$(K^{-1} x_r)^T E (K^{-1} x_l) = 0$$

$$x_r^T (K^{-T}) E (K^{-1}) x_l = 0$$

**Step 4: Identify the fundamental matrix.**

Comparing with $x_r^T F x_l = 0$:

$$\boxed{F = K^{-T} E K^{-1} = K^{-T} [t]_\times R \, K^{-1}}$$

This shows that $F$ encodes both the **intrinsic parameters** ($K$) and the **extrinsic parameters** ($R, t$) of the stereo rig, while $E$ encodes only the extrinsics.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** A parallel stereo camera rig has focal length $f = 800$ pixels, baseline $B = 120$ mm, and principal point at $(c_x, c_y) = (400, 300)$. A scene point projects to $(u_l, v_l) = (450, 300)$ in the left image and $(u_r, v_r) = (420, 300)$ in the right image. Compute the 3D coordinates of the point.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute disparity.**

$$d = u_l - u_r = 450 - 420 = 30 \text{ pixels}$$

**Step 2: Compute depth Z.**

$$Z = \dfrac{f \cdot B}{d} = \dfrac{800 \times 120}{30} = \dfrac{96000}{30} = 3200 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3: Compute X.**

$$X = \dfrac{B(u_l - c_x)}{d} = \dfrac{120 \times (450 - 400)}{30} = \dfrac{120 \times 50}{30} = 200 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4: Compute Y.**

$$Y = \dfrac{B(v_l - c_y)}{d} = \dfrac{120 \times (300 - 300)}{30} = 0 \text{ mm}$$

**Answer:** The 3D point is at $(X, Y, Z) = (200, 0, 3200)$ mm = $(0.2, 0, 3.2)$ meters.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Aspect | Parallel Stereo | Converged Stereo | Converged + Rectified |
|---|---|---|---|
| Epipolar lines | Horizontal scanlines | Oblique lines | Horizontal scanlines |
| Math complexity | Lowest | High | Medium |
| Triangulation | $Z = fB/d$ directly | Linearized via $F, E$ | $Z = fB/d$ after rectification |
| Field of view | Smaller (no overlap at edges) | Larger overlap | Tunable |
| Common use | Calibration benchmarks | Human-vision models | Industrial stereo rigs |
| Real-world example | KITTI dataset baseline | Cinema 3D cameras | Intel RealSense D400 series |
