**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Filters as templates treat the kernel itself as a small "pattern" we want to locate in a larger image. The kernel is slid across the image, and at each location a similarity score is computed. For normalized cross-correlation:

$$ R_{NCC}(x, y) = \frac{\sum_{u,v} (T(u,v) - \bar{T})(I(x+u, y+v) - \bar{I}_{xy})}{\sqrt{\sum_{u,v} (T(u,v) - \bar{T})^2 \cdot \sum_{u,v} (I(x+u, y+v) - \bar{I}_{xy})^2}} $$

**Range:** $R_{NCC} \in [-1, 1]$.
- $R = 1$ indicates a perfect positive match.
- $R = -1$ indicates a perfect anti-correlation.
- $R = 0$ indicates no linear correlation.

**Significance:** NCC is invariant to linear illumination changes and produces a bounded score, making it the preferred metric in real-world applications where lighting varies. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The output map dimensions are $(5-3+1) \times (5-3+1) = 3 \times 3$.

For each of the 9 possible locations, the NCC value is computed. Without loss of generality, the highest NCC value is expected at the location where the patch best matches $T$ in structure. The student is expected to:
- Compute $\bar{T} = 5$.
- For each patch, compute $\bar{I}_{xy}$.
- Apply the NCC formula.

For brevity, the best match in this case is at position $(2, 2)$ (1-indexed) where the image patch:

$$ \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 1 \\ 2 & 3 & 4 \\ 4 & 5 & 6 \end{bmatrix} $$

most closely resembles the gradient pattern of $T$. The student should produce a $3 \times 3$ response map and identify the maximum. **(7 marks — full marks require explicit step-by-step computation of at least 2 patch locations.)**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Gradient is the foundation:** All edge detection starts with $I_x$ and $I_y$ — partial derivatives of the image intensity function.
- **Sobel = Gaussian smoothing + differentiation:** It is preferred over plain finite differences for noisy images.
- **Prewitt vs. Sobel:** Prewitt uses uniform weights; Sobel weights the central row/column more, giving better noise suppression.
- **Roberts is the simplest:** Uses $2 \times 2$ kernels on diagonals — fast but very noise-sensitive.
- **Structure tensor captures local gradient distribution:** $M = \sum w [I_x^2, I_x I_y; I_x I_y, I_y^2]$.
- **Harris R-value classification:**
  - $R > 0$ (large) → Corner
  - $R < 0$ → Edge
  - $R \approx 0$ → Flat
- **NCC is bounded in $[-1, 1]$** and is the **gold standard** for illumination-invariant template matching.
- **Mean subtraction is mandatory** in NCC; without it, the metric is biased by absolute intensity.
- **Output map size** for template matching: $(W - m + 1) \times (H - n + 1)$.
- **Shi-Tomasi improvement:** Uses $R = \min(\lambda_1, \lambda_2)$ instead of the Harris response — often yields more stable corner detection.
- **Non-Maximum Suppression (NMS)** is essential after both edge and corner detection to obtain 1-pixel-wide results.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** For Module 2 questions on this topic, always draw the kernels explicitly, label the gradient components ($G_x$, $G_y$, $\vert \nabla I \vert$, $\theta$), and clearly state the formula for the Harris response or NCC — examiners reward completeness, and missing any one of these four elements typically costs 1–2 marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Machine Learning for Computer Vision: Machine Learning - Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST745 - COMPUTER VISION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Machine Learning for Computer Vision: Machine Learning - Introduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Machine Learning (ML)** is a sub-field of Artificial Intelligence (AI) that provides systems the ability to **automatically learn and improve from experience (data) without being explicitly programmed**. In the context of Computer Vision (CV), ML enables algorithms to infer patterns, recognize objects, segment images, and make pixel-level decisions by learning statistical regularities from annotated or unannotated image datasets.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, Machine Learning for Computer Vision is the **data-driven paradigm** where a model $f_\theta(\cdot)$ parameterized by learnable weights $\theta$ is optimized using an empirical risk minimization principle:

$$\theta^{*} = \arg\min_{\theta} \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathcal{L}\left(f_\theta(x_i), y_i\right) + \lambda \, \Omega(\theta)$$

where:
- $x_i$ = input image (or feature vector)
- $y_i$ = ground-truth label
- $\mathcal{L}$ = loss function
- $\Omega(\theta)$ = regularization term
- $\lambda$ = regularization hyperparameter
- $N$ = total number of training samples

> [!NOTE]
> **Arthur Samuel (1959)** — the pioneer of ML — defined it as: *"Field of study that gives computers the ability to learn without being explicitly programmed."* This is the canonical KTU reference definition.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy: Teaching a Child to Recognize a Dog**
>
> Imagine you want a child to recognize a dog. You do **not** hand them a 500-page rulebook of "if tail = present AND ears = floppy AND bark = true THEN dog." Instead, you **show them 1000 photos of dogs** and 1000 photos of non-dogs. Slowly, the child's brain (the model) tunes its internal parameters (neurons) until it can correctly classify new, unseen photos.
>
> **Machine Learning is exactly this** — instead of hand-coding rules for "what makes a face a face," we feed the algorithm thousands of face images and let it figure out the rules automatically.

### 1.3 The KTU-Standard ML Vocabulary

| Term | Meaning | CV Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Feature** | A measurable property of input data | Pixel intensity, HOG descriptor, edge histogram |
| **Label** | Ground-truth output class/value | "Cat", "Dog", bounding-box coordinates |
| **Training Set** | Labeled data used to fit the model | $D_{train} = \{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$ |
| **Test Set** | Unseen data to evaluate generalization | $D_{test}$ disjoint from $D_{train}$ |
| **Hypothesis** | The learned mapping function | $h: \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$ |
| **Model** | The parameterized function $f_\theta$ | CNN, SVM, Random Forest |
| **Epoch** | One full pass through training data | 50 epochs means 50 passes |
| **Hyperparameter** | Configuration set before learning | Learning rate $\eta$, depth of tree |

## 1.4 Why ML Matters for Computer Vision

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Hand-crafted vision algorithms (edge detection, Hough transform) fail on:
> - **Illumination changes**
> - **Viewpoint variation**
> - **Intra-class diversity** (all "chairs" do not look alike)
>
> ML overcomes this by **learning invariant representations directly from pixels**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Paradigms of Machine Learning

### 2.1.1 Supervised Learning
The model is trained on **input-output pairs** $(x_i, y_i)$ where labels are provided by a human annotator.

$$\text{Learn } f_\theta : \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y} \quad \text{from} \quad \mathcal{D} = \{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$$

- **Classification**: $y_i \in \{1, 2, \ldots, K\}$ (discrete). CV Example → image categorization.
- **Regression**: $y_i \in \mathbb{R}$ (continuous). CV Example → age estimation from face.

### 2.1.2 Unsupervised Learning
No labels are available; the algorithm must discover **inherent structure** in the data.

$$\text{Learn } f_\theta : \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Z} \quad \text{where } \mathcal{Z} \text{ captures data distribution}$$

- CV Examples: image clustering, dimensionality reduction (PCA), autoencoder feature learning.

### 2.1.3 Semi-Supervised Learning
A **small labeled set** combined with a **large unlabeled set**. Particularly useful in CV because labeling images is expensive.

$$\mathcal{D} = \underbrace{\{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N_l}}_{\text{small labeled}} \cup \underbrace{\{x_j\}_{j=1}^{N_u}}_{\text{large unlabeled}}, \quad N_l \ll N_u$$

### 2.1.4 Reinforcement Learning (RL)
An **agent** learns by interacting with an environment, receiving rewards/penalties.

$$a_t = \pi_\theta(s_t) \quad ; \quad r_t = R(s_t, a_t)$$

- CV Example: Active object tracking, robotic vision, game-playing agents.

## 2.2 The Standard ML Pipeline for Computer Vision

| Stage | Operation | CV-Specific Note |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Data Acquisition | Collect raw images | Use COCO, ImageNet, or domain-specific datasets |
| 2. Preprocessing | Resize, normalize, denoise | Standardize pixel range to $[0, 1]$ or $\mathcal{N}(0,1)$ |
| 3. Feature Extraction | HOG, SIFT, or CNN features | Modern CV uses **learned features** via CNN |
| 4. Model Selection | Choose hypothesis class $f_\theta$ | SVM, k-NN, CNN, Random Forest |
| 5. Training | Optimize $\theta$ via gradient descent | Minimize $\mathcal{L}$ on $D_{train}$ |
| 6. Validation | Tune hyperparameters on $D_{val}$ | Use cross-validation or hold-out |
| 7. Testing | Final evaluation on $D_{test}$ | Report accuracy, mAP, IoU, etc. |
| 8. Deployment | Inference on new images | Optimize for latency & memory |

## 2.3 Bias-Variance Decomposition — The KTU High-Yield Theorem

For any learned model $\hat{f}$, the expected prediction error on a new sample can be decomposed as:

$$\mathbb{E}\left[(y - \hat{f}(x))^2\right] = \underbrace{\text{Bias}^2(\hat{f})}_{\text{systematic error}} + \underbrace{\text{Variance}(\hat{f})}_{\text{sensitivity to data}} + \underbrace{\sigma^2}_{\text{irreducible noise}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **High Bias** → model is too simple → **Underfitting**
> - **High Variance** → model is too complex → **Overfitting**
> - The **goal of ML** is to find the sweet spot minimizing total error.

## 2.4 Empirical Risk Minimization (ERM) — The Foundational Principle

> [!NOTE]
> **ERM Theorem (Vapnik, 1995)**: Since the true data distribution $P(x,y)$ is unknown, we minimize the **empirical risk** (average loss on training data) as a proxy for the **expected risk**.

$$R_{emp}(\theta) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathcal{L}(f_\theta(x_i), y_i) \approx R_{true}(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_{(x,y) \sim P}\left[\mathcal{L}(f_\theta(x), y)\right]$$

The bound on generalization gap is given by **VC theory**:

$$R_{true}(\theta) \leq R_{emp}(\theta) + \underbrace{\sqrt{\frac{h \log(2N/h) - \log(\eta/4)}{N}}}_{\text{VC confidence}}$$

where $h$ = VC dimension, $N$ = sample size, $\eta$ = confidence.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Memorize the following — these are the top 5 formulas examiners ask in ESE.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bias-Variance Decomposition | $\mathbb{E}[(y-\hat{f})^2] = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance} + \sigma^2$ | Squared error loss |
| 2 | Empirical Risk | $R_{emp}(\theta) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\mathcal{L}(f_\theta(x_i), y_i)$ | Unitless (averaged) |
| 3 | L2 Regularization | $\Omega(\theta) = \sum_{j} \theta_j^2 = \Vert \theta \Vert_2^2$ | Penalty on weight magnitude |
| 4 | L1 Regularization | $\Omega(\theta) = \sum_{j} \vert \theta_j \vert = \Vert \theta \Vert_1$ | Promotes sparsity |
| 5 | Gradient Descent Update | $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}(\theta_t)$ | $\eta$ = learning rate |
| 6 | Accuracy | $\text{Acc} = \frac{TP+TN}{TP+TN+FP+FN}$ | Range $[0,1]$ or $[0,100\%]$ |
| 7 | Precision | $P = \frac{TP}{TP+FP}$ | Used in detection |
| 8 | Recall | $R = \frac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | Used in retrieval |
| 9 | F1-Score | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P+R}$ | Harmonic mean |
| 10 | Cross-Entropy Loss | $\mathcal{L}_{CE} = -\sum_{k=1}^{K} y_k \log(\hat{y}_k)$ | For classification |

## 2.6 Overfitting vs. Underfitting — Visual & Mathematical

| Property | Underfitting | Good Fit | Overfitting |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bias | High | Low | Very Low |
| Variance | Low | Moderate | Very High |
| Train Error | High | Low | Very Low |
| Test Error | High | Low | High |
| Model Complexity | Too Simple | Just Right | Too Complex |
| Remedy | Add features, deeper model | — | Regularization, more data, dropout |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> ML for CV powers **autonomous vehicles** (Tesla, Waymo), **medical imaging diagnostics** (tumor detection), **face recognition** (smartphone unlock), **industrial defect inspection** (Foxconn assembly lines), and **satellite imagery analysis** (agricultural monitoring). Every domain that produces visual data at scale is a candidate for ML-driven CV.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Bias-Variance Trade-off (Detailed)

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is a **KTU favorite** — be prepared to derive it in Part B.

### Step 1 — Setup the Squared Error
We want to evaluate the expected prediction error at a fixed input $x$, given a training set $\mathcal{D}$ drawn from distribution $P(\mathcal{D})$:

$$\mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}, y}\left[(y - \hat{f}(x;\mathcal{D}))^2\right]$$

### Step 2 — Add and Subtract the Mean Prediction
Let the **expected prediction** across many training sets be:

$$\bar{f}(x) = \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}}\left[\hat{f}(x;\mathcal{D})\right]$$

Add and subtract $\bar{f}(x)$ inside the square:

$$(y - \hat{f})^2 = (y - \bar{f} + \bar{f} - \hat{f})^2$$

### Step 3 — Expand the Square
Using $(a + b)^2 = a^2 + 2ab + b^2$, let $a = (y - \bar{f})$ and $b = (\bar{f} - \hat{f})$:

$$(y - \hat{f})^2 = (y - \bar{f})^2 + 2(y - \bar{f})(\bar{f} - \hat{f}) + (\bar{f} - \hat{f})^2$$

### Step 4 — Take the Expectation
Since $\mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}}[\bar{f}(x) - \hat{f}(x;\mathcal{D})] = 0$ by definition of mean, the cross term vanishes:

$$\mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}}\left[2(y - \bar{f})(\bar{f} - \hat{f})\right] = 2(y - \bar{f}) \cdot 0 = 0$$

### Step 5 — Split the Result
We obtain two clean terms:

$$\mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}}\left[(y - \hat{f})^2\right] = (y - \bar{f})^2 + \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}}\left[(\bar{f} - \hat{f})^2\right]$$

### Step 6 — Identify Each Term

- **First term** $(y - \bar{f}(x))^2$ → depends only on the mean model vs. true value = **Bias²**
- **Second term** $\mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}}[(\bar{f} - \hat{f})^2]$ → variance of predictions = **Variance**

### Step 7 — Add Irreducible Noise
Since $y$ itself contains noise $y = f(x) + \varepsilon$ with $\varepsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$, we add $\sigma^2$:

$$\boxed{\;\mathbb{E}\left[(y - \hat{f}(x))^2\right] = \underbrace{\text{Bias}^2(\hat{f}(x))}_{\text{error from wrong model}} + \underbrace{\text{Variance}(\hat{f}(x))}_{\text{error from sensitivity}} + \underbrace{\sigma^2}_{\text{noise}}\;}$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Gradient Descent for Linear Regression

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **workhorse algorithm** for ML. The KTU board loves asking this.

### Step 1 — Define the Model
A linear model with input features $x \in \mathbb{R}^d$ and parameters $\theta \in \mathbb{R}^d$:

$$\hat{y} = \theta^T x = \sum_{j=1}^{d} \theta_j x_j$$

### Step 2 — Define the Loss (Mean Squared Error)
For $N$ training samples, the empirical risk is:

$$\mathcal{L}(\theta) = \frac{1}{2N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( \theta^T x_i - y_i \right)^2$$

(The factor $\frac{1}{2}$ cancels the 2 from differentiation — a convention.)

### Step 3 — Compute the Gradient
Differentiate $\mathcal{L}$ with respect to $\theta$:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \theta} = \frac{1}{2N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} 2(\theta^T x_i - y_i) \cdot x_i = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (\theta^T x_i - y_i) x_i$$

### Step 4 — Vectorize the Expression
Let $X \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times d}$ be the design matrix and $y \in \mathbb{R}^N$ the label vector. Then:

$$\nabla_\theta \mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{N} X^T (X\theta - y)$$

### Step 5 — Update Rule (Gradient Descent)
At each iteration $t$, update parameters in the direction opposite to the gradient:

$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}(\theta_t)$$

### Step 6 — Closed-Form Solution (Normal Equation)
Setting $\nabla_\theta \mathcal{L} = 0$ gives the optimal $\theta$ in closed form:

$$\theta^* = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T y$$

> [!TIP]
> The normal equation is **non-iterative** and exact — but it requires $X^T X$ to be invertible and is $O(d^3)$ in compute. For Computer Vision where $d$ is huge (millions of pixels), gradient descent is preferred.

## 3.3 Derivation: Cross-Entropy Loss from Maximum Likelihood

> [!NOTE]
> Cross-entropy is the **default loss for classification in CV** (used by virtually every CNN).

### Step 1 — Assume a Generative Model
For $K$-class classification, assume labels follow a **Categorical distribution** parameterized by model softmax outputs $\hat{p}(y=k \vert x)$:

$$P(y \vert x; \theta) = \prod_{k=1}^{K} \hat{p}(y=k \vert x)^{y_k}$$

where $y_k \in \{0,1\}$ is the one-hot encoding.

### Step 2 — Write the Likelihood over the Dataset
Assuming i.i.d. samples:

$$\mathcal{L}(\theta) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} P(y_i \vert x_i; \theta) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} \prod_{k=1}^{K} \hat{p}_{ik}^{y_{ik}}$$

### Step 3 — Take the Log-Likelihood
The log-transform is monotonic, so maximizing likelihood = maximizing log-likelihood:

$$\ell(\theta) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{k=1}^{K} y_{ik} \log \hat{p}_{ik}$$

### Step 4 — Convert to a Loss by Negation
Minimizing the negative log-likelihood gives the **cross-entropy loss**:

$$\mathcal{L}_{CE}(\theta) = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{k=1}^{K} y_{ik} \log \hat{p}_{ik}$$

### Step 5 — Substitute the Softmax Output
For a model with logits $z_k$:

$$\hat{p}_{ik} = \frac{e^{z_{ik}}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{z_{ij}}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \mathcal{L}_{CE} = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{k=1}^{K} y_{ik} \left( z_{ik} - \log \sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{z_{ij}} \right)$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Algorithm Comparison for CV

| Algorithm | Type | Parametric? | Best CV Use-Case | Strength | Weakness |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **k-NN** | Supervised | No | Small datasets, baselines | Zero training time | Slow at inference |
| **Linear Regression** | Supervised | Yes | Age estimation | Closed-form solution | Assumes linearity |
| **Logistic Regression** | Supervised | Yes | Binary classification | Probabilistic output | Linear boundary |
| **Decision Tree** | Supervised | No | Rule-based CV tasks | Interpretable | Prone to overfit |
| **Random Forest** | Supervised | Ensemble | Robust baselines | Reduces variance | Black-box, slow |
| **SVM** | Supervised | No (with RBF) | High-dim features (HOG) | Strong on small data | Scales poorly |
| **k-Means** | Unsupervised | No | Image segmentation (color) | Simple & fast | Needs k upfront |
| **PCA** | Unsupervised | No | Dimensionality reduction | Closed-form | Linear only |
| **CNN** | Supervised | Yes | Image classification, detection | State-of-the-art | Needs lots of data |
| **Q-Learning** | RL | Yes | Active vision | Learns from interaction | Sample-inefficient |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The General Machine Learning Workflow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0([Raw Image Dataset]) --> A1[Data Acquisition]
    A1 --> A2[Preprocessing: Resize, Normalize, Augment]
    A2 --> A3[Feature Extraction: HOG, SIFT, or CNN]
    A3 --> A4[Split: Train / Validation / Test]
    A4 --> B1[Choose Model f_theta]
    B1 --> B2[Initialize Parameters theta_0]
    B2 --> B3[Forward Pass: Compute Predictions]
    B3 --> B4[Compute Loss L of f_theta]
    B4 --> B5[Backprop: Compute Gradient]
    B5 --> B6[Update: theta new = theta old - eta times grad]
    B6 --> B7{Converged or Max Epochs Reached}
    B7 -->|No| B3
    B7 -->|Yes| C1[Validate on Validation Set]
    C1 --> C2{Tune Hyperparameters}
    C2 -->|Yes| B1
    C2 -->|No| D1[Final Evaluation on Test Set]
    D1 --> D2[Deploy Model for Inference]
    D2 --> D3([Real-World Computer Vision Application])
```

## 4.2 The Four Paradigms of Machine Learning

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ML[Machine Learning] --> SL[Supervised Learning]
    ML --> UL[Unsupervised Learning]
    ML --> SSL[Semi-Supervised Learning]
    ML --> RL[Reinforcement Learning]

    SL --> SLC[Classification]
    SL --> SLR[Regression]

    UL --> ULC[Clustering]
    UL --> ULD[Dim. Reduction]
    UL --> ULG[Generative Models]

    SSL --> SSLC[Label Propagation]
    SSL --> SSLF[Self-Training]

    RL --> RLP[Policy Optimization]
    RL --> RLV[Value Iteration]
```

## 4.3 Bias-Variance Trade-off — Conceptual Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Model Complexity Increases] --> B[Bias Decreases]
    A --> C[Variance Increases]
    B --> D[Total Error Curve U-Shape]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Sweet Spot: Lowest Generalization Error]
    D --> F[Left Side: Underfitting Zone]
    D --> G[Right Side: Overfitting Zone]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — A Typical CV Classification Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IMG[Input Image x] --> RESIZE[Resize to fixed dimension]
    RESIZE --> NORM[Normalize: subtract mean, divide by std]
    NORM --> AUG[Data Augmentation: flip, crop, rotate]
    AUG --> FEAT[Feature Extractor: CNN Backbone]
    FEAT --> FC[Fully Connected Classifier]
    FC --> SOFT[Softmax Layer]
    SOFT --> OUT[Predicted Class Probabilities]
    OUT --> LOSS[Cross-Entropy Loss L]
    LABEL[True Label y] --> LOSS
    LOSS --> OPT[Optimizer: SGD or Adam]
    OPT --> UPDATE[Update theta via Backpropagation]
    UPDATE --> FEAT
```

## 4.5 ERM & Overfitting — Training vs. Test Error

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START([Begin Training]) --> EP[Run Epoch]
    EP --> TRAIN[Compute Training Error]
    EP --> VAL[Compute Validation Error]
    TRAIN --> PLOT[Plot Both Curves]
    VAL --> PLOT
    PLOT --> CHECK{Gap Between Train and Val}
    CHECK -->|Both High| UF[Underfitting: Increase Model Capacity]
    CHECK -->|Train Low, Val High| OF[Overfitting: Add Regularization or Data]
    CHECK -->|Both Low and Converged| OK[Good Fit: Deploy]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, PECST745]`
**Define Machine Learning. Differentiate between supervised and unsupervised learning with one CV example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** Machine Learning is a sub-field of AI that enables systems to learn patterns from data and improve performance on a task without being explicitly programmed.

> **Supervised Learning (1 mark):** The model is trained on labeled data $(x_i, y_i)$ where the ground truth is known. *CV Example:* Training a CNN to classify images of cats vs. dogs using labeled image datasets.

> **Unsupervised Learning (1 mark):** The model discovers inherent structure in unlabeled data. *CV Example:* Using k-Means clustering to segment an image into distinct color regions.

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PECST745]`
**What is overfitting? State any two techniques to prevent it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** Overfitting occurs when a model learns the training data — including its noise — too well, resulting in **low training error but high test error**.

> **Technique 1 (1 mark):** **Regularization** — add a penalty term $\lambda \Omega(\theta)$ to the loss, e.g., L2 weight decay $\Omega(\theta) = \Vert \theta \Vert_2^2$, which discourages overly large weights.

> **Technique 2 (1 mark):** **Data Augmentation** — artificially expand the training set by applying random transformations (rotation, flip, crop, color jitter). This forces the model to learn invariant features.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`**

**(a)** Explain the **four main paradigms of machine learning** — supervised, unsupervised, semi-supervised, and reinforcement learning. For each, provide **one computer vision application**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **bias-variance decomposition** of expected prediction error. Explain how this trade-off guides model selection in CV. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question A**

#### Part (a) — Four Paradigms of ML

**(1) Supervised Learning (2 marks):**
The model learns a mapping $f_\theta : \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$ from labeled training data $\mathcal{D} = \{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$. The goal is to minimize a loss $\mathcal{L}(f_\theta(x), y)$.
- **CV Application:** Training a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) to classify X-ray images as *normal* vs. *pneumonia-positive* using radiologist-labeled data.

**(2) Unsupervised Learning (2 marks):**
No labels are available; the algorithm discovers the underlying structure of $P(x)$.
- **CV Application:** Applying k-Means clustering on RGB pixel values to segment a satellite image into land-cover categories (forest, water, urban).

**(3) Semi-Supervised Learning (1.5 marks):**
Combines a small labeled set $\mathcal{D}_l$ with a large unlabeled set $\mathcal{D}_u$ — particularly useful in CV where annotation is costly.
- **CV Application:** Self-training a face recognition system with 1000 labeled faces and 100,000 unlabeled face images scraped from the web.

**(4) Reinforcement Learning (1.5 marks):**
An agent learns a policy $\pi_\theta(a \mid s)$ by interacting with an environment and receiving rewards $r$.
- **CV Application:** Training a vision-based robot arm to grasp objects — the reward is +1 for successful grasp, −1 for failure.

#### Part (b) — Bias-Variance Derivation

(Refer to the exhaustive derivation in Section 3.1 above. Key steps:)

**Step 1:** Define expected error at input $x$ as $\mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}, y}[(y - \hat{f}(x))^2]$.

**Step 2:** Introduce the mean prediction $\bar{f}(x) = \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}}[\hat{f}(x;\mathcal{D})]$.

**Step 3:** Expand $(y - \hat{f})^2 = (y - \bar{f})^2 + 2(y - \bar{f})(\bar{f} - \hat{f}) + (\bar{f} - \hat{f})^2$.

**Step 4:** Take expectation; the cross term vanishes, leaving:

$$\mathbb{E}[(y-\hat{f})^2] = \underbrace{(y - \bar{f})^2}_{\text{Bias}^2} + \underbrace{\mathbb{E}[(\bar{f} - \hat{f})^2]}_{\text{Variance}}$$

**Step 5:** Add irreducible noise $\sigma^2$:

$$\mathbb{E}[(y-\hat{f})^2] = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance} + \sigma^2$$

**Application in CV (2 marks):**
- **Underfitting (high bias):** A linear classifier on a complex dataset like CIFAR-10. Cure: deeper model, more features.
- **Overfitting (high variance):** A 50-layer CNN trained on 500 cat/dog images. Cure: data augmentation, dropout, weight decay.
- The **optimal model** lies at the U-curve minimum of total error. In practice, CV practitioners use **cross-validation** to estimate this point.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** What is **Empirical Risk Minimization (ERM)**? Explain how the VC dimension provides a bound on the generalization error. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With necessary derivations, explain the **gradient descent algorithm** for linear regression. How is it used in training a simple image classifier? **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question B**

#### Part (a) — ERM and VC Theory

**Definition (2 marks):** ERM is the principle of choosing parameters $\theta^*$ that minimize the **average loss on the training set** as a proxy for the unknown true expected risk:

$$\theta^* = \arg\min_{\theta} \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathcal{L}(f_\theta(x_i), y_i)$$

**Why ERM? (2 marks):** The true distribution $P(x,y)$ is unknown, so we cannot directly minimize the expected risk. By the **law of large numbers**, as $N \to \infty$, the empirical risk converges to the true risk.

**VC Bound (3 marks):** Vapnik-Chervonenkis theory provides a probabilistic bound on how far the empirical risk can deviate from the true risk:

$$R_{true}(\theta) \leq R_{emp}(\theta) + \sqrt{\frac{h \left(\ln \frac{2N}{h} + 1\right) - \ln \frac{\eta}{4}}{N}}$$

where $h$ = VC dimension of the hypothesis class, $N$ = number of training samples, $\eta$ = confidence level.

- **Insight:** The bound **tightens** as $N$ grows (more data) and **widens** as $h$ grows (more complex model). This justifies why we need more data for complex models like deep CNNs.

#### Part (b) — Gradient Descent for Linear Regression

**Model (1 mark):** $\hat{y} = \theta^T x$

**Loss (1 mark):** $\mathcal{L}(\theta) = \frac{1}{2N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (\theta^T x_i - y_i)^2$

**Gradient (2 marks):**

$$\nabla_\theta \mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{N} X^T(X\theta - y)$$

**Update rule (2 marks):**

$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}(\theta_t)$$

(For full derivation of each step, refer to Section 3.2.)

**Application in CV (1 mark):** For a simple image classifier (e.g., cat vs. dog with hand-crafted HOG features), we extract a $d$-dimensional feature vector $x$ from each image, then use linear regression followed by a sigmoid activation to predict the probability. Gradient descent iteratively tunes $\theta$ to minimize classification error over the training set.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Bias-Variance Derivation**: Forgetting to add the **irreducible noise $\sigma^2$** at the end. The complete formula MUST contain all three terms.
> 2. **Confusing Bias with the statistical "bias of an estimator"** — in ML, bias = systematic error of the model.
> 3. **In ERM questions**: Students often write the formula but **fail to explain the intuition** (law of large numbers, why empirical risk is a valid proxy).
> 4. **Gradient Descent**: Skipping the **closed-form normal equation** $\theta^* = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T y$ — examiners love this comparison.
> 5. **Supervised vs. Unsupervised**: Always give a **CV-specific example** — generic examples like "email spam" lose marks.
> 6. **Overfitting prevention**: Listing 5+ techniques is fine, but be sure to **state WHY** each works (e.g., dropout prevents co-adaptation of neurons).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!TIP]
> **Memorize these 12 points the night before the exam:**

- [x] **ML Definition** — Learning from data without explicit programming (Samuel, 1959).
- [x] **Four Paradigms** — Supervised, Unsupervised, Semi-Supervised, Reinforcement.
- [x] **Supervised** uses labeled data $(x, y)$; **Unsupervised** has no labels.
- [x] **ERM Principle** — $\theta^* = \arg\min_\theta \frac{1}{N}\sum \mathcal{L}(f_\theta(x_i), y_i)$.
- [x] **Bias-Variance Decomposition** — Error = Bias² + Variance + $\sigma^2$.
- [x] **Underfitting** = high bias, low variance; **Overfitting** = low bias, high variance.
- [x] **Overfitting cures** — Regularization (L1/L2), Dropout, Data Augmentation, More Data.
- [x] **Gradient Descent Update** — $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}$.
- [x] **Normal Equation** — $\theta^* = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T y$ (closed-form, no iterations).
- [x] **Cross-Entropy Loss** — $\mathcal{L}_{CE} = -\sum_k y_k \log \hat{p}_k$ (default for classification).
- [x] **VC Dimension** — Measures model complexity; bound tightens with more data.
- [x] **CV Examples** — Always: image classification (supervised), k-Means segmentation (unsupervised), semi-supervised face recognition, RL-based robotic vision.

> **END OF MODULE 3 NOTES — MACHINE LEARNING INTRODUCTION**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dataset for Machine Perception - Labelled and Unlabelled Data
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST745 - COMPUTER VISION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Dataset for Machine Perception - Labelled and Unlabelled Data

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition: Dataset in Machine Perception

In the context of **Computer Vision (CV)** and **Machine Perception**, a **dataset** is a curated, structured collection of data samples (typically images, videos, or multi-modal sensor readings) along with their associated metadata, used to train, validate, and test machine learning models. Each *sample* is formally represented as a tuple $(x_i, y_i)$ where $x_i$ denotes the input feature vector (e.g., pixel intensities, HOG descriptors, or deep embeddings) and $y_i$ denotes either a ground-truth annotation (labelled) or a null/missing entry (unlabelled).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot:** A dataset is the foundational knowledge base of any perception system. Its quality, diversity, and annotation fidelity directly bound the upper performance limit of any learning algorithm — a principle often quoted as **"Garbage In, Garbage Out" (GIGO)**.

### 1.2 Labelled Data — The "Supervised" Fuel

**Labelled data** is data for which every input sample $x_i$ is paired with a known, human-verified (or sensor-verified) target output $y_i$. In classification, $y_i \in \{1, 2, \dots, C\}$; in regression, $y_i \in \mathbb{R}$; in semantic segmentation, $y_i$ is a per-pixel mask.

**Conceptual Analogy — "The Flashcard Method":**
Imagine a child learning to recognise fruits. The parent shows a picture of an apple and *explicitly says*, "This is an apple." The child sees the input (image) AND the correct answer (label). After seeing thousands of such flashcards, the child learns a mapping $f: x \rightarrow y$. This is exactly how supervised learning consumes labelled data. The label acts as the **"teacher's red pen"** — guiding the model toward correct decisions.

**Common labelling modalities in Computer Vision:**

| Annotation Type | Output $y_i$ | Example Task |
|---|---|---|
| Image Classification | Single class label | Cat / Dog / Car |
| Object Detection | Bounding boxes + class | YOLO-format `.txt` |
| Semantic Segmentation | Per-pixel mask | Cityscapes dataset |
| Instance Segmentation | Per-object mask + ID | COCO dataset |
| Keypoint Detection | $(x, y)$ joint coordinates | Human Pose Estimation |
| Image Captioning | Natural language string | MS-COCO Captions |

### 1.3 Unlabelled Data — The "Unsupervised" Raw Material

**Unlabelled data** is data for which only the input $x_i$ is available, with no corresponding target $y_i$. The model must discover structure, clusters, or representations on its own.

**Conceptual Analogy — "The Curious Toddler":**
A toddler is placed in a room full of toys but receives *no* names for them. Without being told which toy is a "ball" or a "car," the toddler begins *grouping* similar objects together by shape, colour, or texture. The toddler is performing **unsupervised learning** — discovering latent structure without explicit answers.

> [!NOTE]
> Unlabelled data is **abundant, cheap, and scalable** (the internet produces petabytes daily), whereas labelled data is **scarce, expensive, and slow** (a single medical image may require hours of expert radiologist annotation). This asymmetry is the central motivation for transfer learning, self-supervision, and semi-supervised paradigms.

### 1.4 Real-World Engineering Examples

- **Labelled (Autonomous Driving):** Waymo Open Dataset — 3D LiDAR point clouds with manually annotated pedestrian bounding boxes.
- **Unlabelled (Web Crawling):** Raw Google Images scraped for self-supervised pretraining (e.g., Google's JFT-300M used unlabelled at scale).
- **Hybrid (Medical Imaging):** One radiologist-labelled MRI scan + 10,000 unlabelled scans from a hospital PACS system.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Learning Paradigms

The availability of labels dictates the **learning paradigm** that can be applied:

**1. Supervised Learning (Fully Labelled)**

$$\mathcal{D}_{\text{sup}} = \{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}, \quad y_i \text{ is known for all } i$$

The objective is to learn a hypothesis $h_\theta: \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$ by minimising empirical risk:

$$J(\theta) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathcal{L}\big(h_\theta(x_i),\; y_i\big) + \lambda \, \Omega(\theta)$$

where $\mathcal{L}$ is a loss function (e.g., Cross-Entropy, MSE) and $\Omega(\theta)$ is a regulariser.

**2. Unsupervised Learning (Fully Unlabelled)**

$$\mathcal{D}_{\text{unsup}} = \{x_i\}_{i=1}^{M}$$

The objective is to learn the underlying data distribution $p(x)$ or a compressed representation $z = g_\phi(x)$. Common tasks include **clustering**, **dimensionality reduction**, **density estimation**, and **representation learning** (e.g., autoencoders, contrastive methods).

**3. Semi-Supervised Learning (Hybrid — Most Industrially Relevant)**

$$\mathcal{D}_{\text{semi}} = \underbrace{\{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}}_{\text{small labelled set}} \;\cup\; \underbrace{\{x_j\}_{j=1}^{M}}_{\text{large unlabelled set}}, \quad \text{typically } M \gg N$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favourite:** Semi-supervised learning is *the* industry standard because labelled data is the bottleneck. Algorithms like **Pseudo-Labelling**, **Consistency Regularisation (FixMatch)**, and **MixMatch** are high-yield exam topics.

### 2.2 Why Labels Matter — The Information-Theoretic View

Claude Shannon's entropy quantifies the *information content* of labels. For a classification problem with $C$ classes and class distribution $P = (p_1, p_2, \dots, p_C)$:

$$H(Y) = -\sum_{c=1}^{C} p_c \log_2 p_c \quad \text{(bits)}$$

A **labelled** sample contributes this full $H(Y)$ bits of supervised signal, while an **unlabelled** sample only reveals structure in the input distribution $p(x)$, contributing information bounded by $H(X)$. The **mutual information** $I(X; Y)$ quantifies how much knowing $x$ reduces uncertainty about $y$:

$$I(X; Y) = H(Y) - H(Y \mid X)$$

This is the theoretical ceiling on the value of a label.

### 2.3 The Sample Complexity Trade-Off

A fundamental result from statistical learning theory (Vapnik-Chervonenkis, 1971): to learn a hypothesis with error $\epsilon$ and confidence $1 - \delta$, the number of labelled samples required is:

$$N \geq \mathcal{O}\!\left(\frac{\mathrm{VC}(\mathcal{H}) \cdot \log(1/\delta)}{\epsilon^2}\right)$$

where $\mathrm{VC}(\mathcal{H})$ is the Vapnik-Chervonenkis dimension of the hypothesis class. **Key insight:** unlabelled data cannot directly reduce this bound, but it *can* shrink the hypothesis space (via representation learning), indirectly reducing the effective VC dimension.

### 2.4 Annotation Cost Economics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Numerical Favourite:** Students must be able to compute total annotation cost given per-sample cost and dataset size.

$$C_{\text{total}} = N \times c_{\text{per-sample}} \times T_{\text{verifier-multiplier}}$$

For example, **Cityscapes** (semantic segmentation) takes $\approx 90$ minutes per image for fine annotation, and **MS-COCO** costs approximately **$\$$6 per image** for bounding boxes.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Concept | Formula / Definition | Units / Domain | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Dataset Tuple | $(x_i, y_i) \in \mathcal{X} \times \mathcal{Y}$ | Index $i = 1, \dots, N$ | Universal CV notation |
| Labelled Loss | $J(\theta) = \tfrac{1}{N}\sum \mathcal{L}(h_\theta(x_i), y_i)$ | Scalar (nats or bits) | Training objective |
| Cross-Entropy Loss | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{CE}} = -\sum_c y_c \log \hat{p}_c$ | NLL value | Multi-class classification |
| Shannon Entropy | $H(Y) = -\sum p_c \log_2 p_c$ | **bits** | Label information content |
| Conditional Entropy | $H(Y \mid X) = -\sum p(x,y) \log p(y \mid x)$ | **bits** | Residual uncertainty |
| Mutual Information | $I(X;Y) = H(Y) - H(Y \mid X)$ | **bits** | Supervised signal bound |
| Class Imbalance Ratio | $\rho = N_{\max} / N_{\min}$ | $\rho \geq 1$ | Bias detection |
| Annotation Cost | $C_{\text{total}} = N \cdot c_{\text{per}}$ | Currency | Project budgeting |
| Sample Complexity (PAC) | $N \geq \mathcal{O}\!\left(\tfrac{\mathrm{VC}\cdot\log(1/\delta)}{\epsilon^2}\right)$ | Sample count | Feasibility analysis |
| IOU / Jaccard Index | $\mathrm{IoU} = \tfrac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}$ | Scalar $\in [0,1]$ | Segmentation evaluation |
| Mean Average Precision | $\mathrm{mAP} = \tfrac{1}{C}\sum_c \mathrm{AP}_c$ | Scalar $\in [0,1]$ | Detection evaluation |
| Kullback-Leibler Divergence | $D_{\mathrm{KL}}(P \Vert Q) = \sum P \log \tfrac{P}{Q}$ | **nats** | Distribution matching |
| Contrastive Loss (NT-Xent) | $\mathcal{L} = -\log \tfrac{\exp(\mathrm{sim}(z_i,z_j)/\tau)}{\sum_k \exp(\mathrm{sim}(z_i,z_k)/\tau)}$ | Scalar | Self-supervised learning |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Notation Rule:** For absolute value or "such that" in inline math, use `\vert` not the pipe `\` character`, e.g., write $x \in \mathbb{R}$ with $\vert x \vert$ as `$\vert x \vert$` to avoid breaking markdown table renderers.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Healthcare:** Self-supervised pretraining on millions of unlabelled X-rays, then fine-tuning on a few hundred labelled scans from a single hospital.
- **Agriculture:** UAV-captured crop images are unlabelled; farmers manually annotate diseased leaves → labelled subset trains a CNN.
- **Autonomous Vehicles:** Billions of unlabelled driving hours from fleet vehicles; a small fraction is hand-labelled for rare edge cases (pedestrians at night).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Sample Entropy to Effective Label Information

**Problem Setup:** Suppose we have a 3-class classification problem with class prior distribution $P = (0.7, 0.2, 0.1)$ (e.g., "cat," "dog," "bird" in a wildlife dataset). Compute the information content of one labelled sample, and compare to a perfectly uniform distribution $P_{\text{unif}} = (1/3, 1/3, 1/3)$.

**Step 1 — Define Shannon Entropy in base 2:**

$$H(Y) = -\sum_{c=1}^{C} p_c \log_2 p_c$$

**Step 2 — Plug in the skewed distribution values:**

$$H_{\text{skewed}} = -\big(0.7 \log_2 0.7 + 0.2 \log_2 0.2 + 0.1 \log_2 0.1\big)$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate each logarithm numerically:**

$$0.7 \log_2 0.7 = 0.7 \times (-0.5146) = -0.3602$$
$$0.2 \log_2 0.2 = 0.2 \times (-2.3219) = -0.4644$$
$$0.1 \log_2 0.1 = 0.1 \times (-3.3219) = -0.3322$$

**Step 4 — Sum and negate:**

$$H_{\text{skewed}} = -(-0.3602 - 0.4644 - 0.3322) = 1.1568 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 5 — For the uniform distribution:**

$$H_{\text{unif}} = -3 \times \tfrac{1}{3} \log_2 \tfrac{1}{3} = \log_2 3 = 1.5850 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 6 — Compare and interpret:**

$$\Delta H = H_{\text{unif}} - H_{\text{skewed}} = 1.5850 - 1.1568 = 0.4282 \text{ bits}$$

**Conclusion:** A *uniform* class distribution provides $\approx 37\%$ more information per labelled sample. This is why KTU examiners stress **balanced datasets** — class imbalance silently wastes label information.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: PAC Sample Complexity for Labelled vs Semi-Supervised

**Setup:** Hypothesis class with $\mathrm{VC}(\mathcal{H}) = 100$, target error $\epsilon = 0.05$, confidence $\delta = 0.05$.

**Step 1 — Write the PAC bound:**

$$N \geq \frac{\mathrm{VC}(\mathcal{H})}{\epsilon}\left(4 \log_2\!\left(\tfrac{2\mathrm{VC}(\mathcal{H})}{\epsilon}\right) + 2 \log_2\!\left(\tfrac{1}{\delta}\right)\right)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$N \geq \frac{100}{0.05}\Big(4 \log_2(4000) + 2 \log_2(20)\Big)$$

**Step 3 — Compute logarithms:**

$$\log_2 4000 = \log_2(4 \times 10^3) = 2 + 3 \log_2 10 \approx 2 + 9.966 = 11.966$$
$$\log_2 20 = \log_2(4 \times 5) = 2 + 2.322 = 4.322$$

**Step 4 — Compute inner bracket:**

$$4 \times 11.966 + 2 \times 4.322 = 47.864 + 8.644 = 56.508$$

**Step 5 — Compute final sample count:**

$$N \geq 2000 \times 56.508 = 113{,}016 \text{ labelled samples}$$

**Step 6 — Semi-supervised reduction (Co-Training Assumption):**

If unlabelled data shrinks the hypothesis space from $\mathrm{VC} = 100$ to an effective $\mathrm{VC}_{\text{eff}} = 30$:

$$N_{\text{semi}} \geq \frac{30}{0.05}(4\log_2(1200) + 2\log_2(20))$$

$$\log_2 1200 \approx 10.229$$
$$N_{\text{semi}} \geq 600 \times (40.916 + 8.644) = 600 \times 49.560 = 29{,}736$$

**Result:** $N_{\text{semi}} / N_{\text{fully-sup}} = 29{,}736 / 113{,}016 \approx 26.3\%$.

**Conclusion:** With effective use of unlabelled data, the labelled sample requirement drops by nearly **74\%**. This is the engineering justification for self-supervised and semi-supervised methods.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: When to Use What?

| Scenario | Best Dataset Strategy | Rationale | Risk |
|---|---|---|---|
| Abundant budget, clear task | Fully Labelled (Supervised) | Highest accuracy ceiling | Overfitting if $N$ small |
| Abundant raw data, limited budget | Pre-train on unlabelled, fine-tune on labels | Leverage scale | Negative transfer |
| Few labels, lots of data | Semi-supervised (FixMatch, Mean Teacher) | Exploits both regimes | Confirmation bias in pseudo-labels |
| No labels at all | Self-supervised (SimCLR, MAE) | Learns transferable features | Downstream task mismatch |
| Streaming / online data | Active learning — label *informative* samples | Minimises label cost | Sampling bias |
| Class imbalance severe | Cost-sensitive learning + re-labelling | Recovers rare-class info | Mislabel propagation |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Taxonomy of Datasets for Machine Perception

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Dataset for Machine Perception] --> B[Labelled Dataset]
    A --> C[Unlabelled Dataset]
    A --> D[Hybrid Dataset]

    B --> B1[Image Classification]
    B --> B2[Object Detection]
    B --> B3[Semantic Segmentation]
    B --> B4[Pose Keypoints]

    C --> C1[Raw Web Images]
    C --> C2[Sensor Streams]
    C --> C3[Video Footage]

    D --> D1[Semi-Supervised]
    D --> D2[Self-Supervised]
    D --> D3[Active Learning Pool]

    B1 --> E1[Example: ImageNet]
    B2 --> E2[Example: COCO]
    B3 --> E3[Example: Cityscapes]
    B4 --> E4[Example: COCO-Pose]

    C1 --> F1[Example: JFT-300M]
    C2 --> F2[Example: Waymo Raw]
    C3 --> F3[Example: Kinetics Raw]

    D1 --> G1[Example: FixMatch Bench]
    D2 --> G2[Example: SimCLR Pretrain]
    D3 --> G3[Example: AL Decathlon]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Supervised vs Unsupervised Learning Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SUP[Supervised Pipeline]
        direction TB
        A1[Input Image x] --> A2[Feature Extractor CNN]
        A2 --> A3[Classifier Head]
        A3 --> A4[Prediction y_hat]
        A5[Ground Truth Label y] --> A6[Loss Function]
        A4 --> A6
        A6 --> A7[Backprop Update Weights]
    end

    subgraph UNSUP[Unsupervised Pipeline]
        direction TB
        B1[Input Image x] --> B2[Encoder Network]
        B2 --> B3[Latent Representation z]
        B3 --> B4[Reconstruction or Contrastive Loss]
        B1 --> B4
        B4 --> B5[Update Encoder Weights]
    end

    note1[Labels Drive SUP Loss] -.-> SUP
    note2[No Labels in UNSUP - Uses Self-Supervision] -.-> UNSUP
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Annotation Pipeline for Labelled Data

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R0[Raw Image Acquisition] --> R1[Quality Filtering]
    R1 --> R2[Preprocessing Resize and Normalize]
    R2 --> R3[Annotation by Human or Auto-Tool]
    R3 --> R4[Verification by Second Annotator]
    R4 --> R5{Inter Annotator Agreement above Threshold}
    R5 -->|Yes| R6[Store in Labelled Dataset]
    R5 -->|No| R7[Flag for Re-Annotation]
    R7 --> R3
    R6 --> R8[Train Validation Test Split]
    R8 --> R9[Model Training]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Self-Supervised vs Supervised Data Flow Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LAB[With Labels]
        L1[Image] --> L2[CNN]
        L2 --> L3[Feature]
        L3 --> L4[Classifier]
        L4 --> L5[Cross Entropy with True Label]
    end

    subgraph SSL[Without Labels - Contrastive]
        S1[Image X1] --> S2[Augment Twice]
        S2 --> S3[X1a and X1b]
        S3 --> S4[Encoder f]
        S4 --> S5[Projector g]
        S5 --> S6[NT Xent Loss]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The self-supervised branch (right) is *conceptually* attempting to learn a feature extractor $f(\cdot)$ such that augmented views of the same image map to nearby points in embedding space — without ever seeing a class label. This pretraining can later be fine-tuned with a *tiny* labelled set.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Distinguish between labelled and unlabelled data with one example each from a computer vision context.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Labelled Data | Unlabelled Data |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Input paired with ground-truth target $y_i$ | Input $x_i$ only, no target |
| Annotation | Requires human / sensor labelling | Raw, no manual effort |
| Cost | High (e.g., **$\$$6/image** for COCO bbox) | Near zero |
| Learning | Supervised / Semi-supervised | Unsupervised / Self-supervised |
| CV Example | COCO with bounding boxes | YouTube-8M raw videos |

*Conclusion:* Labelled data enables supervised learning with explicit error feedback, while unlabelled data enables representation learning at internet scale but requires clever pretext tasks.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Series 1]**
*What is the role of unlabelled data in semi-supervised learning? State one algorithm.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Unlabelled data in semi-supervised learning provides additional samples that help the model learn the *input distribution* $p(x)$, which regularises the decision boundary and prevents overfitting to the small labelled set. The model is implicitly encouraged to place the boundary in *low-density* regions of $p(x)$ — the **cluster assumption**.

*Example Algorithm:* **Pseudo-Labelling** — the model assigns its high-confidence predictions on unlabelled data as pseudo-labels, then retrains on the augmented dataset. **FixMatch** is a more advanced variant combining pseudo-labelling with consistency regularisation under strong/weak augmentations.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Students often confuse **labelled data** with **structured data** (e.g., a CSV is structured but may be unlabelled). Marks are lost for not defining $y_i$ explicitly.
> 2. In numerical sample-complexity questions, **forgetting the log terms** is the most common partial-credit error. Show every logarithm expansion.
> 3. For semi-supervised questions, you MUST mention the **cluster assumption** or **manifold assumption** to score full marks — naming algorithms alone is incomplete.
> 4. Never confuse **validation set** with **test set** in dataset split questions — the 70/15/15 or 80/10/10 split must be stated precisely.

---

#### **Part B — Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the structure of a labelled dataset for object detection. Use a real-world example (COCO / Pascal VOC) and describe the annotation format. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A labelled dataset for object detection contains three components per image:
1. **Input image** $x_i \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times 3}$
2. **Bounding box annotations** $b_{i,k} = (x_{\min}, y_{\min}, x_{\max}, y_{\max}, c_k)$ for $k = 1, \dots, K_i$ objects
3. **Optional metadata**: image source, license, occlusion flags, truncation flags

**COCO Format Example (in `.json`):**
```json
{
  "images": [{"id": 1, "width": 640, "height": 480}],
  "annotations": [{
    "id": 101,
    "image_id": 1,
    "category_id": 3,        // class "car"
    "bbox": [x, y, w, h],     // top-left + width/height
    "area": 12345.6,
    "iscrowd": 0
  }],
  "categories": [{"id": 3, "name": "car"}]
}
```

**Key Engineering Properties:**
- **IoU evaluation** $\mathrm{IoU} = \vert B_{\text{pred}} \cap B_{\text{gt}} \vert \,/\, \vert B_{\text{pred}} \cup B_{\text{gt}} \vert$
- **mAP computed at IoU thresholds 0.5 to 0.95** in steps of 0.05
- **COCO has 80 object categories** and 1.5 million object instances across 330K images

**Annotation Workflow:** Raw image → bounding box drawn by annotator (e.g., LabelImg, CVAT) → second annotator verifies → stored in standardised format → split into train/val/test.

---

**(b)** Discuss the challenges of creating large-scale labelled datasets. Propose two engineering solutions. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Challenge 1 — Annotation Cost and Time:**
- A single medical CT scan may require $2{-}4$ hours of expert radiologist time
- COCO's 330K images cost approximately **$\$$2 million USD** to annotate
- For 100M images at $\$\!6$ each, total cost is **$\$$600 million USD**

**Challenge 2 — Annotator Bias and Subjectivity:**
- Inter-annotator agreement is often below 80% for ambiguous tasks (e.g., emotion recognition, crowd counting)
- Cultural / regional differences in labelling conventions

**Challenge 3 — Class Imbalance and Long-Tail Distribution:**
- Rare classes (e.g., "fire hydrant") have $1000\times$ fewer samples than common classes ("person")

**Solution 1 — Active Learning:** The model identifies *informative* unlabelled samples (e.g., highest entropy predictions) and requests human labels *only* for those. This can reduce labelling cost by $50{-}80\%$.

**Solution 2 — Weak Supervision / Auto-Labelling:** Use heuristic labelling functions (e.g., keyword search, image metadata) to generate noisy labels at scale, then model the noise using a label model such as **Snorkel**. Combine with a small set of clean labels for denoising.

**Solution 3 (Bonus):** Use **synthetic data generation** via GANs / diffusion models to create labelled samples artificially, especially for rare classes.

---

#### **Part B — Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Series 2]**

**(a)** Derive the information content of a labelled sample in a 4-class classification problem with prior distribution $P = (0.4, 0.3, 0.2, 0.1)$. Compare it with a uniform prior. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Write Shannon entropy formula:**

$$H(Y) = -\sum_{c=1}^{C} p_c \log_2 p_c$$

**Step 2 — Substitute and expand for the skewed prior:**

$$H_{\text{skewed}} = -(0.4 \log_2 0.4 + 0.3 \log_2 0.3 + 0.2 \log_2 0.2 + 0.1 \log_2 0.1)$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate each term:**

$$0.4 \log_2 0.4 = 0.4 \times (-1.3219) = -0.5288$$
$$0.3 \log_2 0.3 = 0.3 \times (-1.7370) = -0.5211$$
$$0.2 \log_2 0.2 = 0.2 \times (-2.3219) = -0.4644$$
$$0.1 \log_2 0.1 = 0.1 \times (-3.3219) = -0.3322$$

**Step 4 — Sum and negate:**

$$H_{\text{skewed}} = -(-0.5288 - 0.5211 - 0.4644 - 0.3322) = 1.8465 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 5 — For the uniform prior $P = (0.25, 0.25, 0.25, 0.25)$:**

$$H_{\text{unif}} = -4 \times 0.25 \log_2 0.25 = \log_2 4 = 2.0000 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 6 — Comparison:**

$$\Delta H = 2.0000 - 1.8465 = 0.1535 \text{ bits}$$

The uniform distribution provides $\frac{0.1535}{1.8465} \approx 8.3\%$ more information per label. *Interpretation:* Class imbalance reduces the *expected* information per label because the most probable class is "easy" to predict. Rebalancing the dataset through oversampling or class-weighted loss recovers this lost information.

---

**(b)** Explain the semi-supervised learning setting. With a neat diagram, describe the **Pseudo-Labelling** algorithm and state one limitation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Setting Definition:** Semi-supervised learning uses a small labelled set $\mathcal{D}_L = \{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$ and a large unlabelled set $\mathcal{D}_U = \{x_j\}_{j=1}^{M}$ with $M \gg N$, to train a model that outperforms one trained on $\mathcal{D}_L$ alone.

**Assumption (Cluster Assumption):** Data points in the same cluster of $p(x)$ are likely to share the same class label $y$.

**Algorithm: Pseudo-Labelling (Lee, 2013)**

**Step 1** — Train initial model $f_\theta$ on $\mathcal{D}_L$ using standard cross-entropy.

**Step 2** — For each unlabelled sample $x_j \in \mathcal{D}_U$, compute predicted class probabilities $\hat{p}_j = \text{softmax}(f_\theta(x_j))$.

**Step 3** — Assign pseudo-label $\tilde{y}_j = \arg\max_c \hat{p}_j(c)$ *only if* the maximum confidence exceeds threshold $\tau$ (e.g., $0.95$); otherwise discard.

**Step 4** — Augment dataset: $\mathcal{D}_{\text{aug}} = \mathcal{D}_L \cup \{(x_j, \tilde{y}_j) \mid \text{conf}(x_j) > \tau\}$.

**Step 5** — Retrain $f_\theta$ on $\mathcal{D}_{\text{aug}}$.

**Step 6** — Repeat Steps 2-5 for $T$ iterations or until convergence.

**Loss Function:**

$$J = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathcal{L}_{\text{CE}}(f_\theta(x_i), y_i) + \frac{\alpha}{M'}\sum_{j=1}^{M'} \mathcal{L}_{\text{CE}}(f_\theta(x_j), \tilde{y}_j)$$

where $M'$ is the count of accepted pseudo-labels and $\alpha$ is a balancing weight ramped up over training.

**Limitation — Confirmation Bias:** If the initial model is wrong, confidently wrong pseudo-labels are *amplified* in subsequent rounds, leading to error accumulation. Mitigation: use confidence thresholding, temperature sharpening, or strong augmentation (as in FixMatch).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Pin This Before the Exam):**

- **Labelled data** = $(x_i, y_i)$ pair with human-verified ground truth → enables supervised learning → expensive, scarce, high signal.
- **Unlabelled data** = $x_i$ only, no $y_i$ → enables unsupervised / self-supervised representation learning → cheap, abundant, low per-sample signal.
- **Hybrid (semi-supervised)** = small labelled + large unlabelled → industrial standard; assumes cluster or manifold structure.
- **Shannon Entropy** $H(Y) = -\sum p_c \log_2 p_c$ quantifies the information content of a label set in **bits**; uniform prior maximises it.
- **Mutual Information** $I(X; Y) = H(Y) - H(Y \mid X)$ is the upper bound on supervised signal per sample.
- **PAC Sample Complexity** $N \geq \mathcal{O}(\mathrm{VC}(\mathcal{H}) \log(1/\delta) / \epsilon^2)$ — labels cannot be replaced by unlabelled data directly, but unlabelled data reduces effective hypothesis complexity.
- **Annotation Cost** scales linearly with $N$ and per-sample cost; medical / segmentation data can be 100× more expensive than classification.
- **Class Imbalance** reduces information per label and biases models toward majority classes; use re-weighting, oversampling, or focal loss.
- **Evaluation Metrics:** Classification → Accuracy, F1, AUC. Detection → mAP at IoU 0.5:0.95. Segmentation → mIoU.
- **Key Datasets to Remember:** ImageNet (1.2M, 1000 cls), COCO (330K, 80 cls + captioning), Cityscapes (5K urban seg), Kinetics (400 action cls), JFT-300M (Google internal, unlabelled at scale).
- **Key Algorithms:** Pseudo-Labelling, FixMatch, SimCLR, MAE, MoCo — all exploit unlabelled data for representation learning.
- **Common Exam Pitfalls:** Do NOT confuse *unlabelled* with *unstructured*; always state the assumption (cluster / manifold / smoothness) in semi-supervised answers; show every log step in entropy derivations.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basics of Classification and Clustering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST745 - COMPUTER VISION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Basics of Classification and Clustering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Classification — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Classification** in Computer Vision is a **supervised learning** paradigm in which a model is trained on a labeled dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$, where $x_i \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ is an input feature vector (or image) and $y_i \in \{1, 2, \dots, K\}$ is its corresponding class label. The goal is to learn a mapping function $f: \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$ that can predict the class of a previously unseen test sample with minimum error.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** context, classification forms the backbone of almost every recognition task — from face identification to medical tumor grading in MRI scans. The learned function $f$ is evaluated on a test set, and its predictive power is quantified using metrics such as **Accuracy**, **Precision**, **Recall**, and the **F1-score**.

### Intuitive Analogy
Imagine a **post office sorting room**. Each letter (image) has a destination address (label) stamped on it during a "training phase" by experienced clerks. After studying thousands of letters, the clerks learn the *pattern* of addresses. When a new letter arrives, they look at the handwriting, the postal code, and the city name, then drop it into the correct tray. Classification works identically: the algorithm studies labeled examples, identifies distinguishing patterns in feature space, and routes new inputs to the correct class bin.

## 1.2 Clustering — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Clustering** is an **unsupervised learning** paradigm that organizes a set of unlabeled samples $\mathcal{X} = \{x_1, x_2, \dots, x_N\}$ into $K$ homogeneous groups (clusters) such that intra-cluster similarity is maximized and inter-cluster similarity is minimized. The objective is to discover the intrinsic structure of the data without any prior label information.

### Intuitive Analogy
Picture a **library with no labels on its shelves**. A librarian walks in at night and starts grouping books by size, color, and weight, even though the titles and subjects are unknown. The librarian forms groups (clusters) where similar-looking books sit together. The next day, the actual subject labels are revealed, and — surprisingly — most clusters correspond to actual genres. Clustering performs this exact "discover-the-hidden-groups" task on raw image features.

## 1.3 Supervised vs. Unsupervised — The Master Distinction

| Aspect | Classification (Supervised) | Clustering (Unsupervised) |
|---|---|---|
| **Labels** | Required during training | Not required |
| **Goal** | Predict a known category | Discover unknown categories |
| **Evaluation** | Accuracy, F1, Confusion Matrix | Silhouette, Davies–Bouldin, Inertia |
| **Output** | Discrete class label $y \in \{1, \dots, K\}$ | Cluster assignment $c_i \in \{1, \dots, K\}$ |
| **Data requirement** | Labeled (expensive to obtain) | Unlabeled (cheap to obtain) |
| **Example algorithm** | K-NN, SVM, CNN | K-Means, DBSCAN, Hierarchical |

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU examinations, a very common 3-mark question is: *"Differentiate between classification and clustering."* Memorize the table above — the *labels-required* axis is the most important differentiator.

## 1.4 Why These Concepts Matter in Computer Vision

- **Classification** powers object recognition (cat vs. dog), facial authentication, and tumor grading.
- **Clustering** enables image segmentation, unsupervised feature learning, document organization, and anomaly detection.
- Both form the **decision-making layer** of any modern CV pipeline (preceded by preprocessing and feature extraction, followed by post-processing).

## 1.5 Key Terminology (KTU-Highlighted)

- **Feature Vector ($x$)**: A numerical representation of an image (e.g., pixel intensities, HOG descriptors, CNN embeddings).
- **Class Label ($y$)**: The ground-truth category.
- **Decision Boundary**: The hypersurface in feature space that separates two or more classes.
- **Centroid ($\mu_k$)**: The mean of all feature vectors assigned to cluster $k$.
- **Inertia / Within-Cluster Sum of Squares (WCSS)**: A clustering cost function minimized by K-Means.
- **Confusion Matrix**: A $K \times K$ table summarizing correct and incorrect predictions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Classification Pipeline

The classical operational flow is:

1. **Image Acquisition** — Capture the raw image $I \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times C}$.
2. **Preprocessing** — Resize, normalize, denoise: $I \rightarrow I'$.
3. **Feature Extraction** — Convert image to vector: $I' \rightarrow x \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ (using HOG, SIFT, or deep CNN).
4. **Model Training** — Learn $f$ from $(x_i, y_i)$ pairs.
5. **Prediction** — For new $x_{\text{test}}$, output $\hat{y} = f(x_{\text{test}})$.
6. **Evaluation** — Compare $\hat{y}$ vs. $y$ using metrics below.

## 2.2 Distance Metrics — The Geometric Core

Both classification (K-NN) and clustering (K-Means) rely on distance. Let $x, y \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$.

### 2.2.1 Euclidean Distance ($L_2$)
$$
d(x, y) = \sqrt{\sum_{j=1}^{d} (x_j - y_j)^2}
$$

### 2.2.2 Manhattan Distance ($L_1$)
$$
d(x, y) = \sum_{j=1}^{d} \vert x_j - y_j \vert
$$

### 2.2.3 Minkowski Distance (Generalization)
$$
d(x, y) = \left( \sum_{j=1}^{d} \vert x_j - y_j \vert^{p} \right)^{1/p}
$$

For $p=1$ → Manhattan, $p=2$ → Euclidean, $p \to \infty$ → Chebyshev.

### 2.2.4 Cosine Similarity
Used when direction matters more than magnitude (e.g., histogram features).
$$
\text{cos\_sim}(x, y) = \frac{x \cdot y}{\|x\| \cdot \|y\|}
$$

### 2.2.5 Mahalanobis Distance
Accounts for feature correlation via covariance matrix $\Sigma$.
$$
d_M(x, y) = \sqrt{(x - y)^{\top} \Sigma^{-1} (x - y)}
$$

## 2.3 K-Nearest Neighbors (K-NN) Classifier

A **lazy learner** that classifies a test sample by majority vote of its $K$ closest training samples in feature space.
$$
\hat{y} = \arg\max_{c \in \{1, \dots, K\}} \sum_{x_i \in N_K(x_{\text{test}})} \mathbb{1}(y_i = c)
$$
where $N_K(x_{\text{test}})$ is the set of $K$ nearest neighbors and $\mathbb{1}(\cdot)$ is the indicator function.

## 2.4 K-Means Clustering Algorithm

A **centroid-based** iterative clustering method that minimizes the within-cluster sum of squares (WCSS):
$$
J = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} \| x_i - \mu_k \|^2
$$
where $\mu_k$ is the centroid of cluster $C_k$.

**Iterative Steps:**
1. **Initialize** $K$ centroids $\mu_1, \dots, \mu_K$ (e.g., randomly or via K-Means++).
2. **Assignment step:** Assign each $x_i$ to the nearest centroid.
$$
c_i = \arg\min_{k} \| x_i - \mu_k \|^2
$$
3. **Update step:** Recompute each centroid as the mean of its assigned points.
$$
\mu_k = \frac{1}{\vert C_k \vert} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} x_i
$$
4. Repeat steps 2–3 until convergence (centroids stop moving or $J$ stabilizes).

## 2.5 Performance Metrics for Classification

Given a binary problem with classes Positive (P) and Negative (N):

| Metric | Formula | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Accuracy** | $\frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Overall correctness |
| **Precision** | $\frac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Quality of positive predictions |
| **Recall (Sensitivity)** | $\frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Coverage of actual positives |
| **Specificity** | $\frac{TN}{TN + FP}$ | Coverage of actual negatives |
| **F1-Score** | $\frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Harmonic mean of P and R |

For multi-class problems, these are computed per class and **macro-averaged** (simple mean) or **micro-averaged** (aggregate counts).

## 2.6 Clustering Quality Metrics

### 2.6.1 Silhouette Coefficient
For a sample $x_i$ in cluster $C_k$:
- $a(i)$ = mean distance to other points in $C_k$ (cohesion).
- $b(i)$ = min mean distance to points in the nearest other cluster (separation).
$$
s(i) = \frac{b(i) - a(i)}{\max\{a(i), b(i)\}}
$$
$s(i) \in [-1, +1]$: closer to $+1$ → better clustering.

### 2.6.2 Davies–Bouldin Index
$$
\text{DB} = \frac{1}{K} \sum_{i=1}^{K} \max_{j \neq i} \left( \frac{S_i + S_j}{M_{ij}} \right)
$$
where $S_i$ is the average scatter of cluster $i$ and $M_{ij}$ is the distance between centroids $i$ and $j$. **Lower is better.**

## 2.7 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Quick-Reference Table)

| Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Euclidean distance | $d = \sqrt{\sum (x_j - y_j)^2}$ | Same as feature units |
| Manhattan distance | $d = \sum \vert x_j - y_j \vert$ | Robust to outliers |
| Cosine similarity | $\cos\theta = \frac{x \cdot y}{\|x\| \|y\|}$ | Range $[-1, 1]$ |
| Mahalanobis distance | $d_M = \sqrt{(x-y)^\top \Sigma^{-1}(x-y)}$ | Accounts for covariance |
| K-Means objective | $J = \sum_k \sum_{i \in C_k} \|x_i - \mu_k\|^2$ | Inertia / WCSS |
| Accuracy | $(TP+TN)/\text{Total}$ | $[0, 1]$ |
| Precision | $TP/(TP+FP)$ | $[0, 1]$ |
| Recall | $TP/(TP+FN)$ | $[0, 1]$ |
| F1-Score | $2PR/(P+R)$ | $[0, 1]$ |
| Silhouette | $s(i) = \frac{b(i)-a(i)}{\max(a,b)}$ | $[-1, 1]$ |

> [!NOTE]
> In every KTU 14-mark question, you are expected to **derive at least one formula from first principles** and **tabulate at least three metrics**. The above table covers 90% of the marks.

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Medical Imaging**: Classifying tumors as benign/malignant from MRI; clustering pixel intensities to segment organs.
- **Autonomous Vehicles**: Classifying pedestrians, signs, lanes; clustering LiDAR points to detect obstacles.
- **Satellite Imaging**: Classifying land cover (forest/water/urban); clustering regions to detect changes over time.
- **Industrial Inspection**: Classifying defects (crack/dent/scratch); clustering production samples to detect anomalies.
- **Biometrics**: Classifying fingerprints; clustering face embeddings for identity grouping.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the K-Means Centroid Update Rule

We want to find the centroid $\mu_k$ that minimizes the within-cluster sum of squares for cluster $C_k$:
$$
J_k = \sum_{x_i \in C_k} \| x_i - \mu_k \|^2
$$

**Step 1: Expand the squared norm.**
$$
J_k = \sum_{x_i \in C_k} (x_i - \mu_k)^\top (x_i - \mu_k)
$$

**Step 2: Differentiate $J_k$ with respect to $\mu_k$ and set to zero.**
$$
\frac{\partial J_k}{\partial \mu_k} = -2 \sum_{x_i \in C_k} (x_i - \mu_k) = 0
$$

**Step 3: Solve for $\mu_k$.**
$$
\sum_{x_i \in C_k} (x_i - \mu_k) = 0
$$

$$
\sum_{x_i \in C_k} x_i - \sum_{x_i \in C_k} \mu_k = 0
$$

**Step 4: Recognize that $\mu_k$ is constant over the summation**, so $\sum \mu_k = \vert C_k \vert \cdot \mu_k$:
$$
\sum_{x_i \in C_k} x_i = \vert C_k \vert \cdot \mu_k
$$

**Step 5: Final closed-form expression.**
$$
\boxed{\mu_k = \frac{1}{\vert C_k \vert} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} x_i}
$$

This proves that **the optimal centroid is the arithmetic mean** of all points in its cluster. The same derivation generalizes to the $K$-cluster joint objective $J = \sum_k J_k$ since the cross-terms vanish when clusters are disjoint.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Silhouette Coefficient

For a sample $x_i \in C_a$:

**Step 1: Cohesion $a(i)$.**
$$
a(i) = \frac{1}{\vert C_a \vert - 1} \sum_{j \in C_a, j \neq i} d(x_i, x_j)
$$

**Step 2: Separation $b(i)$ — mean distance to every *other* cluster, then take the minimum.**
$$
b(i) = \min_{c \neq a} \frac{1}{\vert C_c \vert} \sum_{j \in C_c} d(x_i, x_j)
$$

**Step 3: Combine into the silhouette score.**
$$
\boxed{s(i) = \frac{b(i) - a(i)}{\max\{a(i), b(i)\}}}
$$

**Step 4: Interpretation.**
- $b(i) \gg a(i)$ → $s(i) \to +1$ (well-clustered).
- $b(i) \ll a(i)$ → $s(i) \to -1$ (probably in the wrong cluster).
- $b(i) \approx a(i)$ → $s(i) \to 0$ (on the decision boundary).

## 3.3 Worked Example: Confusion Matrix & Metrics

Suppose a binary classifier produces the following on a test set of 200 samples:

|  | Predicted Positive | Predicted Negative |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual Positive** | 50 (TP) | 10 (FN) |
| **Actual Negative** | 15 (FP) | 125 (TN) |

**Step 1: Accuracy.**
$$
\text{Acc} = \frac{50 + 125}{50 + 10 + 15 + 125} = \frac{175}{200} = 0.875 \; (87.5\%)
$$

**Step 2: Precision.**
$$
P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{50}{50 + 15} = \frac{50}{65} \approx 0.7692
$$

**Step 3: Recall (Sensitivity).**
$$
R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{50}{50 + 10} = \frac{50}{60} \approx 0.8333
$$

**Step 4: F1-Score.**
$$
F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R} = \frac{2 \cdot 0.7692 \cdot 0.8333}{0.7692 + 0.8333} = \frac{1.2821}{1.6025} \approx 0.8000
$$

**Step 5: Specificity.**
$$
\text{Spec} = \frac{TN}{TN + FP} = \frac{125}{125 + 15} = \frac{125}{140} \approx 0.8929
$$

> [!NOTE]
> A common KTU exam pitfall: students compute $P$ using **row totals** instead of **column totals**. Always remember — Precision uses the **predicted class** (column), Recall uses the **actual class** (row).

## 3.4 Worked Example: K-Means by Hand (1-D, K=2)

**Data:** $x = \{1, 2, 8, 9, 10\}$. Initial centroids: $\mu_1 = 1$, $\mu_2 = 10$.

**Iteration 1:**

| Point | Dist to $\mu_1$ | Dist to $\mu_2$ | Assigned |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 | 9 | $C_1$ |
| 2 | 1 | 8 | $C_1$ |
| 8 | 7 | 2 | $C_2$ |
| 9 | 8 | 1 | $C_2$ |
| 10 | 9 | 0 | $C_2$ |

**Step 2: Update centroids.**
$$
\mu_1 = \frac{1+2}{2} = 1.5, \quad \mu_2 = \frac{8+9+10}{3} = 9.0
$$

**Step 3: Recompute WCSS.**
$$
J_1 = (1 - 1.5)^2 + (2 - 1.5)^2 = 0.5
$$
$$
J_2 = (8 - 9)^2 + (9 - 9)^2 + (10 - 9)^2 = 2.0
$$
$$
J_{\text{total}} = 2.5
$$

**Iteration 2:** Reassign with new centroids. Assignments remain the same (verify). Update:
$$
\mu_1 = 1.5, \quad \mu_2 = 9.0
$$
Centroids unchanged → **converged**. Final clusters: $\{1, 2\}$ and $\{8, 9, 10\}$.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Algorithm Selection Guide

| Scenario | Recommended Approach | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Small dataset, clear boundaries, K known | K-Means | Fast, simple, interpretable |
| Arbitrary cluster shapes, noise present | DBSCAN | Density-based, finds outliers |
| Hierarchical groupings needed | Agglomerative | Produces dendrogram |
| Very high-dimensional data | Spectral / Subspace | Handles curse of dimensionality |
| Streaming data, evolving clusters | Online clustering | One-pass updates |
| Labels available, accuracy critical | CNN Classifier | State-of-the-art supervised |
| Few labeled, many unlabeled samples | Semi-supervised (e.g., self-training) | Combines both paradigms |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## 4.1 High-Level Classification vs. Clustering Architecture

```mermaid
    P5 --> P6{Has Centroid Moved?}
    P6 -- Yes --> P3
    P6 -- No --> P7[Output Final Clusters]
```
