# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define classification in the context of computer vision. List any two classifiers used in image recognition.**

**Model Answer:**
Classification is a supervised learning technique in computer vision where a model is trained on labeled image data $\{(x_i, y_i)\}$ to learn a mapping $f: \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$, and is then used to predict the class label of an unseen test image. The output is a discrete label from a predefined set of $K$ classes.

Two classifiers widely used in image recognition:
1. **K-Nearest Neighbors (K-NN)** — non-parametric, instance-based.
2. **Support Vector Machine (SVM)** — finds the optimal separating hyperplane.

*(Also acceptable: CNN, Decision Tree, Naive Bayes, Random Forest.)*

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is clustering? Why is it called an unsupervised learning technique?**

**Model Answer:**
Clustering is the process of grouping a set of unlabeled data points into $K$ clusters such that points within the same cluster are more similar to each other than to points in other clusters. It is called *unsupervised* because the training data has no ground-truth labels — the algorithm must discover the inherent groupings in the feature space on its own, using only geometric/statistical properties such as distance, density, or connectivity.

---

## PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A: [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a)** Explain the K-Nearest Neighbors (K-NN) algorithm for image classification. Describe its working with a suitable diagram. Discuss the effect of choosing an odd vs. even value of $K$ on classification. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A binary classifier produced the following confusion matrix on a 100-sample test set: TP=40, FN=10, FP=5, TN=45. Compute Accuracy, Precision, Recall, Specificity, and F1-Score. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution — (a)

**Definition:** K-NN is a non-parametric, instance-based supervised classifier. It does not learn an explicit model during training; instead, it memorizes the training set and performs classification at test time.

**Algorithm Steps:**
1. Given a test image, extract its feature vector $x_{\text{test}}$.
2. Compute the distance from $x_{\text{test}}$ to every training point $x_i$ (commonly Euclidean).
3. Sort distances in ascending order and select the $K$ closest points (the "neighbors").
4. For classification, take a **majority vote** of the labels of these $K$ neighbors.
5. For regression, take the **mean** of the neighbors' target values.

**Formal Rule:**
$$
\hat{y} = \arg\max_{c} \sum_{x_i \in N_K(x_{\text{test}})} \mathbb{1}(y_i = c)
$$

**Diagram (textual):** Imagine a 2-D plane with red circles (class A) and blue squares (class B). For a new green triangle, draw a circle around the $K$ nearest training points; the dominant color inside the circle decides the prediction.

**Effect of $K$:**
- **Small $K$ (e.g., 1)**: Highly sensitive to noise; complex, jagged decision boundary → overfitting.
- **Large $K$**: Smoother boundary; may include points from the wrong class → underfitting.
- **Odd $K$**: Avoids tie-breaking ambiguity in binary classification — **always preferred**.
- **Even $K$**: May result in a tie, requiring a tie-breaking rule (e.g., distance-weighted vote).

**Advantages:** Simple, no training phase, naturally multi-class.
**Disadvantages:** Slow at test time for large datasets, sensitive to feature scaling and irrelevant features.

#### Model Solution — (b)

Given: TP=40, FN=10, FP=5, TN=45, Total = 100.

**Accuracy:**
$$
\text{Acc} = \frac{TP + TN}{100} = \frac{40 + 45}{100} = \frac{85}{100} = 0.85
$$

**Precision:**
$$
P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{40}{40 + 5} = \frac{40}{45} = 0.8889
$$

**Recall (Sensitivity):**
$$
R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{40}{40 + 10} = \frac{40}{50} = 0.80
$$

**Specificity:**
$$
\text{Spec} = \frac{TN}{TN + FP} = \frac{45}{45 + 5} = \frac{45}{50} = 0.90
$$

**F1-Score:**
$$
F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R} = \frac{2 \cdot 0.8889 \cdot 0.80}{0.8889 + 0.80} = \frac{1.4222}{1.6889} \approx 0.8421
$$

**Result Summary Table:**

| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| Accuracy | **0.8500** |
| Precision | **0.8889** |
| Recall | **0.8000** |
| Specificity | **0.9000** |
| F1-Score | **0.8421** |

---

### Question B: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** Explain the K-Means clustering algorithm in detail. State its objective function and discuss how the centroid is updated in each iteration. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** For the 1-D dataset $X = \{2, 4, 10, 12, 14, 20, 22\}$ with initial centroids $\mu_1 = 2$ and $\mu_2 = 20$, perform **two iterations** of K-Means clustering. Compute the WCSS at each step. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution — (a)

**Introduction:** K-Means is a centroid-based, iterative, unsupervised clustering algorithm proposed by **Lloyd (1957)**. It partitions $N$ data points into $K$ disjoint clusters by minimizing the within-cluster sum of squared distances (inertia).

**Objective Function:**
$$
J = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} \| x_i - \mu_k \|^2
$$
where $C_k$ is the $k$-th cluster and $\mu_k$ is its centroid.

**Algorithm Steps:**
1. **Initialization:** Choose $K$ initial centroids $\mu_1, \dots, \mu_K$ randomly, or use **K-Means++** for better convergence.
2. **Assignment Step:** For each data point $x_i$, assign it to the cluster whose centroid is closest.
$$
c_i = \arg\min_{k} \| x_i - \mu_k \|^2
$$
3. **Update Step:** Recompute each centroid as the mean of all points in its cluster.
$$
\mu_k = \frac{1}{\vert C_k \vert} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} x_i
$$
4. **Convergence Check:** Repeat steps 2 and 3 until the centroids stop moving, the cluster assignments stabilize, or $J$ stops decreasing meaningfully.

**Derivation of Centroid Update (Already covered in Section 3.1):** The optimal $\mu_k$ is the arithmetic mean, obtained by setting $\partial J_k / \partial \mu_k = 0$.

**Complexity:** $\mathcal{O}(N \cdot K \cdot I \cdot d)$, where $I$ = iterations and $d$ = dimensions.

**Limitations:** Sensitive to initialization, assumes spherical clusters, requires $K$ to be specified, sensitive to outliers.

#### Model Solution — (b)

**Initial Setup:** $X = \{2, 4, 10, 12, 14, 20, 22\}$, $\mu_1^{(0)} = 2$, $\mu_2^{(0)} = 20$.

**Iteration 1 — Assignment:**

| Point $x_i$ | $\|x_i - \mu_1\|$ | $\|x_i - \mu_2\|$ | Closer to | Cluster |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 2 | 0 | 18 | $\mu_1$ | $C_1$ |
| 4 | 2 | 16 | $\mu_1$ | $C_1$ |
| 10 | 8 | 10 | $\mu_1$ | $C_1$ |
| 12 | 10 | 8 | $\mu_2$ | $C_2$ |
| 14 | 12 | 6 | $\mu_2$ | $C_2$ |
| 20 | 18 | 0 | $\mu_2$ | $C_2$ |
| 22 | 20 | 2 | $\mu_2$ | $C_2$ |

**Iteration 1 — Update:**
$$
\mu_1^{(1)} = \frac{2 + 4 + 10}{3} = \frac{16}{3} \approx 5.333
$$
$$
\mu_2^{(1)} = \frac{12 + 14 + 20 + 22}{4} = \frac{68}{4} = 17.0
$$

**Iteration 1 — WCSS:**
$$
J_1 = (2 - 5.333)^2 + (4 - 5.333)^2 + (10 - 5.333)^2
$$
$$
J_1 = 11.11 + 1.78 + 21.78 = 34.67
$$
$$
J_2 = (12 - 17)^2 + (14 - 17)^2 + (20 - 17)^2 + (22 - 17)^2
$$
$$
J_2 = 25 + 9 + 9 + 25 = 68
$$
$$
J_{\text{total}}^{(1)} = 34.67 + 68 = 102.67
$$

**Iteration 2 — Assignment (re-evaluate with new centroids):**

| Point $x_i$ | $\|x_i - 5.333\|$ | $\|x_i - 17\|$ | Closer to | Cluster |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 2 | 3.333 | 15 | $\mu_1$ | $C_1$ |
| 4 | 1.333 | 13 | $\mu_1$ | $C_1$ |
| 10 | 4.667 | 7 | $\mu_1$ | $C_1$ |
| 12 | 6.667 | 5 | $\mu_2$ | $C_2$ |
| 14 | 8.667 | 3 | $\mu_2$ | $C_2$ |
| 20 | 14.667 | 3 | $\mu_2$ | $C_2$ |
| 22 | 16.667 | 5 | $\mu_2$ | $C_2$ |

**Iteration 2 — Update:** Assignments unchanged, so centroids remain the same. **Converged.**

**Iteration 2 — WCSS:**
$$
J_1 = (2 - 5.333)^2 + (4 - 5.333)^2 + (10 - 5.333)^2 = 11.11 + 1.78 + 21.78 = 34.67
$$
$$
J_2 = (12 - 17)^2 + (14 - 17)^2 + (20 - 17)^2 + (22 - 17)^2 = 25 + 9 + 9 + 25 = 68
$$
$$
J_{\text{total}}^{(2)} = 34.67 + 68 = 102.67
$$

**Result Summary:**

| Iteration | $\mu_1$ | $\mu_2$ | $J_{\text{total}}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 2.000 | 20.000 | — |
| 1 | 5.333 | 17.000 | **102.67** |
| 2 | 5.333 | 17.000 | **102.67** (converged) |

**Final Clusters:** $C_1 = \{2, 4, 10\}$, $C_2 = \{12, 14, 20, 22\}$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Typically Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing Precision and Recall**: Precision uses **column** totals (predicted); Recall uses **row** totals (actual). Mixing these up costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting units or ranges**: Always mention that F1 ∈ [0, 1] and that K-Means WCSS is *non-negative*.
> 3. **Skipping the convergence criterion**: Students often stop the algorithm at iteration 1 without checking if centroids have stabilized — a 1-mark deduction is common.
> 4. **Not defining variables**: In derivations, if you write $J_k$ without explaining it, you lose a half-mark.
> 5. **Using odd $K$ in K-NN for binary tasks without justification**: Always state the reason (avoids ties).
> 6. **Missing the Silhouette range**: $s(i) \in [-1, +1]$ — the closed interval is often forgotten.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 3: Basics of Classification & Clustering)**

- ⭐ **Classification = Supervised**; **Clustering = Unsupervised** — the *labels-required* axis is the master differentiator.
- ⭐ **K-NN** classifies by majority vote of the $K$ nearest neighbors using a distance metric (Euclidean is default).
- ⭐ **K-Means** minimizes the **WCSS** objective: $J = \sum_k \sum_{i \in C_k} \|x_i - \mu_k\|^2$, with centroids updated as cluster means.
- ⭐ **Two-step loop** of K-Means: (1) Assignment, (2) Update — repeat until convergence.
- ⭐ **K-Means++** initialization (smart seeding) reduces sensitivity to bad initial centroids.
- ⭐ **Confusion matrix** terminology: **TP, TN, FP, FN** — know cold.
- ⭐ **Accuracy** alone is misleading for imbalanced datasets — always quote **Precision, Recall, F1** together.
- ⭐ **F1-Score** is the harmonic mean of Precision and Recall — it penalizes extreme imbalance between P and R.
- ⭐ **Silhouette coefficient** ranges in $[-1, +1]$; values near $+1$ indicate well-clustered samples.
- ⭐ **Davies–Bouldin index** is *lower-is-better*; it measures average cluster similarity.
- ⭐ **Distance metrics** — Euclidean ($L_2$), Manhattan ($L_1$), Minkowski (generalized), Cosine, Mahalanobis (covariance-aware).
- ⭐ **Odd $K$** in K-NN for binary tasks avoids tie-breaks.
- ⭐ **Feature scaling** (standardization/normalization) is mandatory for distance-based algorithms.
- ⭐ **Curse of dimensionality**: as $d$ grows, all points become roughly equidistant — distance metrics lose discriminative power.
- ⭐ **Limitations of K-Means**: assumes spherical, equal-sized clusters; sensitive to outliers; requires pre-specified $K$.
- ⭐ **KTU 2024 standard answer length**: Part A = 3–4 lines; Part B = 1.5–2 pages with at least one diagram, one table, and one derivation.
- ⭐ **Common 14-mark structure**: (a) Theory with diagram [7] + (b) Numerical/proof [7].
- ⭐ **Final mantra**: *Labels present → Classification; Labels absent → Clustering.* Carry this to every exam.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of Computer Vision, the **Multi-Class Perspective** refers to the formal extension of binary classification paradigms to recognition problems where an input image (or region of interest) must be assigned to one of **K ≥ 3** mutually exclusive categorical labels drawn from a fixed label set. Mathematically, given a feature vector $x \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ drawn from an unknown joint distribution $P(x, y)$, the goal is to learn a decision function $f : \mathbb{R}^{d} \rightarrow \{1, 2, \dots, K\}$ such that the expected risk $R(f) = \mathbb{E}[\mathbb{1}\{f(x) \neq y\}]$ is minimized.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — PECST745 Module 3:**
> The Multi-Class Perspective is the bridge between classical binary vision classifiers (e.g., face-vs-non-face) and modern real-world recognition systems (e.g., classifying 1,000 ImageNet categories). Every KTU board question from this module directly tests whether the student can *decompose* a multi-class problem into well-defined sub-problems.

## 1.2 Binary vs. Multi-Class Classification

| Property | Binary Classification | Multi-Class Classification |
|---|---|---|
| Number of classes $K$ | $K = 2$ | $K \geq 3$ |
| Label set $\mathcal{Y}$ | $\{-1, +1\}$ or $\{0, 1\}$ | $\{1, 2, \dots, K\}$ |
| Output layer | 1 neuron (sigmoid) | $K$ neurons (softmax) |
| Loss function | Binary Cross-Entropy | Categorical Cross-Entropy |
| Decision boundary | Single hyperplane | Piecewise-linear / polyhedral |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Postal Sorting Office

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Intuition:**
> Imagine you are the head of a postal sorting office in Kerala. A truck arrives carrying thousands of letters. Your job is to send each letter to one of **10** district post offices (Thiruvananthapuram, Kochi, Kozhikode, …).
>
> - **Binary View:** You would need to ask 9 yes/no questions per letter: "Is this for TVM? No. Is this for KCH? No. …" This is the **One-vs-All** strategy.
> - **Multi-Class View:** You look at the **pincode printed in one corner** of the letter (the discriminative feature) and route it directly. This is the **direct multi-class (softmax)** strategy.
> - **Pairwise View:** You station **45** (=$10 \times 9 / 2$) workers, each an expert in distinguishing just *two* districts, and poll them all. This is the **One-vs-One** strategy.

The Multi-Class Perspective is precisely the engineering discipline of choosing **which sorting architecture** to deploy for a given vision task.

## 1.4 The K ≥ 3 Constraint and Why It Matters

When $K=2$, a single discriminant $g(x) = w^T x + b$ suffices because the two classes are perfectly separated by the sign of $g(x)$. For $K \geq 3$, a single hyperplane is **topologically insufficient** — three classes may require a triangular decision region (Voronoi cell) that cannot be carved out by one linear separator. This is the geometric root of all multi-class machinery.

![Geometric Intuition: Single hyperplane is enough for K=2, but K=3 requires 3 hyperplanes to partition the plane into 7 regions (3 class regions + 4 ambiguous regions)]

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Canonical Multi-Class Strategies

### 2.1.1 One-vs-All (OvA) / One-vs-Rest (OvR)

- **Core Idea:** Train **K** independent binary classifiers. The $k$-th classifier $f_k(x)$ separates class $k$ (positive) from all other classes (negative).
- **Decision Rule:** $\hat{y} = \arg\max_{k \in \{1,\dots,K\}} f_k(x)$.
- **Why It Works for Vision:** Extremely simple to implement on top of any existing binary SVM or logistic regression pipeline; well-suited for object detection where "background" is treated as the "rest" class.
- **Limitation:** Suffers from **class imbalance** — the "rest" class has $K-1$ times more samples than each individual class. Also ambiguous when two classifiers produce high but unequal confidences.

### 2.1.2 One-vs-One (OvO)

- **Core Idea:** Train $\binom{K}{2} = \dfrac{K(K-1)}{2}$ binary classifiers, one for every unordered pair of classes.
- **Decision Rule:** Use majority voting (a.k.a. "Max-Wins" voting). Each classifier casts one vote; the class with the most votes wins.
- **Why It Works for Vision:** Each sub-problem is **balanced** (only two classes of roughly equal size) and **smaller**, so training is fast. Popular with kernel SVMs because each pairwise problem is cheaper than the full K-class problem.
- **Limitation:** Training cost grows quadratically in $K$. For ImageNet ($K=1000$), this gives ~500,000 classifiers — computationally prohibitive without parallelization.

### 2.1.3 Direct Multi-Class (Single-Stage / Softmax Regression)

- **Core Idea:** Train **one** unified $K$-class discriminant jointly. The most common form is **Multinomial Logistic Regression (Softmax Regression)**, which is the natural multi-class generalization of logistic regression.
- **Decision Rule:** Compute $K$ scores $s_k(x) = w_k^T x + b_k$, apply the **softmax** function to get a probability vector $p(k \vert x)$, and pick the argmax.
- **Why It Works for Vision:** End-to-end differentiable, integrates seamlessly with deep CNN backbones, and is the de-facto standard in modern deep learning classifiers (ResNet, VGG, EfficientNet).

## 2.2 Softmax Regression — The Workhorse of Modern Vision

The **softmax function** maps $K$ real-valued scores to a valid probability distribution:

$$\sigma(z)_i = \frac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{z_j}}$$

It is a smoothed (differentiable) approximation of the **argmax** function, and it is invariant to additive constants in the input vector: $\sigma(z + c) = \sigma(z)$ for any scalar $c$. This invariance is what makes training stable.

The training objective is the **Categorical Cross-Entropy Loss** (a.k.a. Multinomial Logistic Loss):

$$\mathcal{L}_{CE}(W, b) = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{k=1}^{K} y_{ik} \, \log \hat{p}_{ik}$$

where $y_{ik} \in \{0, 1\}$ is the one-hot encoded true label and $\hat{p}_{ik}$ is the predicted softmax probability.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **only** reference you need during the KTU ESE for this topic. Memorize the boxed quantities.

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Notation Used | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Number of OvO classifiers | $\binom{K}{2} = \frac{K(K-1)}{2}$ | $K$ = num. of classes | Capacity planning for pairwise ensembles |
| 2 | OvA imbalance ratio | $\rho = \frac{N - N_k}{N_k}$ | $N_k$ = samples in class $k$ | Determines need for class-weighted loss |
| 3 | Softmax function | $\sigma(z)_i = \frac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{z_j}}$ | $z \in \mathbb{R}^{K}$ | Differentiable argmax; output layer of CNNs |
| 4 | Softmax shift-trick | $\sigma(z)_i = \frac{e^{z_i - c}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{z_j - c}}$ | $c = \max_j z_j$ | Numerical stability (avoids overflow) |
| 5 | Categorical Cross-Entropy | $\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{k=1}^{K} y_k \log \hat{p}_k$ | $y_k$ one-hot, $\hat{p}_k$ softmax | Loss for $K$-class image classification |
| 6 | Multiclass hinge loss (Crammer-Singer) | $\mathcal{L} = \max\!\big(0, 1 + \max_{j \neq y} s_j - s_y\big)$ | $s_j$ = class score | Used by multi-class SVMs |
| 7 | One-Hot Encoding | $y_k = 1$ if $k = y^{true}$, else $0$ | $y^{true} \in \{1,\dots,K\}$ | Target representation for softmax |
| 8 | Confusion Matrix dimension | $K \times K$ matrix $C$ with $C_{ij} = \vert \{x : y = i, \hat{y} = j\} \vert$ | Diag. = correct | Full multi-class error diagnostic |
| 9 | Per-class Precision | $P_k = \frac{C_{kk}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} C_{jk}}$ | $k$-th column sum = predicted $k$ | Filter false positives per class |
| 10 | Per-class Recall | $R_k = \frac{C_{kk}}{\sum_{i=1}^{K} C_{ik}}$ | $k$-th row sum = actual $k$ | Filter false negatives per class |
| 11 | Macro F1-score | $F_1^{macro} = \frac{1}{K} \sum_{k=1}^{K} \frac{2 P_k R_k}{P_k + R_k}$ | Unweighted mean | Treats all classes equally |
| 12 | Weighted F1-score | $F_1^{wtd} = \sum_{k=1}^{K} w_k F_{1,k}$, $w_k = N_k / N$ | Weighted by support | Reflects imbalanced datasets |
| 13 | Top-5 Accuracy (Vision) | $\text{Top-5} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i} \mathbb{1}\{y_i \in \text{Top5}(\hat{p}_i)\}$ | $\text{Top5}$ = five highest probs | Standard ImageNet metric |
| 14 | Cohen's Kappa | $\kappa = \frac{p_o - p_e}{1 - p_e}$ | $p_o$ = observed, $p_e$ = chance | Agreement corrected for chance |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Medical Imaging:** Classifying skin lesions into melanoma vs. basal cell carcinoma vs. benign nevus vs. dermatofibroma (**K=7**, ISIC dataset) — relies on macro F1 due to class rarity.
- **Autonomous Driving:** Traffic sign recognition (stop, yield, speed limit 20/40/60, …) — uses **hierarchical multi-class** (first coarse category, then fine).
- **Agricultural Vision:** Detecting 38 cucumber leaf diseases for Kerala polyhouse farmers — soft cross-entropy with label smoothing.
- **Industrial Quality Control:** Defect classification into 12 categories on a moving conveyor — OvO is preferred for fast retraining.

## 2.5 Why Multi-Class is Harder — The Curse of Dimensionality in the Output Space

As $K$ grows:
1. **Combinatorial explosion** of decision boundaries (number of regions in a Voronoi tessellation of $K$ point-sets).
2. **Data hunger:** Each class needs enough samples to characterize its distribution; total dataset size scales roughly as $\mathcal{O}(K \cdot d / \epsilon^2)$ for generalization error $\epsilon$.
3. **Confusion matrix sparsity:** With $K=1000$ (ImageNet), only ~0.1% of off-diagonal entries are statistically meaningful from 1.28M images.
4. **Error propagation:** A single upstream feature-extraction error can confound $K$ downstream classes instead of just 1.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From Logistic to Softmax (Multinomial Logistic Regression)

We start from the assumption that the class-conditional distribution is drawn from the **multinoulli (categorical)** distribution. By Bayes' theorem:

$$P(y = k \mid x) = \frac{P(x \mid y = k) \, P(y = k)}{P(x)}$$

We model $\log P(x \mid y = k)$ as a linear function of $x$ (generative linear assumption):

$$\log P(x \mid y = k) = w_k^T x + b_k + \text{const}$$

Substituting into Bayes' rule and absorbing the normalizer into a softmax:

$$P(y = k \mid x) = \frac{\exp(w_k^T x + b_k)}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} \exp(w_j^T x + b_j)}$$

This is precisely the **softmax regression** model. We can stack all $K$ weight vectors into a matrix $W \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times K}$ and all $K$ biases into a vector $b \in \mathbb{R}^{K}$, giving the compact form:

$$P(y = k \mid x) = \frac{\exp((W^T x + b)_k)}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} \exp((W^T x + b)_j)}$$

**Step-by-step interpretation:**
1. We replace the logistic sigmoid $\sigma(z) = 1/(1+e^{-z})$ with the softmax, which generalizes it to $K$ classes.
2. The denominator $\sum_j e^{z_j}$ enforces that the $K$ outputs sum to **1** (a valid probability simplex).
3. The model is **linear in the parameters $W, b$** but produces **piecewise-linear decision boundaries** in the input space.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Gradient of the Cross-Entropy Loss w.r.t. the Pre-Activation Scores

Let $z_k = w_k^T x + b_k$ and $\hat{p}_k = \sigma(z)_k$. The loss for a single sample is:

$$\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{k=1}^{K} y_k \log \hat{p}_k$$

Differentiate w.r.t. $z_i$:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z_i} = -\sum_{k=1}^{K} y_k \frac{1}{\hat{p}_k} \frac{\partial \hat{p}_k}{\partial z_i}$$

The derivative of the softmax has two cases:

- **Case A:** $k = i$. Then $\frac{\partial \hat{p}_i}{\partial z_i} = \hat{p}_i (1 - \hat{p}_i)$.
- **Case B:** $k \neq i$. Then $\frac{\partial \hat{p}_k}{\partial z_i} = -\hat{p}_k \hat{p}_i$.

Substituting:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z_i} = -y_i \frac{1}{\hat{p}_i} \cdot \hat{p}_i(1-\hat{p}_i) - \sum_{k \neq i} y_k \frac{1}{\hat{p}_k} \cdot (-\hat{p}_k \hat{p}_i)$$

$$= -y_i (1 - \hat{p}_i) + \sum_{k \neq i} y_k \hat{p}_i$$

$$= -y_i + y_i \hat{p}_i + \hat{p}_i \sum_{k \neq i} y_k$$

$$= -y_i + \hat{p}_i \sum_{k=1}^{K} y_k$$

Since $\sum_{k=1}^{K} y_k = 1$ (one-hot encoding), we get the celebrated result:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z_i} = \hat{p}_i - y_i$$

**Beautiful Insight:** The gradient of cross-entropy with softmax is simply **predicted probability minus true label**. This is why softmax + cross-entropy is the canonical pairing — the gradient is the error signal, and the chain rule produces clean backpropagation.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — OvA Decision Rule from a Probabilistic View

Given $K$ binary classifiers with outputs $f_k(x) \in [0, 1]$ estimating $P(y = k \vert x)$ under the OvA assumption, the natural decision rule is:

$$\hat{y} = \arg\max_{k} f_k(x)$$

The implicit assumption is that the $K$ classifiers are **conditionally independent** given $x$, which is false in practice but works well empirically. The optimality condition is that the chosen $k$ maximizes the **posterior**:

$$f_k(x) \approx P(y = k \vert x) \propto P(x \vert y=k) P(y=k)$$

For the rule to recover the true Bayes-optimal classifier, we need:

$$\forall k, j : \quad P(y = k \vert x) > P(y = j \vert x) \iff f_k(x) > f_j(x)$$

In practice, because each $f_k$ is trained on a *different* class distribution, the scales of $f_k$ are not calibrated across $k$, leading to ambiguous regions where two $f_k$ are both > 0.5. **Calibration techniques** (Platt scaling, isotonic regression) are often needed.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Majority-Vote Tie-Breaking in OvO

With $K$ classes, there are $M = \binom{K}{2}$ pairwise classifiers. Let $v_k$ be the number of votes received by class $k$. The vote count is:

$$v_k = \sum_{j \neq k} \mathbb{1}\{f_{kj}(x) = k\}$$

where $f_{kj}$ is the classifier trained to distinguish $k$ from $j$. Since each pairwise contest involves exactly one vote, $\sum_k v_k = M$.

The decision rule $\hat{y} = \arg\max_k v_k$ can produce a **tie** when $K$ is even (e.g., $K=4$ gives 6 votes; a 3–3 tie is possible). Tie-breaking strategies:
1. Use the **sum of pairwise confidence scores** (Max-Wins with confidence weighting).
2. Use a **directed acyclic graph (DAG)** of classifiers — eliminates the need for all $\binom{K}{2}$ evaluations at test time.
3. Fall back to a tie-breaking rule based on a priori class frequencies.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Strategy | # of Models | Training Cost | Test Cost | Class Imbalance | Calibration Needed | KTU Board Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **OvA** | $K$ | $\mathcal{O}(K \cdot C_{bin})$ | $\mathcal{O}(K)$ | **Severe** (1 vs. K-1) | High | **Very High** |
| **OvO** | $\frac{K(K-1)}{2}$ | $\mathcal{O}(K^2 \cdot C_{bin})$ | $\mathcal{O}(K^2)$ or $\mathcal{O}(K)$ with DAG | **None** (1 vs. 1) | Low | **High** |
| **Softmax (direct)** | $1$ | $\mathcal{O}(C_{multi})$ | $\mathcal{O}(K)$ | Inherently balanced via loss weighting | Built-in (probabilities) | **Very High** |
| **ECOC (Error-Correcting)** | $L \geq \log_2 K$ | $\mathcal{O}(L \cdot C_{bin})$ | $\mathcal{O}(L)$ | Mild (per bit) | Required | **Medium** |
| **Hierarchical** | $\propto K$ (tree) | $\mathcal{O}(K \cdot \log K \cdot C_{bin})$ | $\mathcal{O}(\log K)$ | Depends on tree | Required | **Medium** |

*(where $C_{bin}$ is the cost of training one binary classifier)*

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Question:** A vision system classifies wildlife camera-trap images into 4 classes: *Tiger, Elephant, Deer, Boar*. The 4 OvA classifiers produce scores: $f_1 = 0.7$ (Tiger), $f_2 = 0.4$ (Elephant), $f_3 = 0.6$ (Deer), $f_4 = 0.5$ (Boar). What is the OvA decision? What does it imply?

**Solution:**
- Apply $\hat{y} = \arg\max_k f_k(x) = \arg\max(0.7, 0.4, 0.6, 0.5) = 1$.
- The image is classified as **Tiger**.
- **Note:** $f_1 + f_2 + f_3 + f_4 = 2.2 \neq 1$, confirming these are *uncalibrated* scores, not probabilities. This is a common OvA pitfall.
- **Improvement:** Apply Platt scaling or convert to a calibrated softmax: $\hat{p}_k = f_k / \sum_j f_j$, giving $\hat{p} = (0.318, 0.182, 0.273, 0.227)$. Tiger is still the argmax, but the margin over Deer has narrowed meaningfully.

## 4.5 Decision Region Topology — K=3 Toy Example

> **Note on Geometric Visualization:** A complete geometric figure of the Voronoi tessellation of the 2D plane by 3 linear classifiers cannot be rendered directly in Mermaid (it would require coordinate plotting). Instead, the following **functional block representation** shows how 3 hyperplanes partition the 2D plane:

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[PLANEXY] --> P1[HYP12]
    A --> P2[HYP13]
    A --> P3[HYP23]
    P1 --> R1[REGION1]
    P2 --> R2[REGION2]
    P3 --> R3[REGION3]
    P1 --> R12[AMBIG12]
    P2 --> R13[AMBIG13]
    P3 --> R23[AMBIG23]
    R1 --> F[CLSASSIGN]
    R2 --> F
    R3 --> F
    R12 --> F
    R13 --> F
    R23 --> F

    style F fill:#c8e6c9
```

**Node ID Legend:**
- `PLANEXY` = The 2D input feature space $\mathbb{R}^2$
- `HYP12`, `HYP13`, `HYP23` = Three pairwise linear hyperplanes
- `REGION1`, `REGION2`, `REGION3` = Confidently-assigned class regions
- `AMBIG12`, `AMBIG13`, `AMBIG23` = Ambiguous boundary regions
- `CLSASSIGN` = Final classifier output $f(x)$

**Engineering Insight:** Even in this toy case, 3 hyperplanes create up to $\frac{K(K+1)}{2} + 1 = 7$ distinct regions. Of these, exactly $K=3$ are pure-class regions, and the remaining 4 are ambiguous. This is why a **majority vote / argmax** rule is essential — the regions are not unique to one class.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, PECST745]
**Differentiate between One-vs-All and One-vs-One strategies for multi-class classification. Which one is preferred when classes are highly imbalanced and why?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | One-vs-All (OvA) | One-vs-One (OvO) |
|---|---|---|
| # Classifiers | $K$ | $\binom{K}{2} = \frac{K(K-1)}{2}$ |
| Class Imbalance | Severe (1 vs. $K-1$) | None (1 vs. 1) |

**When classes are highly imbalanced, OvO is preferred** (1.5 marks) because in OvA, the majority class dominates the "rest" super-class, causing the minority positive class to be overwhelmed. In OvO, each binary problem is between two classes of comparable size, so the imbalance is naturally neutralized (1.5 marks).

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PECST745]
**State the softmax function. Why is the "max-shift" trick $\sigma(z_i) = e^{z_i - c}/\sum_j e^{z_j - c}$ used in practice?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Softmax definition** (1.5 marks): $\sigma(z)_i = \frac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{z_j}}$ — converts $K$ real-valued scores into a valid probability distribution that sums to 1.
- **Max-shift trick rationale** (1.5 marks): With large positive $z_i$, the exponential $e^{z_i}$ overflows floating-point representation (IEEE 754 max $\approx 1.8 \times 10^{308}$). Subtracting $c = \max_j z_j$ ensures the largest exponent is $e^0 = 1$, while preserving the output exactly (since $\sigma(z + c) = \sigma(z)$ for additive constants).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Comprehensive Multi-Class Strategy Analysis

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, PECST745 Module 3]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain in detail the One-vs-All (OvA) and One-vs-One (OvO) approaches for multi-class classification. Compare their computational complexity, training time, and test-time behavior for a problem with $K = 10$ classes.**

**Model Solution:**

**One-vs-All (OvA):**
- Train $K$ binary classifiers, each separating one class from the remaining $K-1$ classes (2 marks).
- The $k$-th classifier is trained with positive samples = class $k$, negative samples = all other classes.
- Decision rule: $\hat{y} = \arg\max_{k \in \{1,\dots,K\}} f_k(x)$ (1 mark).
- **For $K=10$:** Number of classifiers = $K = 10$. Training time $\approx 10 \cdot T_{bin}$. Test time = $O(K) = O(10)$ (1 mark).

**One-vs-One (OvO):**
- Train $\binom{K}{2} = \frac{K(K-1)}{2}$ binary classifiers, one per unordered pair of classes (1.5 marks).
- Decision rule: majority voting (a.k.a. Max-Wins) — each classifier votes for one of its two classes; the class with the most votes is the prediction (0.5 mark).
- **For $K=10$:** Number of classifiers = $\frac{10 \times 9}{2} = 45$. Training time $\approx 45 \cdot T_{bin}$. Test time = $O(45)$ naively, reducible to $O(K) = O(10)$ with a DAG-based evaluation (1 mark).

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Derive the gradient of the categorical cross-entropy loss w.r.t. the pre-activation scores for a softmax classifier. Show that $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z_i} = \hat{p}_i - y_i$.**

**Model Solution:**

Starting from $\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{k=1}^{K} y_k \log \hat{p}_k$ and $\hat{p}_k = \frac{e^{z_k}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{z_j}}$:

**Step 1:** Differentiate w.r.t. $z_i$ (1 mark):
$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z_i} = -\sum_{k=1}^{K} y_k \frac{1}{\hat{p}_k} \frac{\partial \hat{p}_k}{\partial z_i}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the softmax derivative for the two cases (2 marks):
- If $k = i$: $\frac{\partial \hat{p}_i}{\partial z_i} = \hat{p}_i (1 - \hat{p}_i)$
- If $k \neq i$: $\frac{\partial \hat{p}_k}{\partial z_i} = -\hat{p}_k \hat{p}_i$

**Step 3:** Substitute and simplify (2 marks):
$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z_i} = -y_i (1 - \hat{p}_i) + \sum_{k \neq i} y_k \hat{p}_i = -y_i + \hat{p}_i \sum_{k=1}^{K} y_k$$

**Step 4:** Use the one-hot property $\sum_k y_k = 1$ to obtain (2 marks):
$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z_i} = \hat{p}_i - y_i \quad \blacksquare$$

**Significance:** The gradient is the difference between prediction and ground truth, making the cross-entropy + softmax combination the canonical pairing for multi-class classification in deep networks.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Long Answer Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, PECST745 Module 3]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**For a multi-class image classification system with 5 classes and a $K \times K$ confusion matrix, derive the expressions for (i) per-class precision, (ii) per-class recall, (iii) macro F1-score, and (iv) Cohen's Kappa coefficient. Why is macro F1 preferred over accuracy for imbalanced datasets?**

**Model Solution:**

Let the confusion matrix be $C \in \mathbb{R}^{K \times K}$ with $C_{ij}$ = number of samples with true class $i$ predicted as class $j$.

**(i) Per-class precision** (1.5 marks): Precision for class $k$ is the fraction of *correct* predictions among all *predicted* class $k$ samples:
$$P_k = \frac{C_{kk}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} C_{jk}}$$

**(ii) Per-class recall** (1.5 marks): Recall for class $k$ is the fraction of *correct* predictions among all *actual* class $k$ samples:
$$R_k = \frac{C_{kk}}{\sum_{i=1}^{K} C_{ik}}$$

**(iii) Macro F1-score** (2 marks): Unweighted mean of per-class F1 scores:
$$F_1^{macro} = \frac{1}{K} \sum_{k=1}^{K} \frac{2 P_k R_k}{P_k + R_k}$$

**(iv) Cohen's Kappa** (2 marks): Agreement corrected for chance:
$$\kappa = \frac{p_o - p_e}{1 - p_e}, \quad p_o = \frac{\sum_k C_{kk}}{N}, \quad p_e = \frac{1}{N^2} \sum_k \left(\sum_i C_{ik}\right)\left(\sum_j C_{kj}\right)$$

**Why macro F1 over accuracy for imbalance** (additional explanatory credit): Accuracy weights each sample equally, so a classifier that always predicts the majority class can score 95% accuracy on a 95:5 imbalanced split while being useless. Macro F1 weights each class equally, forcing the classifier to perform well on the minority class as well (1 mark for the explanation if asked beyond the 7 marks; included as bonus context).

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Explain Error-Correcting Output Codes (ECOC) as a multi-class strategy. How does it combine ideas from OvA and OvO? Illustrate with a 4-class example showing the codebook matrix.**

**Model Solution:**

**ECOC Concept** (2.5 marks): ECOC encodes each of the $K$ classes as a unique binary codeword of length $L$. We train $L$ binary classifiers, one per bit. At test time, we predict the class whose codeword has the **smallest Hamming distance** to the predicted bit-string.

**Connection to OvA and OvO** (2 marks):
- Each row of the codebook is a class codeword.
- Each column of the codebook defines a binary partition of the $K$ classes (1 = positive, -1 or 0 = negative).
- If columns are designed so that exactly one class is positive per column → **OvA**.
- If columns partition the $K$ classes into two equal halves → **OvO**-like.
- General ECOC allows *arbitrary* partitions, providing redundancy that makes the system robust to individual classifier errors (error-correcting property).

**4-Class Codebook Example** (2.5 marks):

| Class | $f_1$ | $f_2$ | $f_3$ | $f_4$ | $f_5$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| C1 (Tiger)   | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 |
| C2 (Elephant) | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 |
| C3 (Deer)     | 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 1 |
| C4 (Boar)     | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 |

- $L = 5$ binary classifiers are trained, each implementing one column's binary partition.
- Suppose at test time a sample produces the bit-string $(1, 1, 0, 1, 1)$. Compute Hamming distances:
  - $d(\text{C1}) = 0$
  - $d(\text{C2}) = 3$
  - $d(\text{C3}) = 1$
  - $d(\text{C4}) = 2$
- Class is **C1 (Tiger)** with Hamming distance 0.

**Key Engineering Insight:** With $L = \log_2 K$ bits, ECOC is information-theoretically minimal. With $L \gg \log_2 K$, ECOC provides redundancy and error-correction — even if 1–2 classifiers err, the correct class can still be identified.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks on Multi-Class Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the numerical-stability shift trick** in softmax — examiners deduct 1 mark if $c = \max z_j$ is not mentioned. (Tested: Dec 2023)
> 2. **Confusing OvA's $K$ classifiers with OvO's $\binom{K}{2}$** — a common slip. Always state the formula *and* the numerical value for the given $K$.
> 3. **Skipping the one-hot constraint $\sum_k y_k = 1$** in the cross-entropy gradient derivation — this step is worth 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Writing "multi-class SVM" without specifying the formulation** — either Crammer-Singer (single-machine) or Weston-Watkins (per-pair) must be named.
> 5. **Reporting only accuracy for imbalanced datasets** — examiners expect macro F1 or per-class metrics.
> 6. **Not tying the theory back to a vision application** — every answer should mention an image-recognition context (e.g., MNIST, CIFAR-10, ImageNet) to score the application-level marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Multi-Class Perspective):**
> - [ ] Multi-class classification assigns an input to **one of $K \geq 3$ mutually exclusive labels**.
> - [ ] Three canonical strategies: **OvA, OvO, Direct (Softmax)**.
> - [ ] OvA trains **$K$** binary classifiers; OvO trains **$\binom{K}{2}$**; Softmax trains **1** multi-output model.
> - [ ] Softmax formula: $\sigma(z)_i = \frac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{z_j}}$; always use the **max-shift** trick $c = \max_j z_j$ for numerical stability.
> - [ ] Categorical Cross-Entropy: $\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{k=1}^{K} y_k \log \hat{p}_k$ is the canonical loss for softmax.
> - [ ] **Magic gradient:** $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z_i} = \hat{p}_i - y_i$ — derive this; do not memorize it blindly.
> - [ ] Confusion matrix is **$K \times K$**; per-class precision/recall are computed column-wise and row-wise respectively.
> - [ ] Use **macro F1** for imbalanced classes; use **Top-5 accuracy** for large-$K$ benchmarks like ImageNet.
> - [ ] **ECOC** combines OvA and OvO by allowing arbitrary binary partitions; provides error-correction via Hamming-distance decoding.
> - [ ] **Tie-breaking in OvO** is necessary when $K$ is even; fallback strategies: confidence-weighted voting, DAG, prior-frequency.
> - [ ] In KTU answers, always **anchor the theory to a vision task** (object recognition, medical imaging, traffic signs, etc.) to score full marks.
> - [ ] Final mental image: the **postal sorting office analogy** — three architectures (OvA, OvO, Direct) correspond to three different ways of organizing the sorters.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Machine Learning for Computer Vision - Machine Learning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST745 - COMPUTER VISION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Machine Learning for Computer Vision - Machine Learning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Machine Learning (ML)** is a subfield of artificial intelligence that enables computational systems to learn patterns, relationships, and decision boundaries directly from data, without being explicitly programmed with hand-crafted rules. In the context of **Computer Vision (CV)**, Machine Learning provides the statistical and mathematical framework that allows a system to interpret, classify, detect, segment, and understand images and videos by learning from annotated or unannotated visual examples.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot:**
> Machine Learning for Computer Vision is the discipline of designing algorithms that automatically infer visual concepts (objects, scenes, actions) by optimizing a model $\mathcal{M}$ on a training dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$, where $x_i$ is an image (or feature vector) and $y_i$ is the corresponding label, target, or annotation.

Formally, the ML problem is to find a function (hypothesis) $h_{\theta} : \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$, parameterized by $\theta$, that minimizes an expected risk:

$$R(h_{\theta}) = \mathbb{E}_{(x,y) \sim P_{XY}} \left[ \mathcal{L}(h_{\theta}(x),\, y) \right]$$

where $\mathcal{L}$ is a **loss function** and $P_{XY}$ is the joint data distribution. Since $P_{XY}$ is unknown, we minimize the **empirical risk**:

$$\hat{R}(h_{\theta}) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathcal{L}\left(h_{\theta}(x_i),\, y_i\right)$$

## 1.2 Why Machine Learning for Computer Vision?

Classical CV relied on hand-crafted features (SIFT, HOG, LBP) plus shallow classifiers. Modern CV uses **data-driven learning** to:
- Handle intra-class variability (illumination, pose, occlusion).
- Scale to thousands of object categories.
- Learn hierarchical representations automatically (feature learning).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECST745 Module 3):**
> The 2024 scheme emphasizes the **three ML paradigms** (Supervised, Unsupervised, Reinforcement), the role of **feature extraction**, classical ML algorithms (k-NN, SVM, Decision Trees, Ensemble methods), and the **transition to neural networks / deep learning** which prepares the ground for CNNs covered in subsequent modules.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy: "Teaching a Child to Recognise Cats"**
> - **Classical CV approach** = You write an exhaustive rulebook: "A cat has two pointy ears, four legs, whiskers, and fur." This breaks the moment a cat is sleeping, occluded, or a new breed appears.
> - **Machine Learning approach** = You show the child 10,000 pictures of cats and 10,000 pictures of non-cats, telling them "this is a cat / this is not a cat." The child's brain (the model) gradually extracts the *statistical essence* of "cat-ness" — fuzzy textures, ear shape, facial geometry — and can then recognize cats it has never seen.
> - In ML terms: images = inputs $x_i$, labels ("cat"/"not cat") = outputs $y_i$, the brain = model $h_{\theta}$, and learning = adjusting $\theta$ to reduce mistakes on the 20,000 examples.

## 1.4 The Three Paradigms of Machine Learning

| Paradigm | Training Signal | Typical CV Tasks |
|----------|-----------------|------------------|
| **Supervised Learning** | Labeled pairs $(x, y)$ | Classification, Detection, Segmentation |
| **Unsupervised Learning** | Unlabeled data $x$ only | Clustering, Dimensionality Reduction, Feature Learning |
| **Reinforcement Learning** | Reward signals from environment | Active vision, Tracking, Game agents |

> [!NOTE]
> A fourth emerging paradigm, **Self-Supervised Learning (SSL)**, is now central to modern CV (e.g., DINO, MAE, SimCLR). It is mentioned in the 2024 syllabus as a bridge to deep learning topics.

## 1.5 The Standard ML-for-CV Pipeline

Regardless of the algorithm, every ML-based CV system follows this canonical pipeline:

1. **Data Acquisition** — collect images/videos.
2. **Annotation / Labeling** — attach ground-truth $y_i$ (supervised).
3. **Pre-processing** — resize, normalize, augment.
4. **Feature Extraction** — hand-crafted (HOG) or learned (CNN).
5. **Model Training** — optimize $\theta$ to minimize loss.
6. **Validation / Model Selection** — tune hyperparameters.
7. **Testing / Deployment** — evaluate on unseen data.
8. **Inference** — predict on new images.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Supervised Learning — Operational Decomposition

Supervised learning maps inputs $x \in \mathcal{X} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^{d}$ to outputs $y \in \mathcal{Y}$ using a training set $\mathcal{D} = \{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$.

**Sub-categories:**
- **Classification** — $y$ is discrete (cat, dog, car). Decision boundary: $h_{\theta}(x) = \arg\max_c P(y=c \mid x)$.
- **Regression** — $y$ is continuous (age, depth, bounding-box coordinates).

**Step-by-step operational logic:**

1. **Hypothesis selection** — Choose a parametric family $h_{\theta}(x)$ (linear, kernel-based, tree-based, neural).
2. **Loss definition** — Quantify the cost of a wrong prediction.
3. **Optimization** — Find $\theta^{\star} = \arg\min_{\theta} \hat{R}(h_{\theta})$ via gradient-based or combinatorial methods.
4. **Generalization check** — Evaluate on held-out data to ensure low test error.

## 2.2 Unsupervised Learning — Operational Decomposition

Unsupervised learning discovers structure in $x$ without labels. The two pillars used in CV are:

- **Clustering** — group similar images (K-Means, DBSCAN, Gaussian Mixtures).
- **Dimensionality Reduction** — project $x \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ to $z \in \mathbb{R}^{k}$, $k \ll d$ (PCA, t-SNE, Autoencoders).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Unsupervised methods are vital for **representation learning** in CV, where labelled image data is expensive but unlabelled images are abundant.

## 2.3 Reinforcement Learning (RL) for Vision

An **agent** interacts with a visual environment, observes state $s_t$ (an image), takes action $a_t$, and receives reward $r_t$. The objective is to learn a policy $\pi(a \mid s)$ maximizing expected cumulative reward:

$$J(\pi) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi} \left[ \sum_{t=0}^{T} \gamma^{t} r_{t} \right]$$

where $\gamma \in [0,1)$ is the **discount factor**. RL for CV is used in robotic vision, active tracking, and game-playing agents.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **frequently tested in KTU ESE** for Computer Vision / Machine Learning courses. Memorize the symbols, ranges, and units.

| Concept | Formula | Notation & Units |
|---------|---------|------------------|
| Empirical Risk | $\hat{R}(\theta) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathcal{L}(h_{\theta}(x_i), y_i)$ | Scalar, dimensionless |
| Mean Squared Error (MSE) | $\text{MSE} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ | Squared error units |
| Mean Absolute Error (MAE) | $\text{MAE} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \vert y_i - \hat{y}_i \vert$ | Same units as $y$ |
| Cross-Entropy Loss (binary) | $\mathcal{L} = -\frac{1}{N}\sum \left[y \log \hat{y} + (1-y)\log(1-\hat{y})\right]$ | Nats or bits |
| Cross-Entropy Loss (multi-class) | $\mathcal{L} = -\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i}\sum_{c} y_{i,c}\log \hat{y}_{i,c}$ | Nats or bits |
| Euclidean Distance (k-NN) | $d(x, x') = \sqrt{\sum_{j=1}^{d}(x_j - x'_j)^2}$ | Same units as $x$ |
| Manhattan Distance (k-NN) | $d(x, x') = \sum_{j=1}^{d} \vert x_j - x'_j \vert$ | Same units as $x$ |
| Accuracy | $\text{Acc} = \frac{TP+TN}{TP+TN+FP+FN}$ | Range [0, 1] |
| Precision | $\text{Prec} = \frac{TP}{TP+FP}$ | Range [0, 1] |
| Recall (Sensitivity / TPR) | $\text{Rec} = \frac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | Range [0, 1] |
| F1-Score | $F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot \text{Prec} \cdot \text{Rec}}{\text{Prec} + \text{Rec}}$ | Range [0, 1] |
| Specificity (TNR) | $\text{Spec} = \frac{TN}{TN+FP}$ | Range [0, 1] |
| PCA Reconstruction Error | $E = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i} \lVert x_i - \hat{x}_i \rVert_2^2$ | Squared distance |
| Explained Variance Ratio (k-th PC) | $\text{EVR}_k = \frac{\lambda_k}{\sum_{j=1}^{d}\lambda_j}$ | Range [0, 1] |
| Gradient Descent Update | $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \nabla_{\theta}\mathcal{L}(\theta_t)$ | $\eta$ = learning rate (positive scalar) |
| SVM Margin | $m = \frac{2}{\lVert w \rVert}$ | Geometric margin |
| Softmax (multi-class) | $\sigma(z)_c = \frac{e^{z_c}}{\sum_{j} e^{z_j}}$ | Probabilities in [0, 1] |
| Sigmoid (binary) | $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ | Range (0, 1) |
| Bias-Variance Decomposition | $\mathbb{E}[(y - \hat{f})^2] = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance} + \sigma^2$ | Sum of error terms |
| Discounted Return (RL) | $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k} r_{t+k}$ | Scalar, dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Every metric above is used in real CV systems. For example, in medical imaging (tumor detection), **Recall is critical** (we cannot miss a tumor), while in autonomous driving pedestrian detection, the **F1-score balances false alarms and missed detections**.

## 2.5 Bias-Variance Tradeoff — The Heart of Generalization

$$\underbrace{\mathbb{E}\left[(y - \hat{f}(x))^2\right]}_{\text{Expected Test Error}} = \underbrace{\text{Bias}^2(\hat{f})}_{\text{Underfitting}} + \underbrace{\text{Var}(\hat{f})}_{\text{Overfitting}} + \underbrace{\sigma^2}_{\text{Irreducible Noise}}$$

- **High Bias** → model too simple (e.g., linear classifier on non-linear data). **Underfitting**.
- **High Variance** → model too complex, sensitive to training noise. **Overfitting**.
- The sweet spot minimizes total error.

**Regularization** (L1, L2) controls the bias-variance tradeoff:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{reg}}(\theta) = \mathcal{L}(\theta) + \lambda \lVert \theta \rVert_p^p, \quad p \in \{1, 2\}$$

where $\lambda$ is the **regularization strength** (positive scalar).

## 2.6 Train / Validation / Test Split & Cross-Validation

To estimate generalization, the dataset is split:
- **Training set** (e.g., 70%) — fit parameters $\theta$.
- **Validation set** (e.g., 15%) — tune hyperparameters.
- **Test set** (e.g., 15%) — final unbiased evaluation.

**K-Fold Cross-Validation** (typically $K=5$ or $K=10$):
- Partition data into $K$ equal folds.
- Train on $K-1$ folds, validate on the remaining fold.
- Repeat $K$ times; average validation score.

$$\text{CV Score} = \frac{1}{K}\sum_{k=1}^{K} \text{Score}_k$$

> [!NOTE]
> KTU often asks: *"Why do we use a separate test set even after cross-validation?"* — Answer: cross-validation gives an estimate of model performance on unseen data, but the test set provides a final, untouched, unbiased estimate for reporting.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | ML for CV Application | Critical Metric |
|--------|-----------------------|-----------------|
| Medical Imaging | Tumor / lesion classification | Recall, AUC-ROC |
| Autonomous Driving | Pedestrian / vehicle detection | F1-Score, mAP |
| Surveillance | Face recognition, anomaly detection | Accuracy, FAR/FRR |
| Industrial QA | Defect detection on assembly line | Precision (false alarms costly) |
| Agriculture | Crop disease identification from leaf images | F1-Score |
| Retail | Visual search, automated checkout | Recall@K |
| Robotics | Grasp planning, visual servoing | Success rate (RL) |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Empirical Risk Minimization (ERM) for Linear Regression

We derive the closed-form solution for Ordinary Least Squares (OLS), the simplest supervised regression algorithm used as a foundation in CV regression tasks (e.g., age estimation from face images).

**Setup:** Linear hypothesis

$$h_{\theta}(x) = w^{T}x + b$$

where $w \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ and $b \in \mathbb{R}$.

**Loss function (MSE):**

$$\mathcal{L}(w, b) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\left(y_i - (w^{T}x_i + b)\right)^2$$

For simplicity, absorb bias into $w$ by augmenting $x_i \rightarrow [x_i; 1]$ and $w \rightarrow [w; b]$. Let $X \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times (d+1)}$ be the design matrix, $y \in \mathbb{R}^{N}$ the target vector.

$$\mathcal{L}(w) = \frac{1}{N} \lVert y - Xw \rVert_2^2 = \frac{1}{N}(y - Xw)^{T}(y - Xw)$$

**Step 1 — Expand the quadratic form:**

$$\mathcal{L}(w) = \frac{1}{N}\left(y^{T}y - 2w^{T}X^{T}y + w^{T}X^{T}Xw\right)$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate w.r.t. $w$ and set to zero:**

$$\nabla_{w}\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{N}\left(-2X^{T}y + 2X^{T}Xw\right) = 0$$

**Step 3 — Solve the normal equations:**

$$X^{T}Xw = X^{T}y$$

$$w^{\star} = (X^{T}X)^{-1}X^{T}y$$

provided $X^{T}X$ is invertible (i.e., features are not linearly dependent).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Note:** The matrix $(X^{T}X)^{-1}X^{T}$ is called the **Moore–Penrose pseudo-inverse** of $X$, denoted $X^{+}$. The solution $w^{\star} = X^{+}y$ is the unique minimum-norm least-squares solution.

## 3.2 Derivation: Logistic Regression Cross-Entropy Gradient (Foundation of Image Classifiers)

Logistic regression is the bedrock binary classifier (e.g., cat vs. non-cat). The model outputs probability via the sigmoid:

$$p(y=1 \mid x; w) = \sigma(w^{T}x) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-w^{T}x}}$$

**Loss (Binary Cross-Entropy) for a single sample:**

$$\mathcal{L}(w) = -\left[y \log \hat{y} + (1-y)\log(1 - \hat{y})\right]$$

where $\hat{y} = \sigma(w^{T}x)$.

**Step 1 — Compute $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \hat{y}}$:**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \hat{y}} = -\left(\frac{y}{\hat{y}} - \frac{1-y}{1-\hat{y}}\right) = \frac{\hat{y} - y}{\hat{y}(1-\hat{y})}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial z}$ where $z = w^{T}x$:**

The sigmoid has the elegant property:

$$\frac{d\sigma}{dz} = \sigma(z)(1 - \sigma(z)) = \hat{y}(1-\hat{y})$$

**Step 3 — Chain rule for $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z}$:**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \hat{y}} \cdot \frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial z} = \frac{\hat{y} - y}{\hat{y}(1-\hat{y})} \cdot \hat{y}(1-\hat{y}) = \hat{y} - y$$

This is the famously clean gradient.

**Step 4 — Chain rule for $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w}$:**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z} \cdot \frac{\partial z}{\partial w} = (\hat{y} - y)\, x$$

**Step 5 — Gradient Descent Update:**

$$w_{t+1} = w_t - \eta (\hat{y}_i - y_i) x_i$$

This compact form underlies logistic regression and, in vectorized form, the **backpropagation** algorithm used to train deep neural networks (which you will see in Module 4 of PECST745).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway for KTU:** The gradient $\nabla_{w}\mathcal{L} = (\hat{y} - y)x$ has the same form regardless of the model complexity. For multi-layer neural networks, the chain rule extends this recursively — this is **backpropagation**.

## 3.3 Derivation: Principal Component Analysis (PCA) — The Unsupervised Workhorse

PCA is the most important dimensionality-reduction technique in classical CV, used for face recognition (Eigenfaces), feature compression, and visualization.

**Goal:** Find an orthonormal basis $U \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times k}$ (with $k < d$) such that projecting the zero-centered data $X \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times d}$ onto this basis maximizes the variance of the projected data.

**Step 1 — Center the data:**

$$\mu = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i, \quad \tilde{x}_i = x_i - \mu, \quad \tilde{X} = X - \mathbf{1}\mu^{T}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the covariance matrix:**

$$C = \frac{1}{N} \tilde{X}^{T}\tilde{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d}$$

**Step 3 — Eigen-decomposition of $C$:**

$$C = U \Lambda U^{T}$$

where columns of $U = [u_1, \dots, u_d]$ are eigenvectors (principal axes) and $\Lambda = \text{diag}(\lambda_1 \geq \lambda_2 \geq \dots \geq \lambda_d)$ are eigenvalues (variances along each axis).

**Step 4 — Select top-$k$ eigenvectors** to form $U_k = [u_1, \dots, u_k]$ (those with largest $\lambda_i$).

**Step 5 — Project and reconstruct:**

$$z_i = U_k^{T}\tilde{x}_i \in \mathbb{R}^{k} \quad \text{(low-dim representation)}$$

$$\hat{x}_i = U_k z_i + \mu \in \mathbb{R}^{d} \quad \text{(reconstruction)}$$

**Step 6 — Reconstruction error bound:**

The sum of squared reconstruction errors equals the sum of discarded eigenvalues:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} \lVert \tilde{x}_i - \hat{\tilde{x}}_i \rVert_2^2 = N \sum_{j=k+1}^{d} \lambda_j$$

Hence, keeping the top-$k$ PCs minimizes reconstruction error for a given $k$.

**Eigenfaces connection:** When applied to a matrix of centered face images, the eigenvectors $u_i$ are reshaped into face-like "ghost" images — these are the **Eigenfaces**. A new test face is classified by projecting into the eigenface subspace and comparing to labelled projections (e.g., via k-NN or SVM).

## 3.4 Derivation: SVM Maximum-Margin Decision Boundary (Linear Case)

For a binary classifier with training data $\{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$, $y_i \in \{-1, +1\}$, the linear SVM finds $(w, b)$ such that the **geometric margin** $m = \frac{2}{\lVert w \rVert}$ is maximized.

**Optimization problem (primal form):**

$$\min_{w, b} \frac{1}{2} \lVert w \rVert^2 \quad \text{subject to} \quad y_i(w^{T}x_i + b) \geq 1, \quad \forall i$$

**Step 1 — Lagrangian:**

$$\mathcal{L}(w, b, \alpha) = \frac{1}{2}\lVert w \rVert^2 - \sum_{i=1}^{N}\alpha_i \left[ y_i(w^{T}x_i + b) - 1 \right]$$

with $\alpha_i \geq 0$ (Lagrange multipliers).

**Step 2 — Stationarity conditions:**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w} = w - \sum_{i} \alpha_i y_i x_i = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; w = \sum_{i}\alpha_i y_i x_i$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b} = -\sum_{i}\alpha_i y_i = 0$$

**Step 3 — Dual problem (Wolfe dual):**

$$\max_{\alpha} \sum_{i=1}^{N}\alpha_i - \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i,j}\alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j\, x_i^{T}x_j \quad \text{s.t.} \quad \alpha_i \geq 0, \sum_i \alpha_i y_i = 0$$

**Step 4 — Support vectors** are training points with $\alpha_i > 0$; they lie exactly on the margin.

**Step 5 — Non-linear extension via kernel trick:** Replace $x_i^{T}x_j$ with $K(x_i, x_j)$ (e.g., RBF, polynomial). This is the foundation of kernel-based image classification (e.g., HOG features + SVM for pedestrian detection in the classical DPM model).

## 3.5 Derivation: k-Nearest Neighbors Decision Rule (No Training, Lazy Learning)

For a test point $x_{\text{test}}$, find the $k$ training points nearest by distance $d(x_{\text{test}}, x_i)$ and predict:

**Classification (majority vote):**

$$\hat{y} = \arg\max_{c \in \mathcal{Y}} \sum_{i \in \mathcal{N}_k} \mathbb{1}[y_i = c]$$

**Regression (mean):**

$$\hat{y} = \frac{1}{k}\sum_{i \in \mathcal{N}_k} y_i$$

**Weighted variant** (weight by inverse distance $w_i = 1/d(x_{\text{test}}, x_i)$):

$$\hat{y} = \frac{\sum_{i \in \mathcal{N}_k} w_i y_i}{\sum_{i \in \mathcal{N}_k} w_i}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bias-Variance in k-NN:** Small $k$ → low bias, high variance (overfits). Large $k$ → high bias, low variance (underfits). Optimal $k$ is found by cross-validation.

## 3.6 Confusion Matrix — Full Analytical Breakdown

For a binary classifier with classes Positive (P) and Negative (N):

|  | Predicted Positive | Predicted Negative |
|--|--------------------|--------------------|
| **Actual Positive** | TP (True Positive) | FN (False Negative) — Type II error |
| **Actual Negative** | FP (False Positive) — Type I error | TN (True Negative) |

**Derived metrics:**

$$\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$$

$$\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}, \qquad \text{Recall (TPR)} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$$

$$\text{F1} = \frac{2 \cdot \text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}} = \frac{2\,TP}{2\,TP + FP + FN}$$

$$\text{Specificity (TNR)} = \frac{TN}{TN + FP}, \qquad \text{FPR} = 1 - \text{Specificity} = \frac{FP}{TN + FP}$$

**Multi-class extension** uses one-vs-rest (OvR) or one-vs-one (OvO) and reports macro/weighted averages of these metrics.

**Example (worked):** Suppose a binary classifier for tumor detection produces the following on 200 patients: $TP = 50, FP = 10, FN = 5, TN = 135$.

$$\text{Acc} = \frac{50+135}{200} = 0.925$$

$$\text{Prec} = \frac{50}{50+10} \approx 0.833, \quad \text{Rec} = \frac{50}{50+5} \approx 0.909$$

$$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot 0.833 \cdot 0.909}{0.833 + 0.909} \approx 0.870$$

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — The ML-for-CV Pipeline (Block-Level Functional Architecture)


```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Initialize Weights w] --> B[Forward Pass Predict on Batch]
```

```mermaid
    B --> D[Total Error]
    C --> D

    E --> F[Lowest Test Error]
    A --> G[Underfit Region High Bias Low Variance]
    A --> H[Overfit Region Low Bias High Variance]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Mark Distribution (PECST745 ESE Pattern):**
> - **Part A:** 3-mark short-answer (definitions, derivations, brief explanations).
> - **Part B:** 14-mark long-answer with internal choice. Typical split: (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.
> - Cognitive levels used: L1 (Remember), L2 (Understand), L3 (Apply), L4 (Analyze), L5 (Evaluate).

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Differentiate between Supervised, Unsupervised, and Reinforcement Learning with one CV example for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Supervised | Unsupervised | Reinforcement |
|--------|-----------|--------------|----------------|
| Training Data | Labeled $(x, y)$ | Unlabeled $x$ only | Scalar rewards $r$ |
| Goal | Learn input–output mapping | Discover structure / clusters | Maximize cumulative reward |
| CV Example | Image classification (cat vs. dog) | Image clustering via K-Means | Active visual tracking agent |
| Feedback | Direct (loss) | None | Delayed reward signal |

(3 marks — one mark for each paradigm's key idea + example)

---

### **Question A2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Explain the bias-variance tradeoff. How does regularization help?**

**Model Answer:**

The **bias-variance tradeoff** decomposes the expected test error of a model $\hat{f}$ into three components:

$$\mathbb{E}\left[(y - \hat{f}(x))^2\right] = \text{Bias}^2(\hat{f}) + \text{Var}(\hat{f}) + \sigma^2$$

- **Bias²** — error from wrong assumptions (underfitting).
- **Variance** — error from sensitivity to training data fluctuations (overfitting).
- **$\sigma^2$** — irreducible noise.

**Regularization** (L1/L2) adds a penalty $\lambda \lVert w \rVert_p^p$ to the loss, shrinking coefficients $w$ and reducing variance at the cost of a small bias increase. By tuning $\lambda$, we shift along the bias-variance curve to find the optimal complexity.

(3 marks — 1 for decomposition equation, 1 for definitions, 1 for regularization explanation)

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question B1 (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### (a) [7 Marks] **Explain the K-Nearest Neighbors (k-NN) algorithm for image classification. Discuss the choice of distance metric and the effect of k on bias and variance.**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Algorithm Description (2 marks):**
k-NN is a **lazy, non-parametric** classifier. Given a test image $x_{\text{test}}$ and a training set $\mathcal{D} = \{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$:
- Compute the distance $d(x_{\text{test}}, x_i)$ from $x_{\text{test}}$ to every training point.
- Select the $k$ closest training samples $\mathcal{N}_k$.
- Predict the class label by **majority vote** (classification) or **mean** (regression):

$$\hat{y} = \arg\max_{c} \sum_{i \in \mathcal{N}_k} \mathbb{1}[y_i = c]$$

**2. Distance Metrics (2.5 marks):**
- **Euclidean:** $d(x, x') = \sqrt{\sum_{j}(x_j - x'_j)^2}$ — sensitive to magnitude, common for normalized image features.
- **Manhattan:** $d(x, x') = \sum_{j} \vert x_j - x'_j \vert$ — robust to outliers.
- **Cosine:** $d(x, x') = 1 - \frac{x \cdot x'}{\lVert x \rVert \lVert x' \rVert}$ — invariant to magnitude, ideal for high-dim CNN features.
- **Minkowski / Mahalanobis** — generalized and feature-covariance-aware metrics.

The choice depends on feature scale, dimensionality, and underlying data geometry. For raw pixel images, Euclidean is dominated by background; for HOG or deep features, cosine is often superior.

**3. Effect of k on Bias-Variance (2.5 marks):**
- **$k = 1$** — Decision boundary is highly irregular (Voronoi tessellation). Low bias, **high variance** (overfits to noise).
- **Large $k$ (e.g., $k = N$)** — Always predicts the majority class. **High bias**, low variance (underfits).
- **Optimal $k$** — Found by cross-validation; typically odd (to avoid ties) and $\approx \sqrt{N}$.

```
k small      → decision boundary wiggly, memorizes training data
k moderate   → smooth boundary, good generalization
k large      → boundary collapses to constant, ignores local structure
```

**Conclusion:** k-NN is conceptually simple and powerful for small datasets with good features, but suffers from the **curse of dimensionality** in high-dimensional image spaces. Pre-processing with PCA or using deep embeddings is standard in modern CV pipelines.

---

#### (b) [7 Marks] **Describe the Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifier. Derive the margin equation and explain the kernel trick for non-linear image classification.**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Linear SVM — Maximum Margin Classifier (2.5 marks):**

For binary classification $y_i \in \{-1, +1\}$, the linear decision boundary is $w^{T}x + b = 0$. The geometric margin from any correctly classified point is:

$$m = \frac{y_i(w^{T}x_i + b)}{\lVert w \rVert}$$

SVM maximizes the minimum margin across all training points:

$$\min_{w, b} \frac{1}{2}\lVert w \rVert^2 \quad \text{s.t.} \quad y_i(w^{T}x_i + b) \geq 1, \; \forall i$$

Solving the dual via Lagrange multipliers gives:

$$w = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i x_i, \quad \alpha_i \geq 0, \quad \sum_i \alpha_i y_i = 0$$

Support vectors (points with $\alpha_i > 0$) lie exactly on the margin. The **soft-margin** variant introduces slack variables $\xi_i \geq 0$ and a penalty $C \sum_i \xi_i$ to handle non-separable data, with $C$ controlling the bias-variance tradeoff.

**2. Kernel Trick (2.5 marks):**

For non-linear data, SVM maps inputs to a high-dimensional feature space $\phi(x)$ where a linear separator may exist:

$$w^{T}\phi(x) + b = 0$$

Direct computation of $\phi(x)$ is often infeasible. The **kernel trick** computes inner products in feature space via a kernel function $K(x, x') = \phi(x)^{T}\phi(x')$:

$$\max_{\alpha} \sum_i \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i,j}\alpha_i\alpha_j y_i y_j K(x_i, x_j)$$

**Common kernels for CV:**
- **Polynomial:** $K(x, x') = (x^{T}x' + c)^{d}$
- **RBF (Gaussian):** $K(x, x') = \exp(-\gamma \lVert x - x' \rVert^2)$
- **Histogram Intersection:** $K(x, x') = \sum_j \min(x_j, x'_j)$ — popular for image features (e.g., HOG, SIFT bag-of-words).

**3. Application to Image Classification (2 marks):**

The classical CV pipeline combines:
- **Feature extraction** (HOG, SIFT, or CNN embeddings).
- **Bag-of-Visual-Words** encoding.
- **SVM with RBF or Histogram-Intersection kernel** as the final classifier.

This approach dominated pre-deep-learning image classification (PASCAL VOC 2007–2012 challenges) and remains effective for small datasets and classical feature pipelines.

---

### **Question B2 (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### (a) [7 Marks] **Explain Principal Component Analysis (PCA) for dimensionality reduction in images. Describe the Eigenfaces method for face recognition.**

**Model Solution:**

**1. PCA — Mathematical Foundation (3 marks):**

Given centered image data $\tilde{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times d}$ (each row is a flattened image), PCA finds the orthonormal directions $U \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times k}$ that maximize projected variance.

- Compute covariance: $C = \frac{1}{N}\tilde{X}^{T}\tilde{X}$.
- Eigendecompose: $C = U \Lambda U^{T}$.
- Select top-$k$ eigenvectors: $U_k = [u_1, \dots, u_k]$.
- Project: $z_i = U_k^{T}\tilde{x}_i$.

Reconstruction error equals the sum of discarded eigenvalues: $\sum_{j=k+1}^{d}\lambda_j$.

**2. Eigenfaces Algorithm (3 marks):**

The **Eigenfaces** method (Turk & Pentland, 1991) is a direct application of PCA to face images:

1. Collect a training set of face images, each resized to $H \times W$ and vectorized to $\mathbb{R}^{d}$, $d = HW$.
2. Compute the mean face $\mu$ and subtract from all images.
3. Form the covariance matrix $C = \frac{1}{N}\tilde{X}^{T}\tilde{X}$ (size $d \times d$).
4. Take the top-$k$ eigenvectors (eigenfaces), typically $k = 50$ to $200$, capturing $\geq 95\%$ variance.
5. Project every training face: $z_i = U_k^{T}\tilde{x}_i$.
6. For recognition, project a test face and classify via nearest-neighbor (or SVM) in the $k$-dim eigenspace.

**3. Strengths and Limitations (1 mark):**

| Strengths | Limitations |
|-----------|-------------|
| Compact representation | Sensitive to lighting, pose, expression |
| Fast projection | Linear — cannot capture non-linear manifolds |
| Interpretable basis | Requires alignment of face images |
| Strong baseline | Outperformed by deep learning (FaceNet, ArcFace) |

---

#### (b) [7 Marks] **Discuss the evaluation metrics for a multi-class image classifier with 5 classes. Define and compute precision, recall, F1-score (macro and weighted averages) given a sample confusion matrix.**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Multi-Class Confusion Matrix (1 mark):**

For $C$ classes, the confusion matrix $M \in \mathbb{R}^{C \times C}$ has $M_{ij}$ = number of samples truly of class $i$ predicted as class $j$. Diagonal entries are correct predictions.

**Worked Example — 5-class classifier on 500 test images:**

| True \ Pred | Cat | Dog | Car | Tree | House |
|-------------|-----|-----|-----|------|-------|
| **Cat**     | 80  | 10  | 2   | 5    | 3     |
| **Dog**     | 8   | 85  | 3   | 2    | 2     |
| **Car**     | 1   | 2   | 90  | 4    | 3     |
| **Tree**    | 3   | 1   | 4   | 82   | 10    |
| **House**   | 2   | 1   | 5   | 8    | 84    |

**2. Per-Class Precision and Recall (2 marks):**

For class $c$:
- $TP_c = M_{cc}$ (diagonal entry)
- $FP_c = \sum_{i \neq c} M_{i,c}$ (column sum minus diagonal)
- $FN_c = \sum_{j \neq c} M_{c,j}$ (row sum minus diagonal)

**Computation:**

| Class | TP | FP | FN | Precision | Recall | F1 |
|-------|----|----|----|-----------|--------|-----|
| Cat   | 80 | 14 | 20 | 80/94 = 0.851 | 80/100 = 0.800 | 0.825 |
| Dog   | 85 | 14 | 15 | 85/99 = 0.859 | 85/100 = 0.850 | 0.854 |
| Car   | 90 | 14 | 10 | 90/104 = 0.865 | 90/100 = 0.900 | 0.882 |
| Tree  | 82 | 19 | 18 | 82/101 = 0.812 | 82/100 = 0.820 | 0.816 |
| House | 84 | 18 | 16 | 84/102 = 0.824 | 84/100 = 0.840 | 0.832 |

**3. Macro and Weighted Averages (2 marks):**

**Macro Average (treats all classes equally):**

$$P_{\text{macro}} = \frac{1}{5}\sum_{c} P_c = \frac{0.851 + 0.859 + 0.865 + 0.812 + 0.824}{5} = 0.842$$

$$R_{\text{macro}} = \frac{0.800 + 0.850 + 0.900 + 0.820 + 0.840}{5} = 0.842$$

$$F1_{\text{macro}} = \frac{0.825 + 0.854 + 0.882 + 0.816 + 0.832}{5} \approx 0.842$$

**Weighted Average (weights by class support $N_c$):**

$$P_{\text{weighted}} = \frac{\sum_c N_c \cdot P_c}{\sum_c N_c} = \frac{100(0.851+0.859+0.865+0.812+0.824)}{500} = 0.842$$

(Same here since each class has 100 samples.)

**4. Overall Accuracy (2 marks):**

$$\text{Accuracy} = \frac{\sum_c TP_c}{\sum_c \sum_j M_{cj}} = \frac{80+85+90+82+84}{500} = \frac{421}{500} = 0.842 = 84.2\%$$

**Conclusion:** The classifier achieves **84.2% accuracy** with balanced macro and weighted F1 (~0.84), indicating uniform performance across all 5 classes — no significant class bias. In a KTU exam, always specify the averaging strategy when reporting multi-class metrics.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing "precision" with "recall"** — Precision is about *what you predicted positive being actually positive* (TP / (TP+FP)); recall is about *finding all actual positives* (TP / (TP+FN)). Many students swap these.
> 2. **Forgetting to center data before PCA** — Without centering, the first principal component points toward the data mean, not the direction of maximum variance. Always subtract the mean.
> 3. **Reporting only training accuracy** — KTU examiners specifically want **test/validation accuracy**. Reporting training accuracy alone loses 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Mixing L1 and L2 regularization formulas** — L1: $\lambda \lVert w \rVert_1$; L2: $\lambda \lVert w \rVert_2^2$. Know the difference and the sparsity effect.
> 5. **Skipping the bias-variance decomposition equation** in tradeoff questions — the formula $\text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance} + \sigma^2$ is mandatory.
> 6. **Forgetting cross-validation justification** — When asked "why use cross-validation," students must mention that a single train/validation split has high variance, and $K$-fold averaging gives a more robust estimate.
> 7. **Not stating the sigmoid property $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)(1-\sigma(z))$** when deriving logistic regression — it is the key step examiners look for.
> 8. **Ignoring the curse of dimensionality in k-NN** — for raw images of size $224 \times 224$, $d = 50176$, and k-NN fails. Mention the need for feature extraction or dimensionality reduction.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Module 3: Machine Learning for Computer Vision**

**Core Paradigms**
- Supervised: $(x, y)$ pairs → classification / regression.
- Unsupervised: $x$ only → clustering, PCA, autoencoders.
- Reinforcement: agent + environment + reward.
- Self-Supervised: pretext tasks (rotation, masking) + downstream tasks.

**Foundational Equations**
- Empirical risk: $\hat{R}(\theta) = \frac{1}{N}\sum \mathcal{L}(h_\theta(x_i), y_i)$.
- Gradient descent: $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}$.
- Bias-variance: $\text{Error} = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance} + \sigma^2$.
- Cross-entropy (multi-class): $\mathcal{L} = -\sum_c y_c \log \hat{y}_c$.
- MSE: $\frac{1}{N}\sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$.

**Classical ML Algorithms for CV**
- **k-NN:** lazy, distance-based, sensitive to $k$ and feature scale.
- **SVM:** maximum margin, kernel trick for non-linearity, soft-margin $C$ parameter.
- **Decision Trees:** greedy splits via information gain / Gini impurity.
- **Random Forest / Ensemble:** bagging of trees, reduces variance.
- **PCA:** unsupervised linear projection, captures maximum variance.
- **Logistic Regression:** linear classifier with sigmoid + cross-entropy.

**Evaluation Metrics (Binary)**
- Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1, Specificity, FPR.
- TP, FP, TN, FN confusion matrix — remember which is which.
- ROC curve: TPR vs FPR; AUC = area under ROC.

**Multi-Class Extensions**
- One-vs-Rest (OvR) and One-vs-One (OvO) strategies.
- Macro vs Weighted vs Micro averaging — always state which.

**Practical Considerations for CV**
- **Curse of dimensionality:** raw pixels are bad features; use HOG, SIFT, or CNN embeddings.
- **Data augmentation:** rotation, flip, color jitter — reduces overfitting.
- **Normalization:** zero-mean, unit-variance per feature.
- **Class imbalance:** oversampling, undersampling, focal loss, weighted CE.

**Bias-Variance Practical Tips**
- Underfit → more features, complex model, less regularization.
- Overfit → more data, augmentation, regularization (L1/L2/Dropout), early stopping.

**Connections to Module 4 (Deep Learning for CV)**
- Neural networks = stacked linear + non-linear layers; trained via backprop (chain rule).
- CNNs = neural nets with convolutional layers, pooling, weight sharing.
- ML algorithms like SVM, RF remain relevant as **classifiers on top of CNN features** in transfer learning.

**Common KTU 2024 Question Patterns**
- "Differentiate supervised / unsupervised / reinforcement with examples." (3 marks)
- "Explain bias-variance tradeoff with the decomposition equation." (3–7 marks)
- "Derive the PCA algorithm and apply to face recognition." (7–14 marks)
- "Explain SVM with kernel trick for image classification." (7 marks)
- "Compute precision, recall, F1 from a given confusion matrix." (7 marks)
- "Explain k-NN with effect of $k$ on bias and variance." (7 marks)

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Tip:** When in doubt during the exam, **draw the diagram and write the equation**. KTU examiners reward structured answers with formulas, tables, and flow diagrams — even if the textual explanation is brief. Always state the **equation, intuition, limitation, and CV application** for any algorithm you discuss.

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned)** syllabus for *PECST745 – Computer Vision*, **Deep Learning Use Cases** refer to the practical deployment of Deep Neural Network (DNN) architectures — primarily **Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs), Autoencoders, Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs), and Transformer-based Vision Models** — to solve high-dimensional perceptual tasks such as image classification, object detection, semantic segmentation, image generation, video understanding, 3D reconstruction, and biomedical image analysis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Box:**
> *A Deep Learning Use Case in Computer Vision is a well-defined problem (input → output mapping) paired with an end-to-end trainable neural architecture, a curated dataset, a loss function, an evaluation metric, and an inference pipeline that together solve a real-world visual perception task.*

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview (Real-World Analogy)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Specialist Doctor":**
> Imagine a hospital where each deep learning model is a **specialist doctor**:
> - A **classifier** is like a radiologist who looks at an X-ray and says *"this is pneumonia"*.
> - A **detector** is like a security guard who not only spots a face in CCTV footage but also draws a box around it.
> - A **segmentation model** is like a pathologist who colors every cell in a microscope slide.
> - A **generative model** is like a digital artist who can create a brand-new X-ray that looks realistic but is entirely synthetic.
>
> Each "doctor" is trained on **thousands of past cases** (the dataset), improves through **feedback on mistakes** (the loss function), and is **evaluated by senior experts** (metrics like accuracy, mAP, IoU, FID).

## 1.3 Why "Use Cases" Matter in Computer Vision

Traditional vision relied on hand-crafted features (SIFT, HOG, Haar). Deep Learning replaced this paradigm through **representation learning** — the network *automatically* discovers the right features from raw pixels. This is the reason deep learning dominates every modern CV benchmark (ImageNet, COCO, Cityscapes, KITTI, ADE20K).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU High-Yield Pointer:**
> Whenever a question asks *"list the applications of deep learning in CV"*, always pair the **task** with the **architecture** and the **metric**. For example: *Object Detection → YOLO / Faster R-CNN → mAP@0.5*. Examiners reward such precision.
