# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Deep Learning Use Cases in CV

The KTU Module 3 treatment typically groups deep-learning CV applications into the following **functional clusters**:

1. **Image Classification** — assigning a single label to a whole image.
2. **Object Detection** — localising + classifying multiple objects.
3. **Semantic Segmentation** — pixel-level class labelling.
4. **Instance Segmentation** — pixel labelling *per object instance*.
5. **Image Generation & Synthesis** — GANs, Diffusion Models, VAEs.
6. **Face Recognition & Verification** — metric learning, embeddings.
7. **Pose Estimation & Keypoint Detection** — human/animal skeletal joints.
8. **Optical Character Recognition (OCR)** — text detection + recognition.
9. **Medical / Biomedical Imaging** — tumour segmentation, disease grading.
10. **Autonomous Driving & 3D Vision** — depth estimation, LiDAR fusion.
11. **Video Understanding** — action recognition, tracking, captioning.
12. **Image Enhancement** — super-resolution, denoising, deblurring, style transfer.

## 2.2 Core Architectural Building Blocks

| Use Case Family | Dominant Architecture | Key Innovation |
|---|---|---|
| Classification | VGG, ResNet, EfficientNet, ViT | Residual / attention blocks |
| Detection | R-CNN family, YOLO, SSD, DETR | Region proposals / anchor-free |
| Segmentation | FCN, U-Net, DeepLab, SegFormer | Encoder-decoder + skip / atrous |
| Generation | GAN, VAE, Diffusion, NeRF | Adversarial / probabilistic |
| Recognition | SiameseNet, ArcFace, FaceNet | Metric / angular loss |
| Pose | OpenPose, HRNet, MediaPipe | Heatmap regression |
| Video | I3D, SlowFast, TimeSformer | 3D conv / space-time attention |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:**
> Most 14-mark questions in Module 3 test **architectural understanding** (encoder-decoder flow, skip connections, loss functions) rather than code memorisation. Be fluent with **block diagrams** of U-Net, Faster R-CNN, and GAN.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Universal Notation:**
> - $B$ = batch size, $C$ = number of classes
> - $N$ = number of samples, $K$ = number of top predictions
> - $\hat{y}$ = predicted probability, $y$ = ground-truth label
> - $TP, FP, FN, TN$ = True/False Positive/Negative
> - $p$ = precision, $r$ = recall

| # | Metric / Loss | Formula (LaTeX-safe) | Used In (Use Case) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Softmax | $\sigma(z_i) = \dfrac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_{j=1}^{C} e^{z_j}}$ | Classification, Detection head |
| 2 | Categorical Cross-Entropy | $\mathcal{L}_{CE} = -\sum_{i=1}^{C} y_i \log(\hat{y}_i)$ | Classification, Segmentation |
| 3 | Binary Cross-Entropy | $\mathcal{L}_{BCE} = -\left[ y\log\hat{y} + (1-y)\log(1-\hat{y}) \right]$ | Objectness, OCR |
| 4 | Focal Loss | $\mathcal{L}_{FL} = -\alpha_t (1-p_t)^{\gamma} \log(p_t)$ | Imbalanced Detection |
| 5 | Intersection over Union | $IoU = \dfrac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}$ | Detection, Segmentation |
| 6 | Dice Coefficient | $D = \dfrac{2 \cdot \vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert}$ | Medical Segmentation |
| 7 | Mean Average Precision | $mAP = \dfrac{1}{C}\sum_{c=1}^{C} AP_c$ | Detection, Retrieval |
| 8 | L1 / L2 Loss | $\mathcal{L}_1 = \vert y - \hat{y} \vert$ \;,\; $\mathcal{L}_2 = (y-\hat{y})^2$ | Super-resolution, Pose |
| 9 | Perceptual Loss | $\mathcal{L}_{perc} = \vert \vert \phi(x) - \phi(\hat{x}) \vert \vert_2^2$ | Style Transfer, SR |
| 10 | Adversarial Loss | $\mathcal{L}_{adv} = \mathbb{E}_{x}[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}_{z}[\log(1 - D(G(z)))]$ | GAN |
| 11 | Fréchet Inception Distance | $FID = \vert \vert \mu_r - \mu_g \vert \vert^2 + \mathrm{Tr}\left(\Sigma_r + \Sigma_g - 2(\Sigma_r \Sigma_g)^{1/2}\right)$ | Generation |
| 12 | Triplet Loss | $\mathcal{L}_{trip} = \max\left(d(a,p) - d(a,n) + \alpha,\, 0\right)$ | Face Recognition |
| 13 | ArcFace Loss | $\mathcal{L}_{arc} = -\log\dfrac{e^{s\cos(\theta_{y_i}+m)}}{e^{s\cos(\theta_{y_i}+m)} + \sum_{j\neq y_i} e^{s\cos\theta_j}}$ | Face Recognition |
| 14 | OKS (Object Keypoint Similarity) | $OKS = \dfrac{\sum_i \exp\!\left(-d_i^2 / 2s^2k_i^2\right)\delta(v_i>0)}{\sum_i \delta(v_i>0)}$ | Pose Estimation |

> [!WARNING]

- **Healthcare**: U-Net segmenting tumours in MRI/CT → assists radiologists, reduces diagnostic time.
- **Automotive**: YOLO + depth networks for pedestrian detection → ADAS / autonomous vehicles.
- **Retail**: CNN classifiers for self-checkout → reduces queue, enables cashier-less stores.
- **Agriculture**: Drone imagery + segmentation → crop health monitoring and yield estimation.
- **Surveillance**: FaceNet + tracking → missing-person search, security automation.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Softmax + Categorical Cross-Entropy Joint Gradient

The combination of **Softmax** and **Cross-Entropy** yields an unusually clean gradient, which is why it is the *de-facto* loss for almost every classification-based CV use case.

**Step 1 — Softmax definition:**

$$\sigma(z_i) = \frac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_{j=1}^{C} e^{z_j}}$$

where $z_i$ is the logits vector element for class $i$.

**Step 2 — Cross-entropy loss (one-hot ground truth $y$):**

$$\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{i=1}^{C} y_i \log(\hat{y}_i), \qquad \hat{y}_i = \sigma(z_i)$$

**Step 3 — Compute partial derivative w.r.t. logit $z_k$.** We use the quotient rule on $\sigma(z_i)$:

$$
\frac{\partial \sigma(z_i)}{\partial z_k} = 
\begin{cases}
\sigma(z_k)\bigl(1 - \sigma(z_k)\bigr), & i = k \\[4pt]
-\sigma(z_i)\sigma(z_k), & i \neq k
\end{cases}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into $\partial \mathcal{L}/\partial z_k$:**

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z_k} 
= -\sum_{i=1}^{C} y_i \,\frac{1}{\sigma(z_i)}\,\frac{\partial \sigma(z_i)}{\partial z_k}
$$

**Step 5 — Split the sum at $i = k$:**

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z_k} 
= -y_k \bigl(1 - \sigma(z_k)\bigr) + \sum_{i \neq k} y_i \, \sigma(z_k)
$$

**Step 6 — Use the one-hot property $y_k + \sum_{i \neq k} y_i = 1$:**

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z_k} 
= -y_k + \sigma(z_k)\left(y_k + \sum_{i \neq k} y_i\right) 
= -y_k + \sigma(z_k)
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}_{CE}}{\partial z_k} \;=\; \hat{y}_k \;-\; y_k}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters in CV:** The gradient is simply **(prediction − target)**. This makes training numerically stable, vanishing-gradient-safe for shallow classifiers, and forms the back-prop head of ResNet, YOLO, U-Net, and almost every modern CV model.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Intersection over Union (IoU) and Dice Equivalence

IoU and Dice Coefficient are mathematically monotonic — they always agree on ranking.

**Step 1 — Definitions:**

$$
IoU = \frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}
= \frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert - \vert A \cap B \vert}
$$

$$
D = \frac{2 \cdot \vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert}
$$

**Step 2 — Relate Dice to IoU.** Let $x = \vert A \cap B \vert$, $a = \vert A \vert$, $b = \vert B \vert$. Then:

$$
D = \frac{2x}{a+b}, \qquad IoU = \frac{x}{a+b-x}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $x$ in terms of $IoU$:** $x = IoU\,(a+b-x) \Rightarrow x(1+IoU) = IoU\,(a+b)$, so

$$
x = \frac{IoU\,(a+b)}{1+IoU}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back into $D$:**

$$
D = \frac{2}{a+b} \cdot \frac{IoU\,(a+b)}{1+IoU} = \frac{2 \cdot IoU}{1 + IoU}
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{D \;=\; \dfrac{2 \cdot IoU}{1 + IoU}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Use:** U-Net and medical segmentation models are trained with **Dice Loss** $\mathcal{L}_{Dice} = 1 - D$ because in tumour segmentation the *foreground* is tiny (~1–5 % of pixels) and CE loss would be drowned out by the easy background. Dice Loss implicitly uses the IoU ranking.

---

| Instance Segmentation | $H \times W \times 3$ | Mask + class + ID | Mask R-CNN | Mask CE + Box L1 | mAP (mask) |
| Face Recognition | $112 \times 112 \times 3$ | Embedding $\mathbb{R}^{512}$ | FaceNet, ArcFace | Triplet / ArcFace | TAR @ FAR, Accuracy |

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example: mAP Calculation (Detection)

Suppose a detector produces the following predictions on a dataset with **3 classes**.

**Step 1 — Compute AP per class from the Precision-Recall curve.** For class "cat":

$$
\text{AP}_{cat} = \sum_{n} \bigl(R_n - R_{n-1}\bigr)\, P_n
$$

After integrating the PR curve numerically: $\text{AP}_{cat} = 0.72$

**Step 2 — Repeat for "dog" and "car":** $\text{AP}_{dog} = 0.65$, $\text{AP}_{car} = 0.81$

**Step 3 — Mean Average Precision across $C = 3$ classes:**

$$
mAP = \frac{1}{3}\left(0.72 + 0.65 + 0.81\right) = \frac{2.18}{3} \approx 0.727
$$

$$
\boxed{mAP \approx 72.7\%}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** Examiners love this calculation. Memorise the formula, the meaning of each term, and be ready to compute a 3-class example in under 2 minutes.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**List any three popular deep-learning architectures used for object detection. Mention the metric used to evaluate detection models.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **R-CNN family** (R-CNN, Fast R-CNN, Faster R-CNN) — region-proposal based, two-stage detector.
2. **YOLO family** (YOLOv5, YOLOv8) — single-stage, anchor-free, real-time detector.
3. **DETR** (DEtection TRansformer) — transformer-based, end-to-end set prediction.
4. **Evaluation Metric:** **mAP (mean Average Precision)**, typically reported at IoU threshold 0.5 (`mAP@0.5`) and averaged over `[0.5 : 0.95]` (`mAP@[.5:.95]`).

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between semantic segmentation and instance segmentation with one example use case each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Semantic Segmentation | Instance Segmentation |
|---|---|---|
| Output | One class label per pixel | Class + unique instance ID per pixel |
| Object separation | Cannot separate overlapping objects | Distinguishes each object instance |
| Example Use Case | Land-cover classification in satellite images | Counting individual cells in a microscopy image |
| Architecture | FCN, U-Net, DeepLab | Mask R-CNN, YOLACT |

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the architecture of **U-Net** for semantic segmentation. Discuss the role of **skip connections** and the **encoder–decoder** structure. State the loss function typically used. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss how **deep learning** is applied to **medical image segmentation**. Compare **Cross-Entropy Loss** and **Dice Loss**, and derive the relationship between the **Dice Coefficient** and **IoU**. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) U-Net Architecture**

- **Input:** RGB image of size $H \times W \times 3$ (typically $256 \times 256$ or $512 \times 512$).
- **Contracting Path (Encoder):** Repeated application of two $3 \times 3$ unpadded convolutions, each followed by a ReLU activation and a $2 \times 2$ max-pooling operation with stride 2 for downsampling. Number of feature channels doubles at each level: $64 \to 128 \to 256 \to 512$.
- **Bottleneck:** Two $3 \times 3$ convolutions with $1024$ feature channels at the lowest spatial resolution.
- **Expansive Path (Decoder):** Each step performs a $2 \times 2$ up-convolution (transposed convolution) that halves the number of channels, followed by concatenation with the correspondingly cropped feature map from the encoder (**skip connection**), then two $3 \times 3$ convolutions + ReLU.
- **Final Layer:** $1 \times 1$ convolution maps the 64-channel feature map to the desired number of classes $C$, followed by pixel-wise softmax.

**Role of Skip Connections:**
- The encoder downsamples, losing fine spatial detail.
- Skip connections **directly transfer high-resolution features** from encoder to decoder, enabling **precise pixel-wise localisation**.
- Without skip connections, output masks become blurry — this is the *key innovation* of U-Net.

**Loss Function:** Pixel-wise **Categorical Cross-Entropy** combined with **Dice Loss**:

$$\mathcal{L}_{total} = \mathcal{L}_{CE} + \mathcal{L}_{Dice}, \qquad \mathcal{L}_{Dice} = 1 - \frac{2 \cdot \vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert}$$

---

**(b) Deep Learning in Medical Image Segmentation**

**Application Pipeline:**
1. Acquire 3D volumetric scans (CT, MRI, X-ray).
2. Annotate slice-by-slice by expert radiologists (very expensive).
3. Train U-Net / nnU-Net / V-Net with **patch-based sampling** to fit GPU memory.
4. Post-process with **Conditional Random Fields (CRF)** or **connected-component analysis**.

**Comparison of CE vs Dice Loss:**

| Property | Cross-Entropy Loss | Dice Loss |
|---|---|---|
| Per-pixel gradient | Yes | No — region-level |
| Handles class imbalance | Poorly | Naturally |
| Optimisation surface | Smooth but biased to background | Sharp; directly optimises overlap |
| Best used when | Balanced classes | Imbalanced (small tumour, large background) |

**Derivation of Dice–IoU relationship** (already shown in §3.2):

$$
D = \frac{2 \cdot IoU}{1 + IoU}
$$

**Proof sketch:** Starting from definitions of $D$ and $IoU$, substitute $x = \vert A \cap B \vert$ and simplify to obtain the boxed relation. This proves that **Dice and IoU are monotonically related**, justifying why optimising Dice also improves IoU.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of a **Generative Adversarial Network (GAN)**. State the adversarial loss function and explain how the **Generator** and **Discriminator** are trained alternately. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain any **two** deep-learning-based computer vision use cases **other than segmentation**, with their typical architectures and evaluation metrics. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) GAN Architecture and Training**

**Components:**
- **Generator $G$**: Maps a latent noise vector $z \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$ to a synthetic image $G(z)$ that mimics the real data distribution.
- **Discriminator $D$**: A binary classifier that outputs the probability that an input image is real (from dataset) vs fake (from generator).

**Adversarial Loss (vanilla GAN):**

$$
\min_G \max_D \, V(D, G) = \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{data}(x)}\bigl[\log D(x)\bigr] + \mathbb{E}_{z \sim p_z(z)}\bigl[\log(1 - D(G(z)))\bigr]
$$

- $D$ tries to **maximise** this (correctly distinguish real from fake).
- $G$ tries to **minimise** this (fool $D$).

**Alternating Training Procedure:**
1. **Step 1 — Update $D$:** Sample mini-batch of real images and equal number of fake images $G(z)$. Backpropagate binary cross-entropy loss to update $D$.
2. **Step 2 — Update $G$:** Sample new noise $z$, pass through $G$, get $G(z)$. Compute $D(G(z))$ and backpropagate to update $G$ only (freeze $D$'s weights).
3. Repeat until convergence (Nash equilibrium, in theory).

**Stabilisation Techniques:** Wasserstein loss + gradient penalty (WGAN-GP), spectral normalisation, two-timescale update rule (TTUR).

---

**(b) Two Other Use Cases**

**Use Case 1: Image Super-Resolution**
- **Task:** Recover a high-resolution image $I_{HR}$ from a low-resolution input $I_{LR}$.
- **Architecture:** **SRGAN / ESRGAN** — uses a deep ResNet generator with PixelShuffle (sub-pixel) upsampling at the end and a VGG-based perceptual discriminator.
- **Loss:** $L = L_{perceptual} + \lambda L_{adversarial} + \mu L_1$
- **Metric:** **PSNR**, **SSIM**, and perceptual **LPIPS** score.

**Use Case 2: Face Recognition**
- **Task:** Verify whether two face images belong to the same identity.
- **Architecture:** **FaceNet / ArcFace** — a deep CNN (e.g., Inception-ResNet) that maps each face to a 512-D embedding on a hypersphere.
- **Loss:** **Triplet Loss** (FaceNet) or **ArcFace Loss** (additive angular margin).
- **Metric:** **TAR @ FAR = 0.001** (True Acceptance Rate at 0.1 % False Acceptance Rate), and rank-1 accuracy on LFW / IJB-C benchmarks.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Conflating *Semantic* and *Instance* segmentation** — examiners explicitly deduct 1–2 marks if examples are swapped.
> 2. **Forgetting the loss function** when describing U-Net, YOLO, or GAN — every architecture answer in KTU *must* be paired with its loss.
> 3. **No derivation in numerical questions** — even if the final mAP or IoU value is correct, marks are deducted unless steps are shown.
> 4. **Using `|` inside markdown tables** during answer transcription breaks table rendering in online evaluation portals — students writing answers should use `\vert` style LaTeX in tools like Overleaf.
> 5. **Skipping skip connections** in the U-Net diagram — full 2 marks are reserved for skip-connection explanation in 14-mark questions.
> 6. **Writing "CNN is used for everything"** — vague answers score ≤ 2/7. Be specific: name the architecture (e.g., **ResNet-50**, **YOLOv8**, **Mask R-CNN**).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for "Deep Learning Use Cases":**

- ✅ Deep learning use cases in CV fall into **12 main families**: classification, detection, segmentation, generation, recognition, pose, OCR, medical, autonomous, video, captioning, enhancement.
- ✅ Always pair a use case with its **architecture + loss + metric** triple.
- ✅ **CNN** is the foundational architecture; **Transformers (ViT, DETR, BLIP)** are the modern default.
- ✅ **U-Net** = encoder-decoder + skip connections → used for any pixel-level task.
- ✅ **YOLO / Faster R-CNN** for detection; evaluated by **mAP@0.5** and **mAP@[.5:.95]**.
- ✅ **GAN** = generator + discriminator trained alternately; adversarial loss = $-\log D(G(z))$.
- ✅ **Diffusion models** are now state-of-the-art for generation; trained with noise-prediction MSE; evaluated by **FID**.
- ✅ **ArcFace / FaceNet** use metric / angular losses for face recognition.
- ✅ **Dice Loss** preferred over CE for imbalanced medical segmentation.
- ✅ **IoU–Dice relation**: $D = \dfrac{2 \cdot IoU}{1 + IoU}$ — derivation is a **favourite KTU question**.
- ✅ **Softmax + Cross-Entropy gradient** = $\hat{y}_k - y_k$ — clean, stable, used everywhere.
- ✅ **Skip connections** preserve spatial detail in segmentation architectures.
- ✅ **Evaluation metrics quick recall**:
    - Classification → Accuracy, F1
    - Detection → mAP, IoU
    - Segmentation → mIoU, Dice
    - Generation → FID, IS
    - Recognition → TAR@FAR
    - Captioning → BLEU, CIDEr
    - Super-resolution → PSNR, SSIM, LPIPS
- ✅ KTU 2024 emphasises **architectural diagrams** — practise drawing U-Net, YOLO, GAN, ViT from memory.
- ✅ Loss-function derivations and metric computations carry **3–5 marks each** in 14-mark questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Machine Learning Models for Vision - Image Vision - Pretrained Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST745 - COMPUTER VISION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Machine Learning Models for Vision - Image Vision - Pretrained Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Pretrained Model** in computer vision is a deep convolutional neural network (CNN) whose weights have already been learned on a massive benchmark dataset (typically **ImageNet** with **1.4 million** images across **1000 classes**) and can be reused as a starting point for solving related vision tasks. This paradigm, formally known as **Transfer Learning**, leverages previously acquired feature representations to compensate for the scarcity of labeled data in target domains.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A pretrained model is a neural network trained on a large-scale dataset whose learned parameters (weights and biases) serve as the initialization point for a downstream vision task, eliminating the need for training from scratch.

### 1.2 Mathematical Statement of the Pretrained Concept

Let $f_{\theta^*}: \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$ denote the source model trained on dataset $\mathcal{D}_S = \{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N_S}$.

The pretrained model provides optimal parameters:
$$\theta^* = \arg\min_{\theta} \mathcal{L}_S(f_{\theta}(x), y)$$

For the target task with limited data $\mathcal{D}_T = \{(x_j, y_j)\}_{j=1}^{N_T}$ where $N_T \ll N_S$, we initialize with $\theta^*$ and fine-tune:
$$\theta_T = \arg\min_{\theta} \mathcal{L}_T(f_{\theta}(x), y), \quad \theta \leftarrow \theta^*$$

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Master Chef

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine a master chef trained for 15 years in Italian cuisine. If asked to cook authentic Japanese food, do they start from scratch learning to boil water? **Absolutely not!** They use their existing knowledge of *knife skills, heat control, ingredient pairing, and flavor balance* — and only learn the new techniques specific to Japanese cuisine.
>
> - **Years of Italian cooking** = Training on **ImageNet** (millions of images)
> - **Fundamental skills (knife, heat, balance)** = **Low-level features** (edges, textures, colors learned in early CNN layers)
> - **Learning Japanese cuisine** = **Fine-tuning** the pretrained model on your small custom dataset
> - **Master chef's foundation** = **Convolutional base layers** that already extract meaningful features

### 1.4 The Three Pillars of Pretrained Vision Models

| Pillar | Description | KTU High-Yield Keyword |
|--------|-------------|------------------------|
| **Convolutional Base** | Frozen or fine-tuned feature extractor | "Bottleneck features" |
| **Classifier Head** | New dense layers for target task | "Custom head replacement" |
| **Optimization Strategy** | Freeze vs. fine-tune vs. mixed | "Layer-wise learning rates" |

### 1.5 Why Pretrained Models Exist — The Motivation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Training a deep CNN from scratch requires:
> - **3-30 days** of GPU time on modern hardware
> - **Millions** of labeled images
> - **Millions of dollars** in compute cost
>
> Pretrained models democratize computer vision by providing **ready-made visual intelligence** at the cost of a few hours of fine-tuning.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architecture of a Pretrained CNN — Hierarchical Feature Theory

A pretrained convolutional network operates on the principle of **hierarchical feature composition**. Early layers capture universal, low-level patterns while deeper layers encode task-specific, semantic abstractions.

**Layer-wise Feature Hierarchy:**

1. **Layer 1-2:** Detects **edges**, **color gradients**, and **oriented lines**
2. **Layer 3-5:** Detects **textures**, **corners**, and **simple shapes**
3. **Layer 6-10:** Detects **object parts** (wheels, eyes, wings)
4. **Layer 11+:** Detects **entire objects** and **semantic concepts**

> [!NOTE]
> This is why pretrained models transfer so well — the **early and middle layers** are essentially universal feature extractors that work for almost any image domain.

### 2.2 The Convolution Operation — Core Mathematical Engine

For an input feature map $X \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times C}$ and a kernel $K \in \mathbb{R}^{k \times k \times C}$, the convolution output at position $(i, j)$ is:

$$Y_{i,j,c'} = \sigma\left(\sum_{m=0}^{k-1}\sum_{n=0}^{k-1}\sum_{c=0}^{C-1} X_{i+m,\, j+n,\, c} \cdot K_{m,n,c,c'} + b_{c'}\right)$$

where $\sigma$ is the activation function (ReLU in modern architectures) and $b_{c'}$ is the bias term.

### 2.3 Output Dimension Formula (Critical for Board Exams)

For an input of size $H \times W$ with kernel $k$, padding $p$, and stride $s$:

$$H_{\text{out}} = \left\lfloor \frac{H_{\text{in}} + 2p - k}{s} \right\rfloor + 1$$

$$W_{\text{out}} = \left\lfloor \frac{W_{\text{in}} + 2p - k}{s} \right\rfloor + 1$$

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **exam-critical**. Memorize them with units and conditions.

| Formula | Expression | Symbol Meaning | Unit / Notes |
|---------|-----------|----------------|--------------|
| Softmax Activation | $\sigma(z_i) = \frac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{z_j}}$ | $z_i$ = logit, $K$ = classes | Probabilities summing to **1** |
| Cross-Entropy Loss | $\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{i=1}^{K} y_i \log(\hat{y}_i)$ | $y_i$ = true label, $\hat{y}_i$ = predicted | Unitless; lower is better |
| ReLU Activation | $\text{ReLU}(x) = \max(0, x)$ | $x$ = pre-activation | Non-saturating, fast convergence |
| Conv Output Size | $O = \frac{I + 2p - k}{s} + 1$ | $I$ = input, $k$ = kernel | $p$ = padding, $s$ = stride |
| Parameter Count (Conv) | $P_{\text{conv}} = k^2 \cdot C_{\text{in}} \cdot C_{\text{out}} + C_{\text{out}}$ | $k$ = kernel, $C$ = channels | Add $C_{\text{out}}$ for biases |
| Parameter Count (Dense) | $P_{\text{dense}} = n_{\text{in}} \cdot n_{\text{out}} + n_{\text{out}}$ | $n$ = neurons | Fully connected layer |
| Batch Norm | $\hat{x} = \frac{x - \mu_{\mathcal{B}}}{\sqrt{\sigma_{\mathcal{B}}^2 + \epsilon}}$ | $\mu, \sigma$ = batch statistics | $\epsilon \approx 10^{-5}$ |
| Global Avg Pooling | $g_c = \frac{1}{H \cdot W}\sum_{i,j} X_{i,j,c}$ | Reduces $H \times W$ to scalar | Used before FC layers |
| Transfer Learning Init | $\theta_T^{(0)} = \theta^*_{\text{pretrained}}$ | Initialization | Same architecture or compatible |
| Learning Rate (Fine-tune) | $\eta_{\text{fine}} = 10^{-4}$ to $10^{-5}$ | Much smaller than from-scratch | $\eta_{\text{scratch}} = 10^{-3}$ |

### 2.5 The Two Pretrained Strategies — Feature Extraction vs. Fine-Tuning

#### Strategy A: Feature Extraction (Frozen Base)

The convolutional base remains frozen and acts purely as a **fixed feature extractor**. Only the new classifier head trains.

$$\theta_T^{\text{(FE)}} = \arg\min_{\theta_{\text{head}}} \mathcal{L}_T\left(W_{\text{head}} \cdot \phi_{\theta^*}(x), y\right)$$

where $\phi_{\theta^*}$ is the **frozen pretrained feature extractor**.

#### Strategy B: Fine-Tuning (Unfrozen Top Layers)

The top convolutional blocks are **unfrozen** and continue training with a small learning rate, while earlier layers remain frozen.

$$\theta_T^{\text{(FT)}} = \arg\min_{\theta} \mathcal{L}_T(f_{\theta}(x), y), \quad \theta_{l} \text{ updated for } l > l_{\text{freeze}}$$

### 2.6 Decision Matrix: Which Strategy to Use?

| Available Data Size | Similarity to ImageNet | Recommended Strategy | Reasoning |
|---------------------|------------------------|----------------------|-----------|
| Small ($\vert \mathcal{D}_T \vert \leq 1000$) | High | **Feature Extraction** | Avoid overfitting; preserve pretrained features |
| Small | Low | **Feature Extraction + Data Augmentation** | Domain shift too large to fine-tune safely |
| Large ($\vert \mathcal{D}_T \vert \geq 10{,}000$) | High | **Fine-Tuning** | Sufficient data to safely update weights |
| Large | Low | **Fine-Tuning from scratch end-to-end** | Full retraining may be justified |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

Pretrained vision models form the backbone of:
- **Medical imaging diagnostics** (tumor detection in X-rays with only hundreds of labeled samples)
- **Autonomous vehicle perception** (object detection in novel weather conditions)
- **Satellite imagery analysis** (deforestation tracking, crop disease)
- **Industrial defect inspection** (manufacturing quality control)
- **Facial recognition systems** (security and authentication)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Softmax Activation from Logits

**Problem:** Given raw network output logits $z = [z_1, z_2, \ldots, z_K]$, derive the softmax function.

**Step 1:** We require a function that maps real-valued logits to a valid probability distribution:
$$p_i \in [0, 1] \quad \text{and} \quad \sum_{i=1}^{K} p_i = 1$$

**Step 2:** Apply the exponential to enforce non-negativity. Let $u_i = e^{z_i} > 0$.

**Step 3:** Normalize by dividing by the sum to enforce the unity constraint:

$$p_i = \frac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{z_j}}$$

**Step 4:** Numerical stability improvement — subtract the maximum logit $M = \max(z)$ to prevent overflow:

$$p_i = \frac{e^{z_i - M}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{z_j - M}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This stability trick is **frequently asked** in KTU exams. Always mention it for full marks.

### 3.2 Derivation: Cross-Entropy Loss Gradient (Backpropagation)

**Problem:** Derive $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_{\text{CE}}}{\partial z_i}$ where $\mathcal{L}_{\text{CE}} = -\sum_k y_k \log(\hat{y}_k)$ and $\hat{y}_k = \text{softmax}(z_k)$.

**Step 1:** Apply the chain rule:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_{\text{CE}}}{\partial z_i} = -\sum_k \frac{y_k}{\hat{y}_k} \cdot \frac{\partial \hat{y}_k}{\partial z_i}$$

**Step 2:** Compute $\frac{\partial \hat{y}_k}{\partial z_i}$ considering two cases.

**Case 1:** $k = i$ (diagonal term):

$$\frac{\partial \hat{y}_i}{\partial z_i} = \hat{y}_i(1 - \hat{y}_i)$$

**Case 2:** $k \neq i$ (off-diagonal term):

$$\frac{\partial \hat{y}_k}{\partial z_i} = -\hat{y}_k \hat{y}_i$$

**Step 3:** Substitute back and combine cases using a unified indicator:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_{\text{CE}}}{\partial z_i} = -\sum_k y_k \left(\mathbb{1}_{[k=i]} - \hat{y}_i\right) = \hat{y}_i - y_i$$

**Final Elegant Result:**

$$\boxed{\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_{\text{CE}}}{\partial z_i} = \hat{y}_i - y_i}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This elegant gradient (predicted minus actual) is why cross-entropy pairs so naturally with softmax. **Memorize this result** — it appears in nearly every deep learning question.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example: Forward Pass Through VGG-16 Block

**Problem:** Compute the output volume size when an input image of $224 \times 224 \times 3$ passes through the first VGG-16 conv block (two conv layers with $k=3$, $p=1$, $s=1$, followed by max-pool with $k=2$, $s=2$).

**Step 1:** Input dimensions: $H_{\text{in}} = 224$, $W_{\text{in}} = 224$, $C_{\text{in}} = 3$

**Step 2:** First Conv2D layer (64 filters, $k=3$, $p=1$, $s=1$):

$$H_1 = \left\lfloor \frac{224 + 2(1) - 3}{1} \right\rfloor + 1 = \left\lfloor \frac{223}{1} \right\rfloor + 1 = 224$$

**Step 3:** Second Conv2D layer (64 filters, $k=3$, $p=1$, $s=1$):

$$H_2 = \left\lfloor \frac{224 + 2(1) - 3}{1} \right\rfloor + 1 = 224$$

**Step 4:** MaxPool2D ($k=2$, $s=2$):

$$H_{\text{out}} = \left\lfloor \frac{224 + 0 - 2}{2} \right\rfloor + 1 = 111 + 1 = 112$$

**Result:** Output volume is $112 \times 112 \times 64$ with **0 parameters from input side**, computed as:

$$P_{\text{conv1}} = (3^2 \cdot 3 \cdot 64) + 64 = 1728 + 64 = 1792$$

$$P_{\text{conv2}} = (3^2 \cdot 64 \cdot 64) + 64 = 36864 + 64 = 36928$$

$$P_{\text{block1}} = 1792 + 36928 = 38720 \text{ parameters}$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Popular Pretrained Architectures

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This comparison table is **extremely high-yield** for KTU exams.

| Architecture | Year | Parameters | Top-1 ImageNet | Depth | Key Innovation |
|--------------|------|------------|----------------|-------|----------------|
| **AlexNet** | 2012 | 60 M | 63.3% | 8 | ReLU + GPU training + Dropout |
| **VGG-16** | 2014 | 138 M | 71.3% | 16 | Uniform $3 \times 3$ kernels |
| **VGG-19** | 2014 | 143 M | 71.3% | 19 | Deeper uniform stacking |
| **GoogLeNet (Inception v1)** | 2014 | 6.8 M | 69.8% | 22 | Inception modules; $1 \times 1$ bottleneck |
| **ResNet-50** | 2015 | 25.6 M | 76.0% | 50 | Residual skip connections |
| **ResNet-152** | 2015 | 60.2 M | 77.6% | 152 | Very deep residual learning |
| **Inception v3** | 2016 | 23.8 M | 78.8% | 48 | Factorized convolutions |
| **MobileNet v2** | 2018 | 3.5 M | 72.0% | 53 | Depthwise separable convolutions |
| **EfficientNet-B0** | 2019 | 5.3 M | 77.3% | 18 | Compound scaling of depth/width/resolution |

### 3.5 Derivation: The Skip Connection in ResNet

**Problem:** Show how residual connections solve the vanishing gradient problem.

**Standard Plain Network Block:**

$$H(x) = \mathcal{F}(x, \{W_i\})$$

**Residual Block with Skip Connection:**

$$H(x) = \mathcal{F}(x, \{W_i\}) + x$$

**Backward Pass Gradient:**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial H} \cdot \left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{F}}{\partial x} + 1\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> The crucial term **"$\mathbf{+1}$"** guarantees that the gradient never vanishes because it adds a unit pass-through path, ensuring signal flow even when $\frac{\partial \mathcal{F}}{\partial x} \approx 0$.

### 3.6 KTU Analytical Q&A Style Worked Problem

**Problem:** A pretrained ResNet-50 is used to extract features from a $224 \times 224$ image. The final convolutional layer outputs a $7 \times 7 \times 2048$ feature map. Calculate the parameters required for a new classification head with 5 classes (after Global Average Pooling).

**Step 1:** After Global Average Pooling, feature vector dimension = **2048**.

**Step 2:** Dense layer 1: $2048 \rightarrow 256$ neurons:

$$P_1 = (2048 \times 256) + 256 = 524288 + 256 = 524544$$

**Step 3:** Output Dense layer: $256 \rightarrow 5$ classes (softmax):

$$P_2 = (256 \times 5) + 5 = 1280 + 5 = 1285$$

**Total New Head Parameters:**

$$P_{\text{head}} = 524544 + 1285 = 525829 \text{ parameters}$$

**Comparison:** ResNet-50 has ~25.6 M parameters; we only train **~526 K**, which is just **~2.05%** of the total — illustrating the computational elegance of transfer learning.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Pretrained CNN Feature Extraction Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Image 224x224x3] --> B[Conv Block 1 - 64 filters]
    B --> C[MaxPool 112x112x64]
    C --> D[Conv Block 2 - 128 filters]
    D --> E[MaxPool 56x56x128]
    E --> F[Conv Block 3 - 256 filters]
    F --> G[MaxPool 28x28x256]
    G --> H[Conv Block 4 - 512 filters]
    H --> I[MaxPool 14x14x512]
    I --> J[Conv Block 5 - 512 filters]
    J --> K[MaxPool 7x7x512]
    K --> L[Flatten Layer]
    L --> M[Dense Layer 4096]
    M --> N[Dense Layer 4096]
    N --> O[Softmax Output 1000 classes]
    P[Frozen Base] --> B
    P --> C
    P --> D
    P --> E
    P --> F
    P --> G
    P --> H
    P --> I
    P --> J
    P --> K
    Q[Trainable Head] --> L
    Q --> M
    Q --> N
    Q --> O
```

### 4.2 Transfer Learning Strategy Selection Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start - Have Pretrained Model] --> B{Is Dataset Small?}
    B -->|Yes - less than 1000 images| C{Similar to ImageNet Domain?}
    B -->|No - more than 10000 images| D{Fine-tune Carefully}
    C -->|Yes - similar domain| E[Strategy A - Feature Extraction]
    C -->|No - different domain| F[Strategy A with Augmentation]
    D -->|Yes - similar domain| G[Strategy B - Fine-tune Top Layers]
    D -->|No - different domain| H[Strategy C - Full Fine-tuning]
    E --> I[Freeze Entire Base]
    F --> J[Freeze Base plus Heavy Augmentation]
    G --> K[Unfreeze Top Blocks plus Small LR]
    H --> L[Unfreeze All plus Discriminative LR]
    I --> M[Train Classifier Head Only]
    J --> M
    K --> N[Train Head plus Top Convs]
    L --> O[Train Entire Network]
```

### 4.3 Fine-Tuning vs. Feature Extraction Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph featureExtraction[Strategy A - Feature Extraction]
        A1[Input Image] --> A2[Frozen Pretrained Base]
        A2 --> A3[Trainable New Classifier]
        A3 --> A4[Predictions]
    end
    subgraph fineTuning[Strategy B - Fine-Tuning]
        B1[Input Image] --> B2[Unfrozen Top Convolutional Blocks]
        B2 --> B3[Trainable Classifier]
        B3 --> B4[Predictions]
    end
    subgraph fullRetrain[Strategy C - Full Retraining]
        C1[Input Image] --> C2[Fully Unfrozen Base]
        C2 --> C3[New Classifier Head]
        C3 --> C4[Predictions]
    end
```

### 4.4 ResNet Skip Connection Architecture Block

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input x] --> B[Conv2D 1x1]
    B --> C[BatchNorm]
    C --> D[ReLU]
    D --> E[Conv2D 3x3]
    E --> F[BatchNorm]
    F --> G[ReLU]
    G --> H[Conv2D 1x1]
    H --> I[BatchNorm]
    A --> J[Identity Skip Path]
    I --> K[Element-wise Addition]
    J --> K
    K --> L[ReLU Output]
    L --> M[Next Block]
```

### 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Pretrained Model Deployment

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Source Domain - ImageNet 1.4M Images] --> B[Long Training Process - Days]
    B --> C[Pretrained Weights File - .h5 or .pt]
    C --> D[Load Weights into New Model]
    E[Target Domain - Custom Images] --> F[Data Preprocessing - Resize, Normalize]
    F --> D
    D --> G[Replace Classifier Head]
    G --> H[Phase 1 - Train Head Only]
    H --> I[Phase 2 - Unfreeze Top Layers]
    I --> J[Phase 3 - Joint Fine-tuning]
    J --> K[Deployed Inference Model]
    K --> L[Vision Application - Detect, Classify, Segment]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Define a pretrained model in computer vision. List any two popular pretrained architectures used for image classification.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **pretrained model** is a deep convolutional neural network whose weights have already been learned on a large benchmark dataset (commonly **ImageNet** with 1.4 million images across 1000 categories) and can be reused for related vision tasks through transfer learning.

Two popular pretrained architectures:

1. **VGG-16** — A 16-layer network with uniform $3 \times 3$ convolutions, known for its simplicity and 138 million parameters.

2. **ResNet-50** — A 50-layer network using **residual skip connections** that allow training of very deep networks without vanishing gradients. Contains 25.6 million parameters.

*(Alternative valid answers: AlexNet, GoogLeNet/Inception, MobileNet, EfficientNet — 1 mark each)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Differentiate between feature extraction and fine-tuning in the context of pretrained models.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Feature Extraction | Fine-Tuning |
|--------|-------------------|-------------|
| **Convolutional Base** | Frozen (weights not updated) | Partially or fully unfrozen |
| **Trainable Parameters** | Only new classifier head | Head + top convolutional layers |
| **Computation Cost** | Low | Moderate to High |
| **Data Requirement** | Very small datasets work | Needs moderate to large data |
| **Risk of Overfitting** | Minimal | Higher if data is small |
| **Learning Rate** | Default ($\approx 10^{-3}$) | Very small ($\approx 10^{-4}$ to $10^{-5}$) |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the architecture of VGG-16 with a neat block diagram. Discuss why uniform $3 \times 3$ convolutions are preferred over larger kernels. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A pretrained VGG-16 model is fine-tuned on a custom 4-class dataset. The original VGG-16 has a 1000-class softmax classifier. Describe the step-by-step procedure to adapt this model for the new task, including the rationale for the learning rate choice. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a):

**Architecture of VGG-16 (7 Marks):**

VGG-16 consists of **13 convolutional layers** and **3 fully connected layers** (16 weight layers total), organized into 5 convolutional blocks:

**Block 1:** Two Conv layers with 64 filters ($3 \times 3$, $p=1$, $s=1$) → MaxPool $2 \times 2$ → Output: $112 \times 112 \times 64$

**Block 2:** Two Conv layers with 128 filters ($3 \times 3$) → MaxPool $2 \times 2$ → Output: $56 \times 56 \times 128$

**Block 3:** Three Conv layers with 256 filters ($3 \times 3$) → MaxPool $2 \times 2$ → Output: $28 \times 28 \times 256$

**Block 4:** Three Conv layers with 512 filters ($3 \times 3$) → MaxPool $2 \times 2$ → Output: $14 \times 14 \times 512$

**Block 5:** Three Conv layers with 512 filters ($3 \times 3$) → MaxPool $2 \times 2$ → Output: $7 \times 7 \times 512$

**Classifier:** Flatten → Dense(4096) → Dense(4096) → Dense(1000) with softmax.

**Why $3 \times 3$ kernels? (3 Marks):**

1. **Equivalent receptive field with fewer parameters:** Two stacked $3 \times 3$ convs have a receptive field of $5 \times 5$ but use only $2 \times 9 = 18$ weights versus $25$ for a single $5 \times 5$ filter.

2. **More non-linearity:** Two ReLU activations instead of one, increasing the network's representational power.

3. **Better gradient flow:** Smaller kernels mean less drastic weight changes during backpropagation.

4. **Empirically validated:** VGG's experiments demonstrated that depth with small kernels outperforms shallower networks with larger kernels.

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b):

**Step-by-Step Fine-Tuning Procedure (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Load pretrained VGG-16 without the classifier head**
Use weights from ImageNet training, excluding the top dense layers.

**Step 2: Freeze the convolutional base**
Set `trainable = False` for all conv layers to preserve learned low-level features.

**Step 3: Add a new classifier head for 4 classes**
- GlobalAveragePooling2D layer
- Dense(256, activation='relu')
- Dropout(0.5)
- Dense(4, activation='softmax')

**Step 4: Train the head with default learning rate $\eta = 10^{-3}$**
This allows the randomly initialized head to converge in a few epochs (typically 5-10) without disturbing the pretrained base.

**Step 5: Unfreeze top convolutional blocks (Block 5 and optionally Block 4)**
This allows the network to adapt higher-level features to the new domain.

**Step 6: Recompile with a much smaller learning rate $\eta = 10^{-5}$**
This is **critical** because large gradients would destroy the carefully calibrated pretrained weights. A small learning rate enables gentle adaptation.

**Step 7: Continue training with early stopping and learning rate scheduling**
Monitor validation loss; reduce LR by factor 0.1 on plateau.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> Students commonly lose **2-3 marks** by:
> - Forgetting to set `trainable=False` before initial training (causes catastrophic forgetting)
> - Using the same learning rate for head and fine-tuning phases (destroys pretrained weights)
> - Replacing softmax with sigmoid for multi-class (use softmax for mutually exclusive classes)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the concept of **Residual Learning** in ResNet. Show mathematically how skip connections prevent vanishing gradients. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A pretrained ResNet-50 is to be used for a medical X-ray classification task with only 800 labeled images across 3 classes (Normal, Pneumonia, COVID). Design a complete transfer learning pipeline including preprocessing, augmentation, and training strategy with justification. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a):

**Residual Learning Concept (4 Marks):**

In traditional deep networks, each layer learns a direct mapping $H(x)$. However, **He et al. (2015)** reformulated this to learn a **residual function** $F(x) = H(x) - x$, which is easier to optimize.

The output becomes:

$$H(x) = F(x, \{W_i\}) + x$$

where $F(x, \{W_i\})$ is the stacked convolutional output and $x$ is the identity skip connection.

**Mathematical Gradient Analysis (3 Marks):**

During backpropagation, applying the chain rule:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial H} \cdot \frac{\partial H}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial H} \cdot \left(\frac{\partial F}{\partial x} + 1\right)$$

The crucial additive **$+1$** ensures that even when $\frac{\partial F}{\partial x} \rightarrow 0$ (which happens in very deep networks causing vanishing gradients), the gradient never becomes zero:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x} \geq \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial H}$$

This identity shortcut provides an **uninterrupted gradient highway**, enabling training of networks with **hundreds or thousands of layers** without degradation.

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b):

**Transfer Learning Pipeline for Medical X-Ray Classification (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Data Preprocessing (1 Mark)**
- Resize all images to $224 \times 224 \times 3$ (ResNet-50 input size)
- Convert grayscale X-rays to 3-channel by channel replication (required because ResNet expects 3 channels)
- Pixel normalization: divide by 255 to scale values to $[0, 1]$, then apply ImageNet mean $[0.485, 0.456, 0.406]$ and std $[0.229, 0.224, 0.225]$ standardization

**Step 2: Data Augmentation (2 Marks)**
- Random horizontal flip (X-rays are mirror-symmetric for lungs)
- Random rotation $\pm 15°$ (handles patient positioning variation)
- Random zoom (0.85 to 1.15)
- Brightness adjustment $\pm 20\%$ (handles different X-ray machine calibrations)
- **Justification:** With only 800 images, augmentation effectively expands the dataset 5-10× and prevents overfitting

**Step 3: Model Architecture (1 Mark)**
- Load ResNet-50 pretrained on ImageNet (excluding top)
- Freeze all convolutional layers
- Add: GlobalAveragePooling2D → Dense(128, ReLU) → Dropout(0.5) → Dense(3, softmax)

**Step 4: Training Strategy (2 Marks)**
- **Phase 1:** Train only the head for 10 epochs with $\eta = 10^{-3}$, Adam optimizer, categorical cross-entropy loss
- **Phase 2:** Unfreeze the top 30 layers (last ResNet block) and fine-tune for 20 epochs with $\eta = 10^{-5}$
- Use class weighting because medical datasets often have class imbalance
- Apply early stopping on validation loss with patience = 5
- Use learning rate reduction on plateau (factor 0.5)

**Step 5: Justification (1 Mark)**
- Feature extraction (frozen base) is appropriate because 800 images is insufficient to update 25.6 M parameters
- The pretrained low-level features (edges, textures) transfer excellently to X-ray analysis
- Fine-tuning the top layers allows adaptation to medical-specific high-level features (lung opacities, infiltrates)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> Common mistakes students make:
> - Forgetting to convert grayscale X-rays to 3-channel format (raises a runtime shape-mismatch error)
> - Using **sigmoid** instead of **softmax** for 3 mutually exclusive classes
> - Not applying class weights when COVID samples are rare, leading to biased predictions
> - Training the entire network from scratch with 800 images, which causes severe overfitting

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exams:**

- [x] **Pretrained model** = Network pre-trained on large dataset (typically ImageNet) used as starting point for new tasks
- [x] **ImageNet statistics:** 1.4 M images, 1000 classes, $224 \times 224$ standard input size
- [x] **Transfer learning** has two modes: **Feature Extraction** (freeze base) and **Fine-Tuning** (unfreeze top layers)
- [x] **VGG-16:** 16 layers, uniform $3 \times 3$ kernels, 138 M parameters, 71.3% top-1 ImageNet accuracy
- [x] **ResNet-50:** 50 layers, residual skip connections, 25.6 M parameters, 76.0% top-1 ImageNet accuracy
- [x] **Residual gradient formula:** $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial H}\left(\frac{\partial F}{\partial x} + 1\right)$ — the **$+1$** prevents vanishing gradients
- [x] **Softmax:** $\sigma(z_i) = \frac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_j e^{z_j}}$ — converts logits to probabilities summing to 1
- [x] **Cross-entropy gradient:** $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_{\text{CE}}}{\partial z_i} = \hat{y}_i - y_i$ (predicted minus actual)
- [x] **Conv output size:** $O = \frac{I + 2p - k}{s} + 1$
- [x] **Small dataset rule:** Use feature extraction (freeze base), not fine-tuning, to avoid overfitting
- [x] **Fine-tuning learning rate:** $\eta = 10^{-5}$ to $10^{-4}$ (much smaller than from-scratch $\eta = 10^{-3}$)
- [x] **ReLU activation:** $\text{ReLU}(x) = \max(0, x)$ — non-saturating, fast convergence
- [x] **Global Average Pooling:** Replaces flatten+dense by averaging each feature map — drastically reduces parameters
- [x] **Batch Normalization:** $\hat{x} = \frac{x - \mu}{\sqrt{\sigma^2 + \epsilon}}$ — stabilizes and accelerates training
- [x] **Key rule for small datasets:** Always replace the classifier head with a new one matching your number of target classes
- [x] **Skip connections** (ResNet) enable training of networks with 100+ layers that would otherwise be untrainable

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "PIE" for Pretrained Models:**
> - **P** = Pretrained on massive dataset
> - **I** = Initialize your model with these weights
> - **E** = Either freeze (extract) or Enhance (fine-tune)
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transfer Learning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST745 - COMPUTER VISION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Transfer Learning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Transfer Learning (TL)** is a deep learning paradigm in computer vision wherein a neural network model developed for a *source task* is repurposed as the starting point (initialization or feature extractor) for a model on a *related target task*. It exploits the principle that visual features learned from large-scale datasets (such as **ImageNet** with **1.4 million images across 1000 classes**) generalize across related visual domains, thereby reducing the need for massive labeled datasets and computational resources on the target problem.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECST745 – Module 3):** Transfer learning is examined under deep learning architectures, specifically under the context of CNN pre-trained models and their adaptation strategies. Students must distinguish between **Feature Extraction** and **Fine-Tuning** with surgical precision.

Mathematically, transfer learning involves a **Source Domain** $\mathcal{D}_S$ with learning task $\mathcal{T}_S$ and a **Target Domain** $\mathcal{D}_T$ with learning task $\mathcal{T}_T$, where knowledge from $(\mathcal{D}_S, \mathcal{T}_S)$ is transferred to improve the learning of the predictive function $f_T(\cdot)$ in $\mathcal{D}_T$, under the constraint that $\mathcal{D}_S \neq \mathcal{D}_T$ or $\mathcal{T}_S \neq \mathcal{T}_T$.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Apprentice Chef Analogy 🍳**
>
> Imagine a master chef who has spent **20 years** mastering French cuisine (the *source task*). Now, if you ask this chef to learn Italian cooking (the *target task*), they do not need to start from scratch. They already understand knife skills, heat control, sauce reduction, and flavor balancing. They only need to learn a few **Italian-specific recipes and ingredient combinations**.
>
> In deep learning, this is exactly what Transfer Learning does. The convolutional layers act as the "master chef's fundamental skills" (edge detection, texture recognition, shape identification), while the final fully-connected layers (the "recipe memorization") are retrained to suit the new task.

- **Source Task (Pre-training)**: Trained on massive data (e.g., ImageNet classification).
- **Target Task (Downstream)**: A specialized task (e.g., medical X-ray classification, weed detection, satellite image segmentation) with **limited data**.

### 1.3 Why Transfer Learning is Indispensable in Computer Vision

| Bottleneck in Real CV Projects | How Transfer Learning Solves It |
|-------------------------------|----------------------------------|
| Scarcity of labeled image data | Leverages features from millions of pre-existing labeled images |
| High GPU cost (training ResNet-50 from scratch takes ~**2 weeks on 4 GPUs**) | Fine-tuning requires only **minutes to hours** on a single GPU |
| Overfitting on small datasets | Pre-trained weights act as a strong **inductive bias / regularizer** |
| Need for rapid prototyping | Industry standard for fast deployment in production CV systems |

### 1.4 Pre-trained Architectures Frequently Cited in KTU Examinations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following architectures are **frequently tested** as named entities in KTU ESE papers:
> - **VGG-16 / VGG-19** – Oxford Visual Geometry Group, 2014
> - **ResNet-50 / ResNet-101** – Microsoft Research, 2015 (Residual connections)
> - **InceptionV3 (GoogLeNet)** – Google, 2014 (Multi-scale filters)
> - **MobileNetV2** – Google, 2018 (Lightweight for edge devices)
> - **EfficientNet (B0–B7)** – Google, 2019 (Compound scaling)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Operational Workflow of Transfer Learning

The complete pipeline from a pre-trained model to a deployed custom classifier follows these structured steps:

- **Step 1 — Selection of Source Model (Backbone)**
  Choose a CNN architecture pre-trained on a large visual corpus. The convolutional base is treated as a **universal feature extractor**.

- **Step 2 — Freezing of Convolutional Base**
  The weights of the convolutional layers are **frozen** (made non-trainable) to preserve the generic low-level and mid-level visual features (edges, corners, textures, object parts) that the network has already mastered.

- **Step 3 — Replacement of Classification Head**
  The original fully-connected (FC) "head" (designed for, say, 1000 ImageNet classes) is **removed and discarded**. A new FC head is appended, sized to match the number of classes in the target task (e.g., 2 classes for cats vs. dogs).

- **Step 4 — Feature Extraction Phase (Optional Initial Pass)**
  The target dataset is passed through the frozen convolutional base to compute and cache **bottleneck features**. This converts a CV problem into a much faster ML problem on flat feature vectors.

- **Step 5 — Fine-Tuning Phase (Optional but Powerful)**
  Selectively **unfreeze** the top few convolutional layers (closer to the output) and continue training with a **very low learning rate** ($\eta \approx 10^{-5}$ to $10^{-4}$). This allows the network to adapt higher-level features to the target domain without catastrophically erasing the pre-trained knowledge — a phenomenon known as **catastrophic forgetting**.

- **Step 6 — Deployment**
  The fine-tuned model is validated on the held-out test set and deployed.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind Freezing**
> Early convolutional layers learn highly **generic, transferable** features (Gabor-like edge filters, color blobs). These are useful across virtually all vision tasks. The deeper layers learn increasingly **task-specific** features, which is precisely where fine-tuning is most beneficial.

### 2.2 Two Principal Strategies: A Critical Distinction

| Aspect | **Feature Extraction** | **Fine-Tuning** |
|---|---|---|
| Convolutional base | **Frozen** (weights not updated) | **Partially or fully unfrozen** |
| Computational cost | **Low** (only FC head trains) | **Higher** (more parameters update) |
| Data requirement | Works well with **small** datasets ($\leq 1000$ images) | Requires **moderate** datasets (1000s–10,000s of images) |
| Risk of overfitting | **Low** | Moderate; use dropout and data augmentation |
| Learning rate for backbone | Not applicable | **10× to 100× smaller** than the head's learning rate |
| Use case | Custom cat-vs-dog, defect detection with few images | Medical imaging, satellite, autonomous driving |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Source / Target Domain | $\mathcal{D}_S = \{x_i, y_i\}_{i=1}^{N_S}$, $\mathcal{D}_T = \{x_j, y_j\}_{j=1}^{N_T}$ | The two data distributions involved |
| CNN Forward Pass | $f(x) = \sigma(W^{(L)} * \cdots * \sigma(W^{(1)} * x + b^{(1)}) + b^{(L)})$ | Layer-wise transformation with activation $\sigma$ |
| Frozen Layer Gradient | $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W^{(l)}} = 0, \quad \forall l \in \mathcal{F}$ | Weights in frozen set $\mathcal{F}$ receive **zero update** |
| SGD Weight Update (Trainable) | $W^{(l)}_{t+1} = W^{(l)}_{t} - \eta_t \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W^{(l)}_{t}}$ | Standard gradient descent for unfrozen layers |
| Discriminative Learning Rate | $\eta_{\text{head}} = 10^{-3}, \quad \eta_{\text{backbone}} = 10^{-5}$ | Head learns fast, backbone learns slowly |
| Cross-Entropy Loss (Classification) | $\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{i=1}^{C} y_i \log(\hat{y}_i)$ | Used for the new classification head |
| Transfer Learning Performance Bound | $E_T(h) \leq E_S(h) + d(\mathcal{D}_S, \mathcal{D}_T) + \lambda$ | Covariate-shift-inspired bound (informal) |
| Fine-tune Trigger Rule | If $N_T \geq 1000$ and $\text{sim}(\mathcal{D}_S, \mathcal{D}_T) \approx 1$ | Heuristic to decide if fine-tuning is worthwhile |
| Output Dimension of New Head | $\mathbb{R}^{B \times C_T}$ | $B$ = batch size, $C_T$ = target classes |
| Parameter Reduction via Freezing | $\text{Trainable \%} = \frac{\mid \mathcal{U} \mid}{\mid \mathcal{U} \mid + \mid \mathcal{F} \mid} \times 100$ | $\mathcal{U}$ = unfrozen, $\mathcal{F}$ = frozen parameter count |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Transfer learning is the **de-facto industry standard** in 2024 for medical imaging (tumor segmentation), agriculture (crop disease detection), autonomous vehicles (obstacle classification), and even NLP (BERT, GPT variants are TL of LLMs). Mastery of this concept is mandatory for any computer vision engineer.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Medical Imaging (CheXNet)**: Stanford's pneumonia detector used **DenseNet-121** pre-trained on ImageNet, then fine-tuned on chest X-rays.
- **Wildfire Detection**: Fire and smoke classifiers often start from **EfficientNet-B3** with frozen early layers.
- **Industrial Quality Control**: Surface defect detection on manufacturing lines uses **MobileNetV2** for on-edge inference.
- **Satellite Imagery (Remote Sensing)**: Land-cover classification with **ResNet-50** pre-trained on ImageNet.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Mathematical Foundation: Why Pre-trained Features Generalize

Let us rigorously derive the rationale behind transfer learning by examining the **loss decomposition** during fine-tuning.

Consider a pre-trained model with parameters $W^{(0)}$ obtained by minimizing:

$$
\mathcal{L}_S(W) = \frac{1}{N_S} \sum_{i=1}^{N_S} \ell(f_W(x_i^S), y_i^S) + \Omega(W)
$$

where $\Omega(W)$ is a regularizer. The model converges to $W^* = \arg\min_W \mathcal{L}_S(W)$.

On the target task, we begin from $W^{(0)} = W^*$ and minimize:

$$
\mathcal{L}_T(W) = \frac{1}{N_T} \sum_{j=1}^{N_T} \ell(f_W(x_j^T), y_j^T) + \Omega(W)
$$

Using a **Taylor expansion** around the pre-trained optimum $W^*$, we get:

$$
\mathcal{L}_T(W) \approx \mathcal{L}_T(W^*) + \nabla \mathcal{L}_T(W^*)^{\top} (W - W^*) + \frac{1}{2} (W - W^*)^{\top} H_T (W - W^*)
$$

Here $H_T$ is the **Hessian** of the target loss. The first-order gradient term $\nabla \mathcal{L}_T(W^*)$ measures the **mismatch** between source and target gradients. When the source and target tasks are related, this gradient is small, so initialization near $W^*$ is favorable.

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Gradient Update with Discriminative Learning Rates

This is the most critical derivation for KTU examination purposes. Given a network with frozen layers in set $\mathcal{F}$ and unfrozen layers in set $\mathcal{U}$:

**Step 1 — Identify layer groups**

Partition parameters into $W = \{W^{(l)} : l \in \mathcal{F}\} \cup \{W^{(l)} : l \in \mathcal{U}\}$.

**Step 2 — Compute the gradient of the loss with respect to each layer**

For frozen layers $l \in \mathcal{F}$:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_T}{\partial W^{(l)}} = 0 \quad \text{(gradients are computed but discarded)}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply differential learning rates**

For unfrozen layers $l \in \mathcal{U}$:

$$
W^{(l)}_{t+1} = W^{(l)}_{t} - \eta_l \cdot \nabla_{W^{(l)}} \mathcal{L}_T
$$

where typically:

$$
\eta_l = \begin{cases} \eta_{\text{backbone}} = 10^{-5}, & l \in \text{deep conv layers} \\ \eta_{\text{head}} = 10^{-3}, & l \in \text{new FC head} \end{cases}
$$

**Step 4 — Iterate until convergence**

Repeat Step 3 for $T$ epochs until the validation loss plateaus.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Decision Flow for Choosing a TL Strategy

The following table provides the **examination-ready decision matrix** that students must reproduce:

| Condition | Recommended Strategy | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| $N_T < 500$ (very small dataset) | **Feature Extraction** only | Insufficient data to fine-tune large backbones safely |
| $500 \leq N_T < 5000$ | Feature Extraction → light Fine-Tuning | Two-stage: warm up head, then unfreeze top 1–2 conv blocks |
| $N_T \geq 5000$ | **Full Fine-Tuning** possible | Enough data to update entire network |
| Target images visually similar to ImageNet (natural scenes) | Lower layers stay frozen | Early filters (edges, colors) are already optimal |
| Target images dissimilar to ImageNet (X-rays, microscopy, SAR) | Unfreeze **more** layers | Domain shift requires adapting mid-level features |
| Limited GPU memory | Use **MobileNetV2 / EfficientNet-B0** | Smaller parameter footprint |
| Need >95% accuracy on benchmark | Use **EfficientNet-B5 or larger** + full fine-tuning | Higher capacity models win on big data |

### 3.4 Exhaustive Worked Example: Fine-Tuning VGG-16 on a 3-Class Custom Dataset

**Given:**
- Pre-trained: **VGG-16** on ImageNet (1000 classes, **138 million parameters**).
- Target: 3 classes, training set $N_T = 900$ images (300 per class).
- GPU: single NVIDIA T4 (**16 GB VRAM**).

**Solution Walkthrough:**

**Step 1 — Load VGG-16 without the top (FC) layers**

The convolutional base outputs a feature map of shape $7 \times 7 \times 512$ per image.

**Step 2 — Freeze all 13 convolutional layers**

Total frozen parameters: $\mid \mathcal{F} \vert = 14,714,688$.

**Step 3 — Append a new classification head**

$$
\text{Head} = \text{Flatten}(512 \times 7 \times 7) \rightarrow \text{Dense}(256, \text{ReLU}) \rightarrow \text{Dropout}(0.5) \rightarrow \text{Dense}(3, \text{Softmax})
$$

Trainable parameters of new head: $\mid \mathcal{U} \vert = 6,423,299$.

**Step 4 — Train head for 10 epochs**

$$
\eta_{\text{head}} = 10^{-3}, \quad \text{optimizer} = \text{Adam}, \quad \text{batch size} = 32
$$

Validation accuracy after head-only training: **92.4%**.

**Step 5 — Unfreeze the last convolutional block (block5)**

Unfrozen parameters added: $\mid \mathcal{U}_{\text{new}} \vert = 7,635,264$. Frozen: $\mid \mathcal{F} \vert = 7,079,424$.

**Step 6 — Fine-tune with discriminative learning rates**

$$
\eta_{\text{head}} = 10^{-4}, \quad \eta_{\text{block5}} = 10^{-5}, \quad \text{epochs} = 20
$$

Final validation accuracy: **96.8%**, confirming the value of selective fine-tuning.

> [!NOTE]
> **Performance gain observed: 92.4% → 96.8%** (a **+4.4 percentage point** uplift) is a typical empirical result reported in transfer learning literature for similar dataset sizes.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow of Transfer Learning

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Large Source Dataset e.g. ImageNet 1.4M images] --> B[Pre-trained CNN Backbone e.g. ResNet-50]
    B --> C[Convolutional Base Frozen Layers]
    B --> D[Original FC Head 1000 classes Discarded]
    C --> E[Append New Classification Head C_T classes]
    E --> F[Stage 1 Feature Extraction train head only]
    F --> G{Dataset size N_T sufficient?}
    G -->|No| H[Stop and Deploy]
    G -->|Yes| I[Stage 2 Selective Unfreeze top conv blocks]
    I --> J[Fine-tune with small learning rate 1e-5]
    J --> K[Validate on held out test set]
    K --> L{Accuracy target met?}
    L -->|No| M[Adjust hyperparameters dropout augmentation]
    M --> I
    L -->|Yes| H
```

### 4.2 Schematic of Frozen vs Unfrozen Layer Partitioning

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Backbone [Pre trained VGG 16 Backbone]
        direction LR
        L1[Conv Block 1 Frozen] --> L2[Conv Block 2 Frozen] --> L3[Conv Block 3 Frozen] --> L4[Conv Block 4 Frozen] --> L5[Conv Block 5 Unfrozen Fine tuned]
    end
    L5 --> FC1[Flatten Layer] --> FC2[Dense 256 ReLU] --> FC3[Dropout 0.5] --> FC4[Dense C_T Softmax]
    style L1 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style L2 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style L3 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style L4 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style L5 fill:#ffd9b3,stroke:#cc6600
    style FC1 fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366
    style FC2 fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366
    style FC3 fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366
    style FC4 fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366
```

### 4.3 Decision Tree: Choosing the Right Transfer Learning Strategy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Start with pre trained CNN] --> Q1{What is the size of target dataset N_T?}
    Q1 -->|N_T less than 500| S1[Feature Extraction only freeze entire backbone]
    Q1 -->|500 to 5000| S2[Feature Extraction then fine tune top 1 or 2 conv blocks]
    Q1 -->|N_T greater than 5000| S3[Full fine tuning of entire network]
    Q1 -->|N_T very large greater than 100000| S4[Consider training from scratch with modern architecture]
    S1 --> End[Deploy model]
    S2 --> End
    S3 --> End
    S4 --> End
```

### 4.4 Knowledge Flow from Source to Target Domain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Source Domain ImageNet] -->|Pre training| B[Source Model W star]
    B -->|Transfer W| C[Target Domain Custom Task]
    C --> D[Initialize Target Model with W]
    D --> E[Fine tune on N_T samples]
    E --> F[Adapted Target Model]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define Transfer Learning. Mention any two pre-trained CNN architectures used in computer vision.*

**Model Answer:**
Transfer Learning is a deep learning technique in which a neural network model trained on a large dataset for a source task is reused as the starting point for a related target task. It reduces training time, data requirements, and computational cost by leveraging previously learned visual features.

Two pre-trained CNN architectures:
1. **VGG-16** – A 16-layer CNN from Oxford's Visual Geometry Group, known for its uniform **3×3** convolutional filters.
2. **ResNet-50** – A 50-layer residual network from Microsoft Research that uses **skip connections** to mitigate vanishing gradients.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]**
*Differentiate between Feature Extraction and Fine-Tuning in transfer learning.*

**Model Answer:**

| Feature Extraction | Fine-Tuning |
|---|---|
| The pre-trained convolutional base is kept **frozen**; only the new classifier head is trained. | A few (or all) layers of the pre-trained network are **unfrozen** and continue training. |
| Used when the target dataset is **very small**. | Used when the target dataset is **moderately sized**. |
| Computationally cheap. | Computationally more expensive. |
| Lower risk of overfitting. | Higher risk of overfitting; needs regularization. |

---

### 5.2 Part B: Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE regulations, every Part B Module question must offer an **internal choice**. Below are TWO independent 14-mark alternatives with sub-parts (a) and (b) each carrying 7 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Expected Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of Transfer Learning in deep learning. Discuss why pre-trained CNN models are useful when the target dataset is small. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Transfer Learning is the process of repurposing a model trained on one task (the source task) to a new but related task (the target task). In computer vision, this typically involves taking a CNN pre-trained on a massive dataset such as **ImageNet (1.4M images, 1000 classes)** and adapting it to a custom classification problem.

**Why pre-trained models are valuable for small datasets:**

1. **Inductive Bias / Feature Reusability** – Early convolutional layers learn universal low-level features (edges, corners, color gradients) that are useful for virtually any image classification task. By using these frozen features, we inherit years of visual learning.

2. **Reduced Data Hunger** – Training a deep CNN from scratch requires millions of labeled images. With transfer learning, even a few hundred to a few thousand images can yield high accuracy.

3. **Regularization Effect** – Pre-trained weights act as an implicit regularizer, preventing the model from overfitting to the small target dataset.

4. **Faster Convergence** – Fine-tuning converges in **20–50 epochs** instead of the **hundreds** required for training from scratch.

5. **Lower Hardware Requirements** – A single consumer-grade GPU is sufficient, democratizing deep learning research.

**(b)** With a neat diagram, describe the step-by-step procedure for fine-tuning a pre-trained CNN for a custom classification problem. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The fine-tuning procedure consists of the following steps:

**Step 1 — Backbone Selection:** Choose a pre-trained CNN such as **ResNet-50** trained on ImageNet. Discard its 1000-class softmax head.

**Step 2 — Freeze Convolutional Base:** Set `requires_grad = False` for all convolutional layers to preserve learned low-level features.

**Step 3 — Append New Head:** Add a custom classifier — for example, `GlobalAveragePooling → Dense(128, ReLU) → Dropout(0.5) → Dense(C_T, Softmax)` where $C_T$ is the number of target classes.

**Step 4 — Train the Head (Warm-up Phase):** Train only the new head for **5–10 epochs** with learning rate $\eta = 10^{-3}$.

**Step 5 — Selective Unfreezing:** Unfreeze the last few convolutional blocks (e.g., `layer4` of ResNet-50).

**Step 6 — Discriminative Learning Rates:** Use $\eta_{\text{backbone}} = 10^{-5}$ and $\eta_{\text{head}} = 10^{-4}$. This protects pre-trained knowledge from catastrophic forgetting.

**Step 7 — Fine-Tune End-to-End:** Train for **20–50 epochs** with early stopping and learning rate scheduling.

**Step 8 — Evaluate and Deploy:** Validate on the held-out test set; if accuracy is satisfactory, export the model.

**Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Image] --> B[Pre trained Conv Base layer1 to layer3 frozen] --> C[Conv layer4 Unfrozen] --> D[Global Avg Pool] --> E[Dense 128 ReLU] --> F[Dropout 0.5] --> G[Dense C_T Softmax] --> H[Class Prediction]
    style B fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style C fill:#ffd9b3,stroke:#cc6600
    style D fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366
    style E fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366
    style F fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366
    style G fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366
```

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Compare and contrast three popular pre-trained CNN architectures (VGG-16, ResNet-50, and InceptionV3) used in transfer learning for image classification. Highlight their key design innovations and typical use cases. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | VGG-16 | ResNet-50 | InceptionV3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Year / Origin** | 2014, Oxford VGG | 2015, Microsoft Research | 2014, Google |
| **Depth** | 16 layers | 50 layers (with skip connections) | 48 layers (with parallel branches) |
| **Key Innovation** | Uniform **3×3 conv** filters stacked deep | **Residual / skip connections** to enable very deep networks | **Inception modules** with multi-scale **1×1, 3×3, 5×5** parallel convolutions |
| **Parameters** | ~138 M | ~25.6 M | ~23.8 M |
| **Top-5 ImageNet Accuracy** | 90.1% | 93.0% | 93.7% |
| **Strengths** | Simple, uniform architecture — easy to understand and modify | Solves vanishing gradient problem; high accuracy | Computationally efficient; captures multi-scale features |
| **Weaknesses** | Very memory-heavy (largest model) | Slightly more complex to implement | Architectural complexity |
| **Typical TL Use Case** | Educational projects, feature extraction baselines | Industry-grade fine-tuning for medical/industrial imaging | Mobile/embedded applications needing efficiency |

**Conclusion:** ResNet-50 is the **most commonly used** backbone for transfer learning in 2024 due to its balance of accuracy and parameter efficiency.

**(b)** Suppose you are given a pre-trained ResNet-50 model and a custom dataset of 5,000 images belonging to 4 classes for a weed detection task in precision agriculture. Design a complete transfer learning pipeline, justifying each design choice. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Pipeline Design:**

**Step 1 — Backbone:** Use **ResNet-50** pre-trained on ImageNet. Justification: it offers excellent accuracy-per-parameter and its residual connections make it robust to fine-tuning.

**Step 2 — Data Augmentation:** Apply aggressive augmentation — random rotations ($\pm 30°$), horizontal flips, color jitter, and random crops. Justification: 5,000 images is modest; augmentation virtually expands the dataset and reduces overfitting.

**Step 3 — Freezing Strategy:** Initially freeze **all conv layers** (residual blocks `conv1` through `conv4`). Justification: weeds share low-level visual features (green textures, leaf edges) with ImageNet's natural images.

**Step 4 — Custom Head:**
$$
\text{Head} = \text{GlobalAvgPool2D} \rightarrow \text{Dense}(256, \text{ReLU}) \rightarrow \text{Dropout}(0.5) \rightarrow \text{Dense}(4, \text{Softmax})
$$
Justification: GAP reduces parameters vs. flatten; dropout mitigates overfitting.

**Step 5 — Phase 1 Training (Head Warm-up):** Train the head for 10 epochs with $\eta = 10^{-3}$, Adam optimizer, batch size 32. Expected accuracy: ~85%.

**Step 6 — Phase 2 Fine-Tuning:** Unfreeze `conv5` block only. Use **discriminative learning rates** — $\eta_{\text{conv5}} = 10^{-5}$ and $\eta_{\text{head}} = 10^{-4}$. Train for 30 more epochs with early stopping.

**Step 7 — Class Imbalance Handling:** If weed classes are imbalanced, use **class-weighted cross-entropy** or **focal loss**:

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{focal}} = -\alpha (1 - \hat{y})^{\gamma} \log(\hat{y}), \quad \gamma = 2
$$

**Step 8 — Evaluation:** Report **precision, recall, F1-score, and confusion matrix** (not just accuracy) since weed detection is a real-world safety-critical task.

**Final Expected Outcome:** ~94–97% validation accuracy with ~30 minutes total training on a single GPU.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Examinations:**
> 1. **Confusing "transfer learning" with "training from scratch"** — Always clarify that pre-trained weights are used as initialization, NOT random initialization.
> 2. **Forgetting to freeze layers during feature extraction** — If you say "feature extraction" but write $W_{t+1} = W_t - \eta \nabla \mathcal{L}$ for *all* layers, you will lose 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Not mentioning the learning rate discrepancy** — Examiners specifically look for the **discriminative learning rate** concept in fine-tuning answers.
> 4. **Omitting diagrams** — A 7-mark sub-question in Part B **must** include a labeled diagram (block-level or flowchart). Skip it, lose 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Writing `|x|` with vertical pipes inside a markdown table** — Use `\vert x \vert` or `abs(x)` instead, as pipe characters break table rendering.
> 6. **Mentioning "BERT" or "GPT" as transfer learning examples** — These are NLP, not computer vision. Stick to VGG, ResNet, Inception, MobileNet, EfficientNet.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📌 **Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- ⭐ **Definition:** Transfer learning reuses a model trained on a **source task** for a **target task** with limited data.
- ⭐ **Pre-trained Backbones (CV):** VGG-16, ResNet-50, InceptionV3, MobileNetV2, EfficientNet.
- ⭐ **Two Strategies:** (1) **Feature Extraction** — freeze conv base; (2) **Fine-Tuning** — unfreeze top conv blocks with a tiny learning rate.
- ⭐ **Discriminative Learning Rate:** $\eta_{\text{backbone}} \ll \eta_{\text{head}}$ (typically $10^{-5}$ vs. $10^{-3}$).
- ⭐ **Catastrophic Forgetting:** Aggressive fine-tuning destroys pre-trained knowledge — prevent it by using low learning rates and gradual unfreezing.
- ⭐ **Decision Heuristic:** $N_T < 500$ → feature extraction only; $500 \leq N_T < 5000$ → light fine-tuning; $N_T \geq 5000$ → full fine-tuning.
- ⭐ **Mathematical Anchor:** $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W^{(l)}} = 0$ for frozen layers; normal SGD update for unfrozen layers.
- ⭐ **Loss Functions:** Cross-entropy for classification; focal loss for imbalanced datasets.
- ⭐ **Industry Examples:** CheXNet (pneumonia detection), Tesla's perception stack, agricultural weed detection, satellite land-cover mapping.
- ⭐ **Data Augmentation is Mandatory** when fine-tuning on small datasets to combat overfitting.
- ⭐ **Always use early stopping and learning rate scheduling** during fine-tuning to avoid overfitting and to converge optimally.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fine-Tuning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST745 - COMPUTER VISION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Fine-Tuning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Fine-Tuning** is a transfer learning paradigm in deep learning wherein the weights of a **pre-trained convolutional neural network (CNN)** — originally trained on a large-scale source dataset (e.g., **ImageNet** with 1.4M images across 1000 classes) — are adapted, partially or completely, to a new but related target task using a comparatively smaller target dataset.

Formally, given a pre-trained model $M_{pre}$ with parameters $\theta_{pre}$ optimized for source task $T_S$ on dataset $D_S$, fine-tuning seeks parameters $\theta_{ft}$ for target task $T_T$ on dataset $D_T$ such that:

$$\theta_{ft} = \arg\min_{\theta} \mathcal{L}_{T_T}(D_T, \theta)$$

where the optimization is initialized from $\theta_{pre}$ (i.e., $\theta \leftarrow \theta_{pre}$) and proceeds with a **low learning rate** $\eta_{ft} \ll \eta_{pre}$ to prevent catastrophic forgetting of previously learned representations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Fine-Tuning is positioned under *Module 3: Deep Learning Architectures for Vision* and is examinable under **CO3 (Apply deep learning models for visual recognition tasks)**. Examiner emphasis is typically on the *mathematical formulation*, *freezing strategy*, and *learning rate scheduling*.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Specialist Doctor"**
> 
> Imagine a **general physician** (the pre-trained model) who has spent 10 years learning the entire human body — anatomy, physiology, common diseases. Now suppose this doctor wants to specialize in **ophthalmology** (the target task). They don't discard their decade of medical knowledge. Instead, they:
> 
> 1. **Retain** the foundational knowledge of the human body (frozen early convolutional layers that learn edges, textures, shapes).
> 2. **Re-learn** only the eye-specific patterns using a smaller dataset of retinal scans (fine-tuning later layers).
> 3. **Adjust** their study intensity — they read medical journals much more slowly than a fresh intern (small learning rate), so they don't overwrite their core knowledge.
>
