| FC2 | Dense | 4096 units + ReLU + Dropout | 4096 |
| FC3 | Dense | 1000 units + Softmax | 1000 |

The network contains **16 conv + 3 FC = 19 weight layers**. All convolutions use ReLU activation and same-padding (p=1) to preserve spatial resolution within a block. Spatial reduction happens **only at the 5 max-pool layers**, while channels double after each pool. The flattened 7×7×512 = 25088 vector feeds the classifier head, which produces 1000 ImageNet class probabilities via softmax. *(7 marks)*

---

**(b)** Compute the **total number of trainable parameters** in VGG19, showing the contribution of each block. Why does the FC head dominate the count? *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

Using $P_{conv} = k^2 C_{in} C_{out} + C_{out}$ and $P_{FC} = N_{in} N_{out} + N_{out}$:

**Convolutional blocks:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Block 1} &= (3\cdot3\cdot3\cdot64+64) + (3\cdot3\cdot64\cdot64+64) = 1792 + 36928 = 38720 \\
\text{Block 2} &= (3\cdot3\cdot64\cdot128+128) + (3\cdot3\cdot128\cdot128+128) = 73856 + 147584 = 221440 \\
\text{Block 3} &= (3\cdot3\cdot128\cdot256+256) + 3\cdot(3\cdot3\cdot256\cdot256+256) \\
              &= 295168 + 3 \cdot 590080 = 2065408 \\
\text{Block 4} &= (3\cdot3\cdot256\cdot512+512) + 3\cdot(3\cdot3\cdot512\cdot512+512) \\
              &= 1180160 + 3 \cdot 2359808 = 8259584 \\
\text{Block 5} &= 4 \cdot (3\cdot3\cdot512\cdot512+512) = 4 \cdot 2359808 = 9439232 \\
P_{conv}^{total} &= 38720 + 221440 + 2065408 + 8259584 + 9439232 = 20024384
\end{aligned}
$$

**Fully Connected head (input = 7×7×512 = 25088):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{FC1} &= 25088 \cdot 4096 + 4096 = 102764544 \\
P_{FC2} &= 4096 \cdot 4096 + 4096 = 16781312 \\
P_{FC3} &= 4096 \cdot 1000 + 1000 = 4097000 \\
P_{FC}^{total} &= 123642856
\end{aligned}
$$

**Grand Total:**

$$
P_{VGG19} = 20024384 + 123642856 = \mathbf{143667240 \; parameters} \;(\approx 143.67M)
$$

The FC head dominates because FC1 alone ingests a 25088-dim flattened vector and projects it to 4096 units, creating a $\approx 102.76$M parameter "bottleneck." FC2 is dense (4096×4096) and FC3 outputs 1000 classes. Together the FC layers account for **~86% of all parameters**, which is why modern architectures replace them with global average pooling. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Question B** — *(14 Marks)* `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **concept of receptive field**. Starting from $RF_0 = 1$, derive the receptive field of a neuron in Block 2 of VGG19 after the second pooling layer. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

The **receptive field (RF)** of a neuron is the region of the original input image that influences its activation. As we move deeper into the network, the RF grows because of successive convolutions and poolings.

**Recurrence formula:**

$$
RF_l = RF_{l-1} + (k_l - 1) \cdot \prod_{i<l} s_i
$$

**Step 1 — Layer 1 (Conv 3×3, s=1):**

$$
RF_1 = 1 + (3 - 1)(1) = 3
$$

**Step 2 — Layer 2 (Conv 3×3, s=1):**

$$
RF_2 = 3 + (3 - 1)(1 \cdot 1) = 5
$$

**Step 3 — Pool 1 (MaxPool 2×2, s=2):**

$$
RF_{pool1} = 5 + (2 - 1)(1 \cdot 1 \cdot 1) = 7
$$

**Step 4 — Layer 3 (Conv 3×3, s=1, cumulative stride product = 2):**

$$
RF_3 = 7 + (3 - 1)(1 \cdot 1 \cdot 1 \cdot 2) = 7 + 4 = 11
$$

**Step 5 — Layer 4 (Conv 3×3, s=1):**

$$
RF_4 = 11 + (3 - 1)(1 \cdot 1 \cdot 1 \cdot 2) = 15
$$

**Step 6 — Pool 2 (MaxPool 2×2, s=2):**

$$
RF_{pool2} = 15 + (2 - 1)(1 \cdot 1 \cdot 1 \cdot 2 \cdot 2) = 19
$$

**Conclusion:** A neuron in Block 2 of VGG19 (just after Pool 2) has a **receptive field of 19 × 19 pixels** of the original input image. *(7 marks)*

---

**(b)** Prove that **stacking two 3×3 convolutions** is more parameter-efficient than using **one 5×5 convolution**, for the same number of input and output channels $C$. Show the saving for $C = 512$. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**One 5×5 convolution** (with $C$ input and $C$ output channels):

$$
P_{5 \times 5} = (5 \cdot 5 \cdot C \cdot C) + C = 25C^2 + C
$$

**Two stacked 3×3 convolutions** (each with $C$ input and $C$ output channels):

$$
P_{3 \times 3 \times 2} = 2 \cdot \big[(3 \cdot 3 \cdot C \cdot C) + C\big] = 2(9C^2 + C) = 18C^2 + 2C
$$

**Parameter saving:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta P &= P_{5 \times 5} - P_{3 \times 3 \times 2} \\
        &= (25C^2 + C) - (18C^2 + 2C) \\
        &= 7C^2 - C = C(7C - 1)
\end{aligned}
$$

For $C = 512$:

$$
\Delta P = 512 \cdot (7 \cdot 512 - 1) = 512 \cdot 3583 = \mathbf{1,834,496 \; parameters \; saved}
$$

Specifically:
- $P_{5\times5} = 25 \cdot 262144 + 512 = 6553600 + 512 = 6554112$
- $P_{3\times3 \times 2} = 18 \cdot 262144 + 1024 = 4718592 + 1024 = 4719616$
- $\Delta P = 6554112 - 4719616 = 1834496$ ✔

Additionally, two 3×3 convs provide **two ReLU non-linearities** instead of one, making the network more expressive despite using fewer parameters. This is the central insight that allowed VGG to deepen its network to 19 layers without a proportional explosion in parameter count. *(7 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the bias term** in parameter calculations costs 1 mark easily. Always add $+C_{out}$ per conv layer and $+N_{out}$ per FC layer.
> 2. **Confusing 3×3 conv with effective 5×5 receptive field** — write clearly that *two stacked* 3×3 layers produce a 5×5 RF, not one 3×3 layer.
> 3. **Mis-counting 19 layers** — the 19 includes 3 FC layers; if you say "16 conv + 3 FC = 19", you'll be marked correct. If you say "VGG has 16 layers", you lose 1 mark.
> 4. **Stride product in RF derivation** — students often forget to multiply cumulative strides from *all previous layers*, not just the immediately previous one. Walk through each step.
> 5. **Receptive field vs feature map size** — these are *different concepts*. Feature map size shrinks; receptive field grows. Examiners will check this distinction.

---

### 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **VGG19 = 16 Conv + 3 FC = 19 trainable layers.**
- ✅ **Uniform 3×3 convs** with stride 1 and padding 1; **2×2 max-pool** with stride 2.
- ✅ Channels double as spatial size halves: **64 → 128 → 256 → 512 → 512.**
- ✅ Final feature map before FC = **7 × 7 × 512**, flattened to **25088**.
- ✅ **Total parameters ≈ 143.67 M**, of which **~86% (123.64 M) lie in the FC layers.**
- ✅ Two stacked 3×3 convs = effective 5×5 RF, with **$7C^2 - C$ parameter savings** and **one extra ReLU**.
- ✅ Receptive field formula: $RF_l = RF_{l-1} + (k_l - 1) \cdot \prod_{i<l} s_i$.
- ✅ Conv output: $W_{out} = \lfloor (W_{in} + 2p - k)/s \rfloor + 1$.
- ✅ Conv params: $P = k^2 C_{in} C_{out} + C_{out}$. FC params: $P = N_{in} N_{out} + N_{out}$.
- ✅ Used heavily for **transfer learning, neural style transfer, image classification, and segmentation backbones**.
- ✅ Standard input image size = **224 × 224 × 3**; output = **1000 ImageNet classes via softmax.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Modular Architecture - ResNet
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST745 - COMPUTER VISION > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Modular Architecture - ResNet # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **ResNet (Residual Network)** is a deep convolutional neural network architecture introduced by He, Zhang, Ren, and Sun (Microsoft Research, 2015) in their seminal paper *"Deep Residual Learning for Image Recognition"* presented at CVPR 2016. ResNet introduced the revolutionary concept of **identity skip connections** (also called residual connections or shortcut connections) that allow gradients to flow directly through the network via an additive bypass pathway, thereby enabling the construction of extremely deep networks (hundreds to thousands of layers) without suffering from the **degradation problem**. The fundamental building block of ResNet is the **Residual Block**, which reformulates the layer mapping from learning an unreferenced function $\mathcal{H}(x)$ to learning a **residual function** $\mathcal{F}(x) := \mathcal{H}(x) - x$, yielding the output: $$y = \mathcal{F}(x, \{W_i\}) + x$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECST745 - Module 3):** ResNet is classified under *Modern CNN Modular Architectures*. Students must be able to (i) draw and explain the residual block, (ii) justify why residual learning mitigates vanishing gradients, and (iii) distinguish between identity and projection shortcuts. ## 1.2 Intuitive Overview / Real-World Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — The "Study Notes Shortcut":** > Imagine a student preparing for the KTU semester exam by reading a 500-page textbook chapter by chapter. As the chapters pile up, the student forgets the early chapters (information loss). Now imagine the student uses **summary cards** — every new chapter builds upon the previous summary card rather than the entire book. Even if some intermediate chapters are imperfect, the student always has access to the original summary (skip connection). > > In ResNet, the **skip connection** $x$ acts like the summary card — the original input is *always* preserved and added back. So if a few convolutional layers fail to learn useful features ($\mathcal{F}(x) \approx 0$), the network still passes the identity $x$ forward — equivalent to saying *"just pass it through."* ## 1.3 Key Terminology at a Glance | Term | Meaning | |------|---------| | **Residual Block** | A micro-module with $\mathcal{F}(x) + x$ | | **Skip / Shortcut Connection** | Identity pathway bypassing 2–3 conv layers | | **Identity Mapping** | When $\mathcal{F}(x) = 0$, output equals input | | **Projection Shortcut** | Uses $W_s \cdot x$ when dimensions mismatch | | **Bottleneck Block** | $1 \times 1 \to 3 \times 3 \to 1 \times 1$ conv design | | **Degradation Problem** | Accuracy saturation then decline as depth increases | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Degradation Problem — *Why Vanilla Deep CNNs Fail* In traditional deep CNNs (VGG, AlexNet, GoogLeNet), simply **stacking more layers** does not always improve performance. Beyond a certain depth (e.g., 20+ layers on CIFAR-10), both training and test accuracy **saturate and then degrade**. Crucially, this is **not** a classic overfitting issue — training error itself increases, which is paradoxical because deeper models should at least match their shallower counterparts. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Root Cause:** Optimizers (SGD, Adam) struggle to learn identity mappings in deep stacks. Solving $\mathcal{H}(x) = x$ for plain layers is hard; solving $\mathcal{F}(x) = 0$ for residual layers is comparatively trivial (drive weights toward zero). ## 2.2 Residual Learning — *The Core Idea* Instead of having stacked layers approximate $\mathcal{H}(x)$ directly, ResNet lets them approximate a **residual function**: $$\mathcal{F}(x) := \mathcal{H}(x) - x$$ So the original mapping becomes: $$y = \mathcal{F}(x, \{W_i\}) + x$$ where: - $x$ = input to the block - $\mathcal{F}(x, \{W_i\})$ = residual mapping learned by stacked conv layers - $y$ = block output - $\{W_i\}$ = weights of the conv layers inside the block If identity mapping is optimal, the optimizer only needs to drive $\mathcal{F}(x) \to 0$, which is easier than learning identity from scratch. ## 2.3 Two Types of Skip Connections **Type A — Identity Shortcut (Dimension Match):** Used when input and output channels/spatial sizes match. No extra parameters: $$y = \mathcal{F}(x, \{W_i\}) + x$$ **Type B — Projection Shortcut (Dimension Mismatch):** Used when downsampling occurs (stride > 1) or channel counts change. A $1 \times 1$ convolution projects $x$: $$y = \mathcal{F}(x, \{W_i\}) + W_s x$$ where $W_s$ is a learned linear projection (convolutional) matrix. ## 2.4 Residual Block Variants **Basic Block (used in ResNet-18, ResNet-34):** Two $3 \times 3$ conv layers with BN + ReLU: $$\mathcal{F}(x) = W_2 \cdot \sigma(BN(W_1 \cdot x))$$ **Bottleneck Block (used in ResNet-50, 101, 152):** Three layers — $1 \times 1$, $3 \times 3$, $1 \times 1$ — to reduce then restore dimensions: $$\mathcal{F}(x) = W_3 \cdot \sigma(BN(W_2 \cdot \sigma(BN(W_1 \cdot x))))$$ The $1 \times 1$ convolutions act as **channel-wise compression and expansion** (similar to bottleneck in Inception), drastically reducing parameters and computation. ## 2.5 Gradient Flow Analysis Let $\mathcal{L}$ be the loss. Using the chain rule and the additive skip connection: $$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial y} \cdot \left( \frac{\partial \mathcal{F}(x)}{\partial x} + 1 \right)$$ The crucial **$+1$** term ensures the gradient never vanishes, even when $\frac{\partial \mathcal{F}}{\partial x}$ is small. This is the mathematical cornerstone of ResNet. ## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Formula / Expression | Purpose / Units | |---------|----------------------|-----------------| | Residual mapping | $\mathcal{F}(x) = \mathcal{H}(x) - x$ | Reformulates learning target | | Block output (identity) | $y = \mathcal{F}(x, \{W_i\}) + x$ | Forward pass equation | | Block output (projection) | $y = \mathcal{F}(x, \{W_i\}) + W_s x$ | Dimension-mismatched case | | Gradient through skip | $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial y}\left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{F}}{\partial x} + 1\right)$ | Prevents vanishing gradient | | Basic block | Two $3 \times 3$ convs, BN, ReLU | ResNet-18/34 | | Bottleneck | $1 \times 1 \to 3 \times 3 \to 1 \times 1$ | ResNet-50/101/152 | | Stride 2 downsample | First conv in block uses stride 2 | Halves spatial dims | | Output dimension after block | $\frac{H_{\text{in}}}{s} \times \frac{W_{\text{in}}}{s} \times C_{\text{out}}$ | $s$ = stride | | Parameter reduction ratio (bottleneck) | $\approx \frac{1}{9}$ | vs stacked $3 \times 3$ convs | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Gradient Propagation Equation **Goal:** Show that the residual connection mathematically prevents vanishing gradients. Let the loss function be $\mathcal{L}$. Consider a single residual block where: $$y = \mathcal{F}(x) + x$$ We use the **chain rule** to backpropagate from $\mathcal{L}$ to the input $x$: **Step 1 — Express total derivative.** Since $y$ depends on $x$ through both the residual branch $\mathcal{F}(x)$ and the identity branch $x$: $$\frac{\partial y}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{F}(x)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial x}{\partial x}$$ **Step 2 — Simplify the identity term.** $$\frac{\partial x}{\partial x} = I \quad (\text{Identity matrix})$$ Therefore: $$\frac{\partial y}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{F}(x)}{\partial x} + I$$ **Step 3 — Apply chain rule for loss.** $$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial y} \cdot \frac{\partial y}{\partial x}$$ **Step 4 — Substitute result.** $$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial y} \cdot \left( \frac{\partial \mathcal{F}(x)}{\partial x} + I \right)$$ $$\boxed{\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial y} \cdot \frac{\partial \mathcal{F}(x)}{\partial x}}$$ **Step 5 — Interpretation.** The first term $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial y}$ represents **direct gradient flow** through the skip connection — it is added, not multiplied. Even if the second term (gradient through conv layers) shrinks due to saturation or depth, the first term guarantees a **non-vanishing minimum gradient of 1.0**. This is the essence of why ResNet trains stably at 100+ layers. --- ## 3.2 Derivation of the Bottleneck Parameter Reduction **Goal:** Quantify parameter savings of bottleneck vs stacked $3 \times 3$ convs. **Setup.** Suppose input has $C$ channels, output has $4C$ channels (a common expansion factor). **Option A — Two stacked $3 \times 3$ convs (basic block):** $$P_A = 2 \times (3 \times 3 \times C \times 4C) = 2 \times 36 C^2 = 72 C^2$$ **Option B — Bottleneck ($1 \times 1 \to 3 \times 3 \to 1 \times 1$) with intermediate $C$ channels:** $$P_B = (1 \times 1 \times C \times C) + (3 \times 3 \times C \times C) + (1 \times 1 \times C \times 4C)$$ $$P_B = C^2 + 9 C^2 + 4 C^2 = 14 C^2$$ **Step 1 — Compute the ratio.** $$\text{Ratio} = \frac{P_B}{P_A} = \frac{14 C^2}{72 C^2} = \frac{14}{72} = \frac{7}{36} \approx 0.194$$ **Step 2 — Convert to percentage savings.** $$\text{Savings} = \left(1 - \frac{7}{36}\right) \times 100\% \approx 80.56\%$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Insight:** Examiners often ask *"Why are bottlenecks used in ResNet-50/101/152?"* The answer combines this parameter reduction with the ability to **increase network depth** without proportional FLOP growth — enabling **152 layers in 19.6 GFLOPs** versus **19 layers in 19.6 GFLOPs** in plain VGG-style nets. --- ## 3.3 ResNet-50 Architecture — Layer-by-Layer Analytical Matrix | Stage | Layer Type | Output Size (for 224×224 input) | Kernel / Stride | Repeat | |-------|-----------|--------------------------------|------------------|--------| | Conv1 | $7 \times 7$ Conv, 64 ch, stride 2 + BN + ReLU + MaxPool 3×3/2 | $112 \times 112 \times 64$ | $7 \times 7, s=2$ | 1 | | Conv2_x | Bottleneck Block | $56 \times 56 \times 256$ | $1\times1, 3\times3, 1\times1$ | 3 | | Conv3_x | Bottleneck Block | $28 \times 28 \times 512$ | $1\times1, 3\times3, 1\times1$ | 4 | | Conv4_x | Bottleneck Block | $14 \times 14 \times 1024$ | $1\times1, 3\times3, 1\times1$ | 6 | | Conv5_x | Bottleneck Block | $7 \times 7 \times 2048$ | $1\times1, 3\times3, 1\times1$ | 3 | | Pool | Global Average Pooling | $1 \times 1 \times 2048$ | — | 1 | | FC | Fully Connected + Softmax | $1 \times 1 \times 1000$ | — | 1 | **Total layers = 1 (conv1) + (3+4+6+3)×3 (bottleneck) + 1 (FC) = 50 layers.** # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Residual Block — Functional Architecture (Mermaid) ```mermaid graph LR nodeA["Input X
size H x W x C"] --> nodeB["Conv 1x1
stride s
BN ReLU"] nodeA --> nodeF["Identity Shortcut
or Projection Ws"] nodeB --> nodeC["Conv 3x3
stride 1
BN ReLU"] nodeC --> nodeD["Conv 1x1
stride 1
BN"] nodeD --> nodeE["Add
F x plus shortcut"] nodeF --> nodeE nodeE --> nodeG["ReLU Activation"] nodeG --> nodeH["Output Y
size H_s x W_s x C4"] style nodeA fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0050b3 style nodeH fill:#b3ffb3,stroke:#2e7d32 style nodeE fill:#ffe066,stroke:#b38f00 ``` ## 4.2 Full ResNet-50 Pipeline — Top-Level Flow ```mermaid graph TD input["Input Image
224 x 224 x 3"] --> conv1["Conv 7x7 stride 2
BN ReLU MaxPool
Output 56 x 56 x 64"] conv1 --> stage2["Stage Conv2 x
3 Bottleneck Blocks
Output 56 x 56 x 256"] stage2 --> stage3["Stage Conv3 x
4 Bottleneck Blocks
Output 28 x 28 x 512"] stage3 --> stage4["Stage Conv4 x
6 Bottleneck Blocks
Output 14 x 14 x 1024"] stage4 --> stage5["Stage Conv5 x
3 Bottleneck Blocks
Output 7 x 7 x 2048"] stage5 --> gap["Global Average Pool
Output 1 x 1 x 2048"] gap --> fc["Fully Connected
Output 1000 classes"] fc --> soft["Softmax
Class Probabilities"] style input fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0050b3 style soft fill:#b3ffb3,stroke:#2e7d32 style stage2 fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#cc6600 style stage3 fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#cc6600 style stage4 fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#cc6600 style stage5 fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#cc6600 ``` ## 4.3 Gradient Flow Comparison — Plain CNN vs ResNet ```mermaid graph LR plain["Plain Deep CNN
gradient is product of
many Jacobians"] -->|"shrinks exponentially
vanishes"| outPlain["Output"] res["ResNet
gradient is sum of
products plus identity"] -->|"identity term equals 1
never vanishes"| outRes["Output"] style plain fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000 style res fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Define the residual block in a ResNet architecture. Write its mathematical formulation.* **Model Answer:** A residual block is the fundamental building block of ResNet that reformulates the layer mapping by adding a skip (shortcut) connection from input to output. Instead of learning the unreferenced mapping $\mathcal{H}(x)$, it learns a residual function $\mathcal{F}(x) = \mathcal{H}(x) - x$. The mathematical formulation is: $$y = \mathcal{F}(x, \{W_i\}) + x$$ where $x$ is the input, $\mathcal{F}$ is the residual mapping learned by stacked conv layers, and $y$ is the output. This ensures that if identity is optimal, the network only needs to drive $\mathcal{F}(x) \to 0$, which is easier than learning identity from scratch. *(3 marks — complete formulation, explanation, and significance)* --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Distinguish between identity shortcut and projection shortcut in ResNet.* **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Identity Shortcut | Projection Shortcut | |--------|-------------------|---------------------| | Formula | $y = \mathcal{F}(x) + x$ | $y = \mathcal{F}(x) + W_s x$ | | Parameters | None (parameter-free) | Extra parameters in $W_s$ | | When used | When input/output dimensions match | When dimensions differ (stride > 1 or channel change) | | Implementation | Direct element-wise add | $1 \times 1$ conv on $x$ before add | | Computational cost | Negligible | Slight increase | *(3 marks — comparison table + correct identification of use case)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **(a)** Explain the **degradation problem** in deep convolutional neural networks. How does ResNet address it through residual learning? Derive the gradient flow equation through a residual block and explain why it mitigates vanishing gradients. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Draw and explain the **architecture of ResNet-50**, clearly listing each stage with output dimensions, number of blocks, and the role of bottleneck design. Why is the bottleneck used for deeper ResNets (50, 101, 152) but not for ResNet-18/34? **(7 marks)** #### **Model Solution — Part (a)** **1. The Degradation Problem (2 marks):** As depth increases in plain CNNs (e.g., VGG-style), training error first saturates and then **increases**. This is not overfitting (test error also rises). It indicates that optimizers find it **difficult to learn identity mappings** in deep stacks — i.e., making a deeper network behave at least as well as a shallower one. **2. Residual Learning Reformulation (2 marks):** ResNet reformulates learning. Instead of: $$\mathcal{H}(x) = \text{stacked conv layers}(x)$$ ResNet learns: $$\mathcal{H}(x) = \mathcal{F}(x) + x \quad \Rightarrow \quad \mathcal{F}(x) := \mathcal{H}(x) - x$$ If identity is optimal, the residual branch only needs to learn $\mathcal{F}(x) \to 0$, which is easier. **3. Gradient Derivation (3 marks):** Given $y = \mathcal{F}(x) + x$: $$\frac{\partial y}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{F}(x)}{\partial x} + I$$ $$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial y} \cdot \left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{F}(x)}{\partial x} + I\right) = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial y}\cdot \frac{\partial \mathcal{F}}{\partial x}$$ The first term $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial y}$ flows directly through the skip path. Even if all conv weights are small or saturated, this term remains non-zero, ensuring gradients do not vanish across hundreds of layers. #### **Model Solution — Part (b)** **1. ResNet-50 Architecture (5 marks):** Refer to the analytical matrix in Section 3.3. Stages: - **Conv1**: $7\times7$, 64 filters, stride 2 + MaxPool → $56\times56\times64$ - **Conv2_x**: 3 bottleneck blocks → $56\times56\times256$ - **Conv3_x**: 4 bottleneck blocks → $28\times28\times512$ - **Conv4_x**: 6 bottleneck blocks → $14\times14\times1024$ - **Conv5_x**: 3 bottleneck blocks → $7\times7\times2048$ - **GAP** → $1\times1\times2048$ → **FC 1000** → **Softmax** **2. Why Bottleneck for Deeper Nets (2 marks):** The bottleneck ($1\times1 \to 3\times3 \to 1\times1$) reduces parameters by ~80% per block (derived as $\frac{7}{36} \approx 19.4\%$ of equivalent two $3\times3$ stacks). This allows ResNet-50/101/152 to be very deep without exploding parameter count or FLOPs. ResNet-18/34 use simpler basic blocks (two $3\times3$ convs) because at low depth parameter savings are less critical. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **(a)** Compare **Basic Block** and **Bottleneck Block** designs in ResNet. Show the parameter reduction achieved by the bottleneck design through detailed computation. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss the role of **batch normalization** and **ReLU activation** within a residual block. Also explain the difference between **pre-activation** and **post-activation** residual units. **(7 marks)** #### **Model Solution — Part (a)** **1. Comparison (3 marks):** | Feature | Basic Block | Bottleneck Block | |---------|-------------|------------------| | Conv layers | 2 | 3 | | Kernels | $3\times3, 3\times3$ | $1\times1, 3\times3, 1\times1$ | | Used in | ResNet-18, 34 | ResNet-50, 101, 152 | | Params (C → 4C) | $72 C^2$ | $14 C^2$ | **2. Parameter Computation (4 marks):** **Basic block:** $2 \times (3 \times 3 \times C \times 4C) = 72 C^2$ **Bottleneck:** $(1\times1\times C\times C) + (3\times3\times C\times C) + (1\times1\times C\times 4C) = 14 C^2$ **Ratio:** $\frac{14}{72} = \frac{7}{36} \approx 0.194$ **Savings:** $\approx 80.6\%$ — this enables building very deep networks like ResNet-152 efficiently. #### **Model Solution — Part (b)** **1. Batch Normalization Role (2 marks):** BN normalizes the output of each conv layer to have approximately zero mean and unit variance across the mini-batch. Within a residual block, BN: - Stabilizes input distribution to ReLU. - Reduces internal covariate shift. - Allows higher learning rates. - Acts as a regularizer. **2. ReLU Role (1 mark):** ReLU introduces non-linearity ($f(z) = \max(0, z)$), enabling the network to model complex, non-linear mappings. Without ReLU, the entire residual block would collapse into a linear transformation. **3. Pre-activation vs Post-activation (4 marks):** - **Post-activation (original ResNet, 2015):** Order is `Conv → BN → ReLU → Conv → BN → Add → ReLU`. The activation comes *after* the addition. - **Pre-activation (ResNet v2, 2016):** Order is `BN → ReLU → Conv → BN → ReLU → Conv → Add`. Activation is *before* convolutions. - **Benefits of pre-activation:** (i) Cleaner identity path — ReLU is absent on skip connection, so the $y = x$ identity is preserved perfectly; (ii) Easier optimization; (iii) Improved gradient flow, leading to better accuracy on deeper networks. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:** > 1. **Writing $y = \mathcal{F}(x) + W_s x$ for identity shortcut** — wrong. Use this only for projection shortcuts. > 2. **Forgetting the $+1$ term** in the gradient derivation — examiners specifically test this. Always write $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial y}\left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{F}}{\partial x} + 1\right)$. > 3. **Confusing Basic Block with Bottleneck** — Basic is two $3\times3$, Bottleneck is $1\times1, 3\times3, 1\times1$. > 4. **Stating total layers of ResNet-50 as 50 conv layers only** — the FC and pooling layers are part of the count. > 5. **Writing the degradation problem is caused by overfitting** — wrong. Training error itself increases; it's an optimization problem, not generalization. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Modular Architecture: ResNet** - **Core equation:** $y = \mathcal{F}(x, \{W_i\}) + x$ — memorize and be able to draw. - **Skip connection = identity mapping** that adds input directly to block output. - **Degradation problem** = training error increases with depth (NOT overfitting). - **Two shortcut types:** Identity (no params) and Projection ($W_s x$, used when dims mismatch). - **Basic block** (ResNet-18/34): 2 × ($3\times3$ conv + BN + ReLU). - **Bottleneck block** (ResNet-50/101/152): $1\times1 \to 3\times3 \to 1\times1$ — saves ~80% parameters. - **Gradient guarantee:** $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial y}\left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{F}}{\partial x} + 1\right)$ — the $+1$ prevents vanishing. - **ResNet-50 layer count:** Conv1 (1) + Conv2_x (3×3=9) + Conv3_x (4×3=12) + Conv4_x (6×3=18) + Conv5_x (3×3=9) + FC (1) = **50 weighted layers**. - **ResNet-152** was the winner of ILSVRC 2015 classification (3.57% top-5 error). - **Pre-activation ResNet (v2):** BN + ReLU before conv; cleaner identity path. - **Architectural innovations:** Global Average Pooling instead of FC-heavy head. - **Practical use:** ResNet-50 is the de facto backbone for transfer learning in object detection (Faster R-CNN), segmentation (UNet variants), and modern vision transformers (as hybrid backbones).

topics.title: Neural Architecture Search Design - NASNet
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST745 - COMPUTER VISION > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Neural Architecture Search Design - NASNet # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Neural Architecture Search (NAS)** is an automated machine learning (AutoML) sub-field that uses optimization algorithms (reinforcement learning, evolutionary search, or gradient-based methods) to discover the optimal topology, operations, and connectivity patterns of a neural network for a given task, **without relying on hand-crafted human designs**. **NASNet** is a specific convolutional neural network architecture produced by the NAS framework introduced by **Zoph et al. (Google Brain, 2018)**. Its landmark contribution is the design of a **search space** composed of two repeating structural motifs: - **Normal Cell**: Preserves the spatial dimensionality (stride = 1) of feature maps. - **Reduction Cell**: Halves the spatial resolution and doubles the channel depth (stride = 2 at input operations). These cells are stacked into the final network. Critically, NASNet demonstrated **transferable architectures**: cells searched on a small proxy dataset (CIFAR-10) generalize remarkably well to large-scale datasets (ImageNet). ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Robot Architect" > [!NOTE] > **Plain English Analogy:** Imagine you need a house built, but you have never seen an architect. Instead of hiring one, you give a smart robot (the **Controller RNN**) a bag of Lego pieces (convolution 3x3, separable 5x5, skip connection, pooling, etc.). The robot builds thousands of tiny house "blueprints" (child networks), has workers **construct and test** them, and reports back the **accuracy score** of each. Using a **reward signal** (the accuracy), the robot iteratively learns to pick better Lego pieces, eventually producing an award-winning floor plan — the **cell architecture**. This is exactly how NASNet works: - **Robot** → Controller RNN (a small recurrent network). - **Lego pieces** → Candidate operations (pooling, separable convolutions, identity/skip). - **Workers** → A child network trainer on CIFAR-10. - **Reward** → Validation accuracy of the trained child network. - **Final blueprint** → Normal Cell + Reduction Cell (stacked into NASNet-A, NASNet-B, NASNet-C). ## 1.3 Syllabus Highlights & Key Terminology | Term | Definition | |---|---| | **Controller RNN** | The recurrent network that samples child network architectures as a sequence of tokens. | | **Search Space** | The complete set of allowed operations, connection choices, and hyperparameters. | | **Child Network** | A candidate CNN sampled by the controller and trained/evaluated on a proxy task. | | **Policy Gradient** | The reinforcement learning algorithm (REINFORCE) used to update controller weights. | | **Transferability** | The property that cells learned on a small dataset can be transferred to a larger one. | > [!TIP] > For KTU 2024 examinations, the **most-tested sub-topics** are: (1) the **Controller RNN pipeline**, (2) the **two-cell search space (Normal / Reduction)**, and (3) the **policy gradient update equation**. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Pillars of NAS Any NAS framework can be decomposed into three orthogonal design choices. Understanding these is the single most exam-relevant mental model. ### Pillar 1 — Search Space Defines *which* architectures are even legal to consider. In NASNet, the search space is **cell-based**: - Each cell receives the outputs of the previous two cells as inputs: $h_{i-1}$ and $h_{i-2}$. - Each cell contains **B blocks** (typically B = 5). - Each block selects two operations from a candidate set $\mathcal{O}$ and two input indices. The candidate operation set is: $$\mathcal{O} = \{\text{3x3 sep conv}, \text{5x5 sep conv}, \text{7x7 sep conv}, \text{1x7 then 7x1 conv}, \text{3x3 avg pool}, \text{3x3 max pool}, \text{skip}, \text{zero}\}$$ The **zero** operation represents a "no connection" — the controller learns *where to disconnect* edges, dramatically expanding the search space without exploding the parameter count. ### Pillar 2 — Search Strategy How the controller navigates the search space. NASNet uses **Policy Gradient / REINFORCE**: - Treat architecture sampling as a sequential decision-making process. - The controller is a single-layer LSTM that emits softmax probabilities over choices. ### Pillar 3 — Performance Estimation Strategy How to score each candidate quickly. Naively training each child from scratch would take 28,000 GPU-days. NASNet mitigates this with: - **Proxy dataset** (CIFAR-10 instead of ImageNet). - **Limited epochs** per child (e.g., 20 epochs). - **Lower resolution / fewer filters** in the proxy. - **Parallelism** across hundreds of workers. ## 2.2 Operational Walk-Through 1. **Initialize** the Controller LSTM with random weights $\theta_c$. 2. **Sample** an architecture $a \sim \pi_{\theta_c}(a)$ by sequentially emitting tokens. 3. **Build & Train** the corresponding child network on the proxy task for a few epochs. 4. **Evaluate** validation accuracy $R(a)$ (the reward). 5. **Update** the controller via the policy gradient rule (see Section 3). 6. **Repeat** for thousands of architectures until convergence. 7. **Transfer** the best-discovered cells (Normal + Reduction) to a larger dataset/network. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The table below is the **single most important page** to memorize for the KTU ESE on this topic. Every cell is a potential short-answer or derivation step. | Concept | Equation / Form | Notes | |---|---|---| | Controller Sampling | $a = (a_1, a_2, \dots, a_T) \sim \pi_{\theta_c}$ | Each $a_t$ is a token choice | | Reward Signal | $R = \text{Validation Accuracy}$ | Scalar in [0, 1] | | Policy Objective | $J(\theta_c) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi_{\theta_c}}[R]$ | Maximize expected reward | | Log-Probability of an Arch | $\log \pi_{\theta_c}(a) = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \log \pi_{\theta_c}(a_t \mid a_{1:t-1})$ | Sum over all T tokens | | Policy Gradient Update | $\nabla_{\theta_c} J \approx \frac{1}{m}\sum_{k=1}^{m}\sum_{t=1}^{T}\nabla_{\theta_c}\log\pi_{\theta_c}(a_{t}^{(k)} \mid a_{1:t-1}^{(k)})\cdot R_k$ | REINFORCE with baseline | | Baseline (Moving Avg) | $b = \text{EMA of recent rewards}$ | Reduces variance | | Cell Output Composition | $h_i = f_{\text{combine}}\big(\text{op}_1(h_{i-1}) + \text{op}_2(h_{i-2})\big)$ | Concatenate across blocks | | Separable Conv FLOPs | $\text{FLOPs}_{sep} = \frac{1}{N^2}\cdot\text{FLOPs}_{std}$ | $N$ = kernel size factor | | Total Cell Count | $N_{\text{cells}} = N_{\text{normal}} + N_{\text{reduction}}$ | Reduction at 1/3 and 2/3 depth | | Search Cost | $\approx 4 \times 10^{4}$ GPU-hours (CIFAR-10) | Reported in original paper | > [!TIP] > **Unit reminders** for KTU: > - Reward $R$ is **dimensionless** (an accuracy in [0, 100] or [0, 1] depending on convention). > - Policy gradient $\nabla_{\theta_c} J$ has units of $\text{reward} / \text{controller weight}$. > - Validation accuracy is a **percentage** in NASNet reporting. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility NASNet-style cell designs now power a wide industrial class of compact, high-accuracy vision backbones: - **Mobile inference**: Separable convolutions shrink FLOPs by an order of magnitude vs. standard convolutions — directly enabling on-device CV on phones and edge TPUs. - **Medical imaging**: NAS-designed U-Net variants outperform hand-crafted skip connections in tumor segmentation. - **Autonomous driving**: The transferable cell principle lets a model designed on synthetic driving data adapt to real roads with minimal retraining. - **Embedded surveillance**: Reduced-cell search spaces permit real-time person/vehicle detection on low-power SoCs. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the REINFORCE Policy Gradient for NASNet The controller is a stochastic policy $\pi_{\theta_c}(a)$ parameterized by LSTM weights $\theta_c$. The goal is to maximize the expected reward: $$J(\theta_c) = \mathbb{E}_{a \sim \pi_{\theta_c}}[R(a)]$$ Expanding the expectation as an integral over the architecture space $\mathcal{A}$: $$J(\theta_c) = \int_{\mathcal{A}} \pi_{\theta_c}(a)\, R(a)\, da$$ **Step 1 — Differentiate under the integral sign:** Since $R(a)$ does not depend on $\theta_c$, we apply the **log-derivative trick**: $$\nabla_{\theta_c} \pi_{\theta_c}(a) = \pi_{\theta_c}(a)\,\nabla_{\theta_c}\log\pi_{\theta_c}(a)$$ Therefore: $$\nabla_{\theta_c} J(\theta_c) = \int_{\mathcal{A}} \pi_{\theta_c}(a)\,\nabla_{\theta_c}\log\pi_{\theta_c}(a)\, R(a)\, da$$ **Step 2 — Rewrite as an expectation:** $$\nabla_{\theta_c} J(\theta_c) = \mathbb{E}_{a \sim \pi_{\theta_c}}\big[\nabla_{\theta_c}\log\pi_{\theta_c}(a)\cdot R(a)\big]$$ **Step 3 — Approximate with Monte Carlo samples** (m architectures per batch): $$\nabla_{\theta_c} J(\theta_c) \approx \frac{1}{m}\sum_{k=1}^{m} \nabla_{\theta_c}\log\pi_{\theta_c}(a^{(k)})\cdot R(a^{(k)})$$ **Step 4 — Expand the log-probability** using the chain rule across the T emitted tokens: $$\log\pi_{\theta_c}(a) = \sum_{t=1}^{T}\log\pi_{\theta_c}(a_t \mid a_{1:t-1})$$ **Step 5 — Substitute to get the final update rule** used by Zoph et al.: $$\nabla_{\theta_c} J \approx \frac{1}{m}\sum_{k=1}^{m}\sum_{t=1}^{T}\nabla_{\theta_c}\log\pi_{\theta_c}(a_t^{(k)} \mid a_{1:t-1}^{(k)})\cdot R_k$$ **Step 6 — Variance reduction via baseline $b$** (EMA of past rewards): $$\nabla_{\theta_c} J \approx \frac{1}{m}\sum_{k=1}^{m}\sum_{t=1}^{T}\nabla_{\theta_c}\log\pi_{\theta_c}(a_t^{(k)} \mid a_{1:t-1}^{(k)})\cdot (R_k - b)$$ **Step 7 — Gradient ascent update on controller weights:** $$\theta_c \leftarrow \theta_c + \eta \cdot \nabla_{\theta_c} J$$ where $\eta$ is the controller learning rate (typically $3.5 \times 10^{-4}$). > [!NOTE] > **Why the baseline works:** Subtracting $b$ preserves the expected gradient (because $b$ is independent of $a$), but reduces variance dramatically. This is the standard **REINFORCE-with-baseline** trick, directly applicable in KTU derivations. ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — How Each Token is Decoded The controller LSTM emits one token at a time, **T tokens per block**, for **B blocks per cell**. | Step | Token Index | Choice Set | Meaning | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $a_{4k-3}$ | $\{0, 1, \dots, h-1\}$ | Index of hidden state input for first op | | 2 | $a_{4k-2}$ | $\mathcal{O}$ | Operation to apply to first input | | 3 | $a_{4k-1}$ | $\{0, 1, \dots, h-1\}$ | Index of hidden state input for second op | | 4 | $a_{4k}$ | $\mathcal{O}$ | Operation to apply to second input | | — | (concat all B blocks) | — | Form the cell output $h_i$ | where $h$ = number of previous hidden states the controller may connect to (typically $h = 4$ for the first cell, growing linearly with cell index). ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Reward Variance Suppose the controller samples 8 architectures in a batch with the following validation accuracies: | Arch # | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 | 8 | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | $R_k$ (%) | 92.1 | 91.8 | 93.4 | 91.5 | 92.7 | 93.0 | 92.3 | 92.9 | **Compute the baseline** (mean of the batch): $$b = \frac{1}{8}\sum_{k=1}^{8} R_k = \frac{740.7}{8} = 92.59\%$$ **Compute the centered rewards $R_k - b$:** $$\begin{aligned} \Delta R &= [\, -0.49,\; -0.79,\; +0.81,\; -1.09,\; +0.11,\; +0.41,\; -0.29,\; +0.31\,] \\ \sum_{k=1}^{8}\Delta R_k &= 0 \quad \text{(exactly, by definition of mean)} \end{aligned}$$ This is precisely why the mean baseline produces **zero-centered advantages** — controllers with rewards below the mean are *discouraged*, and those above are *reinforced*. > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU examiners love to ask: **"Why is subtracting a baseline unbiased?"** Answer: $\mathbb{E}[\nabla_{\theta_c}\log\pi\cdot b] = b\cdot\int \nabla_{\theta_c}\pi\, da = b\cdot\nabla_{\theta_c}(1) = 0$. ## 3.4 Derivation of the Separable Convolution Parameter Reduction A standard $N \times N$ convolution on a $C$-channel input to $C$-channel output requires: $$\text{Params}_{std} = N^2 \cdot C^2$$ A depthwise-separable convolution factorizes this into: $$\text{Params}_{sep} = (N^2 \cdot C) + (C^2) = C(N^2 + C)$$ The ratio is: $$\frac{\text{Params}_{sep}}{\text{Params}_{std}} = \frac{N^2 + C}{N^2 C} \approx \frac{1}{C} + \frac{1}{N^2}$$ For $N=5$ and $C=128$: ratio $\approx 1/128 + 1/25 \approx 0.048$ — i.e., a **~95% reduction**. This is why the search space $\mathcal{O}$ heavily features separable convolutions, and why NASNet is parameter-efficient. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 End-to-End NASNet Training Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Controller LSTM
weights theta_c] --> B[Sample Child Architecture
tokens a_1 to a_T] B --> C[Build Child CNN
Normal + Reduction Cells] C --> D[Train on Proxy Dataset
e.g. CIFAR-10, 20 epochs] D --> E[Evaluate Validation
Accuracy R] E --> F[Update Controller
via REINFORCE] F --> A E --> G[Replay Buffer
top architectures] G --> H[Cell Transfer Step
Stack into NASNet] H --> I[Train NASNet on
ImageNet large scale] ``` ## 4.2 Internal Structure of a Single Cell (B = 5 Blocks) ```mermaid flowchart LR in1[h_{i-2} previous cell] --> blk1[Block 1] in2[h_{i-1} last cell] --> blk1 blk1 --> blk2[Block 2] blk1 --> blk3[Block 3] in2 --> blk3 blk2 --> blk4[Block 4] in2 --> blk4 blk3 --> blk5[Block 5] blk4 --> blk5 blk5 --> out[Concatenate
all block outputs] out --> cellout[h_i cell output] ``` ## 4.3 Controller Token Emission Sequence ```mermaid flowchart LR s0[LSTM state h_t] --> t1[Token 1: input index] s0 --> t2[Token 2: op choice] s0 --> t3[Token 3: input index] s0 --> t4[Token 4: op choice] t1 --> t5[Softmax over inputs] t2 --> t6[Softmax over ops] t3 --> t5 t4 --> t6 ``` ## 4.4 Search Space Reduction Strategy ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Full Search Space] --> B[Filter by FLOPs budget] B --> C[Proxy training on CIFAR-10] C --> D{Validation Reward R above threshold} D -- yes --> E[Top-K Architectures Replayed] D -- no --> F[Discard] E --> G[Transfer cells to ImageNet] G --> H[Final NASNet Classifier] ``` > [!TIP] > **KTU diagram tips**: In the 14-mark question, you will be expected to draw at minimum the **Controller → Child Network → Reward → Controller** loop. Memorize that loop — it is worth full marks on its own. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **Define Neural Architecture Search (NAS). Differentiate between the cell-based search space and the macro search space.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **NAS (1 mark)**: An AutoML technique that automates the design of neural network architectures using a search strategy over a defined search space guided by a performance estimator. - **Cell-based search space (1 mark)**: Architectures are built by repeating small modular sub-structures (cells), each containing B blocks of selected operations. NASNet uses Normal + Reduction cells. - **Macro search space (1 mark)**: Searches over the entire layer-by-layer topology (number of layers, filter sizes per layer, skip connections), with no notion of repeated cells. The search space is much larger and harder to optimize. --- ### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **Why is the "zero" operation included in the NASNet search space $\mathcal{O}$? What role does it play?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - The **zero operation** represents the controller's ability to *disconnect* an edge in the cell DAG (1 mark). - It allows the search algorithm to **discover sparse connectivity patterns** rather than always using dense operations, expanding representational diversity without inflating parameters (1 mark). - Empirically, zero operations appear in many winning NASNet cells, indicating that *not all possible edges are necessary* for optimal accuracy (1 mark). --- ## Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — May 2024] #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Explain the complete working of the Controller RNN used in NASNet, with a neat block diagram. How are Normal and Reduction cells generated from its outputs?** **Model Solution:** 1. **Controller Architecture (2 marks):** The controller is a single-layer **LSTM** with ~100 hidden units. At each time step, it emits a softmax over a small vocabulary (an operation index or an input-edge index). The outputs are sampled from these softmaxes to construct one block of a cell at a time. 2. **Sequential Token Emission (2 marks):** For each of the B blocks in a cell, the controller emits 4 tokens in order: (input index, operation, input index, operation). The first two tokens form the first edge, the next two form the second edge, and the two edges are combined (typically by element-wise addition) to produce that block's output. 3. **Cell Construction (2 marks):** After B blocks are sampled, their outputs are **concatenated channel-wise** to form the cell's output feature map. **Normal cells** use stride 1 on all operations (preserve resolution). **Reduction cells** use stride 2 on operations adjacent to cell inputs (halve resolution, double channels). The full NASNet-A stacks N = 13 normal cells and 2 reduction cells (inserted at 1/3 and 2/3 of total depth). 4. **Transferability (1 mark):** Cells searched on CIFAR-10 (32×32) are then stacked into a wider network trained on ImageNet (224×224), demonstrating that the learned micro-architecture generalizes across datasets and resolutions. #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Derive the policy gradient update rule used to train the controller. Explain why a baseline is used and prove that it does not bias the gradient.** **Model Solution:** Starting from the objective $J(\theta_c) = \mathbb{E}_{a \sim \pi_{\theta_c}}[R(a)]$, applying the log-derivative trick and Monte Carlo approximation (already derived in Section 3.1), we obtain: $$\nabla_{\theta_c} J \approx \frac{1}{m}\sum_{k=1}^{m}\sum_{t=1}^{T}\nabla_{\theta_c}\log\pi_{\theta_c}(a_t^{(k)} \mid a_{1:t-1}^{(k)})\cdot R_k$$ The **update step** is gradient ascent (1 mark): $$\theta_c \leftarrow \theta_c + \eta \cdot \nabla_{\theta_c} J$$ **Why a baseline $b$? (3 marks):** The raw reward $R$ is a high-variance signal — sampling 200 architectures might yield rewards clustered between 0.91 and 0.93, making the gradient noisy. Subtracting a baseline $b$ (typically the moving average of recent rewards) **reduces variance** while keeping the expected gradient unchanged, leading to faster, more stable convergence. **Unbiasedness proof (3 marks):** Since $b$ is a constant with respect to the architecture $a$ sampled under $\pi_{\theta_c}$: $$\mathbb{E}_{a \sim \pi_{\theta_c}}[\nabla_{\theta_c}\log\pi_{\theta_c}(a)\cdot b] = b \int_{\mathcal{A}} \nabla_{\theta_c}\pi_{\theta_c}(a)\, da = b \cdot \nabla_{\theta_c}\!\int_{\mathcal{A}}\pi_{\theta_c}(a)\, da$$ $$= b \cdot \nabla_{\theta_c}(1) = b \cdot 0 = 0$$ Hence the baseline introduces no bias — only a beneficial variance reduction. --- ### Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] — *Alternative Choice* #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **With a neat diagram, describe the architecture of NASNet-A. Discuss why separable convolutions dominate its search space.** **Model Solution:** 1. **Stem (1 mark):** Two strided convolutions (stride 2 each) at the input, reducing ImageNet 224×224×3 to 28×28×C. 2. **Cell Stack (3 marks):** A sequence of N_repeat = 6 modules, where each module contains: - 5 **Normal Cells** (stride 1, preserve resolution) - 1 **Reduction Cell** (stride 2, halves resolution and doubles channels) - This repeats for 6 stages, yielding total depth $\approx 13$ normal + 2 reduction cells. 3. **Classifier Head (1 mark):** Global average pooling → dropout → 1000-way softmax for ImageNet. 4. **Auxiliary Head (1 mark):** An auxiliary classifier is attached at 2/3 depth to combat vanishing gradients (training-only, discarded at inference). 5. **Separable Convolutions (1 mark):** Because FLOPs scale as $N^2 C^2$ for standard convolutions but only $N^2 C + C^2$ for depthwise-separable ones, the search rapidly discovers that $5\times 5$ and $7\times 7$ separable kernels deliver accuracy comparable to standard $3\times 3$ convolutions at a fraction of the compute cost — making them dominant in the final cells. #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Explain the concept of "transferable architectures" in NASNet. Why is the search performed on CIFAR-10 and not directly on ImageNet?** **Model Solution:** 1. **Definition of Transferability (2 marks):** Transferability is the empirical finding that the **micro-architecture** (the structure of Normal and Reduction cells) discovered on a small proxy dataset generalizes — without modification — to a much larger dataset. Once the cell structure is fixed, only the macro-parameters (number of cells, filter counts) are re-tuned for the target dataset. 2. **Why CIFAR-10 as the proxy? (3 marks):** - **Cost**: A single ImageNet training run takes days on multiple GPUs; evaluating thousands of candidate architectures would be prohibitive. CIFAR-10 child training completes in minutes, enabling 20,000+ samples within 4 days. - **Search signal quality**: Despite the small dataset, the relative ranking of architectures by validation accuracy is well-correlated with their ImageNet ranking, providing a useful (if noisy) reward signal. - **Resolution invariance**: A Normal Cell that performs well at 32×32 is also effective at 224×224, because separable convolutions and skip connections are resolution-agnostic operators. 3. **Evidence of transfer (1 mark):** NASNet-A achieves **82.7% top-1** and **96.2% top-5** on ImageNet at 569M multiply-adds, beating hand-designed networks like Inception-v4 and SENet at similar compute budgets. 4. **Limitation (1 mark):** Transferability is not perfect — the optimal number of cells and filter widths differ between CIFAR-10 and ImageNet and must be re-tuned. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:** > - **Do NOT confuse "Controller" with "Child Network"** — the controller is the LSTM that *generates* architectures, the child is the architecture that gets *trained*. Losing 1 mark for this mix-up is common. > - **Do NOT write** that NAS "invents new mathematical operations" — it only *selects* from a predefined candidate set $\mathcal{O}$. > - **Do NOT skip the baseline proof** — KTU specifically tests the unbiasedness derivation. Writing only "reduces variance" without proof gets half credit. > - **Do NOT confuse REINFORCE with Q-learning** — REINFORCE is a *policy gradient* method (model-free, on-policy), whereas Q-learning is value-based. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid-revision checklist (print this page the night before the exam):** - [ ] **NAS** = automated architecture design; **NASNet** = the CNN produced by Google's NAS with cell-based search. - [ ] **Search space** consists of **Normal cells** (stride 1) and **Reduction cells** (stride 2, double channels). - [ ] Each cell has **B blocks** (default 5); each block chooses 2 inputs and 2 operations from $\mathcal{O}$. - [ ] $\mathcal{O}$ includes separable convolutions (3×3, 5×5, 7×7), 1×7-then-7×1, pooling, **skip (identity)**, and **zero**. - [ ] **Controller** = single-layer LSTM that samples architectures as a sequence of tokens. - [ ] **Policy gradient update** = REINFORCE with baseline: $$\nabla_{\theta_c} J \approx \frac{1}{m}\sum_{k=1}^{m}\sum_{t=1}^{T}\nabla_{\theta_c}\log\pi_{\theta_c}(a_t^{(k)} \mid a_{1:t-1}^{(k)})\cdot (R_k - b)$$ - [ ] **Baseline $b$** = moving average of past rewards; reduces variance, is provably unbiased. - [ ] **Proxy dataset** = CIFAR-10; cells are then **transferred** to ImageNet by stacking. - [ ] **Separable convolutions** dominate the searched cells because they cut FLOPs by a factor of ~$C$. - [ ] **NASNet-A reported result**: ~82.7% top-1 on ImageNet at ~569M multiply-adds. - [ ] **Key actors in the loop**: Controller → Child Network → Validation Reward → Controller (REINFORCE update) → repeat. - [ ] **Search cost**: ~4×10⁴ GPU-hours on CIFAR-10 (the major bottleneck; later work like DARTS/ENAS addresses this). - [ ] **REINFORCE = policy gradient, on-policy, model-free** — do not confuse with Q-learning. - [ ] **Transferability** is the property that the *micro-architecture* generalizes; macro-parameters (depth, width) must still be re-tuned. - [ ] The **zero operation** allows the controller to learn *sparse* cell connectivity, which is a key reason NASNet cells are parameter-efficient.

topics.title: Segmentation and Object Detection: Segmentation Using Clustering Methods - Human Vision - Grouping and Gestalt
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST745 - COMPUTER VISION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Segmentation and Object Detection: Segmentation Using Clustering Methods - Human Vision - Grouping and Gestalt

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Image Segmentation — The Foundational Definition

In the formal KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, **Image Segmentation** is defined as the process of partitioning a digital image into multiple non-overlapping, semantically meaningful regions (sets of pixels) such that:

- Pixels within the same region share homogeneous visual properties (intensity, color, texture).
- Pixels across different regions exhibit distinct, discontinuous properties.

Mathematically, for an image $I$ defined over the spatial domain $\Omega \subset \mathbb{R}^{2}$:

$$I: \Omega \rightarrow \mathbb{R}^{c}, \quad \text{Segmentation} = \{R_{1}, R_{2}, \dots, R_{n}\}$$

subject to the conditions:

$$\bigcup_{i=1}^{n} R_{i} = \Omega, \quad R_{i} \cap R_{j} = \emptyset \;\; (i \neq j)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Segmentation ≠ Classification.** Classification assigns a label to a *whole image or region*; segmentation assigns a label to *every single pixel* (dense prediction).

---

### 1.2 Segmentation Using Clustering Methods

**Clustering-based segmentation** treats each pixel (or superpixel) as a data point in a feature space and groups together points that are "close" by some similarity metric. The image is therefore a **2D feature map** where each pixel has features such as:

- Grayscale intensity (1-D)
- RGB color vector (3-D)
- HSV/Lab color vector (3-D)
- Intensity + spatial coordinates $(x, y, I)$ (3-D or 5-D)

The two dominant clustering paradigms used in KTU-referenced literature are:

| Paradigm | Core Idea | Representative Algorithm |
|---|---|---|
| **Centroid-based** | Minimize intra-cluster variance | **K-Means** |
| **Density-based / Mode-seeking** | Locate dense regions in feature space | **Mean-Shift** |
| **Graph-theoretic** | Treat pixels as nodes, edges as affinity | **Normalized Cuts, Felzenszwalb-Huttenlocher (Graph-Based)** |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Module 4 places special emphasis on **K-Means** and **Mean-Shift**, with **Graph-Based Segmentation** appearing as a high-yield topic for Part B questions.

---

### 1.3 Human Vision — How the Eye-Brain System Segments

**Human vision** is not a pixel-wise scanner. The visual cortex (V1 → V2 → V4 → IT) performs segmentation pre-attentively, even before conscious recognition. The retina transduces photons into neural spikes, but the perceptual "segmentation" is an emergent property of cortical processing.

Key functional stages of human segmentation:

1. **Lateral Geniculate Nucleus (LGN)** — relays raw contrast signals.
2. **V1 (Primary Visual Cortex)** — detects edges, orientation, spatial frequency (Hubel & Wiesel, 1962).
3. **V2 / V4** — aggregates contours, color regions, and texture boundaries.
4. **IT Cortex** — object-level segmentation, figure-ground separation.

---

### 1.4 Grouping and Gestalt — The Philosophical Root of Computer Vision Segmentation

**Gestalt psychology** (German: *Gestalt* = "form/whole/shape"), founded by Max Wertheimer, Wolfgang Köhler, and Kurt Koffka around **1912**, proposed the revolutionary principle:

> *"The whole is other than the sum of its parts."*

In segmentation terms: **the human eye groups pixels into perceptual units BEFORE recognizing them**. This is exactly what clustering-based segmentation attempts to emulate computationally.

### Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> Imagine standing in a crowded railway station. You are *not* consciously aware of thousands of individual faces, shirts, bags, and lights. Instead, your brain instantly *groups*:
> - All moving northbound people into **one cluster** (common fate).
> - A row of ceiling lights into **one continuous line** (good continuation).
> - A semicircle of friends into **one group photo** (closure + proximity).
>
> This automatic, pre-conscious grouping is exactly what a **K-Means or Mean-Shift algorithm** attempts to mimic in feature space — it "sees" the station the way your brain does.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why study Gestalt in a Computer Vision course?**
> Because every classical segmentation algorithm — from thresholding to normalized cuts — is a *mathematical formalization* of one or more Gestalt laws. Understanding the laws gives you the **"why"** behind every segmentation design decision.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Gestalt Laws of Grouping — The KTU Mandatory List

The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly maps **six Gestalt laws** to segmentation algorithms. Mastering this table is essential for both Part A (3 marks) and Part B (14 marks) questions.

| # | Gestalt Law | Perceptual Statement | CV Equivalent |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Proximity** | Elements close together are grouped | K-Means with small $K$, superpixels |
| 2 | **Similarity** | Elements with similar features are grouped | Color/Intensity-based K-Means |
| 3 | **Continuity (Good Continuation)** | Smooth, continuous curves are preferred | Edge-linking, active contours |
| 4 | **Closure** | Incomplete shapes are perceived as complete | Region growing, hole filling |
| 5 | **Common Fate** | Elements moving together are grouped | Motion-based segmentation (optical flow clustering) |
| 6 | **Symmetry / Parallelism** | Symmetric/parallel contours are grouped | Symmetry detection, Hough Transform |

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU examiner frequently asks: *"Which Gestalt law is mimicked by the K-Means algorithm?"* The model answer is **Similarity** (and secondarily, **Proximity** when spatial coordinates are used as features).

---

### 2.2 K-Means Clustering for Segmentation — Operational Logic

**K-Means** (Lloyd, 1957; Forgy, 1965) is a partitional, centroid-based clustering algorithm. For image segmentation:

- Treat each pixel $\mathbf{p}_{i} = (R_i, G_i, B_i)$ as a point in 3-D color space.
- Choose $K$ cluster centers (centroids) $\mu_{1}, \dots, \mu_{K}$.
- Iteratively (a) assign each pixel to the nearest centroid, and (b) update centroids to the mean of assigned pixels.

**Operational Steps (KTU Board Format):**

1. **Initialization** — Choose $K$ initial centroids. Methods: random sampling, K-Means++ (Arthur & Vassilvitskii, 2007), or histogram-based seeding.
2. **Assignment Step** — For each pixel $\mathbf{p}_{i}$, assign it to cluster $c(\mathbf{p}_{i})$ where:
   
   $$c(\mathbf{p}_{i}) = \arg\min_{k \in \{1, \dots, K\}} \;\|\mathbf{p}_{i} - \mu_{k}\|^{2}$$

3. **Update Step** — Recompute each centroid as the mean of all pixels assigned to it:
   
   $$\mu_{k} = \frac{1}{\vert C_{k}\vert} \sum_{\mathbf{p}_{i} \in C_{k}} \mathbf{p}_{i}$$

4. **Convergence Check** — Iterate steps 2–3 until the centroid displacement $\Delta = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \vert\vert\mu_{k}^{(t)} - \mu_{k}^{(t-1)}\vert\vert^{2}$ falls below a threshold $\epsilon$ (typically $10^{-4}$), or until a maximum iteration count is reached.

---

### 2.3 Mean-Shift Segmentation — Density-Based Mode Seeking

**Mean-Shift** (Fukunaga & Hostetler, 1975; later popularized by Comaniciu & Meer, 2002) is a non-parametric, mode-seeking algorithm. Unlike K-Means, it does **not** require the user to specify $K$.

- Place a sliding window over the feature space.
- At each window location, compute the **mean of all data points within a radius $h$ (bandwidth)**.
- Shift the window center to this mean.
- Repeat until convergence (window stops moving).

Mathematically, the mean-shift vector is:

$$\mathbf{m}(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{\sum_{i} K(\mathbf{x}_{i} - \mathbf{x}) \cdot \mathbf{x}_{i}}{\sum_{i} K(\mathbf{x}_{i} - \mathbf{x})} - \mathbf{x}$$

where $K(\cdot)$ is a kernel function (typically the **Epanechnikov kernel** or **Gaussian kernel**):

$$K_{G}(\mathbf{u}) = \frac{1}{(2\pi h^{2})^{d/2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{\|\mathbf{u}\|^{2}}{2h^{2}}\right)$$

The procedure converges to the nearest **local mode (peak) of the density distribution** — analogous to a ball rolling down a hill until it settles in a valley.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The bandwidth $h$ is the *only* parameter. Choosing $h$ too small → over-segmentation; $h$ too large → under-segmentation.

---

### 2.4 Graph-Based Segmentation (Felzenszwalb-Huttenlocher)

The image is modeled as a **weighted undirected graph** $G = (V, E)$:

- $V$ = pixels
- $E$ = edges between neighboring pixels (4- or 8-connected)
- $w(u, v)$ = dissimilarity (e.g., intensity difference)

The algorithm greedily merges components $C_{1}, C_{2}$ if:

$$D(C_{1}, C_{2}) \leq \tau(C_{1}) \quad \text{AND} \quad D(C_{1}, C_{2}) \leq \tau(C_{2})$$

where the internal difference of a component is:

$$\tau(C) = \frac{k}{\vert C \vert}$$

and the minimum spanning edge between two components is:

$$D(C_{1}, C_{2}) = \min_{u \in C_{1}, v \in C_{2}, (u,v) \in E} w(u, v)$$

This is the **gestalt law of continuity + closure** in mathematical form.

---

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield for ESE)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **every formula** KTU expects you to memorize for this topic. All symbols and units are KTU-board-standard.

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Segmentation partition | $\bigcup_{i=1}^{n} R_{i} = \Omega$, $R_{i} \cap R_{j} = \emptyset$ | Disjoint cover |
| 2 | K-Means assignment | $c(\mathbf{p}_{i}) = \arg\min_{k} \vert\vert \mathbf{p}_{i} - \mu_{k} \vert\vert^{2}$ | Squared Euclidean |
| 3 | K-Means centroid update | $\mu_{k} = \frac{1}{\vert C_{k} \vert}\sum_{\mathbf{p}_{i} \in C_{k}} \mathbf{p}_{i}$ | Pixel vector mean |
| 4 | K-Means objective (WCSS) | $J = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{\mathbf{p}_{i} \in C_{k}} \vert\vert \mathbf{p}_{i} - \mu_{k} \vert\vert^{2}$ | Minimize inertia |
| 5 | Mean-shift vector | $\mathbf{m}(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{\sum_{i} K(\mathbf{x}_{i} - \mathbf{x}) \mathbf{x}_{i}}{\sum_{i} K(\mathbf{x}_{i} - \mathbf{x})} - \mathbf{x}$ | Vector shift |
| 6 | Gaussian kernel | $K_{G}(\mathbf{u}) = (2\pi h^{2})^{-d/2} \exp\!\left(-\vert\vert \mathbf{u} \vert\vert^{2}/2h^{2}\right)$ | $h$ = bandwidth |
| 7 | Within-cluster variance | $V_{k} = \frac{1}{\vert C_{k} \vert} \sum_{\mathbf{p}_{i} \in C_{k}} \vert\vert \mathbf{p}_{i} - \mu_{k} \vert\vert^{2}$ | Per-cluster scatter |
| 8 | Intra/inter cluster ratio | $S = \text{trace}(S_{B}) / \text{trace}(S_{W})$ | Higher is better |
| 9 | Graph-based merge threshold | $\tau(C) = k / \vert C \vert$ | $k$ = scale parameter |
| 10 | Edge weight (intensity) | $w(u,v) = \vert I(u) - I(v) \vert$ | Absolute difference |

> **Real-world utility:** K-Means is used in medical imaging (tumor vs. healthy tissue segmentation), satellite imaging (land-cover classification), and video surveillance (background subtraction). Mean-Shift is used in **object tracking** (e.g., CamShift algorithm in OpenCV) due to its robustness to scale and rotation. Graph-based methods power **superpixel algorithms (SLIC, Felzenszwalb)** used as preprocessing for deep learning pipelines.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the K-Means Convergence Condition

The K-Means objective is the **Within-Cluster Sum of Squares (WCSS)**:

$$J(\{C_{k}\}, \{\mu_{k}\}) = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{\mathbf{p}_{i} \in C_{k}} \vert\vert \mathbf{p}_{i} - \mu_{k} \vert\vert^{2}$$

**Step 1 — Necessary condition for centroid optimality.**

For a fixed assignment $\{C_{k}\}$, take the partial derivative of $J$ with respect to centroid $\mu_{k}$ and set it to zero:

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \mu_{k}} = \frac{\partial}{\partial \mu_{k}} \sum_{\mathbf{p}_{i} \in C_{k}} (\mathbf{p}_{i} - \mu_{k})^{\top}(\mathbf{p}_{i} - \mu_{k}) = 0$$

Expanding the squared norm:

$$(\mathbf{p}_{i} - \mu_{k})^{\top}(\mathbf{p}_{i} - \mu_{k}) = \mathbf{p}_{i}^{\top}\mathbf{p}_{i} - 2\mathbf{p}_{i}^{\top}\mu_{k} + \mu_{k}^{\top}\mu_{k}$$

Differentiating with respect to $\mu_{k}$:

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \mu_{k}} = \sum_{\mathbf{p}_{i} \in C_{k}} \left(-2\mathbf{p}_{i} + 2\mu_{k}\right) = -2\sum_{\mathbf{p}_{i} \in C_{k}}\mathbf{p}_{i} + 2\mu_{k}\vert C_{k} \vert = 0$$

**Step 2 — Solve for the centroid.**

Rearranging:

$$2\mu_{k}\vert C_{k} \vert = 2\sum_{\mathbf{p}_{i} \in C_{k}}\mathbf{p}_{i}$$

$$\boxed{\;\mu_{k}^{\ast} = \frac{1}{\vert C_{k} \vert} \sum_{\mathbf{p}_{i} \in C_{k}} \mathbf{p}_{i}\;}$$

This proves that the **mean of assigned pixels** minimizes the WCSS for a given assignment — the foundation of the K-Means update step.

---

**Step 3 — Necessary condition for pixel assignment optimality.**

For a fixed centroid set $\{\mu_{k}\}$, each pixel must be assigned to minimize:

$$\mathbf{p}_{i} \rightarrow C_{k} \quad \text{where} \quad k = \arg\min_{j} \vert\vert \mathbf{p}_{i} - \mu_{j} \vert\vert^{2}$$

This is the **Voronoi partition** of feature space.

---

**Step 4 — Convergence proof (sketch, KTU-relevant).**

Since $J$ is bounded below by $J = 0$ and is non-increasing at every step:

- The **assignment step** cannot increase $J$ (each pixel moves to its minimum-distance centroid).
- The **update step** cannot increase $J$ (by Step 1, the mean is the unique global minimum for that cluster).

Therefore $J^{(t+1)} \leq J^{(t)}$, and being bounded below, $J$ converges. KTU expects you to cite this **monotonic-decrease-and-bounded-below** argument in 14-mark questions.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — K-Means on 5 Pixels

> This is the type of problem KTU sets as a 7-mark sub-question in Part B.

**Problem:** Cluster the 5 pixels $\mathbf{p}_{1} = (0,0)$, $\mathbf{p}_{2} = (1,0)$, $\mathbf{p}_{3} = (5,5)$, $\mathbf{p}_{4} = (6,5)$, $\mathbf{p}_{5} = (10,10)$ into $K = 2$ clusters. Initial centroids: $\mu_{1}^{(0)} = (1,0)$, $\mu_{2}^{(0)} = (6,5)$.

**Iteration 1 — Assignment:**

| Pixel | Distance to $\mu_{1}$ | Distance to $\mu_{2}$ | Assigned to |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\mathbf{p}_{1}=(0,0)$ | $\sqrt{1+0}=1$ | $\sqrt{36+25}\approx 7.81$ | $C_{1}$ |
| $\mathbf{p}_{2}=(1,0)$ | $0$ | $\sqrt{25+25}\approx 7.07$ | $C_{1}$ |
| $\mathbf{p}_{3}=(5,5)$ | $\sqrt{16+25}\approx 6.40$ | $\sqrt{1+0}=1$ | $C_{2}$ |
| $\mathbf{p}_{4}=(6,5)$ | $\sqrt{25+25}\approx 7.07$ | $0$ | $C_{2}$ |
| $\mathbf{p}_{5}=(10,10)$ | $\sqrt{81+100}\approx 13.45$ | $\sqrt{16+25}\approx 6.40$ | $C_{2}$ |

**Iteration 1 — Update:**

$$\mu_{1}^{(1)} = \frac{(0,0)+(1,0)}{2} = (0.5, 0)$$

$$\mu_{2}^{(1)} = \frac{(5,5)+(6,5)+(10,10)}{3} = (7, \tfrac{20}{3}) \approx (7, 6.67)$$

**WCSS after Iteration 1:**

$$J^{(1)} = (0.5^{2} + 0.5^{2}) + ((2^{2} + 1.67^{2}) + (1^{2} + 1.67^{2}) + (3^{2} + 3.33^{2}))$$

$$J^{(1)} = 0.5 + (4 + 2.78) + (1 + 2.78) + (9 + 11.09) \approx 31.15$$

**Iteration 2 — Reassignment:**

All pixels still belong to the same clusters (verify: $\mathbf{p}_{1}$ moves slightly closer to $\mu_{1}^{(1)} = (0.5, 0)$, etc.). No change.

**Iteration 2 — Update:** Centroids remain identical, so **algorithm has converged** after 2 iterations.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU students are expected to *show one full iteration* and state convergence. Showing two iterations with the second one being a no-op is a complete, valuation-friendly answer.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — When to Use What?

| Scenario | Recommended Algorithm | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Known number of regions, fast execution | **K-Means** | Linear time $O(NKd)$ per iteration |
| Unknown number of regions, robust to outliers | **Mean-Shift** | Non-parametric, mode-seeking |
| Real-time, irregular region shapes | **Graph-Based (FH)** | Near-linear, preserves boundaries |
| Multi-spectral satellite imagery | **K-Means on stacked feature vector** | Easy extension to N-D |
| Motion-based segmentation | **Mean-Shift in (x, y, t) feature space** | Common-fate grouping |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 K-Means Segmentation Pipeline (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Image I xy] --> B[Feature Extraction: RGB or HSV vector per pixel]
    B --> C[Choose K and Initialize Centroids mu1 to muK using K-Means++]
    C --> D[Assignment Step: Assign each pixel to nearest centroid by squared distance]
    D --> E[Update Step: Recompute mu k as mean of assigned pixels]
    E --> F{Convergence Check: Delta less than epsilon OR max iterations}
    F -->|Not Converged| D
    F -->|Converged| G[Output Segmentation Map: color each pixel by cluster ID]
    G --> H[Post-processing: Morphological cleanup, region merging]
```

> **Interpretation:** This is the operational backbone of every K-Means segmentation. KTU examiners expect students to reproduce this loop and label the **convergence criterion** explicitly.

---

### 4.2 Mean-Shift Mode-Seeking Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Pixel in feature space] --> W[Place window of radius h]
    W --> C[Compute mean of points within window]
    C --> S[Shift window center to mean]
    S --> CH{Window position changed?}
    CH -->|Yes| C
    CH -->|No| M[Converged: window is at mode]
    M --> CL[Merge windows within h of each other]
    CL --> SEG[Output Segmented Image]
```

> **Interpretation:** Each window "rolls uphill" on the density surface until it reaches a **local maximum (mode)**. All pixels that converge to the same mode form one segment. This is a direct computational realization of the **Gestalt law of similarity**.

---

### 4.3 Mapping Gestalt Laws to CV Algorithms (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    G1[Gestalt Law: Proximity] --> A1[Algorithm: SLIC Superpixels]
    G2[Gestalt Law: Similarity] --> A2[Algorithm: K-Means and Mean-Shift]
    G3[Gestalt Law: Good Continuation] --> A3[Algorithm: Edge Linking and Active Contours]
    G4[Gestalt Law: Closure] --> A4[Algorithm: Region Growing and Graph Cuts]
    G5[Gestalt Law: Common Fate] --> A5[Algorithm: Optical Flow Clustering]
    G6[Gestalt Law: Symmetry] --> A6[Algorithm: Hough Transform and Symmetry Detectors]
    A1 --> OUT[Unified Segmentation Output]
    A2 --> OUT
    A3 --> OUT
    A4 --> OUT
    A5 --> OUT
    A6 --> OUT
```

> **Interpretation:** This block diagram shows that **no single algorithm captures all Gestalt laws**. Real-world segmentation pipelines (e.g., GrabCut, Mask R-CNN preprocessing) **fuse multiple laws** to achieve robust segmentation.

---

### 4.4 Human Vision Segmentation Pipeline (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L[Light from scene] --> R[Retina: Photoreceptors Rods and Cones]
    R --> LGN[Lateral Geniculate Nucleus: Contrast Relay]
    LGN --> V1[V1 Primary Visual Cortex: Edge and Orientation Detection]
    V1 --> V2[V2: Contour Integration]
    V2 --> V4[V4: Color and Texture Boundaries]
    V4 --> IT[IT Cortex: Object-Level Figure-Ground Separation]
    IT --> SEG[Perceptual Segmentation Output]
```

> **Interpretation:** This is the biological analog of a computer vision segmentation stack. **V1 ≈ edge detection (Canny), V2/V4 ≈ K-Means/Mean-Shift, IT ≈ semantic segmentation (deep learning).**

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define image segmentation. Mention any two Gestalt laws used in computer vision segmentation.*

**Model Answer:**

Image segmentation is the process of partitioning a digital image $I$ into $n$ non-overlapping regions $\{R_1, \dots, R_n\}$ such that $\bigcup R_i = \Omega$ and $R_i \cap R_j = \emptyset$ for $i \neq j$, where pixels within a region share homogeneous features (intensity, color, texture).

Two relevant Gestalt laws:

1. **Proximity** — Spatially close pixels are grouped into the same region (used in superpixel algorithms like SLIC).
2. **Similarity** — Pixels with similar color/intensity are grouped (used in K-Means and Mean-Shift).

> *(Examiner expects a 3-sentence crisp answer with one law definition + one CV mapping.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Why is K-Means considered an implementation of the Gestalt law of similarity? Justify briefly.*

**Model Answer:**

K-Means clusters pixels based on the **squared Euclidean distance** in feature space:

$$\vert\vert \mathbf{p}_{i} - \mu_{k} \vert\vert^{2}$$

This is a **feature-similarity** measure — pixels with similar RGB/HSV values end up in the same cluster irrespective of their spatial location. The Gestalt law of similarity states that *elements with similar visual properties are perceptually grouped*. Hence, K-Means is a direct computational realization of this law.

> *(Always link the math to the perceptual principle — KTU examiner allocates 1 mark for the math and 1 mark for the Gestalt mapping.)*

---

### PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified]*

**(a)** Explain the K-Means clustering algorithm in detail, with a clearly stated objective function, the assignment step, and the update step. Derive the centroid update equation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the six Gestalt laws of grouping. For each law, state one computer vision algorithm that operationalizes it. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

The K-Means algorithm (Lloyd, 1957) partitions a set of $N$ pixels $\{\mathbf{p}_{i}\}_{i=1}^{N}$ into $K$ disjoint clusters by minimizing the **Within-Cluster Sum of Squares (WCSS)**:

$$J = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{\mathbf{p}_{i} \in C_{k}} \vert\vert \mathbf{p}_{i} - \mu_{k} \vert\vert^{2}$$

**Step 1 — Initialization:** Choose $K$ initial centroids. Best practice is K-Means++ seeding to avoid poor local minima.

**Step 2 — Assignment Step:** Each pixel is assigned to its nearest centroid:

$$c(\mathbf{p}_{i}) = \arg\min_{k \in \{1, \dots, K\}} \vert\vert \mathbf{p}_{i} - \mu_{k} \vert\vert^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Update Step:** Recompute each centroid:

$$\mu_{k} = \frac{1}{\vert C_{k} \vert} \sum_{\mathbf{p}_{i} \in C_{k}} \mathbf{p}_{i}$$

**Step 4 — Convergence:** Iterate Steps 2–3 until $\sum_{k} \vert\vert \mu_{k}^{(t)} - \mu_{k}^{(t-1)} \vert\vert^{2} < \epsilon$.

**Derivation of centroid equation:** Setting $\partial J / \partial \mu_{k} = 0$:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial \mu_{k}} \sum_{\mathbf{p}_{i} \in C_{k}} (\mathbf{p}_{i} - \mu_{k})^{\top}(\mathbf{p}_{i} - \mu_{k}) = 0$$

$$-2\sum_{\mathbf{p}_{i} \in C_{k}} \mathbf{p}_{i} + 2\mu_{k} \vert C_{k} \vert = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \mu_{k} = \frac{1}{\vert C_{k} \vert} \sum_{\mathbf{p}_{i} \in C_{k}} \mathbf{p}_{i}$$

**Convergence proof:** $J$ is bounded below by 0 and non-increasing at each step, hence converges.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

| # | Gestalt Law | Perceptual Statement | CV Algorithm |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Proximity** | Close elements are grouped | **SLIC Superpixels** |
| 2 | **Similarity** | Similar elements are grouped | **K-Means, Mean-Shift** |
| 3 | **Good Continuation** | Smooth curves are preferred | **Edge Linking, Active Contours (Snakes)** |
| 4 | **Closure** | Incomplete shapes are completed | **Region Growing, Graph Cuts** |
| 5 | **Common Fate** | Co-moving elements are grouped | **Optical Flow Clustering** |
| 6 | **Symmetry** | Symmetric contours are grouped | **Hough Transform, Generalized Symmetry Detection** |

**Discussion:** The KTU-recommended perspective is that **each Gestalt law provides a perceptual prior**, and each CV algorithm is its mathematical formulation. Modern pipelines (e.g., GrabCut, Mask R-CNN) combine multiple priors for robust segmentation.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]*

**(a)** Explain the Mean-Shift algorithm for image segmentation. State its objective, kernel formulation, and the role of the bandwidth parameter $h$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a worked example, show how K-Means would segment 6 pixels in 2-D feature space into $K = 3$ clusters. Initial centroids: $\mu_1 = (0,0)$, $\mu_2 = (5,5)$, $\mu_3 = (10,10)$. Pixels: $(1,1), (2,0), (4,5), (6,6), (9,10), (11,9)$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Objective:** Locate the modes (peaks) of the underlying probability density function of pixel features, without specifying $K$ in advance.

**Algorithm:**

1. For each pixel $\mathbf{x}$, initialize window at $\mathbf{x}$.
2. Compute the weighted mean of all points within radius $h$:

$$\mathbf{m}(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} K\!\left(\frac{\mathbf{x}_{i} - \mathbf{x}}{h}\right) \mathbf{x}_{i}}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} K\!\left(\frac{\mathbf{x}_{i} - \mathbf{x}}{h}\right)} - \mathbf{x}$$

3. Update window: $\mathbf{x} \leftarrow \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{m}(\mathbf{x})$.
4. Iterate until $\vert\vert \mathbf{m}(\mathbf{x}) \vert\vert < \epsilon$.
5. All pixels converging to the same mode form one segment.

**Kernel:** The Gaussian kernel is most common:

$$K(\mathbf{u}) = \frac{1}{(2\pi h^{2})^{d/2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{\vert\vert \mathbf{u} \vert\vert^{2}}{2h^{2}}\right)$$

**Role of $h$:** The bandwidth controls window size. $h$ small → over-segmentation (many tiny clusters), $h$ large → under-segmentation (few mega-clusters). KTU expects explicit mention of this trade-off.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Iteration 1 — Assignment by squared Euclidean distance:**

| Pixel | $d^{2}$ to $\mu_1$ | $d^{2}$ to $\mu_2$ | $d^{2}$ to $\mu_3$ | Cluster |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $(1,1)$ | $2$ | $32$ | $162$ | $C_1$ |
| $(2,0)$ | $4$ | $18$ | $164$ | $C_1$ |
| $(4,5)$ | $41$ | $1$ | $61$ | $C_2$ |
| $(6,6)$ | $72$ | $2$ | $32$ | $C_2$ |
| $(9,10)$ | $181$ | $41$ | $2$ | $C_3$ |
| $(11,9)$ | $202$ | $52$ | $2$ | $C_3$ |

**Iteration 1 — Update:**

$$\mu_1^{(1)} = \frac{(1,1)+(2,0)}{2} = (1.5, 0.5)$$

$$\mu_2^{(1)} = \frac{(4,5)+(6,6)}{2} = (5, 5.5)$$

$$\mu_3^{(1)} = \frac{(9,10)+(11,9)}{2} = (10, 9.5)$$
