**Iteration 2 — Reassignment:** Re-compute distances with new centroids. Pixels still cluster identically. No changes.

**WCSS (final):**

$$J = [(1-1.5)^{2} + (1-0.5)^{2}] + [(2-1.5)^{2} + (0-0.5)^{2}] + [(4-5)^{2} + (5-5.5)^{2}] + [(6-5)^{2} + (6-5.5)^{2}] + [(9-10)^{2} + (10-9.5)^{2}] + [(11-10)^{2} + (9-9.5)^{2}]$$

$$J = 0.5 + 0.5 + 0.5 + 0.5 + 0.5 + 0.5 = 3.0$$

**Convergence achieved in 2 iterations.** Final segmentation: $C_1 = \{(1,1), (2,0)\}$, $C_2 = \{(4,5), (6,6)\}$, $C_3 = \{(9,10), (11,9)\}$.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mark-loss traps in this topic:**
>
> 1. **Conflating "WCSS" with "MSE":** WCSS is summed over all clusters and all pixels; MSE is per-pixel. KTU deducts 1 mark for confusing the two.
> 2. **Forgetting the disjoint condition:** Always state $R_i \cap R_j = \emptyset$ when defining segmentation — omitting it costs 0.5–1 mark.
> 3. **Not justifying the centroid update:** Simply *writing* the formula $\mu_k = \frac{1}{|C_k|}\sum \mathbf{p}_i$ without showing the derivative-based derivation is a **2-mark penalty** in 7-mark derivation sub-questions.
> 4. **Mean-Shift misconception:** Many students write "Mean-Shift is K-Means with Gaussian weighting." This is **wrong** — Mean-Shift is mode-seeking, not centroid-partitioning. KTU examiners deduct 2 marks for this confusion.
> 5. **Gestalt-to-algorithm mismatch:** Mapping K-Means to "Proximity" is a frequent error. K-Means maps to **Similarity**. Use spatial coordinates *plus* color as features to invoke Proximity as a secondary mapping.
> 6. **Forgetting kernel normalization:** Mean-Shift's $\mathbf{m}(\mathbf{x})$ must be **normalized** by $\sum K(\mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x})$. Writing the un-normalized version costs 1 mark.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density rapid-revision checklist for KTU ESE Module 4:**
>
> - [x] Segmentation is a **pixel-level partition** of $\Omega$ into disjoint, homogeneous regions.
> - [x] **K-Means** minimizes WCSS via iterative assignment and centroid update; convergence is guaranteed.
> - [x] Centroid update: $\mu_k = \frac{1}{|C_k|}\sum_{\mathbf{p}_i \in C_k} \mathbf{p}_i$ (derive this in exams).
> - [x] **Mean-Shift** is non-parametric, mode-seeking, controlled only by bandwidth $h$.
> - [x] **Graph-based (FH)** uses internal difference $\tau(C) = k/|C|$ as merge threshold.
> - [x] **Six Gestalt laws**: Proximity, Similarity, Continuity, Closure, Common Fate, Symmetry.
> - [x] **K-Means ≈ Similarity;** **Mean-Shift ≈ Similarity + Proximity;** **Optical flow clustering ≈ Common Fate.**
> - [x] Human visual segmentation: Retina → LGN → V1 (edges) → V2/V4 (contours) → IT (figure-ground).
> - [x] KTU high-yield formulas: WCSS objective, centroid update, mean-shift vector, Gaussian kernel, FH merge threshold.
> - [x] **Always show one full K-Means iteration** numerically in 7-mark sub-questions.
> - [x] **State the convergence criterion explicitly** ($\Delta < \epsilon$ or max iterations).
> - [x] **Mention K-Means++** if asked about initialization in Part B.
> - [x] **Use Lab color space** (not RGB) for K-Means to get perceptually uniform segmentation — this is a frequent 1-mark bonus point.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST745 - COMPUTER VISION
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Applications - Shot Boundary Detection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Shot Boundary Detection (SBD)**, also known as *Video Temporal Segmentation*, is a fundamental low-level computer vision task that aims to automatically identify the temporal transitions between consecutive camera shots in a video stream. A **shot** is defined as a **continuous, unbroken sequence of frames** recorded by a single camera action, while a **shot boundary** is the precise frame index where one shot ends and another begins.

In KTU 2024 terminology, SBD is positioned as a pre-processing / mid-level feature extraction step that enables higher-level video understanding tasks such as video summarization, content-based video retrieval, video editing automation, and surveillance analytics.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "Reading a Comic Book"

Imagine you are flipping through a comic book. Each **panel (frame)** drawn by the same artist with the same continuous action belongs to one *story panel group (a shot)*. The moment the artwork style abruptly changes — different angle, different scene, new characters — that is a **cut (abrupt boundary)**. Sometimes, the artist shows a gradual transition by fading one page into the next, or wiping a curtain across a scene — these are **gradual boundaries**.

SBD is the "comic-book reader" of the computer vision world — it scans a video frame-by-frame and raises a flag the instant the visual story flow breaks.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standardized Reporting Metrics used in KTU Board Evaluation:**
> - **Precision (P):** Fraction of detected boundaries that are *truly* boundaries.
> - **Recall (R):** Fraction of true boundaries that were *correctly* detected.
> - **F1-Score:** Harmonic mean of Precision and Recall, used as the **single composite benchmark**.

## 1.3 Why Shot Boundary Detection Matters

| Application | Role of SBD |
|---|---|
| Video Summarization | Picks one keyframe per shot to build a skim-through story |
| Content-Based Video Retrieval | Indexes video at the shot level for fast browsing |
| Video Editing Software | Auto-detects cuts for non-linear editing tools |
| Surveillance & Forensics | Detects suspicious "cuts" in CCTV footage to spot tampering |
| Action Recognition | Limits the temporal scope of action to a single shot |
| Semantic Scene Understanding | Provides atomic visual units for higher-level reasoning |

---

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Types of Shot Boundaries (KTU Board Favourite)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU examiner expects students to *classify and visually distinguish* the following categories:

### A. Abrupt Transition (Cut / Hard Cut)
- A single-frame transition — the last frame of Shot 1 is immediately followed by the first frame of Shot 2.
- Mathematically manifests as a **sudden spike** in the inter-frame dissimilarity curve.

### B. Gradual Transitions
1. **Fade** — Shot 1 brightness drops linearly to black (Fade Out); or black brightens to Shot 2 (Fade In).
2. **Dissolve** — Shot 1 fades out *simultaneously* as Shot 2 fades in, producing a cross-blend.
3. **Wipe** — A spatial boundary (line, shape) sweeps across the frame replacing Shot 1 with Shot 2.
4. **Other Effects** — Page peel, iris, spiral, push, etc. (rare in standard test videos).

## 2.2 Operational Pipeline of an SBD System

A typical KTU-style SBD algorithm follows this structured logic:

1. **Frame Extraction:** Decompose the input video $V$ into a discrete frame sequence $F_1, F_2, \dots, F_N$.
2. **Feature Representation:** For every frame (or consecutive frame pair), compute a compact feature vector $f_i$ using one of the methods listed in §2.3.
3. **Dissimilarity Computation:** Compute a pairwise distance $D_i = \text{dist}(f_i, f_{i+1})$ for $i = 1, 2, \dots, N-1$.
4. **Temporal Filtering (Smoothing):** Apply a 1-D low-pass filter to $D_i$ to suppress noise spikes caused by motion / lighting flicker.
5. **Thresholding:** Apply either a *global fixed threshold* or an *adaptive / twin threshold* to convert the dissimilarity curve into a binary boundary decision.
6. **Post-Processing:** Merge adjacent detections, eliminate spurious 1-frame candidates, and label the transition type (cut vs. gradual).

## 2.3 Classical Detection Methods (Board-Relevant)

### 1. Pixel-Based Difference (Naïve)
Direct subtraction of pixel intensities between consecutive frames.

### 2. Histogram-Based Comparison (Industry Standard)
- **Color Histogram:** $\chi^2$, Bhattacharyya, or simple bin-wise difference.
- Robust to small camera and object motion.
- **Most widely used** baseline in KTU problems.

### 3. Edge-Based Difference
Compare edge direction / magnitude histograms between frames. Robust to illumination changes.

### 4. Block-Based / Region-Based
Divide each frame into a grid of $B \times B$ blocks; compare block statistics (mean, variance) block-by-block. A change in spatial distribution of block differences reveals a wipe.

### 5. Motion-Based (Optical Flow)
Estimate optical flow vectors; a sudden change in motion field indicates a cut. Used in high-end surveillance systems.

### 6. Machine Learning / Deep Learning Based
Modern methods use CNNs (e.g., C3D, I3D, TransNetV2) trained on labelled SBD datasets (TRECVID, BBC).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory reference table for KTU 2024 Scheme Board Exams**

| # | Method | Distance Metric $D_i$ | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Pixel Difference | $D_i = \dfrac{1}{MN}\sum_{x,y} \vert I_i(x,y) - I_{i+1}(x,y) \vert$ | Simple cuts |
| 2 | Histogram Intersection | $D_i = 1 - \dfrac{\sum_b \min(H_i(b), H_{i+1}(b))}{\sum_b H_i(b)}$ | Cuts + gradual |
| 3 | Chi-Square Histogram | $D_i = \sum_b \dfrac{(H_i(b) - H_{i+1}(b))^2}{H_i(b) + H_{i+1}(b)}$ | Cuts + gradual |
| 4 | Bhattacharyya | $D_i = \sqrt{1 - \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\bar{H_i}\bar{H_{i+1}}N^2}}\sum_b \sqrt{H_i(b)H_{i+1}(b)}}$ | Distribution shift |
| 5 | Edge Difference | $D_i = 1 - \cos(\theta_i, \theta_{i+1})$ | Illumination robust |
| 6 | Block Mean | $D_i = \sum_{k} \vert \mu_i^k - \mu_{i+1}^k \vert$ | Wipes |

| Evaluation Metric | Formula |
|---|---|
| Precision | $P = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ |
| Recall | $R = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ |
| F1-Score | $F_1 = \dfrac{2 P R}{P + R}$ |
| F-beta Score | $F_\beta = (1+\beta^2) \cdot \dfrac{P R}{\beta^2 P + R}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Twin-Comparison (Boreczky) Thresholds:**
> - $T_h$ (high) → detects abrupt cuts.
> - $T_l$ (low) → cumulative sum over a sliding window $W$ detects gradual transitions.
> - $T_l < T_h$ always.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the **broadcasting industry**, SBD underpins the automated *logging* and *highlight generation* pipelines used by sports channels. In **legal forensics**, even a single undetected cut in a CCTV video can invalidate evidence. In **OTT streaming platforms** (Netflix, Hotstar, Prime), SBD drives *thumbnail generation* — picking the most representative frame of each detected shot for the user interface.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Pixel-Based Inter-Frame Dissimilarity

Let $I_i(x, y)$ denote the grayscale intensity of frame $i$ at pixel location $(x, y)$, where $x \in \{1, \dots, M\}$ and $y \in \{1, \dots, N\}$.

**Step 1:** Define the *raw inter-frame difference image* $\Delta_i$:

$$
\Delta_i(x, y) = \vert I_i(x, y) - I_{i+1}(x, y) \vert
$$

*Conversion Logic:* We use absolute value because we are measuring *magnitude of change*, not direction.

**Step 2:** Sum all absolute differences over the spatial domain of the frame:

$$
S_i = \sum_{x=1}^{M} \sum_{y=1}^{N} \Delta_i(x, y)
$$

*Conversion Logic:* This is the **Sum of Absolute Differences (SAD)** — a scalar summarizing the total pixel-level change between consecutive frames.

**Step 3:** Normalize by the total pixel count to obtain a frame-size-invariant metric:

$$
D_i^{\text{pixel}} = \frac{S_i}{M \cdot N}
$$

*Conversion Logic:* Division by $M \cdot N$ yields an *average* per-pixel change, allowing comparison between videos of different resolutions.

**Step 4:** Apply a *cut decision rule* using a fixed global threshold $T$:

$$
\text{Boundary}(i) = 
\begin{cases}
\text{CUT} & \text{if } D_i^{\text{pixel}} > T \\
\text{NO CUT} & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

*Conversion Logic:* Any spike exceeding $T$ is flagged as a hard cut.

> [!NOTE]
> **Limitation:** The pixel-based metric is **highly sensitive to object and camera motion**. A 2-second pan of a busy street can produce a dissimilarity value comparable to a real cut. This is why the histogram-based method is preferred in practice.

## 3.2 Derivation of Histogram-Based Dissimilarity (Chi-Square)

Let $H_i(b)$ and $H_{i+1}(b)$ be the normalized color histograms of frames $i$ and $i+1$ for bin index $b \in \{1, \dots, B\}$.

**Step 1:** Construct the normalized histogram:

$$
H_i(b) = \frac{\text{count of pixels in bin } b \text{ of frame } i}{\text{total pixels } (M \cdot N)}
$$

*Conversion Logic:* Dividing by total pixels ensures $\sum_b H_i(b) = 1$ (a valid probability mass function).

**Step 2:** Define the chi-square statistic between the two histograms:

$$
\chi^2(i, i+1) = \sum_{b=1}^{B} \frac{\bigl(H_i(b) - H_{i+1}(b)\bigr)^2}{H_i(b) + H_{i+1}(b)}
$$

*Conversion Logic:* The numerator captures squared deviation per bin. The denominator (sum) avoids division-by-zero when both histograms are empty in a bin and weights the statistic by the *expected count* per bin, as in the classical Pearson chi-square test.

**Step 3:** Apply thresholding:

$$
D_i^{\text{hist}} = \chi^2(i, i+1)
$$

$$
\text{Boundary}(i) = 
\begin{cases}
\text{BOUNDARY} & \text{if } D_i^{\text{hist}} > T_h \\
\text{NO BOUNDARY} & \text{if } D_i^{\text{hist}} \leq T_h
\end{cases}
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Method Strengths vs Weaknesses

| Method | Robust to Object Motion | Robust to Illumination | Detects Gradual | Computational Cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Pixel Difference | ❌ Low | ❌ Low | ⚠ Partial | ✅ Very Low |
| Color Histogram (χ²) | ✅ High | ⚠ Moderate | ✅ Yes | ✅ Low |
| Edge Histogram | ✅ High | ✅ High | ✅ Yes | ⚠ Moderate |
| Block-Based | ⚠ Moderate | ❌ Low | ✅ Yes (wipes) | ⚠ Moderate |
| Optical Flow | ✅ High | ✅ High | ⚠ Partial | ❌ High |
| CNN (TransNetV2) | ✅ Very High | ✅ Very High | ✅ Excellent | ❌ Very High |

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A video has 5 frames with the following normalized grayscale histograms (3 bins each):
- $H_1 = [0.4, 0.3, 0.3]$
- $H_2 = [0.4, 0.3, 0.3]$
- $H_3 = [0.1, 0.8, 0.1]$
- $H_4 = [0.1, 0.8, 0.1]$
- $H_5 = [0.5, 0.2, 0.3]$

Compute the chi-square distance $D_i$ for each consecutive pair and identify the cut. Assume threshold $T_h = 0.5$.

**Solution:**

**Pair (1,2):** Both histograms are identical, so each term of the chi-square is zero.

$$
D_1 = \sum_b \frac{(H_1(b) - H_2(b))^2}{H_1(b) + H_2(b)} = 0
$$

**Pair (2,3):** The middle bin shifts from 0.3 to 0.8.

$$
D_2 = \frac{(0.4-0.1)^2}{0.4+0.1} + \frac{(0.3-0.8)^2}{0.3+0.8} + \frac{(0.3-0.1)^2}{0.3+0.1}
$$

$$
D_2 = \frac{0.09}{0.5} + \frac{0.25}{1.1} + \frac{0.04}{0.4} = 0.18 + 0.2273 + 0.10 = 0.5073
$$

**Pair (3,4):** Identical histograms.

$$
D_3 = 0
$$

**Pair (4,5):** Middle bin shifts from 0.8 back to 0.2.

$$
D_4 = \frac{(0.1-0.5)^2}{0.1+0.5} + \frac{(0.8-0.2)^2}{0.8+0.2} + \frac{(0.1-0.3)^2}{0.1+0.3}
$$

$$
D_4 = \frac{0.16}{0.6} + \frac{0.36}{1.0} + \frac{0.04}{0.4} = 0.2667 + 0.36 + 0.10 = 0.7267
$$

**Boundary Decision:**

| Pair | $D_i$ | $> T_h = 0.5$ ? | Result |
|---|---|---|---|
| (1,2) | 0.000 | No | No boundary |
| (2,3) | 0.5073 | Yes | **CUT at frame 2→3** |
| (3,4) | 0.000 | No | No boundary |
| (4,5) | 0.7267 | Yes | **CUT at frame 4→5** |

✅ Two shot boundaries correctly detected: between frames 2–3 and 4–5.

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — End-to-End SBD Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Video Input V] --> B[Frame Extraction]
    B --> C[Feature Vector f_i per frame]
    C --> D[Dissimilarity Computation D_i]
    D --> E[Temporal Smoothing Filter]
    E --> F{D_i greater than T_h?}
    F -->|Yes| G[Flag as CUT]
    F -->|No| H{Cumulative sum over W greater than T_l?}
    H -->|Yes| I[Flag as GRADUAL Transition]
    H -->|No| J[Mark as Within-Shot]
    G --> K[Post-Processing and Merge]
    I --> K
    J --> L[Next Frame Pair]
    L --> D
    K --> M[Final Shot Segment List]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid State Diagram — Transition Categories

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    s1[Within Shot 1] --> s2[Cut Frame] : D_i greater than T_h
    s2 --> s3[Within Shot 2]
    s3 --> s4[Fade In Phase] : D_i in [T_l, T_h]
    s4 --> s5[Within Shot 3]
    s5 --> s6[Dissolve Phase] : cumulative sum greater than T_l
    s6 --> s7[Within Shot 4]
    s7 --> s8[Wipe Region] : spatial block delta
    s8 --> s9[Within Shot 5]
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    end

    end

    end

    F2 --> E1 --> D2
    F2 --> B1 --> D3
    D1 --> T1 --> O1
    D2 --> T1 --> O1
    D3 --> T2 --> O2
```

## 4.4 Textual Schematic of a Dissimilarity Curve

```
Dissimilarity
    |
    |          * (CUT)
    |          |
    |          |       ___ (Dissolve plateau)
    |          |      /   \
    |          |    /       \
    |    noise |  /           \   noise
    |     ||   |/               \  ||
    |____ ||___V_________________V||_________ T_l  (low threshold)
    |                                  T_h (high threshold)
    +----+----+----+----+----+----+----+----> Frame Index
         1    2    3    4    5    6    7    8
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- A **single sharp spike** crossing $T_h$ → **abrupt cut**.
- A **sustained plateau** above $T_l$ (but not $T_h$) for several frames → **gradual transition (dissolve)**.
- Small fluctuations below $T_l$ → **normal intra-shot motion / flicker**.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)]**
*Define shot boundary detection. Differentiate between abrupt and gradual transitions.*

**Model Answer:**
Shot Boundary Detection (SBD) is the process of automatically identifying temporal locations in a video where one camera shot ends and another begins.

| Abrupt Transition (Cut) | Gradual Transition |
|---|---|
| Occurs in a single frame | Spans multiple frames (typically 10–60) |
| Sharp spike in dissimilarity curve | Sustained plateau / bell shape in dissimilarity curve |
| Detected using a high threshold $T_h$ | Detected using cumulative sum with low threshold $T_l$ |
| Example: Newsroom edit cuts | Example: Cross-dissolve, fade in / out, wipe |

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]**
*List any three dissimilarity metrics used in shot boundary detection and state one advantage of histogram-based comparison over pixel-based comparison.*

**Model Answer:**
Three dissimilarity metrics:
1. **Pixel-based SAD** — $\frac{1}{MN}\sum \vert I_i - I_{i+1} \vert$
2. **Histogram Chi-Square** — $\sum_b \frac{(H_i(b) - H_{i+1}(b))^2}{H_i(b) + H_{i+1}(b)}$
3. **Edge Direction Difference** — $1 - \cos(\theta_i, \theta_{i+1})$

**Advantage of histogram-based:** It is **robust to small object and camera motion** because it aggregates pixel statistics into bins, suppressing spatial motion noise that directly affects pixel-level methods.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** *With a neat block diagram, explain the pipeline of a shot boundary detection system. Discuss the role of feature extraction and thresholding stages. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *Explain the twin-comparison method for detecting both abrupt cuts and gradual transitions. How are the high threshold $T_h$ and the low threshold $T_l$ used? (7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**(a) SBD Pipeline — Block Diagram Explanation (7 marks)**

A typical SBD system consists of the following sequential stages:

1. **Frame Extraction:** The input video $V$ is decoded into a sequence of frames $F_1, F_2, \dots, F_N$ sampled at a fixed rate (e.g., 25–30 fps).

2. **Feature Extraction:** Each frame (or pair) is converted into a compact feature vector $f_i$. Common choices include color histograms (HSV, RGB, YCbCr), edge maps, block statistics, or CNN embeddings. **Role:** Reduces dimensionality and exposes a meaningful "signature" of the frame.

3. **Dissimilarity Computation:** Consecutive features are compared using a distance function $D_i = \text{dist}(f_i, f_{i+1})$. The result is a 1-D signal over time known as the *dissimilarity curve*.

4. **Temporal Smoothing:** A 1-D moving average / median filter is applied to the dissimilarity curve to suppress noise from camera shake and lighting flicker.

5. **Thresholding & Decision:** The smoothed signal is compared with threshold(s). A value above $T_h$ → **CUT**; sustained values above $T_l$ for a window → **GRADUAL**.

6. **Post-Processing:** Adjacent detections are merged, isolated 1-frame spikes are removed, and the transition type is finally labelled.

**Role of Feature Extraction:** Determines the *type* of invariance the system has (illumination, motion, etc.). Histograms give motion invariance; edge maps give illumination invariance.

**Role of Thresholding:** Converts a continuous dissimilarity signal into a *binary decision*. Choice of threshold is a **bias–variance trade-off**: high $T_h$ → fewer false positives, more missed cuts.

**(b) Twin-Comparison Method (7 marks)**

The twin-comparison method (Boreczky & Rowe, 1996) uses **two thresholds** to handle the very different amplitudes of cuts and gradual transitions.

- **High threshold $T_h$:** Used to detect *abrupt cuts*. When $D_i > T_h$, a cut is immediately declared at position $i$.
- **Low threshold $T_l$:** Used to detect *gradual transitions*. The algorithm scans for the *start* of a region where $D_i > T_l$, then computes a **cumulative sum** of $D_i - T_l$ over a sliding window $W$ of frames. If the cumulative sum exceeds a third threshold $T_c$, a gradual transition is declared.

**Algorithm Steps:**

1. Smooth the dissimilarity curve $D_i$ using a moving average filter of length $L$.
2. **Cut detection:** For each $i$, if $D_i > T_h$, mark frame $i$ as a CUT.
3. **Gradual detection:** Find the first index $a$ where $D_a > T_l$. Initialize cumulative sum $C = 0$. For $i = a$ onwards, update $C \leftarrow C + (D_i - T_l)$.
4. If $C > T_c$ (or if a CUT is detected while $C$ is still accumulating), declare a gradual transition spanning $[a, i]$.
5. Reset $C$ to 0 once the cumulative sum falls below zero or the transition ends.

**Why two thresholds?**
- A single global threshold cannot serve both transitions because a *gradual transition's* peak amplitude is much smaller than a *cut's* amplitude, yet it is sustained for many frames. $T_l$ allows detection of these lower-amplitude but *cumulatively significant* signals, while $T_h$ prevents false positives in normal motion.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** *Explain the histogram-based method for shot boundary detection. Derive the chi-square distance metric used for comparing two consecutive frames. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *Discuss the performance evaluation of a shot boundary detection system. Define precision, recall, and F1-score with formulas. A system detects 40 shot boundaries in a video. Of these, 35 are true positives, 5 are false positives, and 10 true boundaries are missed. Compute precision, recall, and F1. (7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**(a) Histogram-Based Method (7 marks)**

The histogram-based method is the **most widely used** approach in classical SBD because it is fast, simple, and surprisingly robust to small object / camera motion.

**Why histograms?**
Histograms aggregate pixel intensities over the entire frame, throwing away *spatial information* but preserving *global color/intensity distribution*. Therefore, a small movement of a person within the frame produces almost no change in the histogram, but a *cut* to a completely different scene produces a major shift.

**Steps:**
1. Convert each frame from RGB to a perceptually uniform color space (HSV, YCbCr, or LAB).
2. Compute a normalized histogram $H_i(b)$ with $B$ bins (e.g., $B = 64$ or $256$) for each frame.
3. Compute the pairwise distance $D_i = d(H_i, H_{i+1})$ using one of:
   - Histogram Intersection
   - Chi-Square
   - Bhattacharyya
   - L1 / L2 norm

**Derivation of Chi-Square Distance:**

Let $H_i(b)$ and $H_{i+1}(b)$ be normalized histograms such that $\sum_b H_i(b) = 1$. The chi-square statistic tests the null hypothesis that both histograms are drawn from the same distribution.

For each bin $b$:

$$
\chi_b^2 = \frac{\bigl(H_i(b) - H_{i+1}(b)\bigr)^2}{H_i(b) + H_{i+1}(b)}
$$

Summing over all $B$ bins:

$$
\chi^2(i, i+1) = \sum_{b=1}^{B} \frac{\bigl(H_i(b) - H_{i+1}(b)\bigr)^2}{H_i(b) + H_{i+1}(b)}
$$

*Interpretation:* A large $\chi^2$ value implies the two histograms are statistically different → likely a shot boundary. A small $\chi^2$ value implies similarity → within the same shot.

**Thresholding:** Apply a global threshold $T_h$ to the $\chi^2$ curve. Peaks above $T_h$ are declared as boundaries.

**(b) Performance Evaluation & Numerical Problem (7 marks)**

**Definitions:**
- **True Positive (TP):** System correctly detects a real shot boundary.
- **False Positive (FP):** System falsely reports a boundary where none exists.
- **False Negative (FN):** System misses a real boundary.

**Precision (P):** Of all boundaries reported, how many are correct?

$$
P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}
$$

**Recall (R):** Of all true boundaries, how many were detected?

$$
R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}
$$

**F1-Score:** Harmonic mean of precision and recall — penalizes imbalanced systems.

$$
F_1 = \frac{2 P R}{P + R}
$$

**Numerical Computation:**

Given:
- $TP = 35$
- $FP = 5$
- $FN = 10$

**Precision:**

$$
P = \frac{35}{35 + 5} = \frac{35}{40} = 0.875 \;\; (87.5\%)
$$

**Recall:**

$$
R = \frac{35}{35 + 10} = \frac{35}{45} = 0.7778 \;\; (77.78\%)
$$

**F1-Score:**

$$
F_1 = \frac{2 \times 0.875 \times 0.7778}{0.875 + 0.7778} = \frac{1.3611}{1.6528} = 0.8235 \;\; (82.35\%)
$$

**Interpretation:** The system has high precision (most of its detections are correct) but moderate recall (it misses about 22% of true boundaries). A typical SBD system on standard datasets (TRECVID) achieves F1 ≈ 0.85–0.95, so the computed 0.8235 is **acceptable but improvable** — perhaps by lowering the threshold $T_h$ to catch more boundaries at the cost of a few more false positives.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing the two thresholds:** Students write $T_l > T_h$. The correct relationship is $T_l < T_h$. Losing 1 mark immediately.
> 2. **Forgetting normalization:** Histogram formulas must use *normalized* histograms. Writing raw pixel counts in the chi-square formula costs full marks.
> 3. **Mis-stating the order of pipeline:** Thresholding comes *after* feature extraction and dissimilarity computation, not before.
> 4. **Skipping the cumulative sum explanation:** For gradual transitions, simply stating "use $T_l$" is incomplete. You must explain the *cumulative sum* mechanism.
> 5. **Confusing precision and recall:** Precision = "correctness of detections"; Recall = "completeness of detection". Mixing these up is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.
> 6. **Not labeling diagrams:** Every Mermaid / block diagram in the answer sheet must have arrow labels and stage names. Unlabelled diagrams receive partial credit only.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Must Memorize Before KTU Exam**

- ✅ SBD = **temporal segmentation of video** into shots at the frame level.
- ✅ Two main transition types: **Abrupt Cut** (1-frame spike) and **Gradual Transition** (multi-frame plateau).
- ✅ Gradual sub-types: **Fade, Dissolve, Wipe, Iris, Push**.
- ✅ Pipeline: **Frame Extraction → Feature Extraction → Dissimilarity → Smoothing → Thresholding → Post-Processing**.
- ✅ Most popular feature: **Color Histogram** (Y / HSV / YCbCr).
- ✅ Most popular metric: **Chi-Square Histogram Distance** — derived from the Pearson chi-square test.
- ✅ Twin thresholds: $T_h$ (high) for cuts, $T_l$ (low) + cumulative sum for gradual.
- ✅ Edge-based methods are **illumination-robust**; block-based methods **detect wipes**; optical flow handles **motion-heavy scenes**.
- ✅ Modern SOTA: **CNN-based** models like *TransNetV2*, *AutoShot*, *SBD-Deep*.
- ✅ Evaluation: **Precision, Recall, F1-Score** computed against a ground-truth annotation (e.g., TRECVID, BBC SBD dataset).
- ✅ F1-Score is the **single composite metric** most commonly reported in SBD literature.
- ✅ Pixel-based method is **fragile to motion**; histogram-based is the **industrial default**.
- ✅ Twin-comparison is the **classical SBD algorithm** expected in any KTU board answer on gradual transition detection.
- ✅ Always **smooth the dissimilarity curve** with a moving average filter before thresholding.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Background Subtraction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST745 - COMPUTER VISION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Background Subtraction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Background Subtraction (BGS)** is a classical computer vision technique used in video sequence analysis to detect moving or changed objects (the **foreground**) by computing the difference between the current frame and a reference model of the static scene (the **background**).

In formal KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, background subtraction is defined as a **pixel-level temporal differencing process** in which a statistical or deterministic model of the scene is maintained and updated, and pixels whose intensity significantly deviates from this model are classified as foreground.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> Background Subtraction is a foreground detection technique that builds a model of the static scene and identifies pixels that do not belong to this model in successive frames. It is the foundational step in motion detection, surveillance, and video analytics pipelines.

The general mathematical formulation is expressed as:

$$\text{ForegroundMask}(x, y, t) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } \vert I(x, y, t) - B(x, y, t) \vert > T \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

Where:
- $I(x, y, t)$ = intensity of the current frame at pixel $(x,y)$ and time $t$
- $B(x, y, t)$ = background model intensity at the same pixel
- $T$ = decision threshold (typically a small positive scalar)

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are sitting in a quiet library watching the same bookshelf every day. Over time, your brain builds a **mental model** of what the bookshelf looks like — the colors of the books, their arrangement, the shelf edges. Now, when someone walks into the library carrying a **bright red book**, your brain instantly flags it: *"Wait, this is new! This wasn't here before!"*

This is exactly how background subtraction works:
- The **bookshelf** = the **background model** (what is "normal" and static)
- The **bright red book** = the **foreground** (what is "different" and new)
- The **threshold** of attention in your brain = the **decision threshold** $T$
- Your brain's ability to gradually forget old books and remember new arrangements = the **learning rate** $\alpha$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Anchor:** Background Subtraction is essentially a "change detection" mechanism that separates "static scene" from "moving objects" by modeling what is expected and flagging what is unexpected.

## 1.3 Why Background Subtraction Matters in KTU Curriculum

In the KTU 2024 Scheme PECST745 (Computer Vision) syllabus, background subtraction falls under Module 4, which focuses on **motion analysis and video understanding**. It is a **prerequisite concept** for:

| Application Domain | Role of BGS |
|---|---|
| Video Surveillance | Detect intruders in CCTV feeds |
| Traffic Monitoring | Count and classify vehicles |
| Human-Computer Interaction | Gesture recognition via webcam |
| Sports Analytics | Player and ball tracking |
| Medical Imaging | Cell motion in microscopy |

## 1.4 Standard Performance Metrics in BGS

> [!TIP]
> The following metrics are universally accepted in the computer vision literature for evaluating background subtraction algorithms. They frequently appear in KTU Part A questions.

- **True Positive Rate (Recall / Sensitivity):** $TPR = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$
- **False Positive Rate:** $FPR = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN}$
- **Precision:** $P = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$
- **F-Measure:** $F_1 = \dfrac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}$

Where $TP$ = true positives, $FP$ = false positives, $FN$ = false negatives, $TN$ = true negatives.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Pipeline of Background Subtraction

Every background subtraction algorithm, regardless of sophistication, follows a **five-stage pipeline**:

1. **Frame Acquisition** — Read the current video frame $I_t$ from the camera or file.
2. **Background Modeling** — Maintain (and update) a representation $B_t$ of the static scene.
3. **Comparison / Detection** — Compute a distance or likelihood measure between $I_t$ and $B_t$.
4. **Thresholding** — Apply a decision rule using threshold $T$ to produce a binary foreground mask.
5. **Post-Processing** — Refine the mask using morphological operations (erosion, dilation, opening, closing) and connected-component analysis.

## 2.2 Classification of Background Subtraction Methods

Background subtraction methods are broadly classified into **four families**, each with progressively greater representational power:

### Family 1 — Frame Differencing (Simplest)

The background is approximated by the previous frame $I_{t-1}$ itself.

$$B_t(x, y) = I_{t-1}(x, y)$$

$$\text{Foreground}(x, y, t) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } \vert I_t(x, y) - I_{t-1}(x, y) \vert > T \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Why this works:** If objects in the scene are moving, their pixel intensity will differ between consecutive frames. **Why it fails:** It cannot detect stopped objects, and any camera shake creates massive false positives.

### Family 2 — Running (Temporal) Average

The background is a weighted linear combination of all past frames:

$$B_t(x, y) = \alpha \cdot I_t(x, y) + (1 - \alpha) \cdot B_{t-1}(x, y)$$

Where:
- $\alpha$ = **learning rate** (typically $0 < \alpha < 1$, often $\alpha \approx 0.01$ to $0.05$)
- Larger $\alpha$ = faster adaptation to scene changes (less memory of the past)
- Smaller $\alpha$ = slower adaptation (more stable but slower to recover from disturbances)

**The "Why":** This recursive formula is mathematically equivalent to an exponential moving average. It gives more weight to recent frames while still remembering the older ones. The effective temporal window is approximately $1/\alpha$ frames.

### Family 3 — Single Gaussian Per Pixel

Each pixel is modeled by a Gaussian (Normal) distribution:

$$P(I_t(x,y)) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}} \exp\left(-\frac{(I_t(x,y) - \mu_t)^2}{2\sigma_t^2}\right)$$

A pixel is classified as foreground if it deviates from the mean by more than a threshold:

$$\frac{\vert I_t(x,y) - \mu_t \vert}{\sigma_t} > k$$

Where $k$ is typically chosen between **2.5 and 3.0** (corresponding to 99% to 99.7% confidence).

**Update equations:**

$$\mu_{t+1} = (1 - \alpha) \mu_t + \alpha I_t$$

$$\sigma_{t+1}^2 = (1 - \alpha) \sigma_t^2 + \alpha (I_t - \mu_t)^2$$

### Family 4 — Mixture of Gaussians (MOG / GMM)

The most powerful and practically used approach. Each pixel is modeled by a **mixture of $K$ Gaussians** (typically $K = 3$ to $5$), allowing the model to handle:

- Multi-modal backgrounds (e.g., waving trees, flickering monitors, water surfaces)
- Gradual illumination changes
- Shadows (when one Gaussian models the shadow intensity)

The probability density function is:

$$P(I_t) = \sum_{i=1}^{K} w_{i,t} \cdot \eta(I_t, \mu_{i,t}, \sigma_{i,t}^2)$$

Where $\eta$ is the Gaussian PDF and $w_{i,t}$ is the weight of the $i$-th Gaussian (with $\sum w_i = 1$).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Formula Table — Memorize for ESE**

| Method | Background Equation | Foreground Decision Rule | Key Parameters |
|---|---|---|---|
| Frame Differencing | $B_t = I_{t-1}$ | $\vert I_t - I_{t-1} \vert > T$ | Threshold $T$ |
| Running Average | $B_t = \alpha I_t + (1-\alpha) B_{t-1}$ | $\vert I_t - B_t \vert > T$ | $\alpha$, $T$ |
| Single Gaussian | $\mu_t$, $\sigma_t^2$ maintained per pixel | $\frac{\vert I_t - \mu_t \vert}{\sigma_t} > k$ | $\alpha$, $k$ |
| Mixture of Gaussians | $\sum_{i=1}^{K} w_i \cdot \eta(I_t, \mu_i, \sigma_i^2)$ | Mahalanobis distance from "background" Gaussians | $K$, $\alpha$, $T_b$ |
| MOG Weight Update | $w_{k,t} = (1-\alpha)w_{k,t-1} + \alpha M_{k,t}$ | — | $\alpha$ |
| MOG Mean Update (matched) | $\mu_t = (1-\rho)\mu_{t-1} + \rho I_t$ | — | $\rho = \alpha \eta(I_t)$ |
| MOG Variance Update | $\sigma_t^2 = (1-\rho)\sigma_{t-1}^2 + \rho(I_t - \mu_t)^2$ | — | $\rho$ |
| Background Selection (MOG) | First $B$ Gaussians s.t. $\sum_{i=1}^{B} w_i > T_b$ | — | $T_b \approx 0.7$ |
| Morphological Erosion | $(f \ominus b)(x) = \min_{(y) \in b} f(x+y)$ | Removes small noise | Structuring element $b$ |
| Morphological Dilation | $(f \oplus b)(x) = \max_{(y) \in b} f(x+y)$ | Fills small holes | Structuring element $b$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Caution:** The vertical bar symbol for absolute value is rendered as `\vert` in this table to prevent markdown table column collision. In handwritten answers, students may freely use $\vert \cdot \vert$.

## 2.4 Engineering Significance

The choice of background subtraction algorithm in real-world systems is governed by:

- **Computational budget** — Running average is O(1) per pixel per frame; MOG is O(K).
- **Scene complexity** — Static office vs. outdoor with swaying vegetation.
- **Real-time constraints** — Surveillance at 30 FPS requires efficient algorithms.
- **Hardware** — MOG is now standard in OpenCV (`cv2.createBackgroundSubtractorMOG2`), optimized with SIMD instructions.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Unrolling the Running Average Formula

We begin with the recursive running average:

$$B_t = \alpha I_t + (1 - \alpha) B_{t-1}$$

**Step 1 — Substitute the same expression for $B_{t-1}$:**

$$B_{t-1} = \alpha I_{t-1} + (1 - \alpha) B_{t-2}$$

Substituting back into the original:

$$B_t = \alpha I_t + (1 - \alpha) \left[ \alpha I_{t-1} + (1 - \alpha) B_{t-2} \right]$$

**Step 2 — Distribute the $(1 - \alpha)$ factor:**

$$B_t = \alpha I_t + \alpha(1 - \alpha) I_{t-1} + (1 - \alpha)^2 B_{t-2}$$

**Step 3 — Continue substituting backwards repeatedly:**

After $n$ unrolling steps:

$$B_t = \alpha I_t + \alpha(1 - \alpha) I_{t-1} + \alpha(1 - \alpha)^2 I_{t-2} + \cdots + \alpha(1 - \alpha)^{n-1} I_{t-n+1} + (1 - \alpha)^n B_{t-n}$$

**Step 4 — Take the limit as $n \to \infty$:**

As $n \to \infty$, the term $(1 - \alpha)^n B_{t-n} \to 0$ (provided $\vert 1 - \alpha \vert < 1$, which holds for $0 < \alpha < 2$). Therefore:

$$B_t = \sum_{i=0}^{\infty} \alpha (1 - \alpha)^i I_{t-i}$$

**Step 5 — Verify the coefficients sum to unity:**

$$\sum_{i=0}^{\infty} \alpha (1 - \alpha)^i = \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{1 - (1 - \alpha)} = \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{\alpha} = 1$$

This confirms that $B_t$ is a **weighted average** of all past frames with exponentially decaying weights. The **effective memory window** is approximately $1/\alpha$ frames.

**Step 6 — Compute effective memory for typical values:**

- If $\alpha = 0.01$, then effective window $\approx 1/0.01 = 100$ frames ($\approx 3.3$ seconds at 30 FPS)
- If $\alpha = 0.05$, then effective window $\approx 1/0.05 = 20$ frames ($\approx 0.67$ seconds at 30 FPS)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** This derivation shows that the running average is mathematically equivalent to an **Infinite Impulse Response (IIR) filter** from digital signal processing. KTU examiners often test this conceptual equivalence.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Maximum Likelihood Estimate of Gaussian Parameters

For a set of $n$ observations $X = \{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n\}$ drawn i.i.d. from a Gaussian distribution $N(\mu, \sigma^2)$, the likelihood is:

$$L(\mu, \sigma^2) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}} \exp\left(-\frac{(x_i - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$

**Step 1 — Take the log-likelihood (monotonic transformation):**

$$\ln L = -\frac{n}{2}\ln(2\pi) - \frac{n}{2}\ln(\sigma^2) - \frac{1}{2\sigma^2} \sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \mu)^2$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate with respect to $\mu$ and set to zero:**

$$\frac{\partial \ln L}{\partial \mu} = \frac{1}{\sigma^2} \sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \mu) = 0$$

Solving:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i = n\mu \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \hat{\mu} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate with respect to $\sigma^2$ and set to zero:**

$$\frac{\partial \ln L}{\partial \sigma^2} = -\frac{n}{2\sigma^2} + \frac{1}{2\sigma^4}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \mu)^2 = 0$$

Solving:

$$\hat{\sigma}^2 = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \hat{\mu})^2$$

**Step 4 — Connection to incremental update:**

For real-time background subtraction, the recursive maximum likelihood estimates are:

$$\mu_t = \frac{1}{t}\sum_{i=1}^{t} x_i \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \mu_t = (1 - \tfrac{1}{t})\mu_{t-1} + \tfrac{1}{t}x_t$$

Replacing $\tfrac{1}{t}$ with a constant learning rate $\alpha$ (an approximation valid for large $t$):

$$\mu_t \approx (1 - \alpha)\mu_{t-1} + \alpha x_t$$

This is precisely the running average update — confirming that the running average is the **asymptotic ML estimate** of the mean.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Mahalanobis Distance for MOG Classification

In MOG, when a new observation $I_t$ arrives, we compute the Mahalanobis distance to each Gaussian:

$$D_k = \sqrt{(I_t - \mu_k)^T \Sigma_k^{-1} (I_t - \mu_k)}$$

For a single Gaussian per pixel (scalar case), this reduces to:

$$D_k = \frac{\vert I_t - \mu_k \vert}{\sigma_k}$$

**The "Why" of Mahalanobis:** Unlike Euclidean distance, Mahalanobis distance **normalizes by the variance** of the distribution. A deviation of 5 in a low-noise pixel (small $\sigma$) is more significant than the same deviation in a high-noise pixel (large $\sigma$).

**Classification rule:** A pixel matches the $k$-th Gaussian if $D_k < T$ (typically $T \approx 2.5$).

**Match indicator function:**

$$M_{k,t} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if Gaussian } k \text{ matches } I_t \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step-by-step MOG update (one pixel, one frame):**

**Step 1 — Determine match:**
Find the Gaussian with smallest $D_k$ (above all other Gaussians). If $D_{min} < T$, set $M_{min,t} = 1$ and all other $M_{k,t} = 0$.

**Step 2 — Update weights for all Gaussians:**

$$w_{k,t} = (1 - \alpha) w_{k,t-1} + \alpha M_{k,t}$$

**Step 3 — Update only the matched Gaussian's parameters:**

$$\rho = \alpha \cdot \eta(I_t, \mu_{k}, \sigma_{k}^2)$$

$$\mu_{k,t} = (1 - \rho) \mu_{k,t-1} + \rho I_t$$

$$\sigma_{k,t}^2 = (1 - \rho) \sigma_{k,t-1}^2 + \rho (I_t - \mu_{k,t})^2$$

**Step 4 — If no match found, replace the least-weighted Gaussian:**

Replace the Gaussian with smallest $w/\sigma$ (indicating it represents rare events) with a new Gaussian initialized at $I_t$ with high variance and low weight.

**Step 5 — Re-normalize weights:**

$$w_{k,t} \leftarrow \frac{w_{k,t}}{\sum_{i=1}^{K} w_{i,t}}$$

**Step 6 — Select background Gaussians:**

Sort Gaussians by the ratio $w/\sigma$ in descending order. The first $B$ Gaussians satisfying:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{B} w_i > T_b \quad (T_b \approx 0.7)$$

are classified as background; the rest represent foreground.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Running Average

**Problem:** Consider a pixel that takes the following intensity values over 5 frames:
$I_1 = 100, I_2 = 110, I_3 = 90, I_4 = 105, I_5 = 95$
With learning rate $\alpha = 0.2$ and initial background $B_0 = 100$.

**Solution:**

$$B_1 = 0.2 \cdot 100 + 0.8 \cdot 100 = 100.0$$

$$B_2 = 0.2 \cdot 110 + 0.8 \cdot 100 = 102.0$$

$$B_3 = 0.2 \cdot 90 + 0.8 \cdot 102 = 99.6$$

$$B_4 = 0.2 \cdot 105 + 0.8 \cdot 99.6 = 100.68$$

$$B_5 = 0.2 \cdot 95 + 0.8 \cdot 100.68 = 99.544$$

**Observation:** Despite individual frame intensities fluctuating from 90 to 110, the background estimate stabilizes around $\approx 100$, which is the **true scene intensity**. This demonstrates the **noise-smoothing property** of running averages.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Background Subtraction Pipeline

This flowchart illustrates the complete end-to-end processing chain of a generic background subtraction system, from raw frame input to final foreground mask.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Video Input Frames I_t] --> B[Preprocessing]
    B --> B1[Color Conversion]
    B --> B2[Noise Filtering]
    B --> B3[Resize or ROI Selection]
    B1 --> C[Background Model B_t]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    C --> D{Pixelwise Comparison}
    A --> D
    D --> E[Distance Computation]
    E --> F[Threshold T Decision]
    F --> G{Is Difference Greater Than T?}
    G -->|Yes| H[Foreground Pixel 1]
    G -->|No| I[Background Pixel 0]
    H --> J[Binary Foreground Mask]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Morphological Opening]
    K --> L[Morphological Closing]
    L --> M[Connected Component Analysis]
    M --> N[Final Foreground Objects]
    N --> O[Applications: Tracking, Counting, Classification]
    C --> P[Background Update using Learning Rate alpha]
    P --> C
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Mixture of Gaussians (MOG) Internal Architecture

This block diagram shows the internal state management of the Mixture of Gaussians method, where each pixel maintains $K$ parallel Gaussian components.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[New Pixel Intensity I_t] --> Match{Match Existing Gaussian?}
    Match -->|Yes Match Found| UpdateMatch[Update Matched Gaussian]
    Match -->|No Match| Replace[Replace Weakest Gaussian with New One]
    UpdateMatch --> UpdateParams[Update mu sigma and weight w of matched component]
    Replace --> InitNew[Initialize new Gaussian with mu=I_t, high sigma, low weight]
    UpdateParams --> UpdateWeights[Update Weights of all K Gaussians]
    InitNew --> UpdateWeights
    UpdateWeights --> Normalize[Normalize Weights to Sum to 1]
    Normalize --> Sort[Sort Gaussians by w over sigma ratio]
    Sort --> Select[Select Top B Gaussians as Background where cumulative w exceeds T_b]
    Select --> Decision[Pixel is Background if I_t matches one of selected Gaussians]
    Decision --> Output[Foreground Mask Output]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Comparison of BGS Methods

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph "Method Categories"
        A1[Frame Differencing]
        A2[Running Average]
        A3[Single Gaussian]
        A4[Mixture of Gaussians]
    end
    A1 --> R1[Lowest Complexity, Noisy Results]
    A2 --> R2[Low Complexity, Moderate Accuracy]
    A3 --> R3[Medium Complexity, Good for Static Scenes]
    A4 --> R4[High Complexity, Best for Dynamic Backgrounds]
```

## 4.4 Conceptual Block Diagram — Typical Surveillance Use Case

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Cam[CCTV Camera] --> Stream[Video Stream]
    Stream --> Module[BGS Module]
    Module --> DetMask[Foreground Detection Mask]
    DetMask --> Blob[Blob Detection]
    Blob --> Tracker[Object Tracker]
    Tracker --> Classifier[Activity Classifier]
    Classifier --> Alert[Alarm or Logging System]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Background Subtraction `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** Background Subtraction is a computer vision technique used to detect moving objects in a video by subtracting the current frame from a reference background model and thresholding the difference.

**Principle (1 Mark):** It operates on the principle that the background is mostly static while foreground objects exhibit temporal change. A pixel is classified as foreground if:

$$\vert I(x, y, t) - B(x, y, t) \vert > T$$

**Key Assumption (1 Mark):** The camera is stationary, and the background changes much more slowly than the foreground.

---

### Question 2: List any four challenges in background subtraction `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. **Illumination changes** — Gradual (time of day) or sudden (cloud movement, lights on/off) variation in scene lighting invalidates the background model.
2. **Dynamic backgrounds** — Waving trees, water ripples, flickering monitors introduce pixel variations not caused by foreground objects.
3. **Shadows** — Cast shadows of moving objects are detected as foreground, doubling the apparent object size.
4. **Camouflage** — Foreground objects with intensity similar to the background are not detected (false negatives).
5. **Camera jitter** — Small camera vibrations cause all pixels to appear "moving".
6. **Bootstrapping** — The initial frames may contain moving objects, polluting the background model.

*(Any four well-explained points = 3 marks.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Carefully:**
> 1. **Never write only definitions.** KTU expects algorithm steps, equations, and block diagrams. Missing math = **−3 to −4 marks**.
> 2. **Do not skip the threshold/decision rule.** Many students forget to mention how the foreground mask is obtained.
> 3. **Always specify the role of each parameter** (e.g., $\alpha$, $T$, $K$). Examiners specifically look for parameter justification.
> 4. **In MOG questions, students often confuse Mahalanobis distance with Euclidean distance.** Use the correct normalized formula.
> 5. **Do not omit post-processing steps** (morphological operations). They are considered integral to a complete BGS pipeline.

---

### Question A: Running Average and Mixture of Gaussians `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:** Explain the Running Average method for background subtraction with mathematical formulation. Discuss the role of the learning rate $\alpha$ and its trade-offs.

**Model Answer:**

**Conceptual Introduction (1 Mark):**
The running average method maintains a recursive estimate of the background by combining the current frame with the previous background estimate, weighted by a learning rate $\alpha$.

**Mathematical Formulation (2 Marks):**

$$B_t(x, y) = \alpha \cdot I_t(x, y) + (1 - \alpha) \cdot B_{t-1}(x, y)$$

$$F_t(x, y) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } \vert I_t(x, y) - B_t(x, y) \vert > T \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Unrolled Form (1 Mark):**

$$B_t = \sum_{i=0}^{\infty} \alpha(1-\alpha)^i I_{t-i}$$

This shows the running average is an **exponentially weighted** average of all past frames.

**Role of Learning Rate $\alpha$ (2 Marks):**
- **Small $\alpha$ (e.g., 0.01):** Slow adaptation; background is very stable, but slow to recover from sudden changes (e.g., parked car leaving the scene). Effective memory window $\approx 1/\alpha$ frames.
- **Large $\alpha$ (e.g., 0.1):** Fast adaptation; background quickly absorbs changes, but also quickly absorbs the foreground into the background (foreground becomes "invisible").

**Foreground Decision (1 Mark):**
Binary mask is obtained by thresholding the absolute pixel-wise difference between current frame and background using threshold $T$.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:** Explain the Mixture of Gaussians (MOG) method for background modeling. How does it handle multi-modal backgrounds?

**Model Answer:**

**Concept (1 Mark):**
MOG (proposed by Stauffer & Grimson, 1999) models each pixel as a **mixture of $K$ Gaussian distributions** (typically $K = 3$ to $5$), each representing a different state of the scene.

**Pixel Model (1 Mark):**

$$P(I_t) = \sum_{i=1}^{K} w_{i,t} \cdot \eta(I_t, \mu_{i,t}, \sigma_{i,t}^2)$$

**Algorithm Steps (3 Marks):**

1. **Match:** For each new pixel $I_t$, find the Gaussian $k$ such that $\vert I_t - \mu_k \vert < 2.5 \sigma_k$ (Mahalanobis criterion).
2. **Update matched Gaussian:**
   - $\rho = \alpha \cdot \eta(I_t, \mu_k, \sigma_k^2)$
   - $\mu_k \leftarrow (1-\rho)\mu_k + \rho I_t$
   - $\sigma_k^2 \leftarrow (1-\rho)\sigma_k^2 + \rho(I_t - \mu_k)^2$
3. **Update all weights:** $w_k \leftarrow (1-\alpha)w_k + \alpha M_k$
4. **No match:** Replace the Gaussian with smallest $w/\sigma$ ratio with a new Gaussian centered at $I_t$.
5. **Background Selection:** Sort Gaussians by $w/\sigma$ descending. First $B$ Gaussians with $\sum w_i > T_b$ (typically $T_b = 0.7$) are classified as background.

**Handling Multi-modal Backgrounds (2 Marks):**
In a scene with waving trees, a pixel can take values like "leaf color" or "sky color" alternately. Each is captured by a separate Gaussian. The pixel is background if it matches **any** of the "background" Gaussians. This is the key advantage over single Gaussian methods.

---

### Question B: Challenges and Complete BGS Algorithm `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]`

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:** Discuss in detail the various challenges faced in background subtraction. How does the MOG algorithm address them?

**Model Answer:**

**Challenge 1 — Gradual Illumination Changes (2 Marks):**
Slow shifts in lighting (sunrise, sunset) cause all background pixels to drift in intensity. **MOG solution:** The mean $\mu$ of each Gaussian is continuously updated, so the background model slowly adapts to the new lighting.

**Challenge 2 — Sudden Illumination Changes (1 Mark):**
Sudden events (cloud passing, lights off). **MOG solution:** The high-variance new Gaussian absorbs the change quickly without permanently corrupting all $K$ components.

**Challenge 3 — Dynamic Backgrounds (2 Marks):**
Waving trees, water surface, monitors. **MOG solution:** Each background mode (e.g., "tree branch in position A", "tree branch in position B") gets its own Gaussian. The multiple modes are not mistakenly classified as foreground because the system can recognize recurring pixel values.

**Challenge 4 — Shadows (1 Mark):**
Cast shadows have lower intensity than the background but differ from it. **MOG solution:** Shadows often cluster as a separate Gaussian mode and can be filtered using chromaticity analysis or by detecting low intensity in non-foreground pixels.

**Challenge 5 — Camouflage (1 Mark):**
Foreground objects with intensity matching the background. **MOG solution:** Limited; this remains an open problem and is typically addressed using texture-based or stereo-based enhancements.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:** Explain a complete Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM) based background subtraction algorithm with all update equations and the background selection criterion.

**Model Answer:**

**Setup (1 Mark):**
Each pixel $(x, y)$ maintains a mixture of $K$ Gaussians. For frame $t$, the model parameters are:

$$\{w_{i,t}, \mu_{i,t}, \sigma_{i,t}^2\}_{i=1}^{K}$$

with the constraint $\sum_{i=1}^{K} w_{i,t} = 1$.

**Step 1 — Match (2 Marks):**
For new observation $I_t$, find Gaussian $k$ such that $\vert I_t - \mu_{k,t-1} \vert < 2.5 \sigma_{k,t-1}$. If found, $M_k = 1$, all other $M_i = 0$.

**Step 2 — Weight Update (1 Mark):**

$$w_{i,t} = (1 - \alpha) w_{i,t-1} + \alpha M_{i,t}$$

**Step 3 — Parameter Update for Matched Gaussian (1 Mark):**

$$\rho = \alpha \eta(I_t, \mu_k, \sigma_k^2)$$

$$\mu_{k,t} = (1 - \rho) \mu_{k,t-1} + \rho I_t$$

$$\sigma_{k,t}^2 = (1 - \rho) \sigma_{k,t-1}^2 + \rho (I_t - \mu_{k,t})^2$$

**Step 4 — Replacement (1 Mark):**
If no match, replace Gaussian with smallest $w/\sigma$ using $\mu = I_t$, $\sigma^2 = $ large initial value, $w = $ small initial value.

**Step 5 — Background Selection (1 Mark):**
Sort Gaussians by $w/\sigma$ in descending order. Pick first $B$ Gaussians such that:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{B} w_i > T_b$$

$T_b \approx 0.7$. A pixel is classified as foreground if its best-matching Gaussian is **not** among the first $B$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Bullets — KTU 2024 ESE**

- **Background Subtraction** = pixel-level technique to separate static background from moving foreground.
- The core decision rule is universally: $\vert I_t - B_t \vert > T \Rightarrow$ Foreground.
- **Four method families in increasing complexity:** Frame Differencing → Running Average → Single Gaussian → Mixture of Gaussians.
- **Running Average formula** is recursive: $B_t = \alpha I_t + (1-\alpha) B_{t-1}$. It is mathematically equivalent to an exponential moving average and an IIR filter.
- **Learning rate $\alpha$:** Controls adaptation speed. Small $\alpha$ = stable but slow; large $\alpha$ = adaptive but noisy.
- **Single Gaussian** models pixel intensity with mean $\mu$ and variance $\sigma^2$; uses Mahalanobis distance for classification.
- **MOG** uses $K = 3$ to $5$ Gaussians per pixel; the first $B$ Gaussians (sorted by $w/\sigma$) whose cumulative weight exceeds $T_b \approx 0.7$ constitute the background.
- **MOG handles dynamic backgrounds** (e.g., waving trees) because each recurring mode gets its own Gaussian.
- **Major challenges:** illumination change, shadows, dynamic background, camouflage, camera jitter, bootstrapping.
- **Post-processing:** Morphological operations (opening, closing) and connected component analysis are essential for cleaning up the foreground mask.
- **OpenCV implementations:** `cv2.createBackgroundSubtractorMOG2()`, `cv2.createBackgroundSubtractorKNN()`.
- **Always remember:** The background model is **continuously updated**; it is not a fixed image.
- **Pipelines:** Input → Preprocessing → Background Modeling → Comparison → Thresholding → Post-processing → Output.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Image Segmentation by Clustering Pixels - Simple Clustering Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST745 - COMPUTER VISION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Image Segmentation by Clustering Pixels - Simple Clustering Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Image Segmentation by Clustering Pixels** is an **unsupervised pattern recognition** technique in computer vision that partitions a digital image into multiple disjoint, homogeneous regions (segments) by treating each pixel as a point in a multi-dimensional **feature space** (e.g., intensity, RGB, HSV, or texture descriptors) and grouping pixels that are *similar* according to a chosen **similarity/dissimilarity metric** and **clustering criterion**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme vocabulary, this falls under **low-level / pixel-level segmentation** where the output is a *label map* $L: \mathbb{Z}^{2} \rightarrow \{1, 2, \dots, K\}$ that assigns every spatial pixel coordinate $(x, y)$ to one of $K$ cluster identifiers.

### The "Simple" Tag in Simple Clustering Methods
The qualifier **"Simple"** in the syllabus refers to algorithms that:
- Do **not** require a pre-trained statistical model (unlike GMM/EM).
- Do **not** require user-drawn seeds (unlike graph cuts / random walker).
- Use **deterministic or greedy iterative updates** (e.g., K-Means, K-Medoids, Mean Shift, Agglomerative clustering, and basic histogram thresholding as 1-D clustering).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology Distinction (Frequently Tested in KTU):**
> - **Clustering in Feature Space** ≠ **Clustering in Spatial Domain**.
> Feature-space clustering ignores pixel coordinates and clusters by *appearance* only (a red car and a red fire extinguisher end up in the same cluster even if they are at opposite corners of the image).
> - **Region-based segmentation** considers *spatial contiguity* in addition to feature similarity.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Marble-Sorting Factory**
> Imagine you receive a truckload of 1 million marbles in random colours (red, green, blue, yellow). You have **no instruction manual** and **no idea how many colours exist**. You need to sort them into bins.
>
> 1. You pick a small handful of marbles and lay them on a table — these are your **initial cluster centers (centroids)**.
> 2. Every other marble is moved to the bin whose centre it *looks most like* (smallest colour-distance).
> 3. After sorting, you compute the **average colour of each bin** and update the centre label.
> 4. Repeat steps 2–3 until no marble ever changes its bin.
>
> The marbles are pixels, the colour is the feature vector, the bins are clusters, and the iterative refinement is the K-Means algorithm. **Mean Shift** is like a sliding window that *crawls* toward the densest region of marbles, and **Agglomerative clustering** is like repeatedly *merging the two most similar bins* until a target count remains.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants (Highlighted in Bold)

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Range / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $\mathbf{K}$ | **Number of clusters** (user-defined) | $2 \le K \le 20$ (typical) |
| $\epsilon$ | **Convergence tolerance** | $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-6}$ |
| $T_{\max}$ | **Maximum iterations** | $50$ to $500$ |
| $d(\cdot, \cdot)$ | **Distance / dissimilarity metric** | Euclidean, Manhattan, Mahalanobis |
| $\mu_{j}$ | **Centroid of cluster $j$** | Vector in $\mathbb{R}^{d}$ |
| $\mathbf{x}_{i}$ | **Feature vector of pixel $i$** | Vector in $\mathbb{R}^{d}$ |

---

## 1.4 When Do We Use Clustering-Based Segmentation? (Engineering Utility)

- **Medical Imaging**: Separating tumour vs healthy tissue in MRI/CT scans based on intensity histograms.
- **Satellite Remote Sensing**: Land-cover classification (water, vegetation, urban) in multi-spectral imagery.
- **Industrial Quality Control**: Detecting defective regions on a uniformly coloured product surface.
- **Document Processing**: Separating text, background, and graphics in scanned documents.
- **Traffic Monitoring**: Segmenting vehicles, road, and sky from CCTV frames.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Generic Clustering Pipeline (Universal Block)

Every simple clustering method for image segmentation, regardless of the algorithm, executes the following five logical stages:

1. **Feature Extraction**: Convert each pixel into a feature vector $\mathbf{x}_{i} \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$.
   - 1-D: grayscale intensity $I(x,y) \in [0, 255]$.
   - 3-D: RGB or HSV triplet.
   - 5-D: RGB + $(x, y)$ spatial coordinates (gives spatial-coherent clustering).
2. **Initialization**: Place initial cluster centers $\mu_{1}^{(0)}, \mu_{2}^{(0)}, \dots, \mu_{K}^{(0)}$ in the feature space.
3. **Assignment Step**: For every pixel $\mathbf{x}_{i}$, assign it to the nearest centroid.

$$j^{\star} = \arg\min_{j \in \{1, \dots, K\}} \, d\bigl(\mathbf{x}_{i}, \mu_{j}^{(t)}\bigr)$$

4. **Update Step**: Recompute each centroid as the **mean** of all pixels currently assigned to it.

$$\mu_{j}^{(t+1)} = \frac{1}{\vert C_{j}^{(t)} \vert} \sum_{\mathbf{x}_{i} \in C_{j}^{(t)}} \mathbf{x}_{i}$$

5. **Convergence Check**: Stop when the centroids no longer move beyond $\epsilon$ or $T_{\max}$ is reached.

---

## 2.2 K-Means Clustering — The Workhorse

### 2.2.1 Objective Function (Distortion / Within-Cluster Sum of Squares)

The K-Means algorithm **minimizes** the following objective, known as the **WCSS** (Within-Cluster Sum of Squares) or **distortion** $J$:

$$J(\mathcal{C}, \mu) = \sum_{j=1}^{K} \sum_{\mathbf{x}_{i} \in C_{j}} \bigl\Vert \mathbf{x}_{i} - \mu_{j} \bigr\Vert_{2}^{2}$$

- $C_{j}$ = the set of pixel feature vectors assigned to cluster $j$.
- $\mu_{j}$ = the centroid of cluster $j$.
- $\Vert \cdot \Vert_{2}$ = the **$L_{2}$ (Euclidean) norm**.

### 2.2.2 Why the Mean Update is Optimal

Setting $\frac{\partial J}{\partial \mu_{j}} = 0$ yields:

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \mu_{j}} = -2 \sum_{\mathbf{x}_{i} \in C_{j}} \bigl(\mathbf{x}_{i} - \mu_{j}\bigr) = 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \mu_{j} = \frac{1}{\vert C_{j} \vert} \sum_{\mathbf{x}_{i} \in C_{j}} \mathbf{x}_{i}$$

This is the **intuitive "average colour"** of all pixels in the cluster.

### 2.2.3 Convergence Properties

- K-Means is **guaranteed to converge** in finite steps because $J$ is monotonically non-increasing and bounded below by $0$.
- However, it converges to a **local minimum**, not a global minimum. The final segmentation depends on the initial centroid placement (solved by **K-Means++** initialization).
- **Time complexity per iteration**: $O(N \cdot K \cdot d)$ where $N$ = number of pixels, $K$ = clusters, $d$ = feature dimensionality.

---

## 2.3 Mean Shift Clustering

Mean Shift is a **non-parametric, density-based** technique. It does not require the user to specify $K$ — it discovers clusters as **modes of the underlying probability density function (PDF)**.

### 2.3.1 Kernel Density Estimate (KDE)

The estimated PDF at location $\mathbf{y}$ using a kernel $K(\cdot)$ and bandwidth $h$ is:

$$\hat{f}(\mathbf{y}) = \frac{1}{N h^{d}} \sum_{i=1}^{N} K\!\left(\frac{\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{x}_{i}}{h}\right)$$

### 2.3.2 The Mean Shift Vector

The gradient ascent direction toward the nearest mode is:

$$\mathbf{m}(\mathbf{y}) = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathbf{x}_{i} \, K\!\left(\frac{\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{x}_{i}}{h}\right)}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} K\!\left(\frac{\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{x}_{i}}{h}\right)} - \mathbf{y}$$

The window is iteratively shifted as: $\mathbf{y}^{(t+1)} = \mathbf{y}^{(t)} + \mathbf{m}(\mathbf{y}^{(t)})$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Common Kernel Choice:** The **Epanechnikov kernel** $K_{E}(u) = \frac{3}{4}(1 - u^{2})$ for $\vert u \vert \le 1$ minimizes the asymptotic mean integrated squared error (AMISE).

### 2.3.3 Bandwidth $h$ — The Critical Hyper-parameter

- $h$ too **small** → fragmentation (one true cluster becomes many).
- $h$ too **large** → over-merging (distinct clusters fuse into one).
- Rule of thumb: $h \approx \sigma \cdot \left(\frac{4}{3N}\right)^{1/5}$ for Gaussian-distributed data.

---

## 2.4 Agglomerative (Hierarchical) Clustering

Starts with $N$ singleton clusters and successively merges the two most similar clusters until $K$ clusters remain.

### 2.4.1 Linkage Criteria (Distance Between Two Clusters $A$ and $B$)

| Linkage | Formula | Behaviour |
|---|---|---|
| **Single Linkage** | $d_{\min}(A,B) = \min_{a \in A, b \in B} d(a,b)$ | Tends to produce **chaining** (long, thin clusters) |
| **Complete Linkage** | $d_{\max}(A,B) = \max_{a \in A, b \in B} d(a,b)$ | Tends to produce **compact, spherical** clusters |
| **Average Linkage** | $d_{\text{avg}}(A,B) = \frac{1}{\vert A \vert \vert B \vert} \sum_{a \in A, b \in B} d(a,b)$ | Compromise between single and complete |
| **Ward's Linkage** | $\Delta J = \frac{\vert A \vert \vert B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert} \, \Vert \mu_{A} - \mu_{B} \Vert^{2}$ | Minimizes the increase in WCSS $J$ |

- **Time complexity**: $O(N^{2} \log N)$ using a priority queue. **Not feasible for full images** — usually applied to **super-pixels**.

---

## 2.5 Thresholding as 1-D Clustering (Otsu's Method)

> [!TIP]
> **Frequently asked in KTU as a "simple clustering method"** — multi-level thresholding can be cast as a clustering of intensity values along the 1-D histogram.

For a **bi-level threshold** $T$, **Otsu's method** chooses $T$ to **maximize the between-class variance**:

$$\sigma_{B}^{2}(T) = \omega_{0}(T)\,\omega_{1}(T)\,\bigl[\mu_{0}(T) - \mu_{1}(T)\bigr]^{2}$$

- $\omega_{0}, \omega_{1}$ = class probabilities (normalized histogram bins to the left/right of $T$).
- $\mu_{0}, \mu_{1}$ = class means.
- The optimal $T^{\star} = \arg\max_{T} \sigma_{B}^{2}(T)$ maximizes inter-class separability.
- **For $K$-level thresholding**: exhaustive search over $T_{1}, T_{2}, \dots, T_{K-1}$ is combinatorial; **recursive Otsu** or **GA optimization** is used.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table. It covers ~80% of KTU numerical/derivation questions on this topic.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | K-Means Objective (WCSS) | $J = \sum_{j=1}^{K} \sum_{\mathbf{x}_{i} \in C_{j}} \Vert \mathbf{x}_{i} - \mu_{j} \Vert_{2}^{2}$ | $J \ge 0$ always |
| 2 | Centroid Update | $\mu_{j} = \frac{1}{\vert C_{j} \vert} \sum_{\mathbf{x}_{i} \in C_{j}} \mathbf{x}_{i}$ | Set $\partial J / \partial \mu_{j} = 0$ |
| 3 | Assignment Rule | $j^{\star} = \arg\min_{j} \Vert \mathbf{x}_{i} - \mu_{j} \Vert$ | Hard, non-overlapping |
| 4 | Squared Euclidean Distance | $d^{2}(\mathbf{a}, \mathbf{b}) = \sum_{m=1}^{d} (a_{m} - b_{m})^{2}$ | Standard K-Means metric |
| 5 | Manhattan Distance | $d_{1}(\mathbf{a}, \mathbf{b}) = \sum_{m=1}^{d} \vert a_{m} - b_{m} \vert$ | Use $\vert \cdot \vert$ not $\vert \cdot \vert$ in tables |
| 6 | Mean Shift Vector | $\mathbf{m}(\mathbf{y}) = \frac{\sum \mathbf{x}_{i} K(\frac{\mathbf{y}-\mathbf{x}_{i}}{h})}{\sum K(\frac{\mathbf{y}-\mathbf{x}_{i}}{h})} - \mathbf{y}$ | Window shifts toward PDF mode |
| 7 | KDE Estimate | $\hat{f}(\mathbf{y}) = \frac{1}{N h^{d}} \sum_{i=1}^{N} K(\frac{\mathbf{y}-\mathbf{x}_{i}}{h})$ | Non-parametric PDF |
| 8 | Otsu Between-Class Variance | $\sigma_{B}^{2}(T) = \omega_{0}\omega_{1}(\mu_{0} - \mu_{1})^{2}$ | Maximize to find $T^{\star}$ |
| 9 | Within-Class Variance (Otsu) | $\sigma_{W}^{2} = \omega_{0}\sigma_{0}^{2} + \omega_{1}\sigma_{1}^{2}$ | $\sigma_{T}^{2} = \sigma_{W}^{2} + \sigma_{B}^{2}$ (total) |
| 10 | Ward's Linkage | $\Delta J = \frac{\vert A \vert \vert B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert} \Vert \mu_{A} - \mu_{B} \Vert^{2}$ | Minimizes $J$ increase |

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **K-Means**: Fastest, suitable for *real-time* segmentation (e.g., video surveillance at 30 fps).
- **Mean Shift**: Excellent for *unknown* number of clusters; used in **CamShift object tracking**.
- **Agglomerative**: Used in **medical imaging** when cluster count is unknown and data size is small (post super-pixel reduction).
- **Otsu**: Dominant in **document binarization** and **PCB defect detection** because print/scan images have a strong bimodal histogram.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Centroid Update Rule from First Principles

We want to find the $\mu_{j}$ that minimizes the contribution of cluster $j$ to the distortion $J$:

$$J_{j} = \sum_{\mathbf{x}_{i} \in C_{j}} \bigl\Vert \mathbf{x}_{i} - \mu_{j} \bigr\Vert^{2} = \sum_{\mathbf{x}_{i} \in C_{j}} \sum_{m=1}^{d} (x_{i,m} - \mu_{j,m})^{2}$$

**Step 1**: Take the partial derivative with respect to a single component $\mu_{j,m}$:

$$\frac{\partial J_{j}}{\partial \mu_{j,m}} = \sum_{\mathbf{x}_{i} \in C_{j}} 2(x_{i,m} - \mu_{j,m})(-1) = -2 \sum_{\mathbf{x}_{i} \in C_{j}} (x_{i,m} - \mu_{j,m})$$

**Step 2**: Set the derivative to zero (necessary condition for a minimum, since $J_{j}$ is convex in $\mu_{j}$):

$$\sum_{\mathbf{x}_{i} \in C_{j}} (x_{i,m} - \mu_{j,m}) = 0$$

**Step 3**: Expand the summation. Since $\mu_{j,m}$ is constant within the sum:

$$\sum_{\mathbf{x}_{i} \in C_{j}} x_{i,m} - \sum_{\mathbf{x}_{i} \in C_{j}} \mu_{j,m} = 0$$

$$\sum_{\mathbf{x}_{i} \in C_{j}} x_{i,m} - \vert C_{j} \vert \, \mu_{j,m} = 0$$

**Step 4**: Solve for $\mu_{j,m}$:

$$\mu_{j,m} = \frac{1}{\vert C_{j} \vert} \sum_{\mathbf{x}_{i} \in C_{j}} x_{i,m}$$

**Step 5**: Stack the components into a vector. This yields the well-known centroid update rule:

$$\boxed{\mu_{j} = \frac{1}{\vert C_{j} \vert} \sum_{\mathbf{x}_{i} \in C_{j}} \mathbf{x}_{i}}$$

**Why this matters in KTU exams**: Examiners love asking *"Why does K-Means use the mean and not the median?"* The answer is: *the mean is the unique minimizer of the squared Euclidean distance, derivable via the convexity argument above.* The median minimizes $L_{1}$ distance; using it would yield **K-Medians**, a different algorithm.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Otsu's Threshold from Variance Decomposition

For an image with $L$ intensity levels $\{0, 1, \dots, L-1\}$, let $p(i)$ be the normalized histogram. A threshold $T$ splits the histogram into:
- **Class 0 (background)**: levels $0 \dots T$
- **Class 1 (foreground)**: levels $T+1 \dots L-1$

**Step 1 — Class probabilities (weights)**:

$$\omega_{0}(T) = \sum_{i=0}^{T} p(i), \qquad \omega_{1}(T) = \sum_{i=T+1}^{L-1} p(i) = 1 - \omega_{0}(T)$$

**Step 2 — Class means**:

$$\mu_{0}(T) = \frac{\sum_{i=0}^{T} i \, p(i)}{\omega_{0}(T)}, \qquad \mu_{1}(T) = \frac{\sum_{i=T+1}^{L-1} i \, p(i)}{\omega_{1}(T)}$$

**Step 3 — Global image mean**:

$$\mu_{T} = \omega_{0}\mu_{0} + \omega_{1}\mu_{1}$$

**Step 4 — Total variance (a constant, independent of $T$)**:

$$\sigma_{T}^{2} = \sum_{i=0}^{L-1} (i - \mu_{T})^{2} \, p(i)$$

**Step 5 — Within-class variance**:

$$\sigma_{W}^{2}(T) = \omega_{0} \underbrace{\sum_{i=0}^{T} \frac{(i - \mu_{0})^{2} p(i)}{\omega_{0}}}_{\sigma_{0}^{2}} + \omega_{1} \underbrace{\sum_{i=T+1}^{L-1} \frac{(i - \mu_{1})^{2} p(i)}{\omega_{1}}}_{\sigma_{1}^{2}}$$

**Step 6 — Variance decomposition theorem**:

$$\sigma_{T}^{2} = \sigma_{W}^{2}(T) + \sigma_{B}^{2}(T)$$

Since $\sigma_{T}^{2}$ is **constant**, **minimizing** $\sigma_{W}^{2}(T)$ is **equivalent** to **maximizing** $\sigma_{B}^{2}(T)$:

$$\sigma_{B}^{2}(T) = \omega_{0}\omega_{1}(\mu_{0} - \mu_{1})^{2}$$

**Step 7 — Exhaustive search**:

$$T^{\star} = \arg\max_{T \in \{0, 1, \dots, L-2\}} \sigma_{B}^{2}(T)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip**: Otsu's is the *only* algorithm here that does not need a user-supplied $K$ (it finds the *optimal* $T$ for $K=2$). For $K > 2$, you must sweep over $T_{1} < T_{2} < \dots < T_{K-1}$, which is $\binom{L-1}{K-1}$ combinations.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — K-Means on a 4-Pixel Toy Image

Consider 4 pixels with intensity features $\mathbf{x} = \{1, 2, 8, 9\}$. Let $K=2$, initial centroids $\mu_{1}^{(0)} = 1$, $\mu_{2}^{(0)} = 9$.

| Iteration | Pixel 1 ($\mathbf{x}=1$) | Pixel 2 ($\mathbf{x}=2$) | Pixel 3 ($\mathbf{x}=8$) | Pixel 4 ($\mathbf{x}=9$) | $\mu_{1}$ | $\mu_{2}$ | $J$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **t = 0** | — | — | — | — | $1$ | $9$ | — |
| **t = 1 (assign)** | $\vert 1-1 \vert = 0$ ✓ | $\vert 2-1 \vert = 1$ ✓ | $\vert 8-9 \vert = 1$ ✓ | $\vert 9-9 \vert = 0$ ✓ | $1$ | $9$ | $0$ |
| **t = 1 (update)** | — | — | — | — | $\frac{1+2}{2} = 1.5$ | $\frac{8+9}{2} = 8.5$ | — |
| **t = 2 (assign)** | $\vert 1-1.5 \vert = 0.5$ | $\vert 2-1.5 \vert = 0.5$ | $\vert 8-8.5 \vert = 0.5$ | $\vert 9-8.5 \vert = 0.5$ | $1.5$ | $8.5$ | $4 \times 0.25 = 1$ |
| **t = 2 (update)** | — | — | — | — | $\frac{1+2}{2} = 1.5$ | $\frac{8+9}{2} = 8.5$ | $1$ |
| **Converged** ✓ | (no change) | (no change) | (no change) | (no change) | $1.5$ | $8.5$ | $1$ |

**Interpretation**:
- Final cluster $C_{1} = \{1, 2\}$ with centroid $\mu_{1} = 1.5$.
- Final cluster $C_{2} = \{8, 9\}$ with centroid $\mu_{2} = 8.5$.
- The final WCSS $J = 1$ is the smallest achievable value for this data with $K=2$ — this is a **global optimum** (rare in real K-Means; happens here because data is symmetric).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Frequently Asked Variant**: *"Re-run with $\mu_{1}^{(0)} = 2$, $\mu_{2}^{(0)} = 8$."* Verify the result is the same — this teaches that *K-Means can be initialization-sensitive only in pathological cases.*

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Simple Clustering Methods

| Property | K-Means | Mean Shift | Agglomerative | Otsu Threshold |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **User sets $K$?** | Yes | No (auto) | Yes (cut dendrogram) | No ($K=2$ only) |
| **Cluster shape** | Spherical (isotropic) | Arbitrary (modes of PDF) | Depends on linkage | Histogram modes |
| **Convergence** | Local min of $J$ | Local max of PDF | Deterministic, hierarchical | Global for $K=2$ |
| **Time complexity** | $O(N K d T)$ | $O(N^{2} T)$ | $O(N^{2} \log N)$ | $O(L^{2})$ |
| **Spatial info used?** | No (unless added) | No (unless added) | No (unless added) | No |
| **Best for** | Real-time, large images | Unknown $K$, non-spherical | Small/super-pixel data | Bimodal histograms |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — K-Means Image Segmentation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Image I x y] --> B[Feature Extraction per Pixel]
    B --> C[Feature Space: x_i in R^d]
    C --> D[K-Means Initialization]
    D --> D1{Strategy?}
    D1 -->|Random| E1[Random K Pixels as Centroids]
    D1 -->|K-Means++| E2[Distance-weighted Probabilistic Pick]
    E1 --> F
    E2 --> F
    F[Iteration t = 0 1 2 ...] --> G[Assignment Step]
    G --> G1[For each x_i: j star = argmin d x_i mu_j]
    G1 --> H[Update Step]
    H --> H1[For each j: mu_j new = mean of C_j]
    H1 --> I{Converged?}
    I -->|No| F
    I -->|Yes| J[Label Map L: assign each pixel to final C_j]
    J --> K[Output: Segmented Image / Region Map]
```

**Reading the diagram**: This is the canonical K-Means segmentation pipeline. The *initialization choice* (Random vs K-Means++) is critical — K-Means++ reduces the probability of poor local minima from $O(2^{-K})$ to $O(\epsilon)$ for any $K$.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Generic Clustering Segmentation System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Stage]
        img[Raw Image]
        annot[Optional: ROI Mask]
    end

    subgraph FEAT[Feature Extraction]
        color[Color Space: RGB or HSV]
        coord[Spatial Coords: x y]
        tex[Texture: GLCM or LBP]
    end

    subgraph CLUST[Clustering Engine]
        kmean[K-Means Module]
        mshift[Mean Shift Module]
        agglom[Agglomerative Module]
        otsu[Otsu Threshold Module]
    end

    subgraph POST[Post-Processing]
        morph[Morphological Cleanup]
        filt[Small Region Removal]
        bbox[Bounding Box Extraction]
    end

    subgraph OUT[Output]
        seg[Segmented Label Map]
        overlay[Overlay on Original Image]
    end

    img --> color
    img --> coord
    img --> tex
    annot --> CLUST
    color --> kmean
    color --> mshift
    tex --> agglom
    color --> otsu
    kmean --> morph
    mshift --> morph
    agglom --> filt
    otsu --> morph
    morph --> bbox
    bbox --> seg
    seg --> overlay
```

**Key reading**:
- The **Feature Extraction block** determines what "similarity" means.
- The **Clustering Engine** is plug-and-play: KTU questions may ask you to *replace* one module (e.g., swap K-Means for Mean Shift) and explain the difference in output.
- **Post-Processing** (morphological opening/closing) is essential in practice because clustering does not enforce spatial contiguity.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid State Diagram — Convergence of K-Means

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Initialize
    Initialize --> Assign: set mu_j at t=0
    Assign --> ComputeJ: form clusters C_j
    ComputeJ --> CheckDelta: evaluate J t and J t-1
    CheckDelta --> Converged: delta J less than epsilon
    CheckDelta --> UpdateCentroids: delta J greater or equal epsilon
    UpdateCentroids --> Assign: mu_j t+1 = mean of C_j
    Converged --> OutputLabelMap
    OutputLabelMap --> [*]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip**: When asked *"How does K-Means know it has converged?"*, the two valid answers are **(a)** centroid displacement $\Vert \mu_{j}^{(t+1)} - \mu_{j}^{(t)} \Vert < \epsilon$, **or** **(b)** distortion decrease $\vert J^{(t+1)} - J^{(t)} \vert < \epsilon$. Both are accepted in KTU valuation keys.

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Comparison Diagram — Effect of Bandwidth in Mean Shift

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SMALL[Small h: Fragmentation]
```
