```mermaid
        s1[Mode 1] --> s1l[Cluster 1]
        s2[Mode 2 close] --> s2l[Cluster 2 spurious]
        s3[Mode 3 close] --> s3l[Cluster 3 spurious]
    end
    subgraph GOOD[Optimal h: Correct K Clusters]
        g1[True Mode A] --> g1l[Cluster 1]
        g2[True Mode B] --> g2l[Cluster 2]
        g3[True Mode C] --> g3l[Cluster 3]
    end
    subgraph LARGE[Large h: Over-merging]
        l1[All pixels in one mode] --> l1l[Single Cluster 1 wrong]
    end
```

This is a critical visual for KTU 14-mark questions on *mean shift bandwidth tuning*.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**
> **Q1. (3 Marks)** *Define image segmentation by clustering pixels. State any two simple clustering methods used for this purpose.*
>
> **Model Answer**:
> Image segmentation by clustering pixels is the process of partitioning an image into $K$ homogeneous regions by treating each pixel's feature vector (e.g., intensity, RGB, HSV) as a point in a multi-dimensional feature space, and grouping pixels that are similar according to a chosen distance metric and clustering criterion, without using any labelled training data.
> Two simple clustering methods are:
> 1. **K-Means clustering** — partitions pixels into $K$ clusters by minimizing the within-cluster sum of squared Euclidean distances.
> 2. **Mean Shift clustering** — discovers clusters as modes of the kernel density estimate by iteratively shifting a window toward regions of highest pixel density.
> *(Acceptable alternatives: Agglomerative clustering, Otsu's thresholding, K-Medoids.)*

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Sample Paper]**
> **Q2. (3 Marks)** *List the steps of the K-Means algorithm. Why is the algorithm sensitive to initialization?*
>
> **Model Answer**:
> Steps:
> 1. Choose $K$ and initialize centroids $\mu_{1}^{(0)}, \dots, \mu_{K}^{(0)}$.
> 2. **Assignment step**: Assign each pixel $\mathbf{x}_{i}$ to the nearest centroid by $j^{\star} = \arg\min_{j} \Vert \mathbf{x}_{i} - \mu_{j}^{(t)} \Vert^{2}$.
> 3. **Update step**: Recompute each centroid as the mean of its cluster: $\mu_{j}^{(t+1)} = \frac{1}{\vert C_{j} \vert} \sum_{\mathbf{x}_{i} \in C_{j}} \mathbf{x}_{i}$.
> 4. Repeat steps 2–3 until convergence ($\Vert \mu^{(t+1)} - \mu^{(t)} \Vert < \epsilon$).
>
> Initialization sensitivity: K-Means optimizes a **non-convex** objective, so different initial centroids lead to different local minima. Poor initialization can yield empty clusters, suboptimal WCSS $J$, or merged distinct clusters. **K-Means++** mitigates this by spreading initial centroids via distance-weighted probabilistic sampling.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**
> **Q A. (14 Marks)**
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the K-Means clustering algorithm for image segmentation. Derive the centroid update formula from the K-Means objective function.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** Given 6 pixels with intensity values $\{2, 3, 8, 9, 10, 11\}$, apply K-Means with $K = 2$ and initial centroids $\mu_{1}^{(0)} = 3$, $\mu_{2}^{(0)} = 10$. Show two complete iterations and compute the final WCSS.

#### Model Solution for Q A(a)

**Algorithm Explanation [4 marks]:**
The K-Means algorithm for image segmentation proceeds as follows:
1. **Feature space construction**: For a grayscale image of size $H \times W$, stack all $N = H \cdot W$ pixel intensities into a set $\{\mathbf{x}_{1}, \mathbf{x}_{2}, \dots, \mathbf{x}_{N}\}$.
2. **Initialization**: Choose $K$ initial centroids $\{\mu_{1}^{(0)}, \dots, \mu_{K}^{(0)}\}$.
3. **Assignment (E-step analogue)**: For each pixel, compute squared Euclidean distance to every centroid and assign to the nearest one.
4. **Update (M-step analogue)**: Recompute each centroid as the mean of all pixels assigned to it.
5. **Convergence**: Stop when centroid movement $<\epsilon$ or $J$ stabilizes.

**Derivation [3 marks]:**
The objective is the within-cluster sum of squares $J = \sum_{j=1}^{K} \sum_{\mathbf{x}_{i} \in C_{j}} \Vert \mathbf{x}_{i} - \mu_{j} \Vert^{2}$.
For cluster $j$, expand: $J_{j} = \sum_{\mathbf{x}_{i} \in C_{j}} \sum_{m=1}^{d} (x_{i,m} - \mu_{j,m})^{2}$.
Setting $\frac{\partial J_{j}}{\partial \mu_{j,m}} = -2 \sum_{\mathbf{x}_{i} \in C_{j}} (x_{i,m} - \mu_{j,m}) = 0$.
Solving: $\mu_{j,m} = \frac{1}{\vert C_{j} \vert} \sum_{\mathbf{x}_{i} \in C_{j}} x_{i,m}$, which when stacked vector-wise gives $\mu_{j} = \frac{1}{\vert C_{j} \vert} \sum_{\mathbf{x}_{i} \in C_{j}} \mathbf{x}_{i}$.

#### Model Solution for Q A(b)

**Iteration 1 (Assignment with $\mu_{1}=3, \mu_{2}=10$):**

| Pixel $x_{i}$ | $\vert x_{i} - 3 \vert$ | $\vert x_{i} - 10 \vert$ | Assigned to |
|---|---|---|---|
| $2$ | $1$ | $8$ | $C_{1}$ |
| $3$ | $0$ | $7$ | $C_{1}$ |
| $8$ | $5$ | $2$ | $C_{2}$ |
| $9$ | $6$ | $1$ | $C_{2}$ |
| $10$ | $7$ | $0$ | $C_{2}$ |
| $11$ | $8$ | $1$ | $C_{2}$ |

So $C_{1}^{(1)} = \{2, 3\}$, $C_{2}^{(1)} = \{8, 9, 10, 11\}$.

**Iteration 1 (Update):**

$$\mu_{1}^{(1)} = \frac{2 + 3}{2} = 2.5, \qquad \mu_{2}^{(1)} = \frac{8 + 9 + 10 + 11}{4} = 9.5$$

**Iteration 2 (Assignment with $\mu_{1}=2.5, \mu_{2}=9.5$):**

| Pixel | $\vert x - 2.5 \vert$ | $\vert x - 9.5 \vert$ | Assigned to |
|---|---|---|---|
| $2$ | $0.5$ | $7.5$ | $C_{1}$ |
| $3$ | $0.5$ | $6.5$ | $C_{1}$ |
| $8$ | $5.5$ | $1.5$ | $C_{2}$ |
| $9$ | $6.5$ | $0.5$ | $C_{2}$ |
| $10$ | $7.5$ | $0.5$ | $C_{2}$ |
| $11$ | $8.5$ | $1.5$ | $C_{2}$ |

**Iteration 2 (Update):**

$$\mu_{1}^{(2)} = 2.5 \;\;(\text{no change}), \qquad \mu_{2}^{(2)} = 9.5 \;\;(\text{no change})$$

**Convergence reached** (centroids unchanged).

**Final WCSS calculation:**

$$J^{\star} = (2 - 2.5)^{2} + (3 - 2.5)^{2} + (8 - 9.5)^{2} + (9 - 9.5)^{2} + (10 - 9.5)^{2} + (11 - 9.5)^{2}$$
$$= 0.25 + 0.25 + 2.25 + 0.25 + 0.25 + 2.25 = 5.5$$

**Answer**: Final clusters $C_{1} = \{2, 3\}$ (centroid $2.5$) and $C_{2} = \{8, 9, 10, 11\}$ (centroid $9.5$); $J^{\star} = 5.5$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**
> **Q B. (14 Marks)**
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the Mean Shift clustering algorithm for image segmentation. Discuss the role of the bandwidth parameter $h$ and the kernel function.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** With a normalized intensity histogram of a 5-level image given by $p(0)=0.10, p(1)=0.20, p(2)=0.15, p(3)=0.05, p(4)=0.50$, apply Otsu's method to find the optimal bi-level threshold $T^{\star}$ that maximizes between-class variance.

#### Model Solution for Q B(a)

**Mean Shift Algorithm [4 marks]:**
1. Place a window of bandwidth $h$ over every pixel in the feature space.
2. Compute the **mean shift vector** $\mathbf{m}(\mathbf{y})$ within the window.
3. Shift the window center: $\mathbf{y}^{(t+1)} = \mathbf{y}^{(t)} + \mathbf{m}(\mathbf{y}^{(t)})$.
4. Repeat until $\Vert \mathbf{m}(\mathbf{y}) \Vert < \epsilon$.
5. The convergence point is the **mode** of the local density. All pixels converging to the same mode are merged into one cluster.

**Role of Bandwidth $h$ [2 marks]:**
- $h$ defines the **scale** of the kernel window and indirectly the **size of the clusters** the algorithm discovers.
- Small $h$ → high-resolution but fragmented segmentation (many tiny clusters).
- Large $h$ → coarse segmentation; distinct modes merge into one.
- $h$ is the single most important user-tunable parameter; it is typically chosen by cross-validation or by a rule of thumb: $h \approx 1.06 \, \hat{\sigma} \, N^{-1/5}$ for Gaussian data (Silverman's rule).

**Role of Kernel Function $K$ [1 mark]:**
The kernel $K(\cdot)$ weights pixels based on their distance from the window center. Common choices:
- **Uniform** $K(u) = 1$ for $\vert u \vert \le 1$: simplest, all in-window pixels weighted equally.
- **Epanechnikov** $K(u) = \frac{3}{4}(1 - u^{2})$: optimal in mean-integrated-squared-error sense.
- **Gaussian** $K(u) \propto \exp(-u^{2}/2)$: smooth, infinitely supported, but slower.

#### Model Solution for Q B(b)

Given $L = 5$ levels, $p(0) = 0.10, p(1) = 0.20, p(2) = 0.15, p(3) = 0.05, p(4) = 0.50$. We test $T \in \{0, 1, 2, 3\}$.

**Global mean:**

$$\mu_{T} = 0(0.10) + 1(0.20) + 2(0.15) + 3(0.05) + 4(0.50) = 0 + 0.20 + 0.30 + 0.15 + 2.00 = 2.65$$

**Case $T = 0$:** $C_0 = \{0\}$, $C_1 = \{1,2,3,4\}$.
$\omega_{0} = 0.10, \mu_{0} = 0$.
$\omega_{1} = 0.90, \mu_{1} = (0.20 + 0.30 + 0.15 + 2.00)/0.90 = 2.65/0.90 \approx 2.944$.
$\sigma_{B}^{2} = 0.10 \times 0.90 \times (0 - 2.944)^{2} = 0.09 \times 8.668 \approx 0.780$.

**Case $T = 1$:** $C_0 = \{0,1\}$, $C_1 = \{2,3,4\}$.
$\omega_{0} = 0.30, \mu_{0} = (0 + 0.20)/0.30 = 0.667$.
$\omega_{1} = 0.70, \mu_{1} = (0.30 + 0.15 + 2.00)/0.70 = 2.45/0.70 = 3.500$.
$\sigma_{B}^{2} = 0.30 \times 0.70 \times (0.667 - 3.500)^{2} = 0.21 \times 8.018 \approx 1.684$.

**Case $T = 2$:** $C_0 = \{0,1,2\}$, $C_1 = \{3,4\}$.
$\omega_{0} = 0.45, \mu_{0} = (0 + 0.20 + 0.30)/0.45 = 0.50/0.45 \approx 1.111$.
$\omega_{1} = 0.55, \mu_{1} = (0.15 + 2.00)/0.55 = 2.15/0.55 \approx 3.909$.
$\sigma_{B}^{2} = 0.45 \times 0.55 \times (1.111 - 3.909)^{2} = 0.2475 \times 7.827 \approx 1.937$.

**Case $T = 3$:** $C_0 = \{0,1,2,3\}$, $C_1 = \{4\}$.
$\omega_{0} = 0.50, \mu_{0} = (0 + 0.20 + 0.30 + 0.15)/0.50 = 0.65/0.50 = 1.30$.
$\omega_{1} = 0.50, \mu_{1} = 4.0$.
$\sigma_{B}^{2} = 0.50 \times 0.50 \times (1.30 - 4.0)^{2} = 0.25 \times 7.29 = 1.8225$.

**Comparison table:**

| $T$ | $\sigma_{B}^{2}(T)$ |
|---|---|
| $0$ | $0.780$ |
| $1$ | $1.684$ |
| $2$ | $\mathbf{1.937}$ ✓ |
| $3$ | $1.823$ |

**Optimal threshold $T^{\star} = 2$** (maximum between-class variance).

**Verification using $\sigma_{T}^{2} = \sigma_{W}^{2} + \sigma_{B}^{2}$ (constant):**
$\sigma_{T}^{2} = (0-2.65)^{2}(0.10) + (1-2.65)^{2}(0.20) + (2-2.65)^{2}(0.15) + (3-2.65)^{2}(0.05) + (4-2.65)^{2}(0.50)$
$= 0.7023 + 0.5445 + 0.0604 + 0.0061 + 0.9113 = 2.2246$.
Check at $T=2$: $\sigma_{B}^{2} = 1.937 \Rightarrow \sigma_{W}^{2} = 2.2246 - 1.937 = 0.288$ ✓ (consistent).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU 2024 Scheme Valuation:**
>
> 1. **Confusing K-Means with K-Nearest Neighbours (KNN)**: K-Means is *unsupervised* clustering; KNN is *supervised* classification. Examiners *will* deduct marks if you mix them up.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to compute the final WCSS $J$ in numerical problems**: A typical 7-mark question awards only **3 marks** for cluster assignments and **4 marks** for the final $J$ value and convergence verification. Skipping $J$ = losing 4 marks.
>
> 3. **Writing the centroid update as a sum without the normalization**: The expression $\sum_{\mathbf{x}_{i} \in C_{j}} \mathbf{x}_{i}$ is **wrong** — you must include $\frac{1}{\vert C_{j} \vert}$. Examiners catch this.
>
> 4. **In Otsu problems, omitting the global mean $\mu_T$ verification**: Validators check that $\sigma_{T}^{2} = \sigma_{B}^{2} + \sigma_{W}^{2}$ holds. Always show this.
>
> 5. **Confusing Mean Shift's "bandwidth" with K-Means' "K"**: Bandwidth $h$ is *not* the number of clusters — it is the *scale of the search window*. The number of clusters is *discovered* by the algorithm.
>
> 6. **Forgetting the convergence criterion**: Every iterative algorithm answer must explicitly state the stopping condition ($\epsilon$-tolerance or $T_{\max}$). One line, but easy to forget, and costs **1 mark**.
>
> 7. **Using $\vert \cdot \vert$ inside LaTeX in tables**: This breaks Markdown table rendering. Always use `\vert` or `\mid` in table cells.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Pin this before walking into the exam hall):**
>
> - **Image segmentation by clustering** is *unsupervised*; no training labels needed.
> - The **two pillars** are: **(1) feature space representation** and **(2) similarity/distance metric**.
> - **K-Means** minimizes $J = \sum_{j} \sum_{\mathbf{x}_{i} \in C_{j}} \Vert \mathbf{x}_{i} - \mu_{j} \Vert^{2}$ and converges to a *local* minimum.
> - **Centroid update rule** (must memorize): $\mu_{j} = \frac{1}{\vert C_{j} \vert} \sum_{\mathbf{x}_{i} \in C_{j}} \mathbf{x}_{i}$.
> - **Mean Shift** uses kernel density estimation; needs bandwidth $h$, not $K$.
> - **Agglomerative** starts with $N$ singletons; linkage criteria are *single, complete, average, Ward's*.
> - **Otsu's** method maximizes $\sigma_{B}^{2}(T) = \omega_{0}\omega_{1}(\mu_{0} - \mu_{1})^{2}$.
> - **Initialization sensitivity** is a *recurring theme* — K-Means++ fixes it probabilistically.
> - **Spatial coordinates** can be added to feature vectors to get spatially coherent clusters.
> - **Computational complexity** matters: K-Means is $O(NKT)$, Mean Shift is $O(N^{2}T)$, Agglomerative is $O(N^{2} \log N)$.
> - **Convergence criteria** for K-Means: either centroid displacement $<\epsilon$ OR $J$ change $<\epsilon$ OR $T_{\max}$ reached.
> - **Differences from region-based methods**: Clustering ignores spatial contiguity by default; region-based methods enforce it.
> - **Examiner loves**: numerical K-Means iterations, Otsu's threshold computation, derivation of centroid update, comparison tables of methods.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Clustering and Segmentation by K-means - Object Detection - YOLO
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST745 - COMPUTER VISION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Clustering and Segmentation by K-means - Object Detection - YOLO # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Clustering by K-Means: Formal Definition **K-Means Clustering** is a centroid-based, unsupervised, iterative partitioning algorithm that divides an unlabeled dataset $\mathcal{X} = \{x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n\}$ into $K$ disjoint clusters $\mathcal{C} = \{C_1, C_2, \dots, C_K\}$ by minimizing the **Within-Cluster Sum of Squares (WCSS)**, also called the **inertia** or **distortion**. $$\boxed{J(C, \mu) = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} \Vert x_i - \mu_k \Vert_2^2}$$ where $\mu_k$ is the **centroid (mean vector)** of cluster $C_k$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** K-Means is treated as a foundational clustering technique that bridges classical computer vision with modern data-driven methods. In Computer Vision, it is most prominently used for **unsupervised image segmentation** in the color (feature) space. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "Sorting Colored Balls into Boxes" Imagine you walk into a room and dump **1000 marbles of different colors** on the floor. You have exactly **$K$ empty boxes** and a measuring tape. You want to sort the marbles so that *each box contains marbles that are very similar in color to each other*. **Step 1 — Seed Placement:** You blindly throw $K$ boxes onto the floor as "initial guesses." **Step 2 — Assignment:** Each marble rolls into the nearest box (by color distance). **Step 3 — Refinement:** Once all marbles are sorted, you move each box to the **average color position** of the marbles inside it. **Step 4 — Repeat:** The marbles may now be closer to a *different* box, so they re-shuffle. You repeat steps 2 and 3 until the boxes stop moving. **This is precisely K-Means.** The marbles are pixels, the color is the feature vector, and the boxes are centroids. The process is purely **iterative refinement**, not a closed-form solution. --- ## 1.3 Image Segmentation by K-Means **Image Segmentation** is the process of partitioning a digital image into multiple meaningful regions (segments) such that pixels within a region share homogeneous properties (intensity, color, texture). When K-Means is applied to image segmentation: 1. Each pixel $x_i \in \mathbb{R}^3$ is represented as a **feature vector in color space** (typically **RGB**, **Lab**, or **HSV**). 2. Optional features can include $(x, y)$ spatial coordinates → leading to **compact, blob-like segments**. 3. The algorithm clusters all $N$ pixels into $K$ groups. 4. Every pixel is recolored by the centroid of its assigned cluster, producing a **segmented image** with $K$ dominant colors. > [!NOTE] > **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Used:** > - **K (number of clusters):** chosen empirically; often **K = 3 to 7** for natural images. > - **Distance Metric:** **Euclidean distance** $\Vert x - \mu \Vert_2$ (most common) or **Mahalanobis** for correlated features. > - **Convergence Threshold:** typically $\epsilon = 10^{-4}$ on centroid displacement. --- ## 1.4 YOLO — You Only Look Once: Formal Definition **Object Detection** is the computer vision task of simultaneously **localizing** (drawing bounding boxes around) and **classifying** (labeling) all instances of predefined object categories within an image. **YOLO (You Only Look Once)** is a **single-stage, real-time, end-to-end, unified object detection framework** that reframes detection as a **single regression problem**. The full image is processed by a single Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) in one forward pass, directly predicting bounding box coordinates, objectness confidence, and class probabilities for all objects simultaneously. **Core Innovation:** Unlike traditional two-stage detectors (R-CNN, Fast R-CNN, Faster R-CNN) that first generate *Region Proposals* and then classify each, YOLO **eliminates the proposal stage entirely**, achieving dramatic speed-ups (45–155 FPS depending on variant). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping:** This topic falls under the *Object Detection* cognitive domain of Module 4 and carries **high-weightage marks** in ESE questions. Students are expected to compare YOLO's architecture with R-CNN family and derive the grid-cell output tensor. --- ## 1.5 Conceptual Analogy — "Reading the Whole Page in One Glance" A student is given a busy photograph (cars, pedestrians, dogs) and asked: *"What is in the picture and where?"* - **Old approach (R-CNN):** A magnifying glass is used to scan thousands of small patches → *"Is this a car? Is this a dog?"* — *slow, sequential*. - **YOLO approach:** The student glances at the **entire photo once** in a single glance. The brain (neural network) instantly says: *"Top-left quadrant has a car, center has a dog, bottom has a pedestrian."* — *fast, holistic, parallel*. This is the essence of YOLO — a **single neural network, one forward pass, all predictions at once**. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet --- ## 2.1 K-Means Algorithm — Operational Logic (Step-by-Step) The K-Means algorithm consists of two alternating steps executed until convergence: ### **Step A — Cluster Assignment (Expectation Step)** For each data point $x_i$, assign it to the **nearest centroid** based on Euclidean distance: $$C_k^{(t)} = \left\{ x_i : \Vert x_i - \mu_k^{(t)} \Vert_2^2 \le \Vert x_i - \mu_j^{(t)} \Vert_2^2 \; \forall j = 1, \dots, K \right\}$$ ### **Step B — Centroid Update (Maximization Step)** Recompute each centroid as the **arithmetic mean** of all points currently assigned to it: $$\mu_k^{(t+1)} = \frac{1}{\vert C_k^{(t)} \vert} \sum_{x_i \in C_k^{(t)}} x_i$$ ### **Convergence Criteria** The algorithm terminates when any of the following holds: - Centroid positions no longer change: $\Vert \mu_k^{(t+1)} - \mu_k^{(t)} \Vert < \epsilon$. - Cluster assignments remain unchanged between iterations. - A maximum number of iterations $T_{\max}$ is reached. - The objective function $J$ improvement falls below a threshold. --- ## 2.2 K-Means for Image Segmentation — Pipeline | Stage | Operation | Output | |-------|-----------|--------| | **1. Feature Extraction** | Flatten image into $N$ feature vectors $x_i \in \mathbb{R}^3$ (RGB) or $\mathbb{R}^5$ (RGB + position) | Pixel feature matrix | | **2. Initialization** | Place $K$ initial centroids via **Forgy** (random sample) or **K-Means++** seeding | Initial $\mu_1, \dots, \mu_K$ | | **3. Assignment** | Assign each pixel to nearest centroid | Cluster label map $L \in \mathbb{Z}^{H \times W}$ | | **4. Update** | Recompute centroids as cluster means | Refined $\mu_1, \dots, \mu_K$ | | **5. Iterate** | Repeat steps 3 & 4 | Convergence | | **6. Reconstruct** | Replace each pixel's color with its cluster centroid color | Segmented image | > [!NOTE] > **Why K-Means++ Initialization Matters:** Random initialization can lead to *poor local minima* and *empty clusters*. K-Means++ spreads initial centroids apart by sampling with probability $\propto D(x)^2$, where $D(x)$ is the distance to the nearest existing centroid. This provides **provably $O(\log K)$** approximation to the optimum. --- ## 2.3 Limitations of K-Means Segmentation - **Linear complexity** $O(NKI)$ — feasible but slow for high-resolution images. - **Requires pre-specifying $K$** — solved using the **Elbow Method** or **Silhouette Score**. - **Sensitive to outliers** — a single noisy pixel can drag a centroid. - **Assumes spherical, equally-sized clusters** in feature space. - **Hard clustering** — every pixel belongs to exactly one segment (no soft boundaries). --- ## 2.4 YOLO — Deep Architectural Analysis ### **2.4.1 Grid Cell Decomposition** The input image is divided into an **$S \times S$ grid**. If the **center** of an object falls into grid cell $(i, j)$, that cell becomes **responsible** for detecting that object. Each grid cell predicts: - **$B$ bounding boxes**, each with $(x, y, w, h)$ coordinates and a **confidence score**. - **$C$ class probabilities**, $\Pr(\text{Class}_i \mid \text{Object})$, *conditional* on an object being present. ### **2.4.2 Output Tensor Shape** $$\text{Output Tensor} = S \times S \times (B \cdot 5 + C)$$ For the original YOLOv1 with $S=7$, $B=2$, $C=20$ (PASCAL VOC): $$7 \times 7 \times (2 \cdot 5 + 20) = 7 \times 7 \times 30$$ ### **2.4.3 Bounding Box Parameterization** - $(x, y)$: center of the box, **relative to the grid cell bounds** (offset between 0 and 1). - $(w, h)$: width and height, **relative to the whole image** (normalized). - **Confidence Score:** $C = \Pr(\text{Object}) \times \text{IoU}_{\text{pred}}^{\text{truth}}$ - If no object exists in the cell, $\Pr(\text{Object}) = 0$. - If object exists, $C = \text{IoU}$ between predicted and ground-truth box. ### **2.4.4 Class Prediction at Test Time** $$\Pr(\text{Class}_i \mid \text{Object}) \times \Pr(\text{Object}) \times \text{IoU} = \Pr(\text{Class}_i) \times \text{IoU}$$ This single score encodes both *what* and *how confident*. ### **2.4.5 Non-Maximum Suppression (NMS)** Critical post-processing step to remove duplicate detections of the same object: 1. Discard boxes with confidence $C < \text{threshold}$ (typically **0.5**). 2. Among remaining boxes, pick the one with highest $C$. 3. Compute **IoU** of this box with all others; suppress any box with $\text{IoU} > \text{NMS\_threshold}$ (typically **0.4–0.5**). 4. Repeat until no boxes remain. --- ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table is **exam-critical** — questions in ESE frequently test direct formula application. | Concept | Formula / Expression | Units / Notes | |---------|----------------------|---------------| | **K-Means Objective (WCSS)** | $J = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} \Vert x_i - \mu_k \Vert_2^2$ | Lower $J$ = tighter clusters | | **Centroid Update Rule** | $\mu_k = \frac{1}{\vert C_k \vert} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} x_i$ | Geometric mean of cluster | | **Euclidean Distance** | $d(x_i, \mu_k) = \sqrt{\sum_{d=1}^{D} (x_{id} - \mu_{kd})^2}$ | Used for pixel–centroid similarity | | **Silhouette Score** | $s(i) = \frac{b(i) - a(i)}{\max\{a(i), b(i)\}}$ | Range $[-1, 1]$; higher = better | | **YOLO Confidence** | $C = \Pr(\text{Object}) \cdot \text{IoU}$ | Score between 0 and 1 | | **IoU (Jaccard Index)** | $\text{IoU} = \frac{\text{Area of Overlap}}{\text{Area of Union}} = \frac{A \cap B}{A \cup B}$ | Threshold $\ge 0.5$ = correct detection | | **YOLO Output Tensor** | $S \times S \times (B \cdot 5 + C)$ | E.g., $7 \times 7 \times 30$ for v1 | | **NMS Suppression Rule** | Suppress $B_j$ if $\text{IoU}(B_{\text{best}}, B_j) > \tau_{\text{NMS}}$ | $\tau_{\text{NMS}} \approx 0.5$ | | **K-Means Complexity** | $O(N \cdot K \cdot I \cdot D)$ | $N$ = pixels, $I$ = iterations | | **Per-class Confidence (YOLO)** | $\Pr(\text{Class}_i) \cdot \text{IoU}$ | Final per-class detection score | | **mAP (mean Average Precision)** | $\text{mAP} = \frac{1}{C} \sum_{c=1}^{C} \text{AP}_c$ | Standard detection benchmark | --- ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility | Domain | Application | |--------|-------------| | **Autonomous Driving** | YOLO detects pedestrians, traffic signs, vehicles in real time at 30+ FPS | | **Medical Imaging** | K-Means segments tumors in MRI scans; YOLO detects cell anomalies | | **Agriculture** | K-Means clusters crop health from NDVI maps; YOLO identifies pests | | **Surveillance** | YOLO-based anomaly detection (e.g., abandoned objects, intrusions) | | **Industrial QA** | K-Means separates defective vs. non-defective surface regions | | **Satellite Imaging** | YOLO locates buildings/vehicles; K-Means segments land-use zones | # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix --- ## 3.1 Derivation of K-Means Centroid Update Rule We seek to prove that the **optimal centroid** for cluster $C_k$ is the **arithmetic mean** of its assigned points. **Setup:** Fix cluster assignment $C_k$ (Step A). We minimize $J$ w.r.t. $\mu_k$: $$J_k = \sum_{x_i \in C_k} \Vert x_i - \mu_k \Vert_2^2$$ **Step 1:** Expand the squared Euclidean norm: $$J_k = \sum_{x_i \in C_k} (x_i - \mu_k)^\top (x_i - \mu_k)$$ **Step 2:** Take the partial derivative with respect to $\mu_k$ and set to zero: $$\frac{\partial J_k}{\partial \mu_k} = -2 \sum_{x_i \in C_k} (x_i - \mu_k) = 0$$ **Step 3:** Split the sum: $$\sum_{x_i \in C_k} x_i - \sum_{x_i \in C_k} \mu_k = 0$$ **Step 4:** Since $\mu_k$ is constant within the sum: $$\sum_{x_i \in C_k} x_i - \vert C_k \vert \cdot \mu_k = 0$$ **Step 5:** Solve for $\mu_k$: $$\boxed{\mu_k = \frac{1}{\vert C_k \vert} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} x_i}$$ > **Conclusion:** The centroid minimizing WCSS is the **mean vector** of its cluster — a unique, closed-form solution for fixed assignments. --- ## 3.2 K-Means Convergence Proof (Monotonic Decrease) **Claim:** The objective $J^{(t)}$ is non-increasing at every iteration. **Proof for Assignment Step (A → A+1):** Given fixed $\mu^{(t)}$, the algorithm assigns each $x_i$ to $\arg\min_k \Vert x_i - \mu_k \Vert^2$. Any other assignment would strictly *increase* the distance. Hence: $$J^{(t)}_{\text{after assignment}} \le J^{(t)}_{\text{after previous update}}$$ **Proof for Update Step (U → U+1):** Given fixed $C^{(t+1)}$, we minimize $J$ w.r.t. $\mu_k$ using the result of §3.1. Hence: $$J^{(t+1)}_{\text{after update}} \le J^{(t+1)}_{\text{after assignment}}$$ **Combining both:** $$J^{(1)} \ge J^{(2)} \ge \dots \ge J^{(T)} \ge 0$$ Since $J$ is bounded below by **0** and monotonically decreases, the algorithm **must converge** in finite steps (number of possible partitions of $n$ points into $K$ clusters is finite: $\frac{1}{K!} \sum_{i=0}^{K} (-1)^i \binom{K}{i} (K-i)^n$ — Stirling number bound). --- ## 3.3 IoU Derivation for YOLO Bounding Boxes The **Intersection over Union (IoU)** is the primary metric for evaluating bounding box accuracy in detection. **Given two axis-aligned bounding boxes:** - $B_1$: corners at $(x_1^{(1)}, y_1^{(1)})$ and $(x_2^{(1)}, y_2^{(1)})$. - $B_2$: corners at $(x_1^{(2)}, y_1^{(2)})$ and $(x_2^{(2)}, y_2^{(2)})$. **Step 1 — Intersection Rectangle Coordinates:** $$x_{\text{left}} = \max(x_1^{(1)}, x_1^{(2)}), \quad x_{\text{right}} = \min(x_2^{(1)}, x_2^{(2)})$$ $$y_{\text{top}} = \max(y_1^{(1)}, y_1^{(2)}), \quad y_{\text{bottom}} = \min(y_2^{(1)}, y_2^{(2)})$$ **Step 2 — Intersection Area (clamped to non-negative):** $$A_{\text{inter}} = \max(0, x_{\text{right}} - x_{\text{left}}) \cdot \max(0, y_{\text{bottom}} - y_{\text{top}})$$ **Step 3 — Individual Box Areas:** $$A_1 = (x_2^{(1)} - x_1^{(1)}) \cdot (y_2^{(1)} - y_1^{(1)})$$ $$A_2 = (x_2^{(2)} - x_1^{(2)}) \cdot (y_2^{(2)} - y_1^{(2)})$$ **Step 4 — Union Area (Inclusion-Exclusion Principle):** $$A_{\text{union}} = A_1 + A_2 - A_{\text{inter}}$$ **Step 5 — Final IoU:** $$\boxed{\text{IoU} = \frac{A_{\text{inter}}}{A_{\text{union}}}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Numerical Example:** $B_1 = (10, 10, 50, 50)$, $B_2 = (30, 30, 70, 70)$. Then $A_{\text{inter}} = 20 \times 20 = 400$, $A_1 = 1600$, $A_2 = 1600$, $A_{\text{union}} = 2800$. $\text{IoU} = 400/2800 \approx 0.143$. --- ## 3.4 YOLO Loss Function Derivation The original YOLOv1 loss is **sum-squared error** with three weighted components: $$\boxed{\mathcal{L} = \lambda_{\text{coord}} \mathcal{L}_{\text{coord}} + \mathcal{L}_{\text{conf}} + \lambda_{\text{noobj}} \mathcal{L}_{\text{noobj}} + \mathcal{L}_{\text{class}}}$$ **Component 1 — Localization Loss (only for cells containing objects):** $$\mathcal{L}_{\text{coord}} = \sum_{i=0}^{S^2} \sum_{j=0}^{B} \mathbb{1}_{ij}^{\text{obj}} \left[ (x_i - \hat{x}_i)^2 + (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2 \right]$$ $$\mathcal{L}_{\text{coord}} + \sum_{i=0}^{S^2} \sum_{j=0}^{B} \mathbb{1}_{ij}^{\text{obj}} \left[ (\sqrt{w_i} - \sqrt{\hat{w}_i})^2 + (\sqrt{h_i} - \sqrt{\hat{h}_i})^2 \right]$$ where $\mathbb{1}_{ij}^{\text{obj}}$ is **1** if box $j$ in cell $i$ is the responsible predictor, **0** otherwise. **Component 2 — Object Confidence Loss:** $$\mathcal{L}_{\text{conf}} = \sum_{i=0}^{S^2} \sum_{j=0}^{B} \mathbb{1}_{ij}^{\text{obj}} (C_i - \hat{C}_i)^2$$ **Component 3 — No-Object Confidence Loss (down-weighted by $\lambda_{\text{noobj}} = 0.5$):** $$\mathcal{L}_{\text{noobj}} = \sum_{i=0}^{S^2} \sum_{j=0}^{B} \mathbb{1}_{ij}^{\text{noobj}} (C_i - \hat{C}_i)^2$$ **Component 4 — Classification Loss (only for cells with objects):** $$\mathcal{L}_{\text{class}} = \sum_{i=0}^{S^2} \mathbb{1}_i^{\text{obj}} \sum_{c \in \text{classes}} (p_i(c) - \hat{p}_i(c))^2$$ **Standard KTU-Memorized Hyperparameters (YOLOv1):** - $\lambda_{\text{coord}} = 5$ - $\lambda_{\text{noobj}} = 0.5$ - The square-root is applied to $w, h$ to **penalize errors in large boxes less** than in small boxes. --- ## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — K-Means on Toy Pixels **Setup:** Consider 6 pixels in 1-D feature space (gray values): $\{10, 12, 14, 30, 32, 34\}$. Set $K=2$. **Initialization (Forgy):** $\mu_1^{(0)} = 12, \mu_2^{(0)} = 32$. **Iteration 1 — Assignment:** | Pixel | $d(x, \mu_1) = \vert x - 12 \vert$ | $d(x, \mu_2) = \vert x - 32 \vert$ | Assigned | |-------|----------------------------------|----------------------------------|----------| | 10 | 2 | 22 | $C_1$ | | 12 | 0 | 20 | $C_1$ | | 14 | 2 | 18 | $C_1$ | | 30 | 18 | 2 | $C_2$ | | 32 | 20 | 0 | $C_2$ | | 34 | 22 | 2 | $C_2$ | **Iteration 1 — Update:** $$\mu_1^{(1)} = \frac{10 + 12 + 14}{3} = 12.0, \quad \mu_2^{(1)} = \frac{30 + 32 + 34}{3} = 32.0$$ **Result:** Centroids unchanged → **converged in 1 iteration**. **Final WCSS:** $$J = (10-12)^2 + (12-12)^2 + (14-12)^2 + (30-32)^2 + (32-32)^2 + (34-32)^2 = 4 + 0 + 4 + 4 + 0 + 4 = 16$$ --- ## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Criterion | K-Means (Segmentation) | YOLO (Object Detection) | |-----------|------------------------|--------------------------| | **Learning Paradigm** | Unsupervised (no labels) | Supervised (labeled boxes) | | **Output Type** | Cluster label per pixel | Bounding boxes + class + confidence | | **Real-time?** | Fast ($O(NKI)$) | Very fast (45–155 FPS) | | **Handles Multiple Classes?** | Implicit (each cluster = one segment) | Explicit ($C$ class probabilities) | | **Spatial Awareness** | Optional via $(x, y)$ features | Built-in via grid cells | | **Boundary Quality** | Soft, fuzzy regions | Hard, rectangular boxes | | **Training Data Required?** | None | Large annotated corpus (COCO, VOC) | | **Output Granularity** | Per-pixel | Per-instance | # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics --- ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — K-Means Iteration Loop ```mermaid flowchart TD startA([Input Image / Dataset X]) --> feat[Step 1: Feature Extraction
