Initialize $K$ centroids $\mu_1, \mu_2, \dots, \mu_K$. 2. **Assignment Step:** Assign each data point $x_i$ to the nearest centroid based on Euclidean distance. 3. **Update Step:** Recompute each centroid as the mean of points in its cluster: $\mu_k = \frac{1}{\vert C_k \vert} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} x_i$. 4. Repeat steps 2 & 3 until convergence. **Convergence Criteria:** - Centroid positions no longer change significantly ($\Vert \mu^{(t+1)} - \mu^{(t)} \Vert < \epsilon$). - Cluster assignments remain unchanged. - Maximum iterations $T_{\max}$ reached. --- ### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **What is Non-Maximum Suppression (NMS)? Why is it essential in YOLO?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Non-Maximum Suppression (NMS)** is a post-processing technique used to eliminate redundant overlapping bounding box detections produced by object detection algorithms like YOLO. **Working:** 1. Discard all bounding boxes with confidence score below a threshold (e.g., **0.5**). 2. From remaining boxes, select the one with the **highest confidence** as the current best. 3. Compute **IoU** between this box and all other boxes; suppress (remove) any box with $\text{IoU} > \tau_{\text{NMS}}$ (typically **0.4–0.5**). 4. Repeat the process until no boxes remain. **Why Essential in YOLO:** YOLO predicts multiple bounding boxes per grid cell. Without NMS, the same object would be detected multiple times with overlapping boxes. NMS ensures **one clean detection per object**, making the output visually and computationally meaningful. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > Per **KTU 2024 ESE Module Internal Choice** regulation, both alternatives are to be provided. --- ### **Question B-A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]` **(a)** Describe in detail the K-Means clustering algorithm with its mathematical formulation. Explain how it is applied for **image segmentation** by treating pixel colors as feature vectors. Include a note on the **K-Means++ initialization technique**. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Given the following 8 one-dimensional feature points and $K = 2$: $$\mathcal{X} = \{1, 2, 4, 8, 9, 11, 13, 14\}$$ with initial centroids $\mu_1^{(0)} = 2$ and $\mu_2^{(0)} = 13$. Perform **two complete iterations** of K-Means and compute the WCSS after convergence. **(7 Marks)** --- #### **Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks** **Mathematical Formulation (2 Marks):** K-Means partitions $\mathcal{X}$ into $K$ clusters to minimize: $$J(C, \mu) = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} \Vert x_i - \mu_k \Vert_2^2$$ **Algorithmic Steps (2 Marks):** 1. Initialize $K$ centroids. 2. Assignment: $C_k^{(t)} = \{x_i : \Vert x_i - \mu_k \Vert^2 \le \Vert x_i - \mu_j \Vert^2 \; \forall j\}$. 3. Update: $\mu_k^{(t+1)} = \frac{1}{\vert C_k \vert} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} x_i$. 4. Repeat until convergence. **Image Segmentation Application (2 Marks):** - Each pixel becomes a feature vector $x_i = [R, G, B]^\top \in \mathbb{R}^3$. - Optionally include spatial coordinates: $x_i = [R, G, B, x, y]^\top$ for region-aware segmentation. - After convergence, replace every pixel's color with its assigned cluster centroid's color, yielding a $K$-color segmented image. **K-Means++ Initialization (1 Mark):** - Pick first centroid uniformly at random. - For each subsequent centroid, sample $x_i$ with probability $\propto D(x_i)^2$, where $D(x_i)$ is the distance to the nearest already-chosen centroid. - This spreads initial centroids apart, improving convergence and avoiding poor local minima. --- #### **Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks** **Iteration 1 — Assignment:** | $x_i$ | $d(x_i, \mu_1^{(0)}=2)$ | $d(x_i, \mu_2^{(0)}=13)$ | Assigned | |------|------------------------|--------------------------|----------| | 1 | 1 | 12 | $C_1$ | | 2 | 0 | 11 | $C_1$ | | 4 | 2 | 9 | $C_1$ | | 8 | 6 | 5 | $C_2$ | | 9 | 7 | 4 | $C_2$ | | 11 | 9 | 2 | $C_2$ | | 13 | 11 | 0 | $C_2$ | | 14 | 12 | 1 | $C_2$ | $C_1 = \{1, 2, 4\}, \quad C_2 = \{8, 9, 11, 13, 14\}$ **Iteration 1 — Update:** $$\mu_1^{(1)} = \frac{1+2+4}{3} = \frac{7}{3} \approx 2.33$$ $$\mu_2^{(1)} = \frac{8+9+11+13+14}{5} = \frac{55}{5} = 11.0$$ **Iteration 2 — Assignment (with $\mu_1 = 2.33, \mu_2 = 11.0$):** | $x_i$ | $d(x_i, 2.33)$ | $d(x_i, 11.0)$ | Assigned | |------|----------------|-----------------|----------| | 1 | 1.33 | 10.0 | $C_1$ | | 2 | 0.33 | 9.0 | $C_1$ | | 4 | 1.67 | 7.0 | $C_1$ | | 8 | 5.67 | 3.0 | $C_2$ | | 9 | 6.67 | 2.0 | $C_2$ | | 11 | 8.67 | 0.0 | $C_2$ | | 13 | 10.67 | 2.0 | $C_2$ | | 14 | 11.67 | 3.0 | $C_2$ | **Assignments unchanged → Converged.** **Final WCSS Calculation:** $$J = \sum_{x \in C_1} (x - 2.33)^2 + \sum_{x \in C_2} (x - 11.0)^2$$ $$J_{C_1} = (1 - 2.33)^2 + (2 - 2.33)^2 + (4 - 2.33)^2 = 1.77 + 0.11 + 2.79 = 4.67$$ $$J_{C_2} = (8-11)^2 + (9-11)^2 + (11-11)^2 + (13-11)^2 + (14-11)^2 = 9 + 4 + 0 + 4 + 9 = 26$$ $$\boxed{J_{\text{total}} = 4.67 + 26 = 30.67}$$ --- ### **Question B-B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** Explain the **YOLO (You Only Look Once)** object detection framework in detail. Discuss how it differs from traditional R-CNN based detectors and describe its grid-cell-based prediction mechanism with a clearly labeled diagram description. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Two bounding boxes $B_1$ and $B_2$ are given: - $B_1$: top-left $(10, 10)$, bottom-right $(50, 60)$ - $B_2$: top-left $(30, 40)$, bottom-right $(70, 90)$ Compute the **Intersection over Union (IoU)**. If the confidence of $B_1$ is $C_1 = 0.8$ and $B_2$ has $C_2 = 0.6$, determine which box survives **Non-Maximum Suppression (NMS)** with threshold $\tau_{\text{NMS}} = 0.5$. **(7 Marks)** --- #### **Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks** **YOLO Framework (3 Marks):** YOLO reframes object detection as a **single regression problem**, processed by one CNN in one forward pass. It eliminates the region-proposal stage used by R-CNN family. **Comparison with R-CNN (2 Marks):** | Aspect | R-CNN Family | YOLO | |--------|-------------|------| | Stages | Two (proposal + classification) | One (unified) | | Speed | Slow (5–7 FPS for Fast R-CNN) | Real-time (45–155 FPS) | | Global Context | Limited (per-region) | Full-image | | Architecture | Region proposals + classifier | Single CNN | **Grid-Cell Prediction Mechanism (2 Marks):** - Image divided into $S \times S$ grid (e.g., $7 \times 7$). - Each cell predicts $B$ bounding boxes + $C$ class probabilities. - If the **center** of an object lies in a cell, that cell is responsible for detecting it. - Output tensor: $S \times S \times (B \cdot 5 + C)$ — e.g., $7 \times 7 \times 30$ for VOC. --- #### **Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks** **Step 1 — Compute Intersection Area (2 Marks):** $$x_{\text{left}} = \max(10, 30) = 30, \quad x_{\text{right}} = \min(50, 70) = 50$$ $$y_{\text{top}} = \max(10, 40) = 40, \quad y_{\text{bottom}} = \min(60, 90) = 60$$ $$A_{\text{inter}} = (50 - 30) \times (60 - 40) = 20 \times 20 = 400$$ **Step 2 — Compute Individual Box Areas (2 Marks):** $$A_1 = (50 - 10) \times (60 - 10) = 40 \times 50 = 2000$$ $$A_2 = (70 - 30) \times (90 - 40) = 40 \times 50 = 2000$$ **Step 3 — Compute Union Area and IoU (2 Marks):** $$A_{\text{union}} = A_1 + A_2 - A_{\text{inter}} = 2000 + 2000 - 400 = 3600$$ $$\boxed{\text{IoU} = \frac{400}{3600} = \frac{1}{9} \approx 0.111}$$ **Step 4 — Apply NMS (1 Mark):** - Higher confidence: $B_1$ with $C_1 = 0.8$ wins. - $\text{IoU}(B_1, B_2) = 0.111 < 0.5$ → **NMS threshold not exceeded**. - However, by NMS convention, $B_1$ is kept as the best, and $B_2$ is **suppressed** because both likely refer to the same object instance. > **Conclusion:** $B_1$ survives NMS; $B_2$ is discarded. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Zones for This Topic:** > 1. **Confusing IoU formula:** Students often write $\frac{\text{Union}}{\text{Intersection}}$ — this is **inverted** and worth zero. Always: $\text{IoU} = \frac{\text{Intersection}}{\text{Union}}$. > 2. **Forgetting to clamp the intersection width/height** to zero when boxes don't overlap — this leads to negative areas and absurd IoU values. > 3. **K-Means centroid update:** Writing "average of all points in the dataset" instead of "average of points *in the cluster*" — loses 1 mark. > 4. **YOLO output tensor dimension:** Mixing up $B \cdot 5 + C$ as $B + 5 + C$. The **5** means $(x, y, w, h, C)$ — five values per box, NOT five boxes. > 5. **NMS logic:** Saying "keep the box with higher confidence" without explaining the IoU suppression criterion — partial credit only. > 6. **Not stating assumptions** (e.g., grid cell contains an object center) when deriving YOLO output — loses conceptual clarity marks. > 7. **Forgetting the $\sqrt{w}, \sqrt{h}$ trick** in YOLO loss — a 1-mark detail that examiners expect. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Points — Read Before Exam** - [x] **K-Means Objective:** $J = \sum_k \sum_i \Vert x_i - \mu_k \Vert^2$ — *always minimize this.* - [x] **Centroid Rule:** Mean of assigned points (not the dataset). - [x] **Two-Step Iteration:** Assignment → Update → Check convergence. - [x] **Initialization Variants:** Forgy (random) vs **K-Means++** (probability-weighted). - [x] **K for segmentation:** Typically $K = 3$ to $7$ for natural images. - [x] **Feature Vectors:** Pixels as $[R, G, B]$ or $[R, G, B, x, y]$. - [x] **K-Means Complexity:** $O(NKI \cdot D)$. - [x] **K-Means Convergence:** Guaranteed finite (monotonic $J$ decrease + bounded below). - [x] **YOLO Core Idea:** Single CNN, one forward pass, unified regression. - [x] **Grid Cell Size (v1):** $S = 7$, $B = 2$, $C = 20$ → output $7 \times 7 \times 30$. - [x] **Bounding Box Encoding:** $(x, y)$ relative to cell; $(w, h)$ relative to image. - [x] **Confidence:** $C = \Pr(\text{Object}) \times \text{IoU}$. - [x] **IoU Definition:** $\text{IoU} = \frac{\text{Intersection}}{\text{Union}}$; threshold $\ge 0.5$ = correct. - [x] **NMS Steps:** Threshold → Highest-$C$ box → IoU-suppress duplicates. - [x] **YOLO Loss Components:** Coord ($\lambda=5$) + Confidence + No-object ($\lambda=0.5$) + Class. - [x] **Square-root trick:** $\sqrt{w}, \sqrt{h}$ in localization loss reduces small-box penalty. - [x] **YOLO vs R-CNN:** YOLO = single-stage, real-time; R-CNN = two-stage, slow but accurate. - [x] **Final per-class score:** $\Pr(\text{Class}_i) \times \text{IoU}$. - [x] **mAP:** Standard benchmark = mean of per-class Average Precision.

topics.title: Segmentation - Mask R-CNN and Instance Segmentation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST745 - COMPUTER VISION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Segmentation - Mask R-CNN and Instance Segmentation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Instance Segmentation?

**Instance Segmentation** is a computer vision task that combines the objectives of **object detection** and **semantic segmentation** to produce a per-pixel mask for *every individual object instance* in an image. Each detected object is assigned a unique label and a pixel-precise silhouette, even when multiple objects of the same class overlap.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction (KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight):**
> - **Semantic Segmentation:** Labels every pixel with a class (e.g., "cat", "dog"). It does **not** distinguish between two overlapping cats.
> - **Instance Segmentation:** Distinguishes between *every individual object*, even of the same class. Two adjacent cats receive two different masks (Cat-1, Cat-2).
> - **Panoptic Segmentation:** Hybrid of semantic + instance segmentation (background classes + instance classes).

## 1.2 What is Mask R-CNN?

**Mask R-CNN (Region-based Convolutional Neural Network with Mask Branch)** is a deep learning framework introduced by *He et al. (2017)* that extends **Faster R-CNN** by adding a parallel branch for predicting an **object mask** in parallel with the existing branch for **bounding box recognition**. It is the state-of-the-art baseline model for **instance segmentation** tasks.

$$y_{\text{Mask R-CNN}} = \{c, b, m\}$$

where $c$ = class label, $b$ = bounding box coordinates, and $m$ = binary mask (size $m \times m$, typically $28 \times 28$).

## 1.3 Real-World Analogy — The "Museum Curator" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: A Museum Curator Cataloging Statues**
>
> Imagine you are a curator entering a sculpture gallery with **50 statues** packed together, some overlapping, some of the same marble type, some in shadow.
>
> - **Object Detection** = You write the *location* of each statue: *"A statue is at row 3, column 5."* It does not tell you the *exact shape* of the statue.
> - **Semantic Segmentation** = You paint the entire gallery: *"All statues are beige."* You know the pixels are statues, but you cannot tell where one statue ends and another begins.
> - **Instance Segmentation (Mask R-CNN)** = You carefully **cut out the exact silhouette of every single statue**, including the one partially behind another. Now you can lift each one individually and catalog it.
>
> Mask R-CNN is the *scissor-and-trace tool* of computer vision.

## 1.4 Standard Benchmarks & Metrics

| Metric | Definition | Typical Value (Mask R-CNN) |
|---|---|---|
| **AP** (Average Precision) | Mean Average Precision at IoU thresholds 0.5–0.95 | **~37.1** (COCO test-dev, ResNet-101) |
| **AP@0.5** | AP at IoU = 0.5 | **~60.0** |
| **AP@0.75** | AP at IoU = 0.75 | **~39.4** |
| **AP\_S, AP\_M, AP\_L** | AP for small/medium/large objects | **~16.9, 39.9, 53.5** |
| **Mask AP** | AP computed on instance masks | **~32.0** |

Standard dataset used: **COCO (Common Objects in Context)** with **80 object categories**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Branches of Mask R-CNN

Mask R-CNN produces **three outputs per Region of Interest (RoI)** through parallel heads:

1. **Classification Head** — Outputs a softmax probability over $K+1$ classes (including background).
2. **Bounding Box Regression Head** — Outputs 4 real-valued numbers $\Delta x, \Delta y, \Delta w, \Delta h$ that refine the box coordinates.
3. **Mask Head** — Outputs a $K \cdot m^2$ dimensional tensor; for each of the $K$ classes, an $m \times m$ binary mask is produced by a per-class sigmoid.

## 2.2 The Multi-Task Loss Function

The complete Mask R-CNN loss is a sum of three components, each computed per RoI:

$$L = L_{\text{cls}} + L_{\text{box}} + L_{\text{mask}}$$

### Step-by-Step Logic:

- **Step 1 — Classification Loss $L_{\text{cls}}$:** Standard cross-entropy loss over $K+1$ classes.
$$L_{\text{cls}} = -\sum_{i=1}^{K+1} p_i \log(\hat{p}_i)$$

- **Step 2 — Bounding Box Loss $L_{\text{box}}$:** Smooth L1 loss, applied only to positive RoIs (foreground class).
$$L_{\text{box}} = \sum_{i \in \{x,y,w,h\}} \text{Smooth}_{L1}(t_i - \hat{t}_i)$$

- **Step 3 — Mask Loss $L_{\text{mask}}$:** Average binary cross-entropy, computed **only for the ground truth class** $k$ (decoupled mask prediction).
$$L_{\text{mask}} = -\frac{1}{m^2}\sum_{1 \le i,j \le m} \left[y_{ij} \log \hat{y}_{ij}^{k} + (1-y_{ij}) \log(1-\hat{y}_{ij}^{k})\right]$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why decouple the mask prediction per class?**
> Without decoupling, the network would compete across classes at every pixel. Decoupling allows each class to have an independent mask, eliminating inter-class competition and improving accuracy.

## 2.3 RoIAlign: Solving Misalignment

The original Faster R-CNN used **RoIPool**, which introduced **quantization errors** (rounding to integer coordinates) that hurt pixel-accurate mask prediction. Mask R-CNN replaces it with **RoIAlign**, which:

1. Divides the RoI into $H \times W$ bins (e.g., $7 \times 7$ for box head, $14 \times 14$ for mask head).
2. Samples **4 regularly-spaced points** inside each bin.
3. Applies **bilinear interpolation** at each sample point using the exact (floating-point) feature map coordinates.
4. Aggregates the samples via **max pooling** (for box head) or directly passes them to the mask head.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> RoIAlign eliminates the two quantizations of RoIPool, achieving **~10% relative improvement** in mask accuracy on COCO.

## 2.4 Backbone: ResNet + Feature Pyramid Network (FPN)

- **ResNet-50 / ResNet-101** extracts hierarchical features.
- **FPN (Feature Pyramid Network)** builds a top-down pathway with lateral connections, providing multi-scale feature maps $\left\{P_2, P_3, P_4, P_5\right\}$.
- Each RoI is assigned to the pyramid level based on its scale:
$$k = \lfloor k_0 + \log_2(\sqrt{wh}/224) \rfloor$$
where $k_0 = 4$, $w, h$ are box dimensions, and **224** is the ImageNet pre-training size.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $L = L_{\text{cls}} + L_{\text{box}} + L_{\text{mask}}$ | Multi-task loss | Total training loss |
| 2 | $L_{\text{cls}} = -\sum p_i \log \hat{p}_i$ | Cross-entropy | Class probability |
| 3 | $\text{Smooth}_{L1}(x) = \begin{cases} 0.5x^2, & \vert x \vert < 1 \\ \vert x \vert - 0.5, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$ | Huber-style loss | Box regression |
| 4 | $L_{\text{mask}} = -\frac{1}{m^2}\sum y_{ij}\log \hat{y}_{ij}^{k}$ | Binary cross-entropy | Per-class mask |
| 5 | $k = \lfloor k_0 + \log_2(\sqrt{wh}/224) \rfloor$ | FPN level assignment | Multi-scale RoI |
| 6 | $\text{IoU} = \frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}$ | Intersection over Union | Mask quality |
| 7 | $L_{\text{RPN}} = \frac{1}{N_{\text{cls}}}\sum L_{\text{cls}}^{\text{RPN}} + \lambda \frac{1}{N_{\text{reg}}}\sum L_{\text{reg}}^{\text{RPN}}$ | RPN loss | Region proposals |
| 8 | $\text{AP} = \frac{1}{\vert \text{IoUs} \vert}\sum_{r} \text{AP}_r$ | Mean over IoU thresholds | COCO evaluation |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Mask R-CNN is widely deployed in:

- **Medical Imaging:** Tumor/cell segmentation in CT and MRI scans.
- **Autonomous Driving:** Pixel-precise detection of pedestrians, vehicles, lane markings.
- **Agriculture:** Crop and weed detection for precision spraying.
- **Industrial Inspection:** Defect segmentation on manufacturing lines.
- **Augmented Reality:** Object occlusion and accurate cutouts.
- **Robotics:** Object-level grasping and manipulation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Binary Cross-Entropy Mask Loss

The mask branch produces an output tensor $\hat{Y} \in \mathbb{R}^{K \times m \times m}$ where $K$ is the number of classes and $m$ is the mask resolution (typically 28). For the **ground-truth class** $k^*$, we take the $k^*$-th slice $\hat{Y}_{k^*} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times m}$ and apply a **per-pixel sigmoid**:

$$\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$$

**Step 1: Define the prediction per pixel**
$$\hat{y}_{ij}^{k^*} = \sigma(\hat{Y}_{k^*}[i,j])$$

**Step 2: Construct the binary cross-entropy per pixel**

For a binary ground-truth $y_{ij} \in \{0, 1\}$, the cross-entropy of a single pixel is:

$$\ell(y_{ij}, \hat{y}_{ij}^{k^*}) = -y_{ij} \log \hat{y}_{ij}^{k^*} - (1 - y_{ij})\log(1 - \hat{y}_{ij}^{k^*})$$

**Step 3: Substitute the sigmoid definition**

$$\hat{y}_{ij}^{k^*} = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-\hat{Y}_{k^*}[i,j]}} \implies 1 - \hat{y}_{ij}^{k^*} = \frac{e^{-\hat{Y}_{k^*}[i,j]}}{1 + e^{-\hat{Y}_{k^*}[i,j]}}$$

$$\log \hat{y}_{ij}^{k^*} = -\log(1 + e^{-\hat{Y}_{k^*}[i,j]})$$

$$\log(1 - \hat{y}_{ij}^{k^*}) = -\hat{Y}_{k^*}[i,j] - \log(1 + e^{-\hat{Y}_{k^*}[i,j]})$$

**Step 4: Sum over all pixels in the $m \times m$ mask**

$$L_{\text{mask}} = \frac{1}{m^2} \sum_{1 \le i,j \le m} \left[ y_{ij} \log(1 + e^{-\hat{Y}_{k^*}[i,j]}) + (1 - y_{ij})(\hat{Y}_{k^*}[i,j] + \log(1 + e^{-\hat{Y}_{k^*}[i,j]})) \right]$$

**Step 5: Simplify using $\log(1+e^z) = \text{softplus}(z)$**

$$L_{\text{mask}} = \frac{1}{m^2} \sum_{i,j} \left[ \text{softplus}(\hat{Y}_{k^*}[i,j]) + (1 - y_{ij}) \hat{Y}_{k^*}[i,j] \right]$$

> [!NOTE]
> This form is numerically stable and is the **exact equivalent** of binary cross-entropy, but avoids computing $\log(0)$ for saturated sigmoids.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Smooth L1 Loss Gradient

The Smooth L1 loss is a hybrid of L2 (quadratic) for small errors and L1 (linear) for large errors.

$$\text{Smooth}_{L1}(x) = \begin{cases} 0.5x^2, & \vert x \vert < 1 \\ \vert x \vert - 0.5, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 1: Compute the derivative for the inner region**

$$\frac{d}{dx}(0.5x^2) = x \quad \text{for } \vert x \vert < 1$$

**Step 2: Compute the derivative for the outer region**

$$\frac{d}{dx}(\vert x \vert - 0.5) = \text{sign}(x) \in \{-1, 0, +1\} \quad \text{for } \vert x \vert \ge 1$$

**Step 3: Combined gradient**

$$\frac{d}{dx}\text{Smooth}_{L1}(x) = \begin{cases} x, & \vert x \vert < 1 \\ \text{sign}(x), & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The gradient is **bounded by 1**, preventing gradient explosion in box regression — a critical property that makes training stable compared to pure L2 loss.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: RoIAlign Coordinate Computation

**Problem:** Given an RoI $(x_1, y_1, x_2, y_2) = (20.7, 35.2, 145.4, 168.9)$ and output size $H \times W = 2 \times 2$, compute the 4 sampling points per bin.

**Step 1: Compute RoI dimensions**
$$w = x_2 - x_1 = 145.4 - 20.7 = 124.7$$
$$h = y_2 - y_1 = 168.9 - 35.2 = 133.7$$

**Step 2: Compute bin size**
$$\Delta w = \frac{w}{W} = \frac{124.7}{2} = 62.35$$
$$\Delta h = \frac{h}{H} = \frac{133.7}{2} = 66.85$$

**Step 3: Sample 4 regular points per bin (at offsets 0.25, 0.75)**

For bin $(i, j)$ where $i \in \{0, 1\}$, $j \in \{0, 1\}$:

$$x_{\text{sample}} = x_1 + j \cdot \Delta w + \alpha \cdot \Delta w$$
$$y_{\text{sample}} = y_1 + i \cdot \Delta h + \beta \cdot \Delta h$$

with $\alpha, \beta \in \{0.25, 0.75\}$.

**Step 4: Bilinear interpolation formula**

For a sampling point $(x, y)$ and the 4 nearest integer feature points $(x_0, y_0), (x_1, y_0), (x_0, y_1), (x_1, y_1)$:

$$V(x,y) = \sum_{a,b \in \{0,1\}} w_a \cdot w_b \cdot F(x_a, y_b)$$

where $w_0 = 1 - (x - x_0)$, $w_1 = x - x_0$, and the weights are in $[0, 1]$.

> [!NOTE]
> **RoIAlign vs RoIPool — Numerical Example:** Suppose a true feature location is at $(20.7, 35.2)$. RoIPool rounds to $(21, 35)$, shifting the feature by $\approx 0.3$ pixels. After 4 such rounds in a deep network, the misalignment compounds, leading to a **~10% drop in mask accuracy**. RoIAlign preserves the exact $(20.7, 35.2)$ location.

---

## 3.4 Complete Forward Pass Mathematics

Given input image $I$ and backbone network $f_\theta$:

$$\text{Step 1: } F = f_\theta(I), \quad F \in \mathbb{R}^{H' \times W' \times C'}$$
$$\text{Step 2: } \{R_n\}_{n=1}^{N} = \text{RPN}(F) \quad \text{(proposals)}$$
$$\text{Step 3: } F_n^{\text{align}} = \text{RoIAlign}(F, R_n, H \times W) \quad \forall n$$
$$\text{Step 4: } c_n = \text{Softmax}(W_{\text{cls}} \cdot F_n^{\text{align}})$$
$$\text{Step 5: } b_n = W_{\text{box}} \cdot F_n^{\text{align}}$$
$$\text{Step 6: } m_n = \sigma(W_{\text{mask}} \cdot F_n^{\text{align,mask}}) \in \mathbb{R}^{K \times m \times m}$$
$$\text{Step 7: } \text{Output}_n = \{c_n, b_n, m_n[c_n]\}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mask R-CNN End-to-End Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[INPUT IMAGE HxWx3] --> B[RESNET 50 OR 101 BACKBONE]
    B --> C[FEATURE MAP C1 H/16 x W/16 x 2048]
    C --> D[FEATURE PYRAMID NETWORK FPN]
    D --> D2[P2 LEVEL]
    D --> D3[P3 LEVEL]
    D --> D4[P4 LEVEL]
    D --> D5[P5 LEVEL]
    D5 --> E[REGION PROPOSAL NETWORK RPN]
    E --> F[OBJECTNESS AND BOX DELTAS]
    F --> G[NON MAXIMUM SUPPRESSION]
    G --> H[REFINED ROIs x1 y1 x2 y2]
    C --> I[ROI ALIGN MODULE]
    D2 --> I
    D3 --> I
    D4 --> I
    D5 --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[7x7 ALIGNED FEATURE PER ROI]
    I --> K[14x14 ALIGNED FEATURE PER ROI]
    J --> L[CLASSIFICATION HEAD SOFTMAX]
    J --> M[BOX REGRESSION HEAD SMOOTH L1]
    K --> N[MASK HEAD CONV DECONV]
    L --> O[CLASS LABEL c]
    M --> P[BOX OFFSETS dx dy dw dh]
    N --> Q[K x 28 x 28 BINARY MASKS]
    O --> R[FINAL INSTANCE OUTPUTS]
    P --> R
    Q --> R
```

## 4.2 RoIAlign vs RoIPool — Functional Block Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[RoI x1 y1 x2 y2] --> B{QUANTIZATION}
    B -->|RoIPool| C[ROUND TO INT x1 y1 x2 y2]
    B -->|RoIAlign| D[KEEP FLOATING POINT EXACT]
    C --> E[DIVIDE INTO HxW BINS INTEGER]
    D --> F[DIVIDE INTO HxW BINS FLOAT]
    E --> G[SNAP TO GRID MISALIGNMENT]
    F --> H[4 SAMPLE POINTS PER BIN]
    H --> I[BILINEAR INTERPOLATION]
    I --> J[OUTPUT 7x7 OR 14x14 EXACT]
    G --> K[OUTPUT QUANTIZED ERROR]
```

## 4.3 Mask Head — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[14x14x1024 INPUT FROM ROI ALIGN] --> B[CONV 3x3 RELU 1024]
    B --> C[CONV 3x3 RELU 1024]
    C --> D[CONV 3x3 RELU 1024]
    D --> E[CONV 3x3 RELU 1024]
    E --> F[DECONV 2x2 STRIDE 2]
    F --> G[CONV 1x1 SIGMOID]
    G --> H[Kx28x28 BINARY MASK TENSOR]
    H --> I[SELECT MASK FOR PREDICTED CLASS k]
    I --> J[UPSCALE TO ORIGINAL IMAGE SIZE]
    J --> K[FINAL PIXEL PRECISE MASK]
```

## 4.4 Pipeline Overview — Input to Output

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1[STAGE 1 INPUT PREPROCESSING] --> P2[STAGE 2 BACKBONE FEATURE EXTRACTION]
    P2 --> P3[STAGE 3 FPN MULTI SCALE]
    P3 --> P4[STAGE 4 RPN PROPOSAL GENERATION]
    P4 --> P5[STAGE 5 ROI ALIGN SAMPLING]
    P5 --> P6[STAGE 6 PARALLEL HEADS CLS BOX MASK]
    P6 --> P7[STAGE 7 POST PROCESSING NMS THRESHOLD]
    P7 --> P8[STAGE 8 OUTPUT INSTANCE MASKS CLASSES BOXES]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]`
**Define instance segmentation. How does it differ from semantic segmentation?**

**Model Answer:**

**Instance Segmentation** is a computer vision task that performs **pixel-level labeling for every individual object instance** in an image. It produces a unique binary mask for *each* object, even when multiple objects belong to the same class.

| Aspect | Semantic Segmentation | Instance Segmentation |
|---|---|---|
| Output | One label per pixel (class) | One mask per object instance |
| Class distinction | All instances share the same label | Each instance has a unique ID |
| Overlap handling | Cannot separate overlapping objects | Can distinguish overlapping instances |
| Example | "all pixels = car" | "Car-1 mask, Car-2 mask, Car-3 mask" |

**(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for tabular comparison, 1 for example)**

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]`
**What is RoIAlign? Why is it preferred over RoIPool in Mask R-CNN?**

**Model Answer:**

**RoIAlign** is a region-of-interest feature extraction layer introduced in Mask R-CNN that preserves **exact floating-point spatial coordinates** when sampling features from a convolutional feature map, eliminating the quantization errors of RoIPool.

**Key advantages over RoIPool:**
1. **No Quantization:** RoIPool rounds to integer coordinates, causing ~10% accuracy loss; RoIAlign uses exact values.
2. **Bilinear Interpolation:** RoIAlign samples 4 points per bin and applies bilinear interpolation for sub-pixel accuracy.
3. **Pixel-Accurate Masks:** Essential for mask prediction, which requires per-pixel precision.
4. **Better for Deep Networks:** Misalignment compounds in deep networks; RoIAlign prevents this.

**(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for advantages)**

---

## 📕 PART B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note:** Per KTU ESE (End Semester Exam) regulations, Module 4 carries an **internal choice**. Students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper]`

#### **(a) Explain the complete architecture of Mask R-CNN in detail with a block diagram. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Mask R-CNN is a two-stage instance segmentation framework that extends Faster R-CNN with a parallel **mask prediction branch**. Its architecture consists of the following components:

**1. Backbone — ResNet + FPN:**
- A deep residual network (ResNet-50 or ResNet-101) extracts hierarchical features from the input image.
- A **Feature Pyramid Network (FPN)** is added on top, creating a multi-scale feature pyramid $\left\{P_2, P_3, P_4, P_5\right\}$ that allows the model to detect objects at different scales effectively.

**2. Region Proposal Network (RPN):**
- A small convolutional network slides over the FPN feature maps.
- At each spatial location, it predicts **objectness scores** and **bounding-box deltas** for $k$ anchor boxes (typically $k=3$ aspect ratios × 3 scales = 9 anchors per location).
- Top-N proposals (e.g., N=2000 during training, 1000 during inference) are passed through **Non-Maximum Suppression (NMS)**.

**3. RoIAlign Layer:**
- For each proposed RoI, the corresponding pyramid level is selected using:
$$k = \lfloor k_0 + \log_2(\sqrt{wh}/224) \rfloor$$
- The RoI is divided into $H \times W$ bins, with **4 sample points per bin** taken at exact floating-point locations.
- **Bilinear interpolation** computes the feature value at each sample point, producing a fixed-size feature map of $7 \times 7$ (for box/cls) or $14 \times 14$ (for mask).

**4. Parallel Heads (per RoI):**
- **Classification Head:** 2 FC layers → softmax over $K+1$ classes.
- **Box Regression Head:** 2 FC layers → 4 box offsets $(dx, dy, dw, dh)$.
- **Mask Head:** 4 conv layers → 1 deconv layer (2× upsampling) → 1×1 conv + sigmoid → $K \times 28 \times 28$ mask tensor.

**5. Loss Function:**
$$L = L_{\text{cls}} + L_{\text{box}} + L_{\text{mask}}$$
where the mask loss is computed only for the ground-truth class (decoupled).

**6. Inference:**
- Class, box, and mask predictions are post-processed via NMS and class-specific score thresholds to produce the final instance segmentation output.

*(Marks: 1 backbone, 1 RPN, 1 RoIAlign, 2 parallel heads, 1 loss, 1 inference/flow)*

---

#### **(b) With a neat diagram, explain the working of Mask R-CNN for instance segmentation. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Working Flow — Step by Step:**

**Step 1: Input Image Acquisition**
The input image $I \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times 3}$ is fed to the network. For COCO, images are typically resized so the shorter side is 800 pixels.

**Step 2: Backbone Feature Extraction**
ResNet-101 (with 101 layers) processes the image, producing a feature map $F$ at 1/16 resolution (stride 16). FPN builds top-down feature maps with lateral connections for multi-scale context.

**Step 3: Region Proposal Generation**
RPN scans the feature pyramid and outputs ~2000 candidate object regions. After NMS, the top 300–1000 proposals are forwarded.

**Step 4: RoIAlign Sampling**
For each RoI, the feature map is sampled at 4 sub-bin points using bilinear interpolation, yielding fixed-size aligned features.

**Step 5: Parallel Prediction**
Three heads operate in parallel:
- **Cls head** outputs class probabilities → e.g., 0.95 for "person"
- **Box head** refines box coordinates
- **Mask head** produces a $28 \times 28$ binary mask, upscaled to the original image size

**Step 6: Decoupled Mask Selection**
From the $K \times 28 \times 28$ mask output, only the slice corresponding to the **predicted class** is used (decoupled prediction principle).

**Step 7: Post-Processing**
Apply NMS (IoU threshold 0.5), score threshold (e.g., 0.7), and produce the final instance segmentation map with per-instance color overlays.

**Block Diagram (Recreated Inline):**

```
[Input] → [ResNet+FPN] → [RPN Proposals]
   → [RoIAlign] → [Cls | Box | Mask Heads]
   → [Per-class Mask] → [Resize to image] → [Output]
```

*(Marks: 1 step 1–2, 2 step 3–4, 2 step 5–6, 1 step 7 + diagram, 1 conclusion)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper]`

#### **(a) Discuss the multi-task loss function used in Mask R-CNN with mathematical formulations. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The Mask R-CNN loss is a **multi-task loss** combining classification, bounding-box regression, and mask prediction:

$$L = L_{\text{cls}} + L_{\text{box}} + L_{\text{mask}}$$

**1. Classification Loss $L_{\text{cls}}$:**
$$L_{\text{cls}} = -\frac{1}{N_{\text{cls}}} \sum_{i} -\log p_i^*$$

where $p_i^*$ is the softmax probability of the true class for sample $i$. This is a standard cross-entropy loss over $K+1$ classes (including background).

**2. Bounding-Box Loss $L_{\text{box}}$:**
$$L_{\text{box}} = \frac{1}{N_{\text{reg}}} \sum_{i} p_i^* \cdot \sum_{j \in \{x,y,w,h\}} \text{Smooth}_{L1}(t_j^i - \hat{t}_j^i)$$

where:
- $t^i = (t_x, t_y, t_w, t_h)$ are the predicted offsets
- $\hat{t}^i$ are the target offsets computed from the ground-truth box
- $p_i^*$ is 1 for foreground, 0 for background (loss active only for positive RoIs)

The Smooth L1 function:
$$\text{Smooth}_{L1}(x) = \begin{cases} 0.5x^2, & \vert x \vert < 1 \\ \vert x \vert - 0.5, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**3. Mask Loss $L_{\text{mask}}$:**
$$L_{\text{mask}} = -\frac{1}{m^2} \sum_{1 \le i,j \le m} \left[ y_{ij} \log \hat{y}_{ij}^{k^*} + (1 - y_{ij}) \log(1 - \hat{y}_{ij}^{k^*}) \right]$$

where:
- $k^*$ is the **ground-truth class** (decoupling principle)
- $m$ is the mask resolution (typically 28)
- $\hat{y}_{ij}^{k^*}$ is the sigmoid output for class $k^*$
- $y_{ij} \in \{0, 1\}$ is the ground-truth binary mask

**4. Decoupling Principle (Critical):**
Mask R-CNN generates one mask per class ($K$ masks total). Only the mask for the **true class** contributes to the loss. This eliminates inter-class competition and improves accuracy significantly compared to Faster R-CNN-style multi-class mask outputs.

**5. Total Hyper-parameter Setting:**
Typically, $L_{\text{cls}}$, $L_{\text{box}}$, and $L_{\text{mask}}$ are weighted equally (weight = 1.0) in the original paper.

*(Marks: 1 for total loss, 2 for $L_{\text{cls}}$ and $L_{\text{box}}$, 2 for $L_{\text{mask}}$, 1 for decoupling, 1 for hyper-parameters)*

---

#### **(b) Explain RoIAlign in detail with its advantages over RoIPool. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. The Problem with RoIPool:**
RoIPool, used in Faster R-CNN, performs **two quantizations**:
- First, the RoI coordinates are rounded to integer values $(x_1, y_1, x_2, y_2)$.
- Second, the bin boundaries within the RoI are rounded to integer indices.

These quantizations introduce **misalignment** between the RoI and the extracted features. For detection, this is tolerable. For **pixel-level mask prediction**, this is catastrophic — a 1-pixel shift compounds through 4 max-pooling operations in the mask head, leading to a **~10% drop in mask accuracy**.

**2. RoIAlign Solution:**
RoIAlign completely eliminates quantization:

- **Step 1:** The RoI boundaries $(x_1, y_1, x_2, y_2)$ are kept as **exact floating-point values**.
- **Step 2:** The RoI is divided into $H \times W$ bins (e.g., $7 \times 7$ for box head, $14 \times 14$ for mask head) using **floating-point** boundaries.
- **Step 3:** Inside each bin, **4 regular sample points** are chosen at fractional offsets (typically at relative positions 0.25 and 0.75 of the bin).
- **Step 4:** For each sample point, the feature value is computed using **bilinear interpolation** of the 4 nearest integer feature map locations.
- **Step 5:** The 4 sampled values are **aggregated** (max-pool for box head; direct feature for mask head) into one output value per bin.

**3. Mathematical Formulation of Bilinear Interpolation:**

Given a sample point $(x, y)$ and its 4 neighboring integer feature locations $(x_0, y_0), (x_1, y_0), (x_0, y_1), (x_1, y_1)$:

$$V(x,y) = \sum_{a=0}^{1}\sum_{b=0}^{1} w_a \cdot w_b \cdot F(x_a, y_b)$$

where:
$$w_0 = 1 - (x - x_0), \quad w_1 = x - x_0$$

**4. Advantages of RoIAlign:**

| Aspect | RoIPool | RoIAlign |
|---|---|---|
| Coordinate handling | Integer (quantized) | Floating-point (exact) |
| Sub-pixel accuracy | No | Yes (bilinear interp.) |
| Mask accuracy (COCO) | Baseline | **+10% relative** |
| Speed | Slightly faster | Negligible overhead |
| Pixel-level suitability | Poor | Excellent |

**5. Implementation Details:**
- Implemented as a custom CUDA layer for efficiency.
- Bilinear weights are precomputed for each RoI.
- Backward pass: gradients are propagated to all 4 neighboring feature locations weighted by the bilinear weights.

*(Marks: 1 for RoIPool problem, 2 for RoIAlign steps, 1 for math, 2 for advantages, 1 for implementation)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Semantic vs Instance Segmentation** — Always state the distinction: instance = per-object mask, semantic = per-class label.
> 2. **Forgetting Decoupling Principle** — When asked about mask loss, mention that only the GT-class mask is penalized; this is a **board favorite** question.
> 3. **Confusing RoIAlign with RoIPool** — Many students mix up the two. Always emphasize "no quantization" and "bilinear interpolation" for RoIAlign.
> 4. **Skipping the FPN** — Mask R-CNN with FPN is standard; forgetting to mention FPN loses marks.
> 5. **Forgetting Mask Resolution $m=28$** — A common detail-based mark.
> 6. **Writing Mask R-CNN output as just one mask** — It outputs $K$ masks (one per class).

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Mask R-CNN = Faster R-CNN + Mask Branch** — an extension, not a replacement.
- [x] **Three Parallel Heads:** Classification (Softmax), Box Regression (Smooth L1), Mask Prediction (Sigmoid per class).
- [x] **Multi-Task Loss:** $L = L_{\text{cls}} + L_{\text{box}} + L_{\text{mask}}$, all weighted equally.
- [x] **Decoupled Mask Loss:** Only the ground-truth class mask contributes to the loss.
- [x] **RoIAlign** is the KEY innovation — eliminates quantization, uses bilinear interpolation, samples 4 points per bin.
- [x] **FPN Backbone:** Multi-scale feature pyramid $P_2$–$P_5$; level assignment via $\lfloor k_0 + \log_2(\sqrt{wh}/224) \rfloor$.
- [x] **Mask Resolution:** $m = 28$ (squared = 784 outputs per class).
- [x] **Instance vs Semantic vs Panoptic:** Instance = per-object; Semantic = per-class; Panoptic = both.
- [x] **Smooth L1 Loss Gradient is Bounded by 1** — prevents gradient explosion in box regression.
- [x] **Benchmark:** ~37.1 box AP, ~32.0 mask AP on COCO test-dev (ResNet-101).
- [x] **Applications:** Medical imaging, autonomous driving, AR, agriculture, robotics, industrial inspection.
- [x] **Two-Stage Detector:** RPN generates proposals, then heads refine — this is a TWO-STAGE detector.
- [x] **Author:** He, Gkioxari, Dollár, Girshick (Facebook AI Research, FAIR), 2017.
- [x] **Original Paper Title:** *"Mask R-CNN"* — IEEE International Conference on Computer Vision (ICCV), 2017.
- [x] **Mask Head Architecture:** 4 conv layers → 1 deconv (2× upsample) → 1×1 conv with sigmoid.
- [x] **Inference Time:** ~200 ms per image (ResNet-101, single GPU) — slower than single-stage detectors (YOLO, SSD) but more accurate.
- [x] **Output Post-Processing:** NMS (IoU=0.5) → score threshold (0.7) → per-class mask selection → resize to original image.
- [x] **Training Data:** COCO 2017 — 118K training images, 5K validation, 20K test-dev, 80 classes.
- [x] **Why $m=28$:** Large enough for detail, small enough for memory efficiency (compared to $m=56$ or $m=112$).
- [x] **Single-Stage vs Two-Stage:** Mask R-CNN is two-stage; one-stage instance segmentation models (e.g., YOLACT) exist for speed-critical applications.
- [x] **FPN was originally introduced in:** *"Feature Pyramid Networks for Object Detection"* by Lin et al., 2017.
- [x] **RoIAlign Improvement:** ~5–10% relative mask AP gain on COCO compared to RoIPool.
- [x] **Softmax vs Sigmoid in Mask Head:** Uses **per-class sigmoid** (not softmax) so that masks are decoupled across classes.
- [x] **Bounding Box Parameterization:** $t_x = (x - x_a)/w_a$, $t_y = (y - y_a)/h_a$, $t_w = \log(w/w_a)$, $t_h = \log(h/h_a)$ — for class-specific deltas.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: U-Net and Semantic Segmentation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST745 - COMPUTER VISION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** U-Net and Semantic Segmentation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Semantic Segmentation — Formal Definition

**Semantic Segmentation** is a dense, pixel-level classification task in computer vision where every single pixel in an input image is assigned a categorical class label. Unlike image classification (one label per image) or object detection (bounding boxes), semantic segmentation produces a complete, structured output map $M \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times C}$ where $H$ and $W$ match the input spatial dimensions, and $C$ represents the number of semantic classes.

$$\text{Segmentation: } I \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times 3} \longrightarrow M \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times C}$$

where each pixel location $(i, j)$ in $M$ contains a one-hot encoded class vector of length $C$ (total classes).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Semantic segmentation is a *dense prediction* task. The output preserves the **exact spatial resolution** of the input — this is the defining property that distinguishes it from classification and detection.

**Key Sub-Tasks under Semantic Segmentation in KTU Syllabus:**
1. **Semantic Segmentation** — Class label per pixel (no instance separation).
2. **Instance Segmentation** — Distinguishes individual object instances of the same class.
3. **Panoptic Segmentation** — Combines both (every pixel gets a class + instance ID).

### 1.2 U-Net — Formal Definition

**U-Net** is a *fully convolutional encoder–decoder neural network* introduced by Ronneberger et al. (2015) for biomedical image segmentation. Its defining trait is the symmetric **"U" shaped topology** consisting of a *contracting path* (encoder) for context capture, a *symmetric expanding path* (decoder) for precise localization, and **skip connections** that fuse low-level spatial detail from the encoder with high-level semantic features from the decoder.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Output Channels in U-Net:** With binary segmentation, the final $1 \times 1$ convolution outputs **2 channels** (background + foreground). For $C$ classes, the final layer outputs $C$ channels, applied pixel-wise via **softmax**.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **pathologist examining a biopsy slide** under a microscope. To diagnose cancer, you must:
1. **Zoom out** to understand the overall tissue context (what organ is this? what is the general architecture?).
2. **Zoom in** to precisely trace the boundary of each tumor cell.

A standard CNN (encoder-only) is brilliant at step (1) — understanding context — but throws away the precise pixel locations once the image passes through pooling layers. A naive decoder can zoom in, but has lost the fine detail.

**U-Net is like a doctor with two-way communication:** while zooming in (decoder), the doctor constantly refers back to the original microscope view (skip connections) to ensure cell boundaries are drawn accurately. The encoder "sees the forest" and the decoder "sees the trees," but skip connections let the doctor see **both at once**.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Applications of U-Net:**
> - Tumor segmentation in MRI/CT scans
> - Autonomous driving road/lane detection
> - Satellite imagery land-cover classification
> - Industrial defect detection
> - Agricultural crop-disease mapping

### 1.4 Why U-Net Matters in KTU Context

U-Net is taught in Module 4 because it represents the **paradigm shift** from patch-based classification (e.g., sliding-window CNNs) to **end-to-end dense prediction**. It is the *foundational architecture* upon which virtually all modern segmentation networks (LinkNet, Feature Pyramid Networks, DeepLab variants, Swin-UNet, TransUNet) are built.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Predecessor Architectures to Remember:**
> - **Fully Convolutional Networks (FCN, 2015)** — First end-to-end CNN for segmentation; used skip connections from VGG.
> - **SegNet (2016)** — Used max-pooling indices for upsampling (no learned deconvolution).
> - **U-Net (2015)** — Symmetric encoder-decoder with concatenation-based skip connections. **Most cited architecture in medical AI.**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 U-Net Architecture — Structural Breakdown

The U-Net is composed of **three distinct functional blocks**:

#### (A) The Contracting Path (Encoder / Left Side of "U")

The encoder follows the **typical CNN design philosophy**: repeated application of two $3 \times 3$ unpadded convolutions, each followed by a **ReLU** activation and a $2 \times 2$ **max pooling** operation with stride 2 for downsampling.

**Operational Logic — Step by Step:**

- **Step 1 — Feature Extraction (Conv Block):** Two consecutive $3 \times 3$ convolutions extract increasingly abstract features. Initial layers capture edges and textures; deeper layers capture object parts and semantic context.
- **Step 2 — Downsampling:** $2 \times 2$ max pooling with stride 2 halves the spatial resolution. This **doubles the receptive field** of subsequent convolutions, allowing the network to "see" larger context at the cost of spatial precision.
- **Step 3 — Channel Doubling:** After each pooling, the number of feature channels is **doubled** (e.g., $64 \to 128 \to 256 \to 512$). This compensates for spatial information loss by increasing semantic richness.
- **Step 4 — Bottleneck:** At the bottom of the "U", the network reaches its deepest, most compressed representation — typically **$1024$ channels** at the lowest resolution.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why double the channels?** Pooling loses $75\%$ of pixels. By doubling feature channels, the network preserves the *information capacity* (channel-wise) even as spatial resolution shrinks.

#### (B) The Expanding Path (Decoder / Right Side of "U")

The decoder performs **upsampling** to recover spatial resolution. The structure mirrors the encoder:

- **Step 1 — Upsampling:** Each step uses a $2 \times 2$ **transposed convolution** (also called *up-convolution* or *deconvolution*) that halves the number of feature channels while doubling spatial resolution.
- **Step 2 — Concatenation (Skip Connection):** The upsampled feature map is **concatenated** channel-wise with the corresponding feature map from the encoder at the same resolution level. This is the **defining innovation of U-Net**.
- **Step 3 — Refinement Convolution:** Two $3 \times 3$ convolutions followed by ReLU process the concatenated features, learning to combine low-level detail with high-level semantics.
- **Step 4 — Final Layer:** A $1 \times 1$ convolution maps the final 64-channel feature map to the desired number of output classes $C$.

#### (C) Skip Connections — The "Why" of U-Net's Success

Skip connections are **direct feature-map concatenations** from encoder layer $i$ to decoder layer $i$. They serve three critical purposes:

1. **Spatial Precision Recovery:** Encoders preserve high-resolution edge/gradient information that pooling destroys. Skip connections reinject this.
2. **Gradient Flow:** They provide short paths for backpropagation, mitigating the vanishing gradient problem in deep networks.
3. **Multi-Scale Feature Fusion:** They enable the network to use features at multiple levels of abstraction simultaneously — a precursor to Feature Pyramid Networks (FPN).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often confuse *addition* (used in ResNet) with *concatenation* (used in U-Net). U-Net **concatenates** skip features along the channel axis, creating a thicker feature map. ResNet-style skip connections **add** element-wise.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Formula / Definition** | **Purpose / Notes** |
|---|---|---|
| **Output spatial size (Conv)** | $H_{out} = \lfloor \frac{H_{in} + 2P - K}{S} \rfloor + 1$ | $P$ = padding, $K$ = kernel, $S$ = stride |
| **Output spatial size (Transposed Conv)** | $H_{out} = (H_{in} - 1) \cdot S - 2P + K$ | Used in decoder upsampling |
| **Pixel-wise Softmax** | $p_c(i,j) = \frac{e^{z_c(i,j)}}{\sum_{c'=1}^{C} e^{z_{c'}(i,j)}}$ | Converts logits to class probabilities |
| **Cross-Entropy Loss (per pixel)** | $L_{CE}(i,j) = -\sum_{c=1}^{C} y_c(i,j) \log p_c(i,j)$ | Standard multi-class loss |
| **Mean CE Loss** | $L = -\frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=1}^{N}\sum_{c=1}^{C} y_c^{(n)} \log p_c^{(n)}$ | $N$ = number of pixels |
| **Dice Coefficient (F1)** | $D = \frac{2 \vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert} = \frac{2 TP}{2 TP + FP + FN}$ | Measures region overlap, range $[0,1]$ |
| **Dice Loss** | $L_{Dice} = 1 - D = 1 - \frac{2\sum p_i g_i}{\sum p_i + \sum g_i}$ | Optimized for class imbalance |
| **IoU (Jaccard Index)** | $IoU = \frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP + FN}$ | Standard segmentation metric |
| **IoU-Dice Relationship** | $IoU = \frac{D}{2 - D}$ | Algebraic identity, useful in exams |
| **Pixel Accuracy** | $Acc = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Misleading for imbalanced classes |
| **Receptive Field** | $RF_{l+1} = RF_l + (K_l - 1) \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{l} S_i$ | Grows with each layer |
| **Padding for size preservation** | $P = \frac{K-1}{2}$ for $K=3 \Rightarrow P=1$ | U-Net uses valid padding in original paper |

### 2.3 Loss Functions in U-Net — Deep Dive

#### (a) Weighted Cross-Entropy Loss

To handle **class imbalance** (e.g., tiny tumor region vs. vast healthy tissue), Ronneberger et al. used a *pre-computed weight map*:

$$w(\mathbf{x}) = w_c(\mathbf{x}) + w_0 \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{(d_1(\mathbf{x}) + d_2(\mathbf{x}))^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$

where:
- $w_c(\mathbf{x})$ = frequency-balancing weight for class at pixel $\mathbf{x}$
- $d_1(\mathbf{x})$ = distance to nearest cell boundary
- $d_2(\mathbf{x})$ = distance to second nearest cell
- $w_0 = 10$, $\sigma \approx 5$ pixels (typical defaults)

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition:** $w_0$ pushes the network to learn the *thin borders* between touching cells — a frequent failure mode in medical imaging.

#### (b) Dice Loss (Modern Standard)

For binary segmentation with predicted probabilities $p_i$ and ground truth $g_i \in \{0,1\}$:

$$L_{Dice} = 1 - \frac{2 \sum_{i=1}^{N} p_i g_i + \epsilon}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} p_i + \sum_{i=1}^{N} g_i + \epsilon}$$

where $\epsilon$ (typically $10^{-6}$) prevents division by zero and improves gradient stability.

#### (c) Combined Loss (Best Practice in Modern U-Net Variants)

$$L_{Total} = \alpha \cdot L_{CE} + \beta \cdot L_{Dice}, \quad \alpha + \beta = 1$$

Common choice: $\alpha = 0.5$, $\beta = 0.5$.

### 2.4 Evaluation Metrics — Engineering Utility

| **Metric** | **Range** | **Use Case** | **Limitation** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pixel Accuracy | $[0, 1]$ | Quick baseline check | Fails on imbalanced classes |
| Mean IoU (mIoU) | $[0, 1]$ | KTU/industry standard | Penalizes both FP and FN equally |
| Dice / F1 | $[0, 1]$ | Medical imaging gold standard | Slightly less strict than IoU |
| Hausdorff Distance | $[0, \infty)$ | Boundary precision | Sensitive to outliers |
| Precision/Recall | $[0, 1]$ | Class-specific analysis | Need threshold tuning |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU Exams:** Always report **mIoU** as the primary metric. Dice is acceptable in medical-imaging contexts. Never use plain pixel accuracy as the sole metric for imbalanced segmentation.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

U-Net is the de-facto backbone in:
- **NVIDIA Clara** (medical imaging platform)
- **Tesla Autopilot** lane/net segmentation
- **Planet Labs** satellite mapping
- **Agriculture:** plant disease phenotyping, weed detection
- **Materials Science:** grain boundary segmentation in microscopy
- **Industrial QA:** surface defect detection on manufacturing lines
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Output Spatial Dimension at Each U-Net Level

**Given:** Input image $I \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times 3}$, with 4 downsampling levels in the encoder.

**Step 1 — Identify convolution parameters (U-Net paper defaults):**
- Convolution: $K = 3$, $P = 0$ (valid padding), $S = 1$
- Pooling: $K_p = 2$, $S = 2$

**Step 2 — Apply general convolution output formula at each layer:**

$$H_{out} = \left\lfloor \frac{H_{in} + 2P - K}{S} \right\rfloor + 1 = H_{in} - 2$$

Each $3\times 3$ conv with no padding reduces spatial size by 2 (one pixel on each side).

**Step 3 — Compute size after each "double conv" block (before pooling):**

At encoder level $l$, with input size $H_l$:

$$H_{l,\text{after-conv}} = H_l - 2 - 2 = H_l - 4$$

**Step 4 — Apply pooling (halve the size):**

$$H_{l+1} = \frac{H_{l,\text{after-conv}}}{2} = \frac{H_l - 4}{2}$$

**Step 5 — Concrete example for $H_0 = 572$ (Ronneberger's original input):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_0 &: H = 572 \xrightarrow{\text{2 convs}} 568 \xrightarrow{\text{pool}} 284 \\
L_1 &: H = 284 \xrightarrow{\text{2 convs}} 280 \xrightarrow{\text{pool}} 136 \\
L_2 &: H = 136 \xrightarrow{\text{2 convs}} 132 \xrightarrow{\text{pool}} 64 \\
L_3 &: H = 64 \xrightarrow{\text{2 convs}} 60 \xrightarrow{\text{pool}} 28 \\
L_4 &: H = 28 \xrightarrow{\text{2 convs}} 24 \xrightarrow{\text{pool}} 12
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Verify using input size $H_0 = 572$ and $L = 4$ pooling operations:**

$$H_{\text{bottleneck}} = \frac{572 - 4 \cdot 4}{2^4} = \frac{556}{16} = 34.75 \approx \text{with cropping} \rightarrow 28$$

The exact 28 in the paper comes from the **cropping** done before concatenation (since valid padding makes encoder/decoder sizes mismatch). The modern implementation uses **same padding** ($P = 1$) so sizes match exactly.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Modern U-Net Implementation (Same Padding):** For $H_0 = 256$:
> - Encoder levels: $256 \to 128 \to 64 \to 32 \to 16 \to 8$ (bottleneck)
> - Decoder levels: $8 \to 16 \to 32 \to 64 \to 128 \to 256$ (output)
> - Channels: $64 \to 128 \to 256 \to 512 \to 1024$ (bottleneck), then mirror back.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Dice Coefficient to IoU Algebraic Identity

**Claim:** $IoU = \frac{D}{2 - D}$

**Given:**
- $IoU = \frac{TP}{TP + FP + FN}$
- $D = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}$

**Step 1 — Express Dice in terms of IoU components:**

Let $T = TP$, $F = FP + FN$. Then:

$$D = \frac{2T}{2T + F}, \quad IoU = \frac{T}{T + F}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $F$ in terms of $T$ and $D$:**

$$D(2T + F) = 2T \implies 2TD + DF = 2T \implies F = \frac{2T(1 - D)}{D}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $F$ into IoU expression:**

$$IoU = \frac{T}{T + \frac{2T(1-D)}{D}} = \frac{T}{\frac{TD + 2T(1-D)}{D}} = \frac{TD}{T(D + 2 - 2D)} = \frac{D}{2 - D}$$

**Step 4 — Verify with edge cases:**
- If $D = 1$ (perfect overlap): $IoU = \frac{1}{1} = 1$ ✓
- If $D = 0$ (no overlap): $IoU = 0$ ✓
- If $D = 0.5$: $IoU = \frac{0.5}{1.5} = \frac{1}{3} \approx 0.333$

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** This identity appears frequently in KTU viva questions. Memorize the direction — Dice is always $\geq$ IoU, and both are bounded in $[0,1]$.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Cross-Entropy Gradient for Pixel-Wise Softmax

**Setup:** Pixel $\mathbf{x}$ has logits $\mathbf{z} = (z_1, z_2, \ldots, z_C)$, softmax output $\mathbf{p}$, and one-hot ground truth $\mathbf{y}$.

**Step 1 — Softmax definition:**

$$p_c = \frac{e^{z_c}}{\sum_{k=1}^{C} e^{z_k}}$$

**Step 2 — Logits gradient of cross-entropy:**

$$L = -\sum_{c=1}^{C} y_c \log p_c$$

**Step 3 — Apply chain rule for $\frac{\partial L}{\partial z_j}$:**

First, compute $\frac{\partial p_c}{\partial z_j}$:

- If $c = j$: $\frac{\partial p_j}{\partial z_j} = p_j(1 - p_j)$
- If $c \neq j$: $\frac{\partial p_c}{\partial z_j} = -p_c p_j$

In compact form: $\frac{\partial p_c}{\partial z_j} = p_c(\delta_{cj} - p_j)$ where $\delta_{cj}$ is the Kronecker delta.

**Step 4 — Substitute and simplify:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial L}{\partial z_j} &= -\sum_{c=1}^{C} y_c \cdot \frac{1}{p_c} \cdot \frac{\partial p_c}{\partial z_j} \\
&= -\sum_{c=1}^{C} y_c \cdot \frac{1}{p_c} \cdot p_c(\delta_{cj} - p_j) \\
&= -\sum_{c=1}^{C} y_c (\delta_{cj} - p_j) \\
&= -\left(y_j - p_j \sum_{c=1}^{C} y_c\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply constraint $\sum_c y_c = 1$ (one-hot encoding):**

$$\boxed{\frac{\partial L}{\partial z_j} = p_j - y_j}$$

This beautifully simple result is why cross-entropy + softmax is the universal default for classification — the gradient is simply the **prediction error**.

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Receptive Field Growth in U-Net

**Given:** Two $3\times3$ convs (each $K=3$, $S=1$) followed by $2\times2$ pooling (effective $S=2$) per encoder block.

**Step 1 — Receptive field formula for stacked layers:**

$$RF_{l} = RF_{l-1} + (K_l - 1) \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{l-1} S_i$$

**Step 2 — Initialize:** $RF_0 = 1$ (single pixel).

**Step 3 — Compute per block. Each block contributes: conv1 + conv2 + pool.**

- After conv1 of block 1: $RF = 1 + 2 \cdot 1 = 3$
- After conv2 of block 1: $RF = 3 + 2 \cdot 1 = 5$
- After pool of block 1: $RF = 5 + 1 \cdot 1 = 6$ (pool itself is $K_p=2$, so adds $1$)
- After conv1 of block 2: $RF = 6 + 2 \cdot 2 = 10$
- After conv2 of block 2: $RF = 10 + 2 \cdot 2 = 14$
- After pool of block 2: $RF = 14 + 1 \cdot 2 = 16$

**Step 4 — General formula for $L$ blocks:**

$$RF_{\text{bottleneck}} = \sum_{l=1}^{L} 2^{l+1} - 2^{l} + 2 = 2^{L+2} - 2$$

Wait, recomputing properly. Per block $l$, the cumulative stride product before it is $2^{l-1}$, and each block adds $(3-1) \cdot 2^{l-1} + (3-1) \cdot 2^{l-1} + (2-1) \cdot 2^{l-1} = 5 \cdot 2^{l-1}$.

**Step 5 — Total over $L=4$ blocks:**

$$RF_{\text{bottleneck}} = \sum_{l=1}^{4} 5 \cdot 2^{l-1} = 5(1 + 2 + 4 + 8) = 5 \cdot 15 = 75 \text{ pixels}$$

Wait, the correct derivation: the first $3\times3$ conv sees a $3\times3$ region, the second (still stride 1) sees a $5\times5$ region, the pool (stride 2) doubles this to a $6\times6$ effective stride. So each block roughly doubles the receptive field, with $+1$ correction per pool.

$$RF_{L} \approx 2^{L+1} \cdot 1 + \text{lower order terms}$$

For $L=4$: $RF \approx 32$ at the bottleneck from pooling alone, plus $20$ from convs = approximately $52$ pixels (depends on exact interpretation).

> [!NOTE]
> **For exam purposes:** The key takeaway is that the **bottleneck's receptive field covers the entire input image** for typical U-Net depths (4-5 levels), which is what enables global context understanding.

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| **Property** | **FCN (2015)** | **SegNet (2016)** | **U-Net (2015)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Skip Connection Type | Addition (from VGG) | None (uses indices) | **Concatenation** |
| Upsampling Method | Bilinear / Transposed Conv | Max-unpooling (no learn) | **Transposed Conv (learned)** |
| Symmetric Topology | No (encoder only) | Yes | **Yes (mirror image)** |
| Designed For | Natural images | Road scene understanding | **Biomedical images** |
| Input Size Constraint | Flexible | Flexible | **Original: 572×572 (mirror-padded)** |
| Parameter Efficiency | Medium | High | **High** |
| Boundary Precision | Moderate | Good | **Excellent** |
| Training Data Requirement | Large (ImageNet pretrain) | Large | **Small (data aug suffices)** |
| KTU Exam Frequency | High | Low | **Very High** |

### 3.6 Derivation 5: Weighted Cross-Entropy with Frequency Balancing

**Step 1 — Class frequency weight:**

For class $c$ with pixel count $n_c$ in training set:

$$w_c = \frac{1}{\log(\kappa + n_c)}$$

where $\kappa$ is a hyperparameter (e.g., $\kappa = 1.02$ in the original U-Net paper).

**Step 2 — Combined weight map:**

$$w(\mathbf{x}) = w_{c(\mathbf{x})} + w_0 \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{(d_1(\mathbf{x}) + d_2(\mathbf{x}))^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Final loss for a batch of $B$ images:**

$$L = -\sum_{b=1}^{B} \sum_{i,j} w^{(b)}(i,j) \cdot \log p^{(b)}(i,j, c^{(b)}(i,j))$$

**Step 4 — Justification of $w_0$ term:** The exponential term peaks at $1.0$ when $d_1 = d_2 = 0$ (pixel is at the boundary between two cells of the same class). This forces the network to learn **boundary pixels more aggressively**, which would otherwise be a small minority of the loss.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU Module 4 Exam:** You should be able to sketch the weight map's shape — it forms a Gaussian "ridge" along cell boundaries, decaying to $w_c$ in the cell interior.

### 3.7 Derivation 6: Computational Cost of U-Net

**Setup:** Standard U-Net with 4 levels, $C_0 = 64$ base channels.

**Step 1 — Encoder parameters per level:**

At level $l$, the spatial size is $\frac{H}{2^l}$ and channels are $C_0 \cdot 2^l$. The two $3\times3$ convs have:

$$\text{Params}_l = 2 \cdot (3^2 \cdot C_l \cdot C_{l+1}) = 18 \cdot C_l^2$$

(assuming channels double, $C_{l+1} = 2C_l$)

**Step 2 — Total encoder conv parameters:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Params}_{enc} &= \sum_{l=0}^{3} 18 \cdot (C_0 \cdot 2^l)^2 \\
&= 18 C_0^2 \sum_{l=0}^{3} 4^l \\
&= 18 \cdot 64^2 \cdot (1 + 4 + 16 + 64) \\
&= 18 \cdot 4096 \cdot 85 \\
&= 6,266,880
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Bottleneck:** $18 \cdot 1024^2 = 18,874,368$ parameters

**Step 4 — Decoder (similar to encoder):** Approximately $6,266,880$ parameters (symmetric)

**Step 5 — Total:** Approximately $\mathbf{31.4 \text{ million parameters}}$ for the original U-Net with $C_0 = 64$.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory-Saving Variants:**
> - **Attention U-Net:** Adds attention gates on skip connections (+5% params, better accuracy).
> - **U-Net++:** Nested skip connections (significantly more params, +3-5% mIoU).
> - **nnU-Net:** Auto-configures depth, channels, and pre-processing (still uses U-Net backbone).
