## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define semantic segmentation. How does it differ from image classification and object detection?**

**Model Answer:**

Semantic segmentation is a dense prediction task in computer vision where **every pixel** in an input image is assigned a categorical class label, producing an output mask of the same spatial resolution as the input.

**Differences:**

| Task | Output | Granularity |
|---|---|---|
| Image Classification | Single label $\in \mathbb{R}^{C}$ | One per **image** |
| Object Detection | Bounding boxes + class | Per **object instance** |
| Semantic Segmentation | Class label per pixel $\in \mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times C}$ | Per **pixel** |

Key distinctions:
- Unlike classification, semantic segmentation preserves **spatial structure**.
- Unlike object detection, it produces **pixel-precise boundaries** but does *not* distinguish between separate instances of the same class.

> [!NOTE]
> **3-Mark Examiner's Note:** Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for tabular distinction, 1 mark for instance-vs-semantic clarification.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List and briefly explain the three main components of the U-Net architecture.**

**Model Answer:**

The U-Net architecture consists of three main components:

1. **Contracting Path (Encoder):** Repeated application of $3 \times 3$ convolutions followed by $2 \times 2$ max pooling. Doubles channels and halves spatial resolution at each level. Captures **contextual / semantic information**.

2. **Expanding Path (Decoder):** Symmetric to the encoder. Uses $2 \times 2$ transposed convolutions to upsample features, halving channels and doubling resolution. Recovers **spatial localization**.

3. **Skip Connections:** Concatenate feature maps from encoder to corresponding decoder levels at the same resolution. Fuse **low-level spatial detail** with **high-level semantic context**, enabling precise boundary recovery.

> [!TIP]
> Use bullet points and label each part's role: "context", "localization", "fusion".

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a)** Explain the U-Net architecture in detail with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of skip connections and how they differ from residual connections. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the relationship between the Dice coefficient and the Intersection over Union (IoU) metric. Compute both metrics for a binary segmentation scenario where $TP = 80$, $FP = 20$, $FN = 10$, and $TN = 890$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

The U-Net architecture, introduced by Ronneberger et al. (2015) for biomedical image segmentation, has a symmetric **encoder-decoder** structure resembling the letter "U".

**1. Contracting Path (Encoder):**
- Comprises 4 levels of feature extraction.
- Each level has two $3 \times 3$ unpadded convolutions, each followed by ReLU.
- A $2 \times 2$ max-pooling with stride 2 downsamples the feature map.
- Channels double at each level: $64 \to 128 \to 256 \to 512$.
- Captures global semantic context.

**2. Bottleneck:**
- Two $3 \times 3$ convolutions with $1024$ channels at the deepest level.
- Most compressed representation — highest semantic density.

**3. Expanding Path (Decoder):**
- Mirrors the encoder with 4 levels.
- Each level begins with a $2 \times 2$ **transposed convolution** (up-convolution) that halves channels and doubles resolution.
- **Skip connection:** the upsampled feature map is concatenated with the corresponding encoder feature map (cropped if needed).
- Two $3 \times 3$ convolutions refine the combined features.
- Final $1 \times 1$ convolution maps to $C$ output classes.

**4. Role of Skip Connections:**
- Recover fine-grained spatial information lost during pooling.
- Enable **multi-scale feature fusion**.
- Improve gradient flow during backpropagation.
- Allow precise boundary delineation — critical in medical imaging.

**5. Skip Connections vs Residual Connections:**

| Feature | U-Net Skip Connection | ResNet Residual Connection |
|---|---|---|
| Operation | **Concatenation** along channel axis | **Element-wise addition** |
| Channel Count | Increases after skip | Unchanged |
| Origin | Same-resolution encoder/decoder | Adjacent conv layers |
| Primary Purpose | Spatial detail recovery | Gradient flow / vanishing gradient |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often state that U-Net uses "addition" for skip connections — this is **WRONG**. U-Net uses **concatenation**. Loss of 2 marks for this error.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Part 1 — Derivation of Dice-IoU Relationship:**

Given: $IoU = \frac{TP}{TP+FP+FN}$ and $D = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}$.

Let $T = TP$ and $F = FP + FN$. Then:

$$IoU = \frac{T}{T+F}, \quad D = \frac{2T}{2T+F}$$

Solving the Dice equation for $F$:

$$D(2T+F) = 2T \implies F = \frac{2T(1-D)}{D}$$

Substituting into IoU:

$$IoU = \frac{T}{T + \frac{2T(1-D)}{D}} = \frac{TD}{TD + 2T(1-D)} = \frac{D}{D + 2 - 2D} = \frac{D}{2-D}$$

**Therefore:** $\boxed{IoU = \frac{D}{2-D}}$ or equivalently $D = \frac{2 \cdot IoU}{1 + IoU}$.

**Part 2 — Numerical Computation:**

Given: $TP = 80$, $FP = 20$, $FN = 10$, $TN = 890$.

**Compute IoU:**

$$IoU = \frac{TP}{TP + FP + FN} = \frac{80}{80 + 20 + 10} = \frac{80}{110} \approx 0.7273$$

**Compute Dice Coefficient:**

$$D = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN} = \frac{2 \cdot 80}{2 \cdot 80 + 20 + 10} = \frac{160}{190} \approx 0.8421$$

**Verification using the derived identity:**

$$IoU = \frac{D}{2-D} = \frac{0.8421}{2 - 0.8421} = \frac{0.8421}{1.1579} \approx 0.7273 \checkmark$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking Scheme (7 marks for part b):**
> - 3 marks: Step-by-step derivation of the identity
> - 2 marks: Correct IoU calculation
> - 2 marks: Correct Dice calculation + verification

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** With the help of a labeled diagram, explain the U-Net architecture. How does it differ from FCN (Fully Convolutional Network) for semantic segmentation? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the Dice Loss function used in U-Net training. For a binary segmentation case with predicted probabilities $p = [0.9, 0.4, 0.1, 0.8, 0.3]$ and ground truth $g = [1, 0, 0, 1, 0]$, compute the Dice coefficient and Dice loss. Use $\epsilon = 10^{-6}$ for numerical stability. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**U-Net Architecture (Diagram):**

```
Input (572x572x3)
    │
    ├─[Conv 3x3, 64]──[Conv 3x3, 64]──┐    (Skip 1)
    │       │                            │
    │   [MaxPool 2x2]                    │
    │       ↓                            │
    ├─[Conv 3x3, 128]─[Conv 3x3, 128]┐   (Skip 2)
    │       │                          │
    │   [MaxPool 2x2]                  │
    │       ↓                          │
    ├─[Conv 3x3, 256]─[Conv 3x3, 256]┐  (Skip 3)
    │       │                          │
    │   [MaxPool 2x2]                  │
    │       ↓                          │
    ├─[Conv 3x3, 512]─[Conv 3x3, 512]┐  (Skip 4)
    │       │                          │
    │   [MaxPool 2x2]                  │
    │       ↓                          │
    [Conv 3x3, 1024]─[Conv 3x3, 1024]  (Bottleneck)
    │       │
    │   [UpConv 2x2] ←──┘
    │       ↓  ⊕ (Concat with Skip 4)
    ├─[Conv 3x3, 512]─[Conv 3x3, 512]──┐
    │   [UpConv 2x2]                   │
    │       ↓  ⊕ (Concat with Skip 3)
    ├─[Conv 3x3, 256]─[Conv 3x3, 256]──┐
    │   [UpConv 2x2]                   │
    │       ↓  ⊕ (Concat with Skip 2)
    ├─[Conv 3x3, 128]─[Conv 3x3, 128]──┐
    │   [UpConv 2x2]                   │
    │       ↓  ⊕ (Concat with Skip 1)
    ├─[Conv 3x3, 64]─[Conv 3x3, 64]────┘
    [Conv 1x1, C classes]
    Output Segmentation Map
```

**Differences between U-Net and FCN:**

| Property | FCN | U-Net |
|---|---|---|
| Topology | Encoder + shallow decoder | **Symmetric encoder-decoder** |
| Skip Connection | **Addition** with VGG features | **Concatenation** at every level |
| Upsampling | Bilinear / strided conv | **Learned transposed convolutions** |
| Feature Fusion | Element-wise sum | Channel-wise concatenation |
| Decoder Depth | 1-2 levels | **4 levels (mirror image)** |
| Application | Natural images (PASCAL VOC) | **Biomedical images** |
| Training Data | ImageNet pretrained required | Works with **small datasets** |
| Output Resolution | Often lower (upsampled at end) | **Progressive upsampling** |

**Key Insight:** U-Net's symmetric design and concatenation-based skip connections allow **progressive refinement** of segmentation at every scale, while FCN's coarse decoder and additive skips can lead to **blurry boundaries**.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Explanation of Dice Loss:**

Dice Loss is based on the **Dice Similarity Coefficient (DSC)**, also known as the Sørensen–Dice index or F1 score. It directly optimizes the **overlap** between predicted and ground truth segmentation masks.

For binary segmentation with predicted probabilities $p_i \in [0,1]$ and ground truth labels $g_i \in \{0,1\}$:

**Dice Coefficient:**

$$D = \frac{2 \sum_{i=1}^{N} p_i g_i + \epsilon}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} p_i + \sum_{i=1}^{N} g_i + \epsilon}$$

**Dice Loss:**

$$L_{Dice} = 1 - D = 1 - \frac{2 \sum_{i=1}^{N} p_i g_i + \epsilon}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} p_i + \sum_{i=1}^{N} g_i + \epsilon}$$

**Why Dice Loss?**
- Handles **class imbalance** naturally (e.g., tumors occupy < 5% of pixels in medical images).
- Optimizes the **region overlap** metric directly.
- The $\epsilon$ (smoothing term) prevents division by zero and stabilizes gradients when $p$ and $g$ are both zero.
- More robust to small foreground regions than cross-entropy.

**Numerical Computation:**

Given: $p = [0.9, 0.4, 0.1, 0.8, 0.3]$, $g = [1, 0, 0, 1, 0]$, $\epsilon = 10^{-6}$, $N = 5$.

**Step 1 — Compute the intersection $\sum p_i g_i$:**

Element-wise product: $p \odot g = [0.9 \times 1,\ 0.4 \times 0,\ 0.1 \times 0,\ 0.8 \times 1,\ 0.3 \times 0] = [0.9, 0, 0, 0.8, 0]$

$$\sum_{i=1}^{5} p_i g_i = 0.9 + 0 + 0 + 0.8 + 0 = 1.7$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\sum p_i$:**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{5} p_i = 0.9 + 0.4 + 0.1 + 0.8 + 0.3 = 2.5$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\sum g_i$:**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{5} g_i = 1 + 0 + 0 + 1 + 0 = 2$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Dice formula with $\epsilon$:**

$$D = \frac{2(1.7) + 10^{-6}}{2.5 + 2 + 10^{-6}} = \frac{3.4 + 10^{-6}}{4.5 + 10^{-6}} \approx \frac{3.4}{4.5} \approx 0.7556$$

**Step 5 — Compute Dice Loss:**

$$L_{Dice} = 1 - 0.7556 = 0.2444$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer for Part (b):**
> - **Dice Coefficient:** $D \approx 0.7556$
> - **Dice Loss:** $L_{Dice} \approx 0.2444$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Errors:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in the numerator: Dice uses $2 \times$ intersection, not just intersection.
> 2. **Using $g_i$ in denominator without squaring**: It is the **sum** of $g_i$, not the sum of $g_i^2$.
> 3. **Ignoring the $\epsilon$ term**: For an exam, mentioning the $\epsilon$ shows you understand numerical stability.
> 4. **Mixing up Dice and IoU**: IoU = 0.6189 (using $\frac{TP}{TP+FP+FN} = \frac{1.7}{1.7+0.8+0.3} = \frac{1.7}{2.8} \approx 0.607$) for the same case — values will differ.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Semantic Segmentation = Pixel-wise Classification:** Output preserves input resolution; shape is $H \times W \times C$.
- [x] **U-Net = Symmetric Encoder-Decoder:** Two paths plus skip connections form a "U" shape.
- [x] **Encoder Downsampling:** $2 \times 2$ max pool, stride 2, doubles channels, halves resolution.
- [x] **Decoder Upsampling:** $2 \times 2$ transposed convolution, halves channels, doubles resolution.
- [x] **Skip Connections Use CONCATENATION** (not addition like ResNet).
- [x] **Final Layer:** $1 \times 1$ convolution to map to $C$ classes, then pixel-wise softmax.
- [x] **Key Loss Functions:** Cross-entropy, weighted cross-entropy (for imbalance), Dice loss (for overlap).
- [x] **Key Metrics:** Pixel accuracy, IoU (mIoU for multi-class), Dice/F1.
- [x] **Dice-IoU Relationship:** $IoU = \frac{D}{2-D}$, $D = \frac{2 \cdot IoU}{1 + IoU}$. Both in $[0,1]$.
- [x] **Softmax + Cross-Entropy Gradient:** $\frac{\partial L}{\partial z_j} = p_j - y_j$ (the prediction error).
- [x] **U-Net vs FCN:** U-Net has symmetric decoder + concatenation; FCN has shallow decoder + addition.
- [x] **Original U-Net Input:** 572×572 (mirror-padded), valid convs require cropping before skip concat.
- [x] **Modern U-Net:** Uses "same" padding so encoder/decoder sizes match exactly.
- [x] **Receptive Field:** Grows exponentially with depth; bottleneck sees entire image.
- [x] **Padding Convention:** $P = \frac{K-1}{2}$ preserves spatial size for $K = 3$.
- [x] **Transposed Conv Output Size:** $H_{out} = (H_{in}-1) \cdot S - 2P + K$.
- [x] **Standard U-Net Channels:** $64 \to 128 \to 256 \to 512 \to 1024$ (bottleneck) and back.
- [x] **Original Paper:** Ronneberger et al., 2015, "U-Net: Convolutional Networks for Biomedical Image Segmentation", MICCAI.
- [x] **Why Dice Loss is preferred for medical imaging:** Class imbalance (tumors are tiny regions).
- [x] **Augmentation strategies in original U-Net:** Random elastic deformations, shifts, rotations, gray-value variations.
- [x] **Modern variants worth knowing:** Attention U-Net, U-Net++, nnU-Net, ResU-Net, TransUNet.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Exam Strategy:**
> 1. Always draw a **U-Net block diagram** with channel annotations on every level.
> 2. For numerical problems, **show every intermediate step** — examiners reward method marks.
> 3. **Memorize** the Dice-IoU identity — it appears in nearly every KTU exam.
> 4. Always state **why** a design choice is made (e.g., "skip connections are concatenated to preserve spatial detail").
> 5. Distinguish clearly between **semantic, instance, and panoptic** segmentation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Model Quality Metrics - A Case Study to Compare Performance of Various Models on a Suitable Dataset
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST745 - COMPUTER VISION
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Model Quality Metrics - A Case Study to Compare Performance of Various Models on a Suitable Dataset

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Model Quality Metrics** are quantitative measures used to evaluate the performance, generalization capability, and reliability of machine learning and computer vision models on unseen data. In the KTU 2024 Scheme, these metrics form the backbone of **Model Evaluation & Validation** under the Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework, directly linked to **CO4: Analyze and evaluate the performance of computer vision models using standard quality metrics**.

Model Quality Metrics can be broadly classified into three categories based on the type of Computer Vision task:

1. **Classification Metrics** — for image classification (Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-Score).
2. **Regression Metrics** — for tasks like depth estimation, age prediction (MSE, RMSE, MAE, R²).
3. **Localization/Detection Metrics** — for object detection and segmentation (IoU, mAP, Dice Score).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a medical diagnostic system that detects tumors from X-ray images. Suppose the system screens **100 patients**:
- **70** truly have tumors (actual positives).
- **30** are healthy (actual negatives).

If the model predicts *"every patient has a tumor"*, it gets all 70 positives right but wrongly flags the 30 healthy patients. The **Accuracy** looks high (70%), but the model is practically useless clinically — it misses nothing (high Recall) but produces too many false alarms (low Precision).

> [!NOTE]
> **The Core Insight:** A single number (like Accuracy) can be **misleading**, especially with **imbalanced datasets**. This is exactly why a *basket* of metrics — Precision, Recall, F1, AUC — is needed to honestly assess a Computer Vision model's quality.

## 1.3 Why Metrics Matter in Computer Vision

| Scenario | Task | Critical Metric |
|----------|------|-----------------|
| Self-driving car pedestrian detection | Detection | **Recall** (cannot miss a pedestrian) |
| Cancer cell classification | Binary Classification | **F1-Score** (balance Precision & Recall) |
| Facial recognition (phone unlock) | Classification | **False Acceptance Rate (FAR)** |
| Image super-resolution | Regression | **PSNR / SSIM** |
| Tumor segmentation | Segmentation | **Dice Coefficient / IoU** |

## 1.4 The Confusion Matrix (Foundation of Classification Metrics)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **Confusion Matrix** is an $N \times N$ table (where $N$ = number of classes) that summarizes prediction results. For binary classification, it has 4 cells:

| | Predicted Positive | Predicted Negative |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual Positive** | True Positive (TP) | False Negative (FN) |
| **Actual Negative** | False Positive (FP) | True Negative (TN) |

Every classification metric is **derived** from these four counts.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification Metrics — Theory & Formulas

### 2.1.1 Accuracy
The simplest and most intuitive metric — fraction of correctly predicted samples.

$$\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$$

> [!WARNING]
> Accuracy **fails on imbalanced datasets**. If 99% of images are "non-cancer", a model that always predicts "non-cancer" gets 99% accuracy.

### 2.1.2 Precision (Positive Predictive Value)
*"Of all the samples I labeled positive, how many are truly positive?"*

$$\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$$

### 2.1.3 Recall (Sensitivity / True Positive Rate)
*"Of all the actual positive samples, how many did I correctly find?"*

$$\text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$$

### 2.1.4 F1-Score
The **harmonic mean** of Precision and Recall — penalizes extreme imbalance between them.

$$F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{\text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Generalized Fβ Score:** $F_\beta = (1 + \beta^2) \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{\beta^2 P + R}$. When $\beta = 1$, equal weight; $\beta > 1$ favors Recall.

### 2.1.5 Specificity & False Positive Rate
$$\text{Specificity} = \frac{TN}{TN + FP}, \quad \text{FPR} = 1 - \text{Specificity} = \frac{FP}{TN + FP}$$

### 2.1.6 ROC Curve & AUC
By varying the **decision threshold** from 0 to 1, we plot Recall (TPR) vs FPR. The **Area Under Curve (AUC)** summarizes performance across all thresholds.

$$\text{AUC} = \int_0^1 \text{TPR}(\text{FPR}) \, d(\text{FPR})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **AUC Interpretation:** AUC = **0.5** means random guessing; AUC = **1.0** means perfect classifier. AUC is **threshold-independent** and **scale-invariant**.

---

## 2.2 Regression Metrics — Theory & Formulas

For tasks like depth estimation, age prediction, image quality assessment, the target is continuous, so classification metrics do not apply.

### 2.2.1 Mean Squared Error (MSE)
$$MSE = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$$

### 2.2.2 Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE)
$$RMSE = \sqrt{MSE} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}$$

### 2.2.3 Mean Absolute Error (MAE)
$$MAE = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert y_i - \hat{y}_i \vert$$

### 2.2.4 Coefficient of Determination ($R^2$ Score)
$$R^2 = 1 - \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \bar{y})^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> $R^2 = 1$ is perfect; $R^2 = 0$ means model is no better than the mean; $R^2 < 0$ means model is worse than predicting the mean.

---

## 2.3 Detection & Segmentation Metrics

### 2.3.1 Intersection over Union (IoU / Jaccard Index)
For object detection bounding boxes or segmentation masks:

$$\text{IoU} = \frac{\text{Area of Overlap}}{\text{Area of Union}} = \frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}$$

A prediction is "correct" if **IoU ≥ threshold** (commonly 0.5 → called **mAP@0.5**).

### 2.3.2 Dice Coefficient (F1 for segmentation)
$$\text{Dice} = \frac{2 \vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert} = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dice and IoU Relationship:** $\text{Dice} = \frac{2 \cdot \text{IoU}}{1 + \text{IoU}}$. Dice weights overlap regions more heavily than IoU.

### 2.3.3 Mean Average Precision (mAP)
The primary metric for object detectors (YOLO, Faster R-CNN, SSD):

$$mAP = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} AP_i$$

where $AP_i$ is the **Average Precision** for class $i$, computed as the area under the Precision-Recall curve.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| Metric | Formula | Range | Use Case |
|--------|---------|-------|----------|
| Accuracy | $(TP+TN)/(TP+TN+FP+FN)$ | $[0,1]$ | Balanced datasets |
| Precision | $TP/(TP+FP)$ | $[0,1]$ | When False Positives costly |
| Recall (TPR) | $TP/(TP+FN)$ | $[0,1]$ | When False Negatives costly |
| F1-Score | $2PR/(P+R)$ | $[0,1]$ | Imbalanced classes |
| Specificity (TNR) | $TN/(TN+FP)$ | $[0,1]$ | Negative class performance |
| FPR | $FP/(TN+FP)$ | $[0,1]$ | ROC Y-axis complement |
| AUC-ROC | $\int TPR \, d(FPR)$ | $[0,1]$ | Threshold-free comparison |
| MSE | $\frac{1}{n}\sum(y_i-\hat{y}_i)^2$ | $[0, \infty)$ | Regression, penalizes large errors |
| RMSE | $\sqrt{MSE}$ | $[0, \infty)$ | Same unit as target |
| MAE | $\frac{1}{n}\sum \vert y_i-\hat{y}_i \vert$ | $[0, \infty)$ | Robust to outliers |
| $R^2$ | $1 - SS_{res}/SS_{tot}$ | $(-\infty, 1]$ | Variance explained |
| IoU | $\vert A \cap B \vert / \vert A \cup B \vert$ | $[0,1]$ | Bounding boxes, masks |
| Dice | $2 \vert A \cap B \vert / (\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert)$ | $[0,1]$ | Medical segmentation |
| mAP | $\frac{1}{N}\sum AP_i$ | $[0,1]$ | Object detection |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Medical Imaging**: Dice Score and IoU are gold standards for tumor segmentation (U-Net, nnU-Net benchmarks).
- **Autonomous Vehicles**: mAP@0.5:0.95 (COCO metric) evaluates detectors across IoU thresholds.
- **Aerial Surveillance**: Recall is prioritized — missing a small UAV is catastrophic; a few extra detections are tolerable.
- **Image Quality**: PSNR and SSIM evaluate super-resolution and denoising models.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of F1-Score from Precision and Recall

We want a single metric combining Precision $P$ and Recall $R$. The **arithmetic mean** is suboptimal — if $P=1$ and $R=0$, arithmetic mean is $0.5$, falsely suggesting "OK" performance.

**Step 1:** Use the **harmonic mean** because it punishes the smaller of the two values heavily.

$$\text{HM} = \frac{2}{\frac{1}{P} + \frac{1}{R}}$$

**Step 2:** Simplify by taking common denominator:

$$F_1 = \frac{2}{\frac{1}{P} + \frac{1}{R}} = \frac{2}{\frac{R + P}{PR}} = \frac{2PR}{P + R}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute into Confusion Matrix entries:

$$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}$$

This is the **Dice Coefficient** form, proving the deep link between classification F1 and segmentation Dice.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of mAP (Mean Average Precision)

**Step 1:** For one class, sort predictions by **descending confidence score**.

**Step 2:** Compute Precision and Recall at each rank:

$$\text{Precision}(k) = \frac{TP(k)}{TP(k) + FP(k)}, \quad \text{Recall}(k) = \frac{TP(k)}{\text{Total Positives}}$$

**Step 3:** Plot the **Precision-Recall curve** and integrate:

$$AP = \int_0^1 P(R) \, dR$$

**Step 4:** Approximate using the **11-point interpolated AP** (Pascal VOC legacy):

$$AP = \frac{1}{11} \sum_{r \in \{0, 0.1, \dots, 1.0\}} P_{\text{interp}}(r)$$

where $P_{\text{interp}}(r) = \max_{r' \geq r} P(r')$.

**Step 5:** Average AP across all $N$ classes:

$$mAP = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} AP_i$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Case Study

> [!NOTE]
> **Case Study Setup:** We compare **three image classifiers** — Logistic Regression (LR), Random Forest (RF), and a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) — on the **CIFAR-10** dataset (10 classes, 60,000 images). For this analytical exercise, we focus on the **binary sub-problem: "Cat" vs "Not-Cat"** using a held-out test set of **1000 images**.

### 3.3.1 Confusion Matrices (Hypothetical Test Results)

| Model | TP | FN | FP | TN |
|-------|----|----|----|----|
| Logistic Regression | 420 | 80 | 90 | 410 |
| Random Forest | 460 | 40 | 60 | 440 |
| CNN (ResNet-18) | 485 | 15 | 25 | 475 |

Total positives (actual cats) = 500; Total negatives = 500.

### 3.3.2 Step-by-Step Metric Computation

**Logistic Regression:**
$$\text{Accuracy} = \frac{420 + 410}{1000} = \frac{830}{1000} = 0.830$$
$$\text{Precision} = \frac{420}{420 + 90} = \frac{420}{510} = 0.824$$
$$\text{Recall} = \frac{420}{420 + 80} = \frac{420}{500} = 0.840$$
$$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot 0.824 \cdot 0.840}{0.824 + 0.840} = \frac{1.384}{1.664} = 0.832$$

**Random Forest:**
$$\text{Accuracy} = \frac{460 + 440}{1000} = \frac{900}{1000} = 0.900$$
$$\text{Precision} = \frac{460}{460 + 60} = \frac{460}{520} = 0.885$$
$$\text{Recall} = \frac{460}{460 + 40} = \frac{460}{500} = 0.920$$
$$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot 0.885 \cdot 0.920}{0.885 + 0.920} = \frac{1.628}{1.805} = 0.902$$

**CNN (ResNet-18):**
$$\text{Accuracy} = \frac{485 + 475}{1000} = \frac{960}{1000} = 0.960$$
$$\text{Precision} = \frac{485}{485 + 25} = \frac{485}{510} = 0.951$$
$$\text{Recall} = \frac{485}{485 + 15} = \frac{485}{500} = 0.970$$
$$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot 0.951 \cdot 0.970}{0.951 + 0.970} = \frac{1.845}{1.921} = 0.961$$

### 3.3.3 Comparative Result Matrix

| Metric | Logistic Regression | Random Forest | CNN (ResNet-18) |
|--------|---------------------|---------------|------------------|
| Accuracy | 0.830 | 0.900 | **0.960** |
| Precision | 0.824 | 0.885 | **0.951** |
| Recall | 0.840 | 0.920 | **0.970** |
| F1-Score | 0.832 | 0.902 | **0.961** |
| Specificity | 0.820 | 0.880 | **0.950** |

**Inference:** The CNN clearly outperforms classical ML baselines on CIFAR-10. However, if the application demands *zero false negatives* (e.g., security threat detection), we should look at **Recall** specifically, where CNN's 0.970 is acceptable.

### 3.3.4 AUC-ROC Computation (Conceptual)

Plotting **TPR vs FPR** by varying the classification threshold from 0.1 to 0.9 yields the following AUC values:

| Model | AUC-ROC |
|-------|---------|
| Logistic Regression | 0.880 |
| Random Forest | 0.948 |
| CNN (ResNet-18) | **0.989** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> AUC values confirm CNN's superiority is **threshold-independent**, not an artifact of a particular cutoff.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Model Evaluation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Image Dataset] --> B[Train Validation Test Split 70 15 15]
    B --> C1[Model A Logistic Regression]
    B --> C2[Model B Random Forest]
    B --> C3[Model C CNN ResNet 18]
    C1 --> D[Predictions on Test Set]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    D --> E[Build Confusion Matrix]
    E --> F[Compute Classification Metrics]
    F --> G1[Accuracy]
    F --> G2[Precision]
    F --> G3[Recall]
    F --> G4[F1 Score]
    F --> G5[AUC ROC]
    G1 --> H[Comparative Analysis Table]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    G4 --> H
    G5 --> H
    H --> I[Select Best Model for Deployment]
```

## 4.2 Confusion Matrix Block Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Actual Positive] -->|Model Says Positive| B[True Positive TP]
    A -->|Model Says Negative| C[False Negative FN]
    D[Actual Negative] -->|Model Says Positive| E[False Positive FP]
    D -->|Model Says Negative| F[True Negative TN]
    B --> G[Correct Detection]
    F --> G
    C --> H[Missed Detection Type II Error]
    E --> I[False Alarm Type I Error]
```

## 4.3 Metric Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start What Is The Task] --> B{Classification}
    A --> C{Regression}
    A --> D{Detection or Segmentation}
    B --> B1{Balanced Dataset}
    B --> B2{Imbalanced Dataset}
    B1 --> R1[Use Accuracy]
    B2 --> R2[Use F1 Score and AUC ROC]
    B2 --> R3[Examine Precision Recall Tradeoff]
    C --> C1{Outliers Sensitive}
    C1 --> C2[Use MSE or RMSE]
    C1 --> C3[Use MAE for Robustness]
    D --> D1{Bounding Boxes}
    D --> D2{Pixel Masks}
    D1 --> R4[Use mAP and IoU]
    D2 --> R5[Use Dice and IoU]
```

## 4.4 ROC-AUC Curve Conceptual Sketch

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Threshold 0.0 Recall 1.0 FPR 1.0] --> B[Threshold Increases]
    B --> C[Recall Decreases]
    B --> D[FPR Decreases]
    C --> E[Curve Sweeps Toward Origin]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Area Under Curve Equals AUC]
    F --> G{AUC Value}
    G -->|0.5| H[Random Guess]
    G -->|0.7 to 0.8| I[Fair]
    G -->|0.8 to 0.9| J[Good]
    G -->|0.9 to 1.0| K[Excellent]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between Precision and Recall. Why is Accuracy insufficient for imbalanced datasets?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Precision** $= TP / (TP + FP)$ — measures, of all predicted positives, how many are truly positive. (1 mark)
- **Recall** $= TP / (TP + FN)$ — measures, of all actual positives, how many we found. (1 mark)
- **Accuracy** fails on imbalanced data because a trivial model predicting the majority class yields deceptively high accuracy (e.g., 99% in a 99:1 imbalance) while completely failing the minority class. **F1-Score** is preferred as it balances both errors. (1 mark)

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define IoU and Dice Coefficient. Show their mathematical relationship.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **IoU** (Intersection over Union) $= \vert A \cap B \vert / \vert A \cup B \vert$ — measures overlap between predicted and ground truth regions. (1 mark)
- **Dice Coefficient** $= 2 \vert A \cap B \vert / (\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert)$ — harmonic form of overlap measure. (1 mark)
- **Relationship:** Using $|A \cup B| = |A| + |B| - |A \cap B|$, substituting into IoU and simplifying yields $\text{Dice} = 2 \cdot \text{IoU} / (1 + \text{IoU})$. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the Confusion Matrix with a neat diagram. Derive the formulas for Accuracy, Precision, Recall, and F1-Score. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Confusion Matrix (2 marks):**
A Confusion Matrix is an $N \times N$ table comparing predicted vs actual classes. For binary classification:

| | Predicted Positive | Predicted Negative |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual Positive** | TP | FN |
| **Actual Negative** | FP | TN |

- **TP** = correctly predicted positives
- **TN** = correctly predicted negatives
- **FP** = Type I error (false alarm)
- **FN** = Type II error (missed detection)

**2. Derivations (5 marks):**

$$\text{Accuracy} = \frac{\text{Correct Predictions}}{\text{Total Predictions}} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$$

$$\text{Precision} = \frac{\text{True Positives}}{\text{All Predicted Positives}} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$$

$$\text{Recall} = \frac{\text{True Positives}}{\text{All Actual Positives}} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$$

$$F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{\text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}$$

F1 is the **harmonic mean** of Precision and Recall, chosen because it heavily penalizes imbalance — if either metric is 0, F1 collapses to 0.

**(b)** With a suitable case study, compare the performance of three different computer vision models (Logistic Regression, Random Forest, CNN) on the CIFAR-10 dataset. Discuss which metrics you would use and justify. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Case Study Setup:** CIFAR-10 has 60,000 32×32 color images across 10 classes (50,000 train + 10,000 test). For binary comparison ("Cat" vs "Not-Cat"), we use 1000 test samples (500 per class).

**Step 1 — Train Three Models (2 marks):**
- Logistic Regression (linear baseline)
- Random Forest (200 trees, classical ensemble)
- CNN (ResNet-18 fine-tuned)

**Step 2 — Generate Confusion Matrices & Metrics (3 marks):**

| Metric | Logistic Regression | Random Forest | CNN |
|--------|---------------------|---------------|-----|
| Accuracy | 0.830 | 0.900 | **0.960** |
| Precision | 0.824 | 0.885 | **0.951** |
| Recall | 0.840 | 0.920 | **0.970** |
| F1-Score | 0.832 | 0.902 | **0.961** |
| AUC-ROC | 0.880 | 0.948 | **0.989** |

**Step 3 — Metric Justification & Conclusion (2 marks):**
- **F1-Score and AUC-ROC** are chosen because CIFAR-10 classes are roughly balanced but each binary sub-task may have subtle skew.
- **CNN wins decisively** due to its ability to learn hierarchical spatial features.
- For high-stakes CV applications (medical diagnosis, surveillance), **Recall should be weighted higher** since missing a critical class is more dangerous than false alarms.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** What is the ROC Curve? Explain AUC and its significance. Derive the relationship between TPR and FPR. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. ROC Curve (2 marks):**
The **Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC)** curve plots **True Positive Rate (Recall)** on the Y-axis against **False Positive Rate** on the X-axis as the classification threshold varies from 0 to 1.

**2. TPR and FPR (2 marks):**
$$TPR = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}, \quad FPR = \frac{FP}{FP + TN} = 1 - \text{Specificity}$$

**3. AUC and Significance (3 marks):**
$$\text{AUC} = \int_0^1 TPR(FPR) \, d(FPR)$$

- AUC represents the **probability that a randomly chosen positive sample is ranked higher than a randomly chosen negative sample** by the classifier.
- **AUC = 1.0** → perfect classifier; **AUC = 0.5** → random guessing (diagonal line).
- AUC is **threshold-independent** and **scale-invariant**, making it ideal for comparing models across operating points.

**(b)** Discuss the mAP metric for object detection. How is it computed for a multi-class scenario? Compare YOLO and Faster R-CNN on COCO using mAP. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. mAP Definition (2 marks):**
**Mean Average Precision (mAP)** is the primary metric for object detection. It averages the **Average Precision (AP)** across all $N$ object classes:

$$mAP = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} AP_i$$

**2. AP Computation Pipeline (3 marks):**
- Sort all predictions by **descending confidence score**.
- At each rank $k$, compute Precision and Recall.
- Plot the Precision-Recall curve and compute area under it (using 11-point or all-point interpolation).
- AP is this area; mAP is the mean AP across classes.

**3. COCO Benchmark Comparison (2 marks):**
On the **COCO 2017 val** set (80 classes):

| Model | Backbone | mAP@0.5 | mAP@0.5:0.95 | FPS |
|-------|----------|---------|---------------|-----|
| YOLOv8-n | CSPDarknet | 0.637 | 0.372 | ~500 |
| YOLOv8-x | CSPDarknet | 0.789 | 0.537 | ~80 |
| Faster R-CNN | ResNet-101 | 0.621 | 0.400 | ~10 |
| DETR | ResNet-50 | 0.620 | 0.420 | ~25 |

**Conclusion:** YOLO models offer best **real-time speed**; Faster R-CNN/DETR offer better **small-object accuracy**. mAP@0.5:0.95 (averaging across IoU thresholds 0.5 to 0.95) is stricter than mAP@0.5.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Precision with Recall** — writing Precision formula when asked for Recall (or vice versa) costs the entire 2-mark formula credit.
> 2. **Ignoring dataset imbalance** — quoting Accuracy alone for imbalanced CV datasets like medical imaging is a 1-mark deduction.
> 3. **No justification of metric choice** — KTU 2024 OBE explicitly tests "Justify why F1-Score over Accuracy". Always write a 2-line reasoning.
> 4. **Missing the threshold-independence of AUC** — forgetting to mention this in ROC-AUC answers results in partial credit loss.
> 5. **Confusing Dice and IoU** — these are related but **not equal**. Always derive the relationship explicitly.
> 6. **mAP calculation errors** — forgetting to sort by confidence score before computing AP loses 2 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**
> - **Accuracy** = overall correctness; fails on imbalance.
> - **Precision** = how reliable a positive prediction is ($TP/(TP+FP)$).
> - **Recall (TPR)** = how well we find all positives ($TP/(TP+FN)$).
> - **F1-Score** = harmonic mean of P & R; use for imbalance.
> - **Specificity (TNR)** = $TN/(TN+FP)$; complement of FPR.
> - **ROC-AUC** = threshold-independent; AUC > 0.9 is excellent.
> - **MSE** penalizes large errors quadratically; **MAE** is robust to outliers.
> - **$R^2$** can be negative (worse than mean baseline).
> - **IoU** for bounding boxes & segmentation; **Dice** for medical masks.
> - **mAP** = mean of per-class AP; **mAP@0.5:0.95** is the COCO standard.
> - **Always justify metric choice** in KTU 2024 Scheme answers (OBE rubric).
> - **Case Study Format:** Dataset description → Model list → Confusion Matrix → Metric computation → Comparative table → Inference.
> - **Golden Rule:** No single metric is sufficient — always use a basket (Accuracy + F1 + AUC for classification; IoU + Dice for segmentation).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
